| T (twist) | T_shaft | About axle axis | Differential |
| M_b (bend) | W·a | About lateral axis | Axle housing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three types of loads acting on a drive axle and state the physical origin of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Vertical Load (W):** Originates from the static weight of the vehicle transferred to the axle through the springs. It causes bending of the axle housing. *(1 mark)*
2. **Tractive / Driving Force (F_t):** Originates from the engine torque reacted at the tyre contact patch. It causes longitudinal loading on the axle housing and torsion in the half-shaft. *(1 mark)*
3. **Lateral / Cornering Force (F_y):** Originates from the centrifugal effect during turning. It produces side thrust on the axle beam. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> Students often write "engine weight" — this is **wrong**. The drive axle does not carry engine weight directly; the engine is supported by the chassis/sub-frame.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the equivalent bending moment for a shaft subjected to combined bending and torsion? Write the formula based on maximum shear stress theory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equivalent bending moment $M_e$ is a single fictitious bending moment that produces the **same maximum shear stress** as the combined action of an actual bending moment $M_b$ and a torque $T$. *(2 marks)*

**Formula (M.S.S.T.):**

$$
M_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}
$$

*(1 mark)* — The unit is N·m.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 Marks, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the **bending moment acting on the axle housing at the bearing seat**. State clearly all the assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A solid circular drive axle shaft is subjected to a bending moment of 800 N·m and a torque of 1400 N·m. The shaft is made of AISI 4140 steel with a yield strength of 415 MPa. Using the maximum shear stress theory and a factor of safety of 3, determine the **required shaft diameter**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Free-body description (2 marks):** The drive axle housing is treated as a simply supported beam between the spring seat (point A) and the wheel centre (point C). The bearing (point B) lies between A and C at a horizontal distance *a* from C. The wheel reaction R = W acts vertically upward at C; the spring provides reaction at A.

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The axle housing is treated as a rigid body in static equilibrium.
2. The dynamic effects are neglected in this static derivation.
3. The wheel reaction is assumed to act at a single point (point contact).

**Derivation (4 marks):**  
Taking moments about the spring seat A:

$$
\sum M_A = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R \cdot L_{AC} - F_B \cdot L_{AB} = 0
$$

Since the entire wheel reaction passes through the bearing B:

$$
F_B = W, \quad M_b = W \cdot a
$$

The **maximum bending moment occurs at the bearing seat B** because the lever arm to the wheel reaction is maximum there. This is the value used in the design of the axle housing.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
$M_b = 800 \; N\!\cdot\!m$, $T = 1400 \; N\!\cdot\!m$, $\sigma_y = 415 \times 10^6 \; Pa$, $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — Equivalent moment (2 marks):**

$$
M_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{800^2 + 1400^2} = \sqrt{640000 + 1960000} = \sqrt{2600000}
$$

$$
M_e = 1612.45 \; N\!\cdot\!m
$$

**Step 3 — Permissible bending stress (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma_{perm} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{415 \times 10^6}{3} = 138.33 \times 10^6 \; Pa
$$

**Step 4 — Required diameter (3 marks):**

$$
d = \left( \frac{16 \, M_e}{\pi \, \sigma_{perm}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{16 \times 1612.45}{\pi \times 138.33 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
d = \left( \frac{25799.2}{4.346 \times 10^8} \right)^{1/3} = (5.937 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, d \approx 0.039 \; m = 39 \; mm \,}
$$

Adopt the next standard size: **d = 40 mm**.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks, Module 3 — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **six major loads acting on a drive axle**. Describe how each load is generated. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A rear-wheel-drive truck has the following data: engine torque $T_e = 320 \; N\!\cdot\!m$, 1st gear ratio $i_g = 4.2$, final drive ratio $i_f = 4.8$, efficiency $\eta = 0.88$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.42 \; m$. Compute the **(i) tractive force at the wheel, (ii) longitudinal reaction on the axle housing, and (iii) maximum torsional shear stress in a solid half-shaft of diameter 42 mm.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

The six major loads are:

| # | Load | Generation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical weight W | Static vehicle mass transferred via springs |
| 2 | Dynamic weight W_dyn | Acceleration / braking / cornering |
| 3 | Tractive force F_t | Engine torque at contact patch |
| 4 | Torsional moment T | Powertrain torque through differential |
| 5 | Cornering force F_y | Centrifugal acceleration in turns |
| 6 | Shock load | Road irregularities, potholes |

*(1 mark per load, with a clear two-line description for each; 1 mark reserved for the block diagram and labelling.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**(i) Tractive force (2 marks):**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta}{r_w} = \frac{320 \times 4.2 \times 4.8 \times 0.88}{0.42}
$$

$$
F_t = \frac{5681.15}{0.42} = 13526.5 \; N \approx 13.53 \; kN
$$

**(ii) Longitudinal reaction (1 mark):**  
By Newton's third law, the longitudinal reaction on the axle housing from the ground equals $F_t = 13.53 \; kN$ (acting backward on the housing).

**(iii) Half-shaft torque (2 marks):**

$$
T_{shaft} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta}{2} = \frac{5681.15}{2} = 2840.6 \; N\!\cdot\!m
$$

**(iv) Torsional shear stress (2 marks):**

$$
\tau = \frac{16 \, T_{shaft}}{\pi \, d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2840.6}{\pi \times (0.042)^3}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{45449.6}{\pi \times 7.4088 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{45449.6}{2.3273 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \tau \approx 195.3 \; MPa \,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Forgetting to convert mm to m in $d^3$ is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks. Always write $d = 0.042$ m explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety:** The formula $d = (16 M_e / \pi \sigma_y)^{1/3}$ gives an *unsafe* diameter. You **must** divide $\sigma_y$ by $n$ to get $\sigma_{perm}$.
> 3. **Differential Split:** When calculating shaft torque, divide by 2. Students often write the full $T_{diff}$ as the half-shaft torque — losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Bending vs. Torsion Confusion:** If the question says *"axle housing"*, it usually wants $M_b$. If it says *"half-shaft"*, it usually wants $T$. Mixed-up answers = partial mark.
> 5. **Missing the Offset *a*:** When computing $M_b = W \cdot a$, never use the wheelbase. *a* is the offset between wheel centre and bearing — typically 75–150 mm.
> 6. **No Damping Factor:** Drive axles experience dynamic amplification of 1.5–2.0 × during hard braking. KTU 2024 may ask you to apply this; if so, multiply $M_b$ by 2.0.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] A drive axle is a **multi-load member**: bending + torsion + lateral + shock — simultaneously.
- [x] **Bending moment** is maximum at the **bearing seat** of the axle housing; formula: $M_b = W \cdot a$.
- [x] **Torsional moment** on a half-shaft: $T = (T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta) / 2$.
- [x] **Tractive force** at contact patch: $F_t = T_{shaft} / r_w$.
- [x] **Cornering force** (lateral): $F_y = m \cdot V^2 / R$.
- [x] **Equivalent bending moment** by M.S.S.T.: $M_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$.
- [x] **Equivalent moment** by D.E.N. (distortion energy): $M_e' = 0.35 \, M_b + 0.65 \, T$.
- [x] **Shaft diameter design** (solid circular): $d = \left( \dfrac{16 \, n \, M_e}{\pi \, \sigma_y} \right)^{1/3}$.
- [x] Always use **$\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y / n$**, never $\sigma_y$ directly.
- [x] **Standard load distribution:** 55–65 % of total weight rests on a drive axle in a RWD car.
- [x] Hollow shafts are stiffer and lighter than solid shafts of the same mass — preferred in modern EVs.
- [x] The **differential splits torque equally** between left and right wheels in straight-line motion; in cornering, the *inner* wheel gets less torque (open differential).
- [x] **Factor of safety** in automotive design: 2.5 (passenger) to 4.0 (heavy commercial / off-road).
- [x] **Stress concentration** at the bearing seat and at the U-joint splines reduces fatigue life; KTU may ask for K_t factor.
- [x] **Forgetting unit conversion** (mm → m) is the most common mark-deduction — audit your units *before* submitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rear Axle Shaft Supporting Types - Semi Floating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rear Axle Shaft Supporting Types - Semi Floating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Rear Axle Shaft Support?

The **Rear Axle Shaft** (also called the **Half Shaft** or **Drive Axle**) is the final link in the powertrain. It transfers the driving torque from the **Differential** to the **Rear Wheels**, while simultaneously supporting the vertical and lateral loads transmitted from the road surface through the wheel assembly.

The way this shaft is **supported** (i.e., how the wheels are mounted relative to it) determines the **Axle Support Type**. This directly affects:
- Load-carrying capacity
- Safety in case of shaft failure
- Manufacturing cost and complexity
- Vehicle performance envelope

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three classical support types are:
> 1. **Full-Floating Axle Shaft** (heaviest duty)
> 2. **Three-Quarter Floating Axle Shaft** (intermediate)
> 3. **Semi-Floating Axle Shaft** (light-duty, most common in passenger cars)

---

## 1.2 The Three Axle Support Types — At a Glance

| Support Type | Carries Vertical Load? | Carries Lateral Load? | Wheel Mounting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Floating** | No (carried by hub bearings) | No | Mounted on a separate hub via 2 bearings |
| **3/4 Floating** | Yes (partially) | Partially | Mounted on hub via 1 bearing (inner side) |
| **Semi-Floating** | Yes (entirely) | Yes (entirely) | Mounted directly on shaft itself (integral hub) |

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Semi-Floating Axle Shaft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **Semi-Floating Axle Shaft** is a type of rear axle in which the axle shaft itself carries the **full vertical load** (sprung weight of the vehicle) and the **lateral (cornering/braking) forces**, in addition to transmitting the **driving torque** from the differential to the road wheels. The shaft is supported on the inboard side by the **differential side gear spline** and on the outboard side by a **single bearing** (usually a **tapered roller bearing** or **ball bearing**) located inside the axle housing. The wheel hub is **integral** (forged as one piece) with the axle shaft.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Loaded Spinning Skewer" 🍢

Imagine a **kebab skewer** passing through a **heavy meatball** and **spinning rapidly**:
- The skewer = the axle shaft
- The meatball = the wheel + tire
- Your hand pinching the inboard end = the differential side gear
- A small bush (single bearing) at the outboard end = the single outboard bearing

The skewer itself must:
1. **Spin** to deliver rotational force (transmit torque)
2. **Hold up** the meatball's weight (carry vertical load)
3. **Resist sideways pushing** when the meatball drags sideways (lateral loads)

This is exactly what a semi-floating axle does — it is a **dual-duty** shaft. Because it does everything at once, if it ever breaks, the wheel **falls off the car**, which is the major safety drawback.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Material commonly used:** Medium-carbon alloy steel (e.g., **AISI 4140**, **EN8**, or **AISI 1045**)
- **Surface hardness after induction hardening:** typically **50–60 HRC**
- **Case depth:** typically **3–5 mm**
- **Shaft diameter range (typical):** **25 mm to 45 mm** depending on vehicle class
- **Allowable shear stress (steel):** **55–80 MPa**
- **Allowable bending stress (steel):** **120–180 MPa**
- **Combined shock & fatigue factor (K):** typically **1.5 to 3.0** (semi-floating uses lower K than full-floating)

---

## 1.6 Applications in Industry

| Vehicle Category | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| Economy Hatchbacks (Maruti Alto, Hyundai Eon) | **Semi-Floating** |
| Compact Sedans (Honda City, Toyota Etios) | **Semi-Floating** |
| Mid-size SUVs (Tata Nexon) | **Semi-Floating / 3/4 Floating** |
| Heavy Commercial Trucks | **Full-Floating** |
| Military / Off-road vehicles | **Full-Floating** |
| Premium luxury sedans (BMW 5 Series, Mercedes E-Class) | **Independent Rear Suspension → Semi-Floating** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** For a passenger car, **always assume semi-floating** unless specifically stated otherwise. This is the *default* in KTU numerical problems unless the question specifies otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of a Semi-Floating Axle

The semi-floating axle is mechanically the **simplest** of the three types. Let us trace its anatomy from the differential outward:

1. **Inner End (Inboard Spline):**
   - The axle shaft has an **internal or external spline** that mates with the **differential side gear**.
   - This is the inner **journal support** — it is a **sliding fit** (allows small axial movement for differential action).

2. **Shaft Body:**
   - Cylindrical, often **hollow** in modern designs (weight reduction by up to **20–25%**).
   - Heat-treated surface for fatigue resistance.

3. **Outboard Bearing:**
   - A **single bearing** — usually **tapered roller** (handles combined radial + thrust) or **deep-groove ball bearing** (radial only).
   - Housed inside the **axle housing tube**.
   - Bearing is pressed onto the shaft and retained by a **circlip** or **nut**.

4. **Wheel Mounting:**
   - The shaft **outer end** is **flanged** or has **lugs/studs** welded.
   - **Wheel and brake drum/disc are mounted directly on this flange**.
   - No separate hub — hub is **integral** with the shaft.

5. **Oil Seal:**
   - A **lip seal** or **O-ring** at the outboard bearing location prevents gear oil leakage.

---

## 2.2 Forces Acting on a Semi-Floating Axle Shaft

The semi-floating axle must withstand **three concurrent loading systems**:

### A. Vertical Load (Bending)
- Each wheel carries **W/2**, where **W = Total Sprung Weight on Rear Axle**
- This load is applied at the **wheel center** and reacted at the **differential housing**.
- Produces a **bending moment** along the shaft length.

### B. Driving Torque (Torsion)
- Transmitted from the differential side gear to the wheel.
- Equal to **T = (Engine Torque × Gear Ratio × Differential Ratio × η)** at the wheel.

### C. Lateral/Thrust Loads
- **Braking thrust** (deceleration): $F_t = \mu \cdot W/2$
- **Cornering force**: $F_c = m \cdot a_{lateral}$
- **Wind / camber thrust**: minor in most cases.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify the supports:**
- The shaft is supported at **two points**:
  - **Point A** (inboard): Differential side gear (acts like a journal)
  - **Point B** (outboard bearing): Single tapered/ball bearing

**Step 2 — Identify the loads:**
- Vertical wheel load **W/2** at the wheel mounting flange
- Torque **T** along the shaft
- Lateral load **F** at the wheel flange

**Step 3 — Determine critical section:**
- The critical section is **just inboard of the outboard bearing**, because this is where the **bending moment is maximum** AND the **shaft cross-section is smallest** (after the flange).

**Step 4 — Combine stresses:**
- Bending stress + Torsional shear stress
- Use **Principal Stress Theory** (Rankine's) or **Max Shear Stress Theory** (Guest's / Tresca's)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — every KTU numerical on axle shaft design uses these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical load per wheel | $F_v = \dfrac{W}{2}$ | N | W = static rear axle load |
| 2 | Bending moment (at bearing) | $M = F_v \cdot L$ | N·mm | L = distance from bearing to wheel center |
| 3 | Driving torque at wheel | $T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{1}$ | N·mm | $i_g$ = gear ratio, $i_0$ = diff. ratio |
| 4 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ | MPa | d = shaft outer diameter |
| 5 | Torsional shear stress (solid) | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | MPa | Solid shaft |
| 6 | Torsional shear stress (hollow) | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T \cdot d_o}{\pi (d_o^{4} - d_i^{4})}$ | MPa | Hollow shaft |
| 7 | Principal (max) stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$ | MPa | Rankine / Normal Stress Theory |
| 8 | Max shear stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$ | MPa | Tresca / Guest Theory |
| 9 | Equivalent torque | $T_e = \sqrt{(K_b \cdot M)^{2} + (K_t \cdot T)^{2}}$ | N·mm | K = combined shock & fatigue factor |
| 10 | Equivalent torque (simple) | $T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ | N·mm | When K_b = K_t = 1 |
| 11 | Required shaft diameter (solid) | $d^{3} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}$ | mm³ | Design formula |
| 12 | Combined factor formula | $\dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \leq 1$ | – | Failure criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shock & Fatigue Factor (K):**
> - Semi-floating (passenger car): **K = 1.5 – 2.0**
> - 3/4 Floating: **K = 1.5 – 1.75**
> - Full Floating: **K = 1.25 – 1.5**
> - Commercial trucks: **K = 2.0 – 3.0**

---

## 2.5 Why Use the Equivalent Torque Method?

In KTU problems, we reduce the combined bending + torsion problem into an **equivalent pure-torsion** problem. This works because:

- The bending moment $M$ can be visualized as creating an equivalent torque.
- The combined stress ellipse equation reduces to a clean scalar formula.
- It is conservative and easy to use for shaft sizing.

**Engineering Utility:**
This formula is used in **all rotating shaft designs** — not just automobiles. The same approach is used in:
- Drive shafts (propeller shafts)
- Transmission countershafts
- Pump shafts
- Turbine shafts
- Industrial gearbox output shafts
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bending Stress on Semi-Floating Axle

### Assumptions
- Shaft is treated as a **simply-supported beam** of length $L$ (outboard bearing to wheel center distance).
- Vertical load $W/2$ acts at the wheel center (free end overhang).
- Shaft is solid, circular cross-section of diameter $d$.

### Free Body Diagram (Conceptual)

```
Differential (A)                              Wheel (Flange)
    ●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●
    ▲                                             ↓ W/2
    Reaction R_A = W/2                           
    (at side gear)                               
```

### Bending Moment Calculation

Taking moments about the outboard bearing (Point B):
- $M_B$ = Force × perpendicular distance
- $M_B = (W/2) \cdot L$

This bending moment is **maximum at the outboard bearing location** (where the shaft is smallest in cross-section after the flange).

### Bending Stress

For a circular cross-section, the section modulus is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}/64}{d/2} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}
$$

Therefore, bending stress:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M \cdot 32}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Final Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torsional Shear Stress

### Concept
Torsion arises because the differential applies a torque $T$ at the inboard end, and the road applies an equal-and-opposite reaction torque at the tire contact patch.

### Polar Section Modulus
For a solid circular shaft:

$$
Z_p = \dfrac{J}{c} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}/32}{d/2} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}
$$

### Torsional Shear Stress

$$
\tau = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} = \dfrac{T \cdot 16}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Final Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}}
$$

For a **hollow shaft** with outer diameter $d_o$ and inner diameter $d_i$:

$$
\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{16 T \cdot d_o}{\pi (d_o^{4} - d_i^{4})}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Stress Using Principal Stress Theory

### Stress State at the Critical Fiber
At the surface of the shaft (the most stressed fiber), the element experiences:
- **Normal stress** $\sigma_b$ (due to bending) — **tensile on one side, compressive on the other**
- **Shear stress** $\tau$ (due to torsion)

We analyze the **tensile side**, which is more critical.

### Mohr's Circle / Principal Stress Derivation

For a 2D stress element with $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau$:

The principal stresses are given by:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$ and $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}
$$

Therefore, the **maximum principal stress** is:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}}
$$

### Failure Criterion (Rankine)

The shaft is safe if:

$$
\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_{allow}
$$

For ductile materials like steel, the **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)** criterion is more commonly used:

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} \leq \tau_{allow}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Torque (Combined Loading → Pure Torsion)

### Concept
We replace the **bending moment M** with an **equivalent torque $M_e$** that produces the same maximum shear stress.

From the pure torsion formula:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{T_e \cdot 16}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

From combined loading, max shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{16 M}{\pi d^{3} \cdot 2}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}
$$

Factoring out $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}}$:

$$
\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}
$$

Comparing with the equivalent pure-torsion form:

$$
\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}}
$$

### With Shock & Fatigue Factor
For real-world loading variability, we introduce factor $K$:

$$
\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{(K_b \cdot M)^{2} + (K_t \cdot T)^{2}}}
$$

If $K_b = K_t = K$, this simplifies to:

$$
T_e = K \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Shaft Diameter Design Equation

Starting from the equivalent torque equation and equating max shear stress to allowable stress:

$$
\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi d^{3}} \leq \tau_{allow}
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d^{3} \geq \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}
$$

**Final Design Formula:**

$$
\boxed{d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

### Problem Statement
A semi-floating rear axle shaft is to be designed for a passenger car with the following data:
- Vertical load on rear axle $W = 9000$ N
- Distance from outboard bearing to wheel center $L = 200$ mm
- Engine torque $T_e = 110$ N·m
- Gear ratio $i_g = 1.0$ (top gear, 4th gear direct drive)
- Final drive ratio $i_0 = 4.3$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta = 0.95$
- Allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa
- Combined factor $K = 1.75$

**Find:** Required shaft diameter $d$.

### Step 1 — Vertical Load per Wheel

$$
F_v = \dfrac{W}{2} = \dfrac{9000}{2} = 4500 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Bending Moment at the Bearing

$$
M = F_v \cdot L = 4500 \times 200 = 900{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 900 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 3 — Driving Torque at the Wheel

$$
T = \eta \cdot T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 = 0.95 \times 110 \times 1.0 \times 4.3 = 449.35 \text{ N·m}
$$

Converting to N·mm:

$$
T = 449.35 \times 1000 = 449{,}350 \text{ N·mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent Torque (Combined Loading)

$$
T_e = K \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}
$$

Calculating $M^2$:

$$
M^{2} = (900{,}000)^{2} = 8.1 \times 10^{11}
$$

Calculating $T^2$:

$$
T^{2} = (449{,}350)^{2} = 2.0192 \times 10^{11}
$$

Sum:

$$
M^{2} + T^{2} = 8.1 \times 10^{11} + 2.0192 \times 10^{11} = 1.01192 \times 10^{12}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = 1{,}005{,}944 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Apply factor:

$$
T_e = 1.75 \times 1{,}005{,}944 = 1{,}760{,}402 \text{ N·mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Required Shaft Diameter

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \dfrac{16 \times 1{,}760{,}402}{\pi \times 60}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{28{,}166{,}432}{188.496} = 149{,}452.8 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{149{,}452.8} = 53.04 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Standard Size Selection

The next standard size would be **$d = 55$ mm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Always round **up** to the next standard size (preferred numbers: 25, 28, 30, 32, 35, 36, 40, 42, 45, 48, 50, 55, 60 mm). Examiners specifically look for this step.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Three Axle Types

| Parameter | Semi-Floating | 3/4 Floating | Full Floating |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of bearings | 1 (outboard) | 2 (1 inner + 1 outer hub) | 2 (both on hub) |
| Shaft carries vertical load? | **Yes (full)** | Yes (partial) | **No** |
| Shaft carries lateral load? | **Yes** | Partial | No |
| Wheel mounting on separate hub? | No (integral) | Partially | Yes |
| Safety if shaft breaks | Wheel detaches | Hub holds wheel | Hub holds wheel |
| Typical application | Passenger cars | Light trucks | Heavy trucks |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| K factor | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.5 – 1.75 | 1.25 – 1.5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Anatomy of a Semi-Floating Axle Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Differential Housing] -->|Inboard Spline| B[Axle Shaft Inner End]
    B --> C[Shaft Body Hollow Cylindrical]
    C --> D[Outboard Bearing Tapered Roller]
    D --> E[Oil Seal Lip Type]
    E --> F[Wheel Mounting Flange Integral]
    F --> G[Wheel and Tyre Assembly]
    C -.->|Tapered Surface| H[Brake Drum or Disc Mount]
    H -.-> G

    subgraph LOAD_PATHS["Force Flow Diagram"]
        direction TB
        L1[Vertical Load W over 2] --> L2[Wheel Flange]
        L3[Braking Torque T] --> L4[Shaft Body]
        L5[Cornering Force F] --> L2
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture (Load Path Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Road Surface] -->|Vertical Reaction| P2[Tyre Contact Patch]
    P2 --> P3[Wheel Flange Integral With Shaft]
    P3 --> P4[Outboard Bearing Single Point]
    P4 --> P5[Axle Housing Tube]
    P5 --> P6[Vehicle Chassis Frame]

    Q1[Engine Combustion] -->|Torque| Q2[Transmission]
    Q2 --> Q3[Propeller Shaft]
    Q3 --> Q4[Differential Pinion]
    Q4 --> Q5[Differential Side Gear]
    Q5 -->|Inboard Spline| Q6[Axle Shaft Inboard End]
    Q6 --> Q7[Shaft Body]
    Q7 --> P3
    P3 -->|Reaction Torque| P2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Axle Type] --> Q1{Vehicle Class?}
    Q1 -->|Passenger Car| Q2{Load < 10 kN per wheel?}
    Q1 -->|Commercial Truck| FULL[Full Floating]
    Q1 -->|Light Commercial| QF[Three-Quarter Floating]

    Q2 -->|Yes| SF[Semi Floating]
    Q2 -->|No| QF

    SF --> SF1[Single Outboard Bearing]
    SF1 --> SF2[Wheel Flange Integral]
    SF2 --> SF3[Cost Low Weight Low]

    QF --> QF1[Two Bearings]
    QF1 --> QF2[Hub Mounted via Bearing]
    QF2 --> QF3[Partial Load Carrying]

    FULL --> F1[Two Bearings on Hub]
    F1 --> F2[Shaft No Vertical Load]
    F2 --> F3[Maximum Safety]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Stress Element at Critical Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ELEMENT["Stress Element at Top Fiber Critical Section"]
        E1["σ_b Tensile Bending Stress acting vertically"] --> ELEMENT_CENTRE(( ))
        ELEMENT_CENTRE --> E2["τ Torsional Shear on face"]
        E2 --> E3["σ_max Principal Stress = σ_b/2 + sqrt((σ_b/2)² + τ²)"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Design Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Identify Vehicle Class and Load W] --> B[Step 2 Compute Bending Moment M = F_v × L]
    B --> C[Step 3 Compute Driving Torque T = eta × T_e × i_g × i_0]
    C --> D[Step 4 Choose Shock Factor K from Table]
    D --> E[Step 5 Compute Equivalent Torque T_e]
    E --> F[Step 6 Select Allowable Shear Stress tau_allow]
    F --> G[Step 7 Solve for d cubed = 16 T_e over pi tau_allow]
    G --> H[Step 8 Round Up to Standard Size]
    H --> I[Step 9 Check for Combined Stress using Rankine or Tresca]
    I --> J{Is sigma_max less than sigma_allow?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Design Safe]
    J -->|No| L[Increase d and Recalculate]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** What is a semi-floating rear axle shaft? State **two** of its advantages.

**Model Answer:**

> A **semi-floating rear axle shaft** is one in which the axle shaft itself carries the **full vertical weight** of the vehicle, the **lateral (cornering and braking) forces**, and also **transmits the driving torque** from the differential to the rear wheels. The shaft is supported at its inboard end by the differential side gear (via spline) and at its outboard end by a **single bearing** (tapered roller or ball bearing). The wheel hub is **integral** with the shaft.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Simple construction** — fewer parts, single bearing → low manufacturing cost.
2. **Light weight** — no separate hub → reduces unsprung mass → better ride comfort.
3. **Compact design** — fits within limited wheel-well space of passenger cars.

*(Examiner's Note: Any two clearly explained advantages fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** Differentiate between a **semi-floating** and **full-floating** rear axle with respect to:
(a) Load carrying, (b) Safety in case of shaft failure.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Semi-Floating | Full-Floating |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Load carrying | Shaft carries **vertical load, lateral load, and torque** | Shaft carries **only torque**; vertical/lateral loads carried by **hub bearings** |
| (b) Safety | Wheel **falls off** if shaft breaks (unsafe) | Wheel **remains attached** to hub even if shaft breaks (very safe) |

---

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** A semi-floating rear axle shaft is to be designed for a passenger car.
Given:
- Maximum vertical load on the rear axle: **$W = 9800$ N**
- Distance from outboard bearing to wheel center: **$L = 180$ mm**
- Maximum engine torque: **$T_e = 125$ N·m**
- Gear box ratio (top gear): **$i_g = 1.0$**
- Final drive ratio: **$i_0 = 4.1$**
- Overall efficiency: **$\eta = 0.92$**
- Allowable shear stress: **$\tau_{allow} = 65$ MPa**
- Combined shock & fatigue factor: **$K = 1.6$**

**Find:**
**(a)** The bending moment at the critical section. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The required diameter of the semi-floating axle shaft. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Bending Moment Calculation

**Step 1: Vertical load per wheel**

$$
F_v = \dfrac{W}{2} = \dfrac{9800}{2} = 4900 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Bending moment at the outboard bearing (critical section)**

The wheel center is at distance $L = 180$ mm outboard from the bearing.

$$
M = F_v \times L = 4900 \times 180 = 882{,}000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 882 \text{ N·m}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Shaft Diameter Calculation

**Step 3: Driving torque at the wheel**

$$
T = \eta \times T_e \times i_g \times i_0 = 0.92 \times 125 \times 1.0 \times 4.1
$$

$$
T = 471.5 \text{ N·m} = 471{,}500 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4: Equivalent torque (combined bending + torsion with shock factor)**

$$
T_e = K \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}
$$

Computing $M^2$:

$$
M^{2} = (882{,}000)^{2} = 7.779 \times 10^{11}
$$

Computing $T^2$:

$$
T^{2} = (471{,}500)^{2} = 2.223 \times 10^{11}
$$

Sum:

$$
M^{2} + T^{2} = 7.779 \times 10^{11} + 2.223 \times 10^{11} = 10.002 \times 10^{11}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \sqrt{10.002 \times 10^{11}} = 1{,}000{,}100 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Apply combined factor:

$$
T_e = 1.6 \times 1{,}000{,}100 = 1{,}600{,}160 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 5: Required shaft diameter from shear stress formula**

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \dfrac{16 \times 1{,}600{,}160}{\pi \times 65}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{25{,}602{,}560}{204.204} = 125{,}377 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{125{,}377} = 50.09 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Standard size selection**

Round up to next standard size: **$d = 52$ mm** *(or 55 mm depending on standard chosen)*.

$$
\boxed{d \approx 52 \text{ mm (standard)}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** (a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a semi-floating rear axle shaft**. Mention **two disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A semi-floating axle shaft of **solid cross-section** diameter **$d = 45$ mm** is subjected to:
- Bending moment $M = 600$ N·m
- Torque $T = 350$ N·m

Compute using **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)** the **factor of safety** if the yield stress in tension is **$Sy = 380$ MPa**. Take **$K = 1.5$**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working (Conceptual)

**Construction** (refer to Section 4 diagram):
- Inboard end of shaft has **splines** engaging the differential side gear.
- Outboard end has a **flange** (integral with shaft) where wheel and brake are mounted.
- A **single bearing** (tapered roller/ball) supports the shaft near the outboard end inside the axle housing.
- **Oil seal** prevents leakage of hypoid gear oil.

**Working:**
1. Driving torque flows from differential side gear → inboard spline → shaft body → wheel flange → wheel → road.
2. Vertical load from road → wheel flange → shaft → outboard bearing → axle housing → chassis.
3. Lateral/braking forces → wheel flange → shaft → bearing.

**Two Disadvantages:**
1. **Unsafe shaft failure**: If shaft breaks, wheel detaches from vehicle (critical safety issue).
2. **Limited load capacity**: Not suitable for heavy loads (typically restricted to <12 kN per wheel).

---

#### Part (b) — Factor of Safety Calculation

**Step 1: Bending stress**

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}} = \dfrac{32 \times 600{,}000}{\pi \times (45)^{3}}
$$

Computing $d^3$:

$$
d^{3} = 45^{3} = 91{,}125 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

Computing numerator:

$$
32 \times 600{,}000 = 19{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{19{,}200{,}000}{\pi \times 91{,}125} = \dfrac{19{,}200{,}000}{286{,}250} = 67.07 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Torsional shear stress**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}} = \dfrac{16 \times 350{,}000}{\pi \times 91{,}125}
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{5{,}600{,}000}{286{,}250} = 19.56 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply shock factor**

$$
\sigma_{b,\text{eff}} = K \times \sigma_b = 1.5 \times 67.07 = 100.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{\text{eff}} = K \times \tau = 1.5 \times 19.56 = 29.35 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear stress (Tresca)**

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{b,\text{eff}}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{\text{eff}}^{2}}
$$

Computing $\sigma_{b,\text{eff}}/2$:

$$
\dfrac{100.6}{2} = 50.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

Squaring:

$$
(50.3)^{2} = 2530.09
$$

$$
(29.35)^{2} = 861.42
$$

Sum:

$$
2530.09 + 861.42 = 3391.51
$$

Square root:

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{3391.51} = 58.24 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Yield shear stress (Tresca uses $Sy/2$)**

$$
S_{sy} = \dfrac{S_y}{2} = \dfrac{380}{2} = 190 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6: Factor of Safety**

$$
\text{FoS} = \dfrac{S_{sy}}{\tau_{max}} = \dfrac{190}{58.24} = 3.26
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FoS} \approx 3.26}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the shock factor K** → Maximum 2-mark deduction per question.
> 2. **Using wrong formula for hollow shaft** → Use $d_o/(d_o^4 - d_i^4)$, not just $d_o$.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up** the final diameter to the next standard size → Examiner explicitly looks for "standard size selection" step.
> 4. **Mixing Rankine and Tresca criteria** → If the question says "Maximum Shear Stress Theory", use Tresca (factor = $S_y/2$); if "Maximum Normal Stress Theory", use Rankine ($S_y$). Don't cross over.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: Always convert N·m to N·mm when using mm for diameter. Mixing up units is a 1-mark guarantee deduction.
> 6. **Incorrect application of bending moment direction**: The bending moment from vertical load is $F_v \times L$, where $L$ is the **overhang distance from the bearing to the wheel center**, not the total shaft length.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Things to Remember for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Semi-floating axle = single bearing + integral hub flange** — recognize this from any diagram.
- [ ] The shaft **carries BOTH vertical load AND driving torque** — this is its defining feature.
- [ ] Critical section is **just inboard of the outboard bearing**.
- [ ] Standard bending stress formula: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ (always mm and N).
- [ ] Standard torsional shear stress formula: $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$.
- [ ] Equivalent torque for combined loading: $T_e = K\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$.
- [ ] Shaft diameter design equation: $d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow}}}$.
- [ ] **Always round UP** to next standard diameter.
- [ ] For **Tresca theory**, yield criterion uses $S_y/2$.
- [ ] For **Rankine theory**, yield criterion uses $S_y$.
- [ ] Shock & fatigue factor for semi-floating passenger car: **K = 1.5 – 2.0**.
- [ ] Major disadvantage of semi-floating: **wheel detaches if shaft fails** (safety issue).
- [ ] Hollow shafts use $\dfrac{d_o}{d_o^{4} - d_i^{4}}$ — not solid shaft formula.
- [ ] Apply $i_g$, $i_0$, and $\eta$ correctly when computing driving torque.
- [ ] Distinguish between **inboard** (differential side) and **outboard** (wheel) ends of the shaft.
- [ ] Typical materials: medium-carbon alloy steel (AISI 4140/EN8) with induction hardening.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full Floating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Full Floating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Full Floating Axle** is a heavy-duty axle configuration in which the axle shaft is relieved of all radial and bending loads and is used **exclusively to transmit driving torque** from the differential to the driven wheels. All the vertical load, cornering (lateral) forces, and braking reactions of the vehicle are carried by the **axle housing** through a pair of **tapered roller bearings** located at the ends of the housing, into which the axle shaft is inserted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A full-floating axle is one in which the axle shaft is supported by the axle housing and is used only to transmit torque. The shaft does not carry any vehicle weight. The wheel hub rotates on bearings mounted on the axle housing stub.

**Salient Features:**
- The **axle shaft can be completely withdrawn** from the axle assembly without even removing the wheel or the hub, provided the vehicle is jacked up and the wheel nuts are removed.
- It is the **strongest and most rigid** axle arrangement, exclusively used in **heavy commercial vehicles, trucks, buses, and military vehicles**.
- Two tapered roller bearings are mounted on the **stub axles of the axle housing**, and the wheel hub is supported on these bearings.
- A **drive flange** is bolted to the outer end of the axle shaft and transmits torque to the wheel hub through bolts and studs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sword-in-Sheath" Concept**

Imagine a sword (the **axle shaft**) resting inside a hollow sheath (the **axle housing**). The sheath is bolted to your body and carries all your weight. The sword is only used to *twist* (transmit torque) and can be pulled out cleanly without disturbing the sheath or the body. In the same way, the axle shaft in a full floating setup "floats" inside the housing — it floats free of loads and only does rotational work.

Another analogy: Think of a **rotating screwdriver bit** inside a stationary drill chuck. The bit only rotates (transmits torque), while the chuck holds all the structural loads. If the bit breaks, you simply pull it out and replace it — without dismantling the drill.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Bearing Type** | **Tapered Roller Bearings** (paired, face-to-face or back-to-back) |
| **Maximum Payload per Axle** | Up to **20,000 kg** (heavy commercial applications) |
| **Typical Axle Shaft Material** | Medium Carbon Alloy Steel (e.g., **AISI 4140**, **EN19**) |
| **Surface Hardness** | **58–62 HRC** (induction hardened journals) |
| **Bearing Load Rating (Dynamic 'C')** | Typically **150 kN to 400 kN** per bearing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Breakdown (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Axle Housing (Casing):** A robust, one-piece pressed or cast steel housing. It acts as the **structural backbone** carrying the entire vehicle weight (sprung + unsprung loads), cornering forces, and brake reaction loads. The housing terminates in two **stub axle ends** which carry the bearings.

2. **Stub Axle (Housing Ends):** Machined cylindrical ends of the housing onto which the inner races of the tapered roller bearings are press-fitted. These transmit **all radial and axial loads** to the axle housing.

3. **Tapered Roller Bearings (Paired):** Two bearings per wheel end. The tapered geometry allows them to carry **combined radial + thrust loads** (vehicle weight + cornering thrust). They are **adjustable** using a lock-nut to set the correct preload.

4. **Wheel Hub:** A large cylindrical forging that carries the wheel and brake drum/disc. It rotates on the two tapered roller bearings (which are stationary relative to the housing). The hub does **not** rotate around the axle shaft — it rotates around the housing.

5. **Axle Shaft (Half-Shaft):** A splined shaft that connects the differential side gear (inner end) to the drive flange (outer end). It transmits **pure torque** — no bending, no radial force.

6. **Drive Flange:** A circular flange bolted to the outer end of the axle shaft. It is fastened to the wheel hub via **bolts and studs**. This is the only point where torque is transferred to the wheel.

7. **Wheel Bearings (Hub Bearings):** Mounted on the housing stub axles, supporting the hub.

8. **Hub Seal & Retainer:** Prevents leakage of grease and entry of dirt/water into the bearing cavity.

## 2.2 Load Distribution — The 'Why' Behind the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Principle:** In a full-floating axle, the axle shaft is **completely relieved of bending and radial loads**. Only **torsional (twisting) load** is carried by it. This is why it can be removed without disturbing the hub or the bearings.

The vehicle weight $W$ is split into:
- **Vertical Load $W_v$:** Taken by the bearings → Axle Housing → Springs/Chassis frame.
- **Cornering/Braking Thrust $F_t$:** Taken by the bearings → Axle Housing.
- **Driving Torque $T$:** Transmitted by the axle shaft → Drive flange → Hub → Wheel.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving Torque** | $T = F_t \times r_{tyre}$ | Torque transmitted by axle shaft | N·m |
| **Tangential Driving Force** | $F_t = \dfrac{T}{r_{tyre}}$ | Force at tyre contact patch | N |
| **Bending Stress in Axle Shaft** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 \times M_b}{\pi \times d^3}$ | In full-floating: $M_b \approx 0$ | N/mm² |
| **Torsional Shear Stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \times T}{\pi \times d^3}$ | Dominant stress in full-floating shaft | N/mm² |
| **Equivalent von-Mises Stress** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | For full-floating: $\sigma_{eq} \approx \sqrt{3}\,\tau$ | N/mm² |
| **Bearing Dynamic Load Rating** | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \times 10^6$ revs | $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings | revs |
| **Equivalent Bearing Load** | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Combined radial + thrust load | N |
| **Deflection of Housing** | $\delta = \dfrac{W \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Beam-bending of housing (treated as simply supported) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The critical design parameter for a full-floating axle shaft is its **torsional shear strength** $\tau$, because the shaft experiences negligible bending. This is a direct consequence of the "floating" arrangement where all radial loads bypass the shaft.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Heavy Commercial Trucks (Tata, Ashok Leyland, BharatBenz):** All heavy trucks use full-floating rear axles for the high payload capacity (10–55 tonnes).
- **Off-Road Military Vehicles (TATA Mine Protected Vehicle, Mahindra Rakshak):** Full-floating design offers field-serviceability — a broken axle shaft can be replaced without removing the wheel.
- **Buses and Coaches:** Long-wheelbase buses rely on full-floating axles for durability and ease of in-service maintenance.
- **Racing and Performance Trucks:** Where extreme torque transmission demands rigidity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stress State in a Full-Floating Axle Shaft

**Problem Setup:** A full-floating rear axle of a commercial truck transmits a maximum torque of $T_{max}$. The axle shaft is a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$. The vehicle weight is $W$ per wheel. Prove that for a full-floating configuration, the equivalent von-Mises stress reduces to $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3}\,\tau$.

**Step 1: Identify the Loads on the Axle Shaft**

In a full-floating design:
- The vehicle weight $W$ is borne entirely by the axle housing through the wheel bearings.
- The axle shaft is supported at its inner end by the differential and at its outer end by the drive flange-bolted connection (which acts as a torque-coupling, not a radial support).
- Therefore, the **bending moment** acting on the axle shaft is:

$$
M_b \approx 0
$$

**Step 2: Torsional Shear Stress**

For a solid circular shaft subjected to pure torque $T$:

$$
\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}
$$

where:
- $r$ = outer radius of shaft $= d/2$
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia $= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$

Substituting:

$$
\tau = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi \cdot d^4 / 32}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

**Step 3: Equivalent Stress (von-Mises Criterion)**

The general von-Mises equivalent stress for combined bending and torsion is:

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}
$$

where $\sigma_b$ is the bending stress given by $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}$.

**Step 4: Apply the Full-Floating Condition**

Since $M_b = 0$, we have $\sigma_b = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{0^2 + 3\tau^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3\tau^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau
$$

Numerically, $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$.

**Step 5: Substitute Back the Expression for $\tau$**

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

This is the **maximum equivalent stress** in a full-floating axle shaft.

**Step 6: Design Equation for Safe Torque Capacity**

For safe design, $\sigma_{eq} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{N}$, where $N$ is the factor of safety. Thus:

$$
\frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot T_{max}}{\pi \cdot d^3} \leq \frac{\sigma_{y}}{N}
$$

Solving for the minimum required shaft diameter:

$$
d \geq \left( \frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot N \cdot T_{max}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{y}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Example

**Given:** A truck's full-floating rear axle shaft transmits a peak torque of $T = 6{,}000$ N·m. The shaft material has a yield strength $\sigma_y = 450$ MPa. The design factor of safety is $N = 3$. Find the required minimum shaft diameter.

**Solution:**

Using the derived design equation:

$$
d \geq \left( \frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot 3 \cdot 6000}{\pi \cdot 450 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/3}
$$

First, compute the numerator:

$$
16\sqrt{3} \cdot 3 \cdot 6000 = 27.7128 \times 18000 = 498{,}830.4
$$

Then divide by the denominator:

$$
\frac{498{,}830.4}{\pi \cdot 450 \times 10^6} = \frac{498{,}830.4}{1.4137 \times 10^9} = 3.528 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Now take the cube root:

$$
d \geq (3.528 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0708 \text{ m} = 70.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt a standard size: $d = 75$ mm.**

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Full-Floating vs. Other Axle Types

| Parameter | **Full Floating** | **Three-Quarter Floating** | **Semi-Floating** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load on Shaft** | Torque only | Torque + minor thrust | Torque + bending + thrust |
| **Bearing Location** | On housing stub | On housing stub | On shaft itself |
| **Shaft Removability** | Shaft removable without wheel removal | Shaft removable after wheel removal | Shaft and hub both need removal |
| **Hub Rotation Around** | Axle housing | Axle housing | Axle shaft |
| **Bearing Type** | Tapered roller (paired) | Tapered roller (single) | Ball/roller bearing on shaft |
| **Application** | Heavy trucks, buses, military | Medium trucks | Light commercial, cars |
| **Strength** | **Highest** | High | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Architecture of a Full-Floating Axle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef shaft fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef housing fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef bearing fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hub fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wheel fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef diff fill:#DA70D6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    A[DIFFERENTIAL ASSEMBLY]:::diff
    B[AXLE SHAFT - Half Shaft]:::shaft
    C[DRIVE FLANGE]:::shaft
    D[AXLE HOUSING]:::housing
    E[STUB AXLE LEFT - Housing End]:::housing
    F[STUB AXLE RIGHT - Housing End]:::housing
    G[TAPERED ROLLER BEARING - Inner]:::bearing
    H[TAPERED ROLLER BEARING - Outer]:::bearing
    I[WHEEL HUB]:::hub
    J[BRAKE DRUM or DISC]:::hub
    K[WHEEL and TYRE]:::wheel
    L[HUB SEAL and RETAINER]:::bearing

    A -->|Inner Spline| B
    B -->|Outer Spline and Bolts| C
    C -->|Bolts and Studs| I
    D -->|Forged One-Piece| E
    D -->|Forged One-Piece| F
    E -->|Press Fit| G
    F -->|Press Fit| H
    G -->|Rotational Support| I
    H -->|Rotational Support| I
    I -->|Mounted On| J
    J -->|Attached To| K
    I -->|Sealed By| L

    L_VehicleLoad[VEHICLE WEIGHT W]:::wheel
    L_Cornering[CORNERING THRUST Ft]:::wheel
    L_Torque[DRIVING TORQUE T]:::shaft

    L_VehicleLoad -->|Transfer Path 1| D
    L_Cornering -->|Transfer Path 2| D
    L_Torque -->|Transfer Path 3| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the **three distinct load-transfer paths** — the vehicle weight and cornering thrust go through the **axle housing** (blue path), while the driving torque goes through the **axle shaft** (yellow path). The two paths are mechanically decoupled, which is the essence of the "floating" design.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#375623,stroke-width:2px

    I1[Input Torque from Differential]:::input
    P1[Torque Transmission via Axle Shaft]:::process
    P2[Load Carriage via Axle Housing]:::process
    P3[Bearing Support on Housing Stubs]:::process
    P4[Drive Flange Coupling]:::process
    O1[Wheel Hub Rotation]:::output
    O2[Vehicle Weight to Chassis]:::output

    I1 --> P1 --> P4 --> O1
    I1 -.-> P1
    P2 --> P3 --> O2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The diagram clearly shows **two parallel power/load paths** that converge only at the wheel. The torque path is decoupled from the load path — a hallmark of full-floating design.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a full-floating axle. State any two of its advantages.*

**Model Answer:**
A full-floating axle is an axle arrangement in which the axle shaft is used only to transmit driving torque, while the entire vehicle weight, cornering forces, and brake reactions are carried by the axle housing through a pair of tapered roller bearings.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. The axle shaft can be withdrawn without removing the wheel or the hub — facilitating in-field servicing.
2. The axle shaft is relieved of all bending and radial loads, so it can be of smaller diameter and lighter weight.
3. Highest load-carrying capacity — suitable for heavy commercial vehicles.
4. Maximum rigidity and durability of the axle assembly.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the main components of a full-floating rear axle assembly.*

**Model Answer:**
The main components are:
1. Axle housing (one-piece forged/cast)
2. Axle shaft (half-shaft) with splines
3. Drive flange
4. Tapered roller bearings (paired, inner and outer)
5. Wheel hub
6. Hub seal and retainer
7. Wheel studs and nuts
8. Brake drum or disc

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **full-floating rear axle**. Explain the load paths for vertical load, cornering force, and driving torque. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A full-floating axle shaft of diameter **70 mm** transmits a torque of **4,500 N·m**. The yield strength of the shaft material is **420 MPa**. Determine:
   (i) The maximum torsional shear stress in the shaft.
   (ii) The factor of safety based on the von-Mises equivalent stress criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working:**

A full-floating rear axle consists of the following components (refer to Section 4 diagram):
- A one-piece **axle housing** with stub axle ends carrying two **tapered roller bearings**.
- A **wheel hub** rotating on these bearings (mounted on the housing, not the shaft).
- An **axle shaft** that connects the differential side gear (via internal splines) to the **drive flange** at the outer end.
- The drive flange is bolted to the wheel hub through wheel studs.

**Load Paths:**
- **Vertical Load ($W_v$):** Wheel → Hub → Tapered Bearings → Stub Axle of Housing → Axle Housing → Leaf Springs → Chassis frame.
- **Cornering Force ($F_c$):** Tyre contact patch → Wheel → Hub → Bearings → Housing.
- **Driving Torque ($T$):** Differential side gear → Axle shaft (via splines) → Drive flange → Bolts → Hub → Wheel → Tyre contact patch.

**Key Feature:** The axle shaft carries **only torque**. It does not carry any radial or bending load, which is why it "floats" inside the housing.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 70$ mm, $T = 4{,}500$ N·m, $\sigma_y = 420$ MPa.

**(i) Maximum Torsional Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 4500}{\pi \times (0.070)^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{72{,}000}{\pi \times 3.43 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{72{,}000}{1.0775 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.682 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 66.82 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(ii) Factor of Safety (von-Mises Criterion):**

For a full-floating shaft, $M_b = 0$, so:

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_{max} = 1.732 \times 66.82
$$

$$
\sigma_{eq} = 115.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

Factor of Safety:

$$
N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{420}{115.7}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 3.63}
$$

Since $N > 1$, the design is **safe**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Compare the **full-floating**, **three-quarter floating**, and **semi-floating** axle arrangements in terms of load-carrying capacity, bearing location, and shaft removability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the axle shaft of a full-floating arrangement can be designed for pure torsional loading only. A truck's full-floating rear axle shaft transmits a maximum torque of **8,000 N·m**. If the factor of safety is **4** and the yield strength of the material is **500 MPa**, calculate the required shaft diameter. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | **Full Floating** | **Three-Quarter Floating** | **Semi-Floating** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load on Shaft | Torque only | Torque + minor thrust | Torque + bending + thrust |
| Bearing Location | On housing stubs | On housing stubs (single bearing) | On shaft itself |
| Hub Rotation | Around housing | Around housing | Around shaft |
| Shaft Removability | Without removing wheel | After removing wheel | Shaft & hub both removed |
| Load Capacity | **Highest (10–25 t)** | High (5–10 t) | Moderate (up to 3 t) |
| Application | Heavy trucks, buses, military | Medium trucks | Light commercial vehicles, cars |

**Part (b) — Shaft Diameter Calculation:**

**Given:** $T_{max} = 8{,}000$ N·m, $N = 4$, $\sigma_y = 500$ MPa.

**Design Equation (derived in Section 3):**

$$
d \geq \left( \frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot N \cdot T_{max}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 1: Compute the Numerator**

$$
16\sqrt{3} \cdot N \cdot T_{max} = 16 \times 1.732 \times 4 \times 8000
$$

$$
= 27.7128 \times 32{,}000 = 8.868 \times 10^5
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Denominator**

$$
\pi \cdot \sigma_y = \pi \times 500 \times 10^6 = 1.5708 \times 10^9
$$

**Step 3: Form the Ratio**

$$
\frac{8.868 \times 10^5}{1.5708 \times 10^9} = 5.645 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4: Take the Cube Root**

$$
d \geq (5.645 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3}
$$

Computing:

$$
(5.645 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0827 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \geq 82.7 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Adopt a standard size: $d = 85$ mm.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing bearing location:** Many students wrongly state that the bearings are mounted on the axle shaft in a full-floating design. **Correction:** Bearings are on the **axle housing stub**, NOT on the shaft. The shaft is *inside* the hub.
> 2. **Forgetting the "removability" advantage:** When asked about advantages, students often miss the unique serviceability feature — the shaft can be removed without disturbing the wheel.
> 3. **Wrong stress formula:** Students use bending stress $\sigma_b$ for the full-floating shaft, but the design is **pure torsion**. Always set $M_b = 0$ first.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert mm to m (or vice versa) in the numerical $d^3$ term. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 0.5–1 mark** for unit mismatches.
> 5. **Factor of safety placement:** Some students put $N$ in the denominator of the stress equation. **Correct placement:** $N$ multiplies the load, or equivalently divides the strength.
> 6. **Confusing "floating" terminology:** "Full floating" does NOT mean the shaft is loose or unsupported. It means the shaft is **decoupled from radial loads**, floating free of bending.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Full Floating Axle**

- ✅ **Definition:** Axle shaft carries **only torque**; all radial and bending loads are taken by the **axle housing** through **tapered roller bearings**.
- ✅ **Bearing Type:** Paired **tapered roller bearings** on **housing stub axles**.
- ✅ **Hub rotates on:** Axle **housing** (NOT on the shaft).
- ✅ **Key Advantage #1:** Shaft can be **removed without removing the wheel**.
- ✅ **Key Advantage #2:** **Highest load capacity** — used in **heavy trucks, buses, military vehicles**.
- ✅ **Critical Stress:** **Torsional shear stress** $\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ is the dominant design stress.
- ✅ **Equivalent Stress:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{3}\,\tau$ (since $M_b = 0$).
- ✅ **Design Equation for Diameter:** $d \geq \left( \dfrac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot N \cdot T_{max}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y} \right)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Load Path Summary:** Vertical load + cornering thrust → Housing → Springs → Frame. Driving torque → Shaft → Drive flange → Hub → Wheel.
- ✅ **Drive Flange Role:** Bolted coupling between shaft and hub; **does not carry radial load**.
- ✅ **Comparison Hook:** Full-floating > Three-quarter floating > Semi-floating (in terms of load capacity, in that order).
- ✅ **Application Memory Trick:** "**Full** floating = **Full** loads on housing = **Full**-size trucks."
- ✅ **Material of Construction:** Medium-carbon alloy steels (AISI 4140 / EN19) with **induction-hardened bearing journals**.
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Never write "the wheel rotates on the axle shaft" — this is a **semi-floating** description.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Quarter Floating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three Quarter Floating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Quarter Floating Axle (TQF Axle):** A type of live rear axle arrangement in which the **axle shaft** carries (i) the driving torque from the differential, (ii) the vertical load (sprung weight) of the vehicle, and (iii) the side thrust developed during cornering, while being supported by **two bearings** — one mounted on the **differential housing** (inner bearing) and one mounted on the **wheel hub** (outer bearing). The outer end of the axle shaft is **splined to the hub**, so the hub and shaft rotate together as a single unit, and the hub is allowed to rotate relative to the axle shaft only through the outer bearing.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this arrangement is taught under **Module 3 – Rear Axle and Final Drive**, and is positioned between the *Semi-Floating* and *Full-Floating* axle arrangements as a **transitional design** offering a balance between cost, strength, and serviceability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Terminology to remember:**
> - **Live Axle:** A driven axle in which the axle shafts also transmit torque to the wheels.
> - **Stub Axle / Steering Knuckle:** A non-rotating (or semi-rotating) arm carrying the wheel hub.
> - **Wheel Hub:** The central rotating part to which the wheel and brake drum/disc are mounted.
> - **Differential Housing / Carrier:** The assembly containing the crown wheel, pinion, and side gears.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **gripping a heavy rod** with both hands — your **left hand** at one end (near your body) and your **right hand** at the other end (near the load). The rod passes through a **rotating wheel** mounted on the right hand. If you try to twist the rod, the wheel rotates with it because the rod is **splined (keyed) to the wheel hub** at the right hand. The weight of the load is supported by both your hands — your left hand presses down on the inner side and your right hand takes the cornering push from the side.

- The **rod** = the **axle shaft**
- The **left hand (inner)** = the **inner bearing on the differential housing**
- The **right hand (outer)** = the **outer bearing on the wheel hub**
- The **twisting force** = the **driving torque** from the differential
- The **load hanging on the rod** = the **vehicle weight** and **side thrust** from the road

This is exactly how a three-quarter floating axle behaves.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Three-Quarter" Floating?**
> Because in a **Full-Floating** axle, the axle shaft is **completely relieved of all loads except torque** (100% of the load is "floated" off the shaft). In a **Semi-Floating** axle, the axle shaft carries **all three loads** (torque, weight, side thrust) using only **one bearing** (0% floated). The three-quarter floating design carries all three loads like a semi-floating axle, but **splits the support into two bearings** — thus floating about **75% of the bending and side loads** away from any single concentrated point. Hence, "Three-Quarter Floating."

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Typical TQF axle shaft diameter:** **38 mm – 50 mm** for light commercial vehicles; up to **65 mm** for medium/heavy commercial vehicles.
> - **Number of bearings per shaft:** **2** (one inner, one outer).
> - **Hub-to-shaft connection:** **Splines** (typically 6 to 10 splines).
> - **Common application segment:** **Light-to-medium commercial vehicles, SUVs, and older sedans** where moderate loads are expected.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The three-quarter floating axle is composed of the following key parts. Every part has a specific load-sharing role:

| # | Component | Function | Load Carried |
|---|-----------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Axle Shaft (Half Shaft)** | Transmits torque from differential side gear to the wheel hub | Torque + Vertical Load + Side Thrust |
| 2 | **Inner Bearing** (on differential housing) | Supports the inboard end of the axle shaft | Vertical (weight) + Side thrust |
| 3 | **Outer Bearing** (on wheel hub) | Supports the outboard end of the axle shaft; allows hub to rotate on shaft | Vertical (weight) + Side thrust |
| 4 | **Wheel Hub** | Carries the wheel and brake assembly; splined to the axle shaft | Rotational mounting |
| 5 | **Splines (on shaft and inside hub)** | Transmit torque from shaft to hub | Torque |
| 6 | **Differential Housing / Carrier** | Houses crown wheel, pinion, side gears; supports inner bearing | Casing loads |
| 7 | **Stub Axle / Tube End** | Provides outer support to the hub bearing | Reaction load |
| 8 | **Oil Seal** (at hub end) | Prevents gear oil leakage | Sealing |

---

## 2.2 Operating Logic — How the Loads Are Distributed

The three-quarter floating axle is best understood by tracing **each load type** from road → wheel → hub → axle shaft → bearings → differential housing → chassis.

### Step-by-Step Load Transfer:

1. **Vertical Load (Sprung Weight) Transfer:**
   - Road reaction $\rightarrow$ Tyre $\rightarrow$ Wheel rim $\rightarrow$ **Wheel Hub** $\rightarrow$ **Splines** $\rightarrow$ **Axle Shaft** $\rightarrow$ **Inner + Outer Bearings** $\rightarrow$ Differential Housing $\rightarrow$ Leaf Springs / Chassis Frame.

2. **Driving Torque Transfer:**
   - Engine $\rightarrow$ Propeller Shaft $\rightarrow$ Differential Pinion $\rightarrow$ Crown Wheel $\rightarrow$ Side Gear $\rightarrow$ **Axle Shaft (splined end)** $\rightarrow$ **Splines** $\rightarrow$ **Wheel Hub** $\rightarrow$ Wheel Rim $\rightarrow$ Tyre $\rightarrow$ Road.

3. **Side Thrust (Cornering) Transfer:**
   - During cornering, centrifugal force pushes the vehicle outward, and the road exerts an inward side thrust.
   - Tyre side wall $\rightarrow$ Wheel $\rightarrow$ Hub $\rightarrow$ **Outer Bearing** $\rightarrow$ **Axle Shaft** $\rightarrow$ **Inner Bearing** $\rightarrow$ Differential Housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Full-Floating Axle:**
> In a **Full-Floating** axle, the axle shaft carries **only torque**; the hub is supported by **two separate large bearings** on the stub axle, and the shaft is held in place by a **retaining bolt** at the hub end. In a **Three-Quarter Floating** axle, the axle shaft is **directly splined into the hub** and is **itself supported by two bearings**, so the **shaft bends under the vertical and side loads**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for Three-Quarter Floating axle numerical and design problems. **Memorize this entire table** before the exam.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Vertical Reaction at Inner Bearing ($R_I$)** | $R_I = W \cdot \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in} + L_{out}}$ | N (Newtons) | $W$ = total vertical load on one wheel |
| 2 | **Vertical Reaction at Outer Bearing ($R_O$)** | $R_O = W \cdot \dfrac{L_{in}}{L_{in} + L_{out}}$ | N | Both reactions add up to $W$ |
| 3 | **Maximum Bending Moment on Shaft** | $M_{max} = R_I \cdot L_{in} = R_O \cdot L_{out}$ | N·m | Occurs between the two bearings |
| 4 | **Side Thrust Force ($F_s$)** | $F_s = \dfrac{W \cdot v^2}{g \cdot R}$ | N | $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s), $R$ = turning radius (m) |
| 5 | **Torsional Shear Stress ($\tau$)** | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | Pa (N/m²) | For solid circular shaft, $T$ = torque (N·m), $d$ = shaft diameter (m) |
| 6 | **Equivalent Bending + Torsion Stress** | $\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{16}{\pi \cdot d^3} \left( \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right)$ | Pa | Maximum shear stress theory |
| 7 | **Bending Stress ($\sigma_b$)** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 \cdot M}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | Pa | For solid shaft |
| 8 | **Shaft Diameter (from strength)** | $d = \left( \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$ | m | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ |
| 9 | **Bearing Life (L10)** | $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^p$ | million revs | $C$ = dynamic load rating, $P$ = equivalent load, $p = 3$ for ball bearings |
| 10 | **Torsional Rigidity** | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | rad | $G$ = shear modulus, $J$ = polar moment of inertia |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **bending moment** direction. The axle shaft is a **simply supported beam** with the load $W$ applied at the wheel hub (overhang beyond the outer bearing). This makes the shaft a **propped cantilever with overhang** — and the **maximum bending moment occurs between the two bearings, NOT at the wheel end.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The three-quarter floating axle is widely used in:

- **Light Commercial Vehicles (LCVs)** — Tata Ace, Mahindra Pickup, Ashok Leyland Dost.
- **Older sedans and SUVs** where moderate torque and payload are required.
- **Mid-size trucks** operating on relatively smooth roads and within rated payload limits.
- **Agricultural tractors** (rear axle side) for moderate draft loads.

Its primary engineering advantage is the **balanced trade-off**: it is **stronger and safer than a semi-floating axle** (because loads are shared between two bearings), yet it is **cheaper and simpler than a full-floating axle** (because no separate hub bearings and no retaining bolts are needed). The shaft can be **withdrawn for servicing** by simply removing the outer bearing cap and pulling the shaft outward — a clear serviceability advantage.

> [!TIP]
> **Why TQF is disappearing from modern heavy vehicles:**
> Modern heavy trucks have moved to **Full-Floating axles** because the axle shafts can be replaced without disturbing the wheel hub, the brake assembly, and the wheel bearings — critical for **minimal downtime** in fleet operations. TQF is still preferred in **cost-sensitive, light-duty applications**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reactions at Inner and Outer Bearings

Consider the **right-side axle shaft** of a three-quarter floating rear axle. Treat it as a **beam supported on two bearings** (Inner at A, Outer at B) with the **wheel load $W$ applied at the wheel hub (Point C)**, which is **beyond the outer bearing (overhang)**.

### Setup:
- Distance from Inner bearing to Outer bearing: $L_{in}$ (let's call it $a$)
- Distance from Outer bearing to Wheel centre (overhang): $L_{out}$ (let's call it $b$)
- Vertical load at the wheel centre: $W$ (downward)
- Reactions: $R_I$ at inner bearing (upward), $R_O$ at outer bearing (upward)

### Free Body Diagram (conceptual):
The beam from A to C has supports at A and B, with the load $W$ acting downward at C (the wheel centre, past B).

### Equilibrium Equations:

**Step 1: Sum of vertical forces = 0**

$$R_I + R_O = W$$

**Step 2: Sum of moments about Inner bearing (Point A) = 0**

Taking counter-clockwise as positive, the moment of $R_O$ about A is positive (upward force to the right of A), and the moment of $W$ about A is negative (downward force to the right of A):

$$R_O \cdot (L_{in} + L_{out}) - W \cdot (L_{in} + L_{out}) = 0$$

Wait — this is incorrect. The load $W$ is applied at the wheel centre C, which is at distance $(L_{in} + L_{out})$ from A, but the load is **not** at the same location as $R_O$. Let us redo correctly.

The load $W$ is at distance $(L_{in} + L_{out})$ from A, and $R_O$ is at distance $L_{in}$ from A:

$$R_O \cdot L_{in} - W \cdot (L_{in} + L_{out}) = 0$$

**Solving for $R_O$:**

$$R_O = W \cdot \dfrac{L_{in} + L_{out}}{L_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is critical:** Because $W$ is applied **beyond the outer bearing** (overhang), $R_O$ actually becomes **greater than $W$**! This is a **classic propped cantilever with overhang** behavior.

Let us redo the analysis correctly with proper sign convention. Taking moment about inner bearing A, the load $W$ is to the right of A at distance $(L_{in} + L_{out})$, while $R_O$ is also to the right of A at distance $L_{in}$. Since the load is **outside** the support, it creates a **clockwise moment**, and $R_O$ must create a **counter-clockwise moment** to balance. Therefore:

$$R_O \cdot L_{in} = W \cdot (L_{in} + L_{out})$$

This gives:

$$R_O = W \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \right)$$

Substituting into the force equilibrium:

$$R_I = W - R_O = W - W \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \right) = -W \cdot \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that $R_I$ acts **downward** (the inner bearing is being **pulled down** by the overhanging load). This is the well-known **uplift reaction** in propped cantilevers.

**Final Reactions (with correct signs):**

$$R_O = W \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \right) \quad \text{(upward, larger than } W\text{)}$$

$$R_I = -W \cdot \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \quad \text{(downward, magnitude} < W \text{ if } L_{out} < L_{in}\text{)}$$

### Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment

The bending moment **at the outer bearing (B)** is:

$$M_B = -W \cdot L_{out}$$

The bending moment **at the inner bearing (A)** is:

$$M_A = 0$$

Between A and B, the bending moment varies linearly from $M_A = 0$ to $M_B = -W \cdot L_{out}$.

**Therefore, the maximum bending moment magnitude is:**

$$M_{max} = W \cdot L_{out}$$

This maximum occurs at the **outer bearing location** (B), and is caused by the **overhang of the load** beyond the support.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Torque and Shaft Diameter

The axle shaft is subjected to **combined bending and torsion**. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's Theory)**, the equivalent torque is:

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $d$:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot d^3}$$

For safe design, $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$:

$$\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot d^3}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}$$

$$d = \left( \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem):

**Given:** A three-quarter floating rear axle carries a vertical load of $W = 12{,}000$ N per wheel. The distance from the inner bearing to the outer bearing is $L_{in} = 250$ mm, and the overhang (outer bearing to wheel centre) is $L_{out} = 100$ mm. The driving torque is $T = 1{,}800$ N·m. The allowable shear stress is $\tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa.

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

$$M_{max} = W \cdot L_{out} = 12{,}000 \times 0.100 = 1{,}200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Torque**

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{1200^2 + 1800^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{eq} &= \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000 + 3{,}240{,}000} \\
&= \sqrt{4{,}680{,}000} \\
&= 2{,}163.33 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Required Shaft Diameter**

$$d = \left( \dfrac{16 \times 2{,}163.33}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$d = \left( \dfrac{34{,}613.3}{1.885 \times 10^8} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$d = \left( 1.836 \times 10^{-4} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$d \approx 0.0568 \text{ m} = 56.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** A shaft diameter of **approximately 57 mm** is required for this application.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparative table is the **most-tested content** in KTU exams for Module 3.

| Parameter | Semi-Floating | **Three-Quarter Floating** | Full-Floating |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| **Number of bearings** | 1 | **2** | 2 (on stub axle, shaft not loaded) |
| **Loads on shaft** | Torque + Vertical + Side Thrust | **Torque + Vertical + Side Thrust** | Torque only |
| **Shaft-bending?** | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| **Shaft removal without disturbing wheel** | No | **Partial** | Yes |
| **Cost** | Lowest | **Moderate** | Highest |
| **Strength** | Lowest | **Moderate** | Highest |
| **Application** | Light cars, two-wheelers | **LCVs, SUVs, mid-trucks** | Heavy trucks, buses |
| **Hub-to-shaft connection** | Splines only | **Splines + outer bearing** | Bolted flange |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

Since the physical layout of a three-quarter floating axle requires a cross-sectional mechanical drawing (which is difficult in plain text), the following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the **functional architecture and load-flow** through the assembly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Road Reaction and Tyre Forces] --> B[Wheel Rim and Tyre Assembly]
    B --> C[Wheel Hub with Internal Splines]
    C --> D[Axle Shaft with External Splines]
    D --> E[Outer Bearing Mounted on Stub Axle]
    D --> F[Inner Bearing Mounted on Differential Housing]
    E --> G[Stub Axle connected to Axle Tube]
    F --> H[Differential Housing and Carrier Assembly]
    H --> I[Crown Wheel and Pinion Receiving Engine Torque]
```
