- The **rear axle** of economy and mid-segment passenger cars (cost, integrated parking brake).
- **All four wheels** of light commercial vehicles (LCVs), heavy trucks, and buses.
- The **parking brake** of vehicles that have discs on the front (drum-in-hat / duo-servo unit inside the disc rotor).

This is because drum brakes are **cheap, durable, and naturally self-energizing**, making them ideal for hand-brake (parking) duty.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Internal Expanding Drum Brake

A complete internal expanding drum brake assembly consists of the following components:

### 2.1.1 Major Sub-Assemblies
1. **Brake Drum** — A hollow cylindrical cast-iron (or aluminum/cast composite) shell. The interior surface is precisely machined (typically Ra ≤ 1.6 μm) to provide a uniform friction interface.
2. **Brake Shoes** — Two curved steel shoes (primary/secondary) on which friction linings are bonded or riveted.
3. **Wheel Cylinder** — A double-acting hydraulic piston cylinder mounted between the two shoes at the top. When brake fluid pressure enters, it pushes two opposed pistons outward, forcing the shoes apart.
4. **Anchor Pin** — A fixed pivot at the bottom of the brake assembly, against which the trailing end of each shoe reacts.
5. **Return Springs** — A heavy main spring pulls both shoes back to their resting (un-braked) position when hydraulic pressure is released.
6. **Adjuster Mechanism** — A screw or ratchet-type mechanism at the bottom anchor that compensates for lining wear as the friction material thins over kilometers of use.
7. **Backing Plate** — A rigid steel plate that supports all of the above and is bolted to the axle housing or steering knuckle.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (Why and How)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind every step is critical for KTU's "Explain" type 14-mark questions.**

**Step 1 — Pedal Input (Why does braking occur?)**
The driver depresses the brake pedal, which (via the brake booster) raises the master-cylinder fluid pressure. This pressure is transmitted equally through the brake lines to all four wheel cylinders.

**Step 2 — Wheel Cylinder Actuation (How is fluid pressure converted to shoe force?)**
The pressurized fluid enters the wheel cylinder between two opposed pistons. By Pascal's law, the fluid exerts an equal axial force `F_cyl = P × A_p` on each piston, where `A_p` is the piston cross-sectional area.

**Step 3 — Shoe Expansion (How is piston motion transmitted to the drum?)**
The pistons push the **webs** (non-lined body) of the two shoes outward. The shoes pivot about the **anchor pin** at the bottom, swinging outward so that the linings contact the inner drum surface.

**Step 4 — Friction Generation (How does contact create braking force?)**
Once the linings are pressed against the drum with normal force `N`, friction develops tangentially. The **friction force** is:

$$F_f = \mu \times N$$

This tangential friction force, acting at the lining's effective radius `r_eff`, creates a **braking torque**:

$$T_b = F_f \times r_{eff} = \mu \times N \times r_{eff}$$

**Step 5 — Self-Energization (Why is the actuation force *less* than the friction force output suggests?)**
If the shoe is "leading" (rotating drum surface moves *into* the lining), the friction force rotates the shoe *into* the drum, increasing the normal force `N` beyond the simple hydraulic value. This **self-amplification** is called **self-energization**, quantified by the **brake factor (BF)**.

**Step 6 — Drum-Heat Buildup (Why does continuous braking cause "brake fade"?)**
All the kinetic energy of the vehicle is converted to heat inside the drum. Because the drum is largely enclosed, heat dissipation is poor. When temperature rises above ~**300 °C**, organic friction linings begin to outgas, and the friction coefficient `μ` drops sharply → **brake fade**.

**Step 7 — Release (How does the shoe return to its rest position?)**
When the pedal is released, hydraulic pressure drops to zero. The heavy **return spring** (and a smaller **hold-down spring** for each shoe) pulls the shoes back inward, away from the drum, allowing the wheel to spin freely.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Drum Brakes by Shoe Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This classification appears in almost every KTU Module-4 question paper. Memorize it.**

| Brake Type | Description | Self-Energizing? | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Leading Shoe (SLS)** | Both shoes act as **trailing** in one direction and as a *single* leading in the other. | Yes, only in **one direction** of rotation. | Light rear brakes (older cars). |
| **Two Leading Shoe (TLS) / Double Leading** | **Both** shoes are leading shoes, with separate anchor pins and **two** wheel cylinders (or one bidirectional cylinder). | Yes, in **both directions**. | Heavy trucks, buses, front of older cars. |
| **Duo-Servo (Twin Leading)** | Shoes are mechanically linked by a **floating anchor / adjuster link** at the bottom; the secondary shoe gets "servo" force from the primary shoe. | Yes, both directions; very high BF. | Most common automotive **rear drum brake** (passenger cars). |
| **Single Trailing Shoe (STS)** | Both shoes are trailing; no self-energization. | **No** self-energization. | Center/Rear of some vehicles; parking brake. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap for Exam Use:**
> - **Leading Shoe:** The drum surface, at the contact point, moves *toward* the anchor. Friction rotates the shoe *into* the drum → self-energization.
> - **Trailing Shoe:** The drum surface, at the contact point, moves *away from* the anchor. Friction rotates the shoe *out of* the drum → self-**de**-energization.

---

## 2.4 Self-Energization — The Central Concept

The self-energizing action is what gives drum brakes their trademark "grabby" feel and their long service life. The mathematical representation:

$$F_{self} = \frac{\mu \times N_{input}}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta}$$

where:
- `N_input` = the input actuation force normal to the drum.
- `θ` = angle between the line of action of `N_input` and the drum-radius line at the lining's center.
- When `μ tan(θ) → 1`, the denominator → 0 and the brake **self-locks** (theoretically infinite BF). Real designs keep `μ tan(θ) < 0.4` to avoid lockup.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Trick:** Examiners often ask: "Why is the leading shoe in a drum brake called self-energizing?"
> **Model answer:** *Because the friction force at the lining-drum interface creates a moment about the anchor pin that presses the shoe harder into the drum, thereby augmenting the normal force produced by the wheel cylinder. This reduces the hydraulic input required to generate a given braking torque.*

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every** drum-brake formula tested in the 2024 KTU scheme. Reproduce the values exactly (no `|` pipe symbols — use `\vert`).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction force | $F_f = \mu \cdot N$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $N$ = normal force | N |
| 2 | Braking torque | $T_b = F_f \cdot r_{eff} = \mu \cdot N \cdot r_{eff}$ | $r_{eff}$ = effective lining radius | N·m |
| 3 | Wheel-cylinder actuation force | $F_{cyl} = P \cdot A_p$ | $P$ = hydraulic pressure, $A_p$ = piston area | N |
| 4 | Brake Factor (Leading) | $BF_{lead} = \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\phi - \mu}$ | $\phi$ = shoe-contact half-angle | — |
| 5 | Brake Factor (Trailing) | $BF_{trail} = \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\phi + \mu}$ | same as above | — |
| 6 | Self-energizing normal force | $N_{self} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot N_{input}}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta}$ | $\theta$ = angle of input force to radius | N |
| 7 | Drum thermal expansion | $\Delta D = D \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $D$ = drum diameter, $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coeff. (cast iron $\approx 11 \times 10^{-6} /K$) | m |
| 8 | Heat dissipation rate (convective) | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $h$ = convective heat-transfer coeff., $A$ = surface area | W |
| 9 | Vehicle kinetic energy (per wheel) | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot k_{wheel}$ | $k_{wheel}$ = brake-share factor (e.g., 0.6 for front) | J |
| 10 | Pneumatic actuation force | $F_{pneu} = P_{pneu} \cdot A_{diaph}$ | $P_{pneu}$ = air pressure (typ. **0.6 MPa**), $A_{diaph}$ = diaphragm area | N |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students frequently write `BF = μ/tan φ` only, forgetting the **± μ** sign in the denominator. The sign is **minus** for leading (self-energizing) and **plus** for trailing (de-energizing). Losing this sign = 2 marks lost.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Drum Brake is Used |
|---|---|
| **Rear axle of passenger cars** | Low cost, integrated drum-in-hat parking brake, long service life. |
| **Heavy commercial vehicles (trucks/buses)** | High torque capacity, robust against contamination, easy to integrate with **S-cam** air actuators. |
| **Railway rolling stock** | **Clasp brake** design; very high energy absorption per unit mass. |
| **Industrial cranes, winches, elevators** | **External contracting band brake** — high torque, simple, failsafe (spring-applied). |
| **Parking brake** | Naturally "fail-on" — a spring holds the shoes engaged; hydraulic release disengages them. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Braking Torque for a Single Leading Shoe

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** It is the foundational equation from which the **brake factor (BF)** is defined — and BF is a guaranteed 7-mark question in Part B of the KTU ESE.

### Setup
Consider a single leading shoe pressed against the inner surface of a brake drum. The shoe is pivoted at the anchor `A` and actuated by a force `F` applied at the top. The contact between the lining and the drum extends over a subtended angle of `2φ` (a small angle, so the contact pressure is treated as a concentrated normal force `N` at the center of the lining arc).

### Geometry
- Effective radius (center of lining to drum axis) = $r$
- Angle between `N` and the vertical = $φ$
- Angle between the input force `F` and the vertical = $θ` (often taken as 90° for a horizontal actuation, simplifying the trigonometry)
- Friction force at the contact = $F_f = \mu N$ acting **tangentially** to the drum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the moment equilibrium about the anchor pin A:**

Taking moments about A, the actuation force `F` and the friction `F_f` both produce moments that press the shoe into the drum, while the normal force `N` produces a moment that pushes the shoe away. At the incipient-contact (just-touching) condition:

$$F \cdot a = N \cdot r \cos\phi - F_f \cdot r \sin\phi$$

where `a` is the moment arm of `F` from A. (In the simple uniform-pressure model, $a = r \cos\phi$.)

**Step 2 — Substitute $F_f = \mu N$ into the equation:**

$$F \cdot a = N \cdot r \cos\phi - \mu N \cdot r \sin\phi$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $N \cdot r$ on the right side:**

$$F \cdot a = N \cdot r \left( \cos\phi - \mu \sin\phi \right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the input normal force $N$ in terms of $F$:**

$$N = \frac{F \cdot a}{r \left( \cos\phi - \mu \sin\phi \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Write the friction torque about the drum axis:**

The friction force $F_f = \mu N$ acts at radius $r$, so:

$$T_b = F_f \cdot r = \mu N \cdot r$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expression for $N$ from Step 4:**

$$T_b = \mu \cdot \frac{F \cdot a}{r \left( \cos\phi - \mu \sin\phi \right)} \cdot r$$

$$T_b = \frac{\mu \cdot F \cdot a}{\cos\phi - \mu \sin\phi}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Brake Factor (BF):**

The brake factor is the ratio of the friction torque to the input actuation torque ($F \cdot a$):

$$BF = \frac{T_b}{F \cdot a} = \frac{\mu}{\cos\phi - \mu \sin\phi}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to a tangent-form (typical textbook representation):**

Using $\cos\phi \approx 1$ for small $\phi$, and $\tan\phi \approx \sin\phi$ for small angles:

$$\boxed{\,BF_{leading} = \frac{\mu}{\tan\phi - \mu}\,}$$

**Step 9 — Repeat the analysis for the trailing shoe (sign flip):**

For a trailing shoe, the friction moment about the anchor **opposes** the input force, so the denominator sign changes:

$$\boxed{\,BF_{trailing} = \frac{\mu}{\tan\phi + \mu}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of the signs:**
> - For a **leading** shoe, $\tan\phi > \mu$ keeps $BF > 0$ and the brake is **stable** (no self-lock).
> - For a **trailing** shoe, $BF$ is always positive regardless of $\mu$, but it is **smaller** than the leading-shoe BF.
> - If $\mu \to \tan\phi$ for a leading shoe, $BF \to \infty$ → **shoe self-locks** → uncontrolled drag.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Expansion of the Brake Drum (Fade Analysis)

**Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks: *"Why does a drum brake lose effectiveness after continuous heavy braking?"* The answer lies in thermal expansion exceeding the adjuster's take-up range, plus μ drop from outgassing.

### Given
- Drum material: **Gray cast iron** with coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$.
- Initial drum diameter: $D_0 = 280 \text{ mm} = 0.280 \text{ m}$.
- Temperature rise during a mountain descent: $\Delta T = 350 \text{ °C}$ (from 50 °C ambient to 400 °C surface).

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Apply the linear thermal expansion formula to the drum diameter:**

$$\Delta D = D_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\Delta D = 0.280 \text{ m} \times (11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}) \times 350 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\Delta D = 0.280 \times 11 \times 350 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta D = (0.280 \times 3850) \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1078 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta D \approx 1.08 \text{ mm}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **What this means in practice:** The drum grows by **~1 mm** in diameter, while the shoe-to-drum radial clearance (set by the adjuster) is only **0.2 – 0.3 mm**. The shoes are pushed *away* from the drum by the expanding metal, and the wheel cylinder must travel an extra ~0.7 mm to re-engage. The driver feels this as a "soft pedal" and reduced braking — a key symptom of **thermal brake fade** in drum brakes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for KTU Two-Mark / Three-Mark Questions

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is **the most exam-relevant** consolidated comparison. Memorize the bolded entries.

| Property | Single Leading Shoe | Two Leading Shoe | Duo-Servo | Disc Brake (for context) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of wheel cylinders** | 1 | 1 (reversible) or 2 | 1 | 1 per caliper |
| **Self-energization** | One direction only | Both directions | Both directions (via link) | None (passive) |
| **Brake factor (typical)** | 0.5 – 0.7 | 0.8 – 1.2 | 1.5 – 2.5 (very high) | 0.7 – 1.0 |
| **Heat dissipation** | Poor (enclosed) | Poor | Poor | Excellent (open) |
| **Fade resistance** | Low | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| **Parking brake suitability** | Fair | Poor (no fixed anchor) | Excellent | Poor (needs integral drum) |
| **Modern usage (2024)** | Rare | Trucks/buses | **Most car rear brakes** | Most car front brakes |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Pneumatic Actuator Force (Heavy Vehicle Context)

**Why this matters:** Trucks and buses use **air-brake-actuated drum brakes** with an **S-cam** mechanism. KTU includes this in the 2024 commercial-vehicle syllabus.

### Setup
- Supply pressure: $P_{pneu} = 0.65 \text{ MPa} = 0.65 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Diaphragm area: $A_d = 130 \text{ cm}^2 = 130 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.013 \text{ m}^2$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Force on the diaphragm:**

$$F_{diaph} = P_{pneu} \times A_d$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$F_{diaph} = (0.65 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (0.013 \text{ m}^2)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$F_{diaph} = 8450 \text{ N} \approx 8.45 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical advantage of the S-cam mechanism:**

The push rod of the actuator acts on the S-cam via a slack adjuster. With a typical mechanical-advantage ratio of $i = 4:1$ (lever ratio) and cam-shoe contact geometry that adds another ~2.5:1, the effective shoe-actuation force is:

$$F_{shoe} = F_{diaph} \times i_{lever} \times i_{cam}$$

$$F_{shoe} = 8450 \times 4 \times 2.5 = 84{,}500 \text{ N} \approx \boxed{84.5 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** ~85 kN per wheel on a heavy truck. With four wheels, total available clamping force is **~340 kN** — more than enough to lock the wheels and trigger the ABS modulator.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Expanding Drum Brake Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** and force flow of a single internal expanding drum brake assembly, including the hydraulic actuation path. It follows the Mermaid-safe node-identifier rules (alphanumeric, no reserved words, no markdown formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    PEDAL[Brake Pedal Input] --> BOOSTER[Vacuum Booster]
    BOOSTER --> MASTERCYL[Master Cylinder]
    MASTERCYL --> PIPELINE[Brake Fluid Pipeline]
    PIPELINE --> WHEELCYL[Wheel Cylinder]
    WHEELCYL --> PISTONL[Left Piston]
    WHEELCYL --> PISTONR[Right Piston]
    PISTONL --> SHOEPRI[Primary Brake Shoe]
    PISTONR --> SHOESEC[Secondary Brake Shoe]
    SHOEPRI --> LINK[Duo Servo Link or Anchor]
    SHOESEC --> LINK
    LINK --> ANCHOR[Fixed Anchor Pin]
    SHOEPRI --> LININGPRI[Primary Friction Lining]
    SHOESEC --> LININGSEC[Secondary Friction Lining]
    LININGPRI --> DRUM[Brake Drum Inner Surface]
    LININGSEC --> DRUM
    DRUM --> ROTATION[Wheel Rotation]
    ROTATION --> FRICTION[Friction Force Generation]
    FRICTION --> TORQUE[Braking Torque Tb]
    FRICTION --> SELFEN[Self Energization Force]
    SELFEN --> SHOEPRI
    SELFEN --> SHOESEC
    RETURNSPRING[Return Spring] --> SHOEPRI
    RETURNSPRING --> SHOESEC
    ADJUSTER[Manual or Automatic Adjuster] --> SHOEPRI
    ADJUSTER --> SHOESEC
    HEATSRC[Vehicle Kinetic Energy] --> DRUM
    DRUM --> HEATSINK[Heat Dissipation to Air]
    PARKING[Parking Brake Cable] --> SHOEPRI
    PARKING --> SHOESEC
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force & Energy Flow Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Driver Pedal Force Fp] --> B[Booster Amplification Fb]
    B --> C[Master Cylinder Pressure Pm]
    C --> D[Brake Line Transmission]
    D --> E[Wheel Cylinder Pressure Pw]
    E --> F[Piston Force Fcyl equals Pw times Ap]
    F --> G[Shoe Normal Force N]
    G --> H[Friction Force Ff equals mu times N]
    H --> I[Braking Torque Tb equals Ff times r eff]
    I --> J[Wheel Deceleration]
    J --> K[Vehicle Kinetic Energy Ek to Heat Q]
    K --> L[Drum Temperature Rise Delta T]
    L --> M[Thermal Expansion of Drum]
    M --> N[Possible Brake Fade]
    G --> O[Self Energization Feedback F self]
    O --> G
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Block: Classification Hierarchy of Drum Brakes

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Drum Brake Family]
    ROOT --> INT[Internal Expanding]
    ROOT --> EXT[External Contracting]
    INT --> SLS[Single Leading Shoe]
    INT --> TLS[Two Leading Shoe]
    INT --> DUO[Duo Servo Brake]
    INT --> STS[Single Trailing Shoe]
    SLS --> APP1[Rear Axle Light Vehicles]
    TLS --> APP2[Trucks Buses Front Axle]
    DUO --> APP3[Rear Axle Passenger Cars]
    STS --> APP4[Industrial Holding Brake]
    EXT --> APP5[Crane Winch Band Brake]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Block: Pneumatic Drum Brake Actuation (Commercial Vehicle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AIRCYL[Air Compressor and Reservoir] --> GOV[Governor 0.8 MPa]
    GOV --> BRKVALVE[Brake Foot Valve]
    BRKVALVE --> FRONTCHAMBER[Front Chamber Actuator]
    BRKVALVE --> REARCHAMBER[Rear Chamber Actuator]
    FRONTCHAMBER --> SCAMFR[Front S Cam and Slack Adjuster]
    REARCHAMBER --> SCAMRR[Rear S Cam and Slack Adjuster]
    SCAMFR --> SHOEFR[Front Drum Brake Shoes]
    SCAMRR --> SHOERR[Rear Drum Brake Shoes]
    SHOEFR --> DRUMFR[Front Brake Drum]
    SHOERR --> DRUMRR[Rear Brake Drum]
    PARKBRAKE[Spring Parking Actuator] --> REARCHAMBER
```

---

## 4.5 Component Cross-Section (ASCII Representation for Quick Recall)

```
            +-----------------------------+
            |       BRAKE DRUM (rotates)  |
            |   .-------------------.     |
            |  /  /  /  /  /  /  /  /|    |
            | /  /  /  /  /  /  /  / |    |  ← Inner machined surface
            |/  /  /  /  /  /  /  /  |    |
            +-----------------------------+
              |  ↑                 ↑  |
              |  |  friction       |  |
              |  |  force          |  |
       +------+--+                 +--+------+
       |  PRIMARY SHOE      SECONDARY SHOE  |
       |  (with lining)     (with lining)   |
       +-------------+-----------+----------+
                     |           |
                     |  WHEEL    |  ← hydraulic pistons
                     |  CYLINDER |
                     |   (P, A)  |
                     +-----+-----+
                           |
                     +-----+-----+
                     | ANCHOR PIN|
                     |  (fixed)  |
                     +-----------+
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Differentiate between a "leading shoe" and a "trailing shoe" in a drum brake. Draw neat sketches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **leading shoe** is one in which the drum surface, at the point of contact, moves **toward the anchor pin**. Friction therefore rotates the shoe *into* the drum, increasing the normal force — this is the **self-energizing** action. The brake factor is given by $\displaystyle BF_{leading} = \frac{\mu}{\tan\phi - \mu}$.
- A **trailing shoe** is one in which the drum surface, at the point of contact, moves **away from the anchor pin**. Friction rotates the shoe *out of* the drum, **reducing** the normal force. Its brake factor is $\displaystyle BF_{trailing} = \frac{\mu}{\tan\phi + \mu}$.
- Leading shoes give higher braking torque for the same actuation force but are more prone to self-lock; trailing shoes are inherently stable. (1 mark for sketches, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for the contrasting BF formulas.)

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: List any four components of an internal expanding drum brake and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Brake Drum** — Rotates with the wheel; provides the friction surface for the shoes. (0.75)
2. **Brake Shoe with Lining** — Carries the friction material that generates the braking torque when pressed against the drum. (0.75)
3. **Wheel Cylinder** — Converts hydraulic pressure into mechanical force that pushes the shoes outward. (0.75)
4. **Return Spring** — Pulls the shoes back to their rest position when hydraulic pressure is released. (0.75)

*(Accept also: Anchor Pin, Adjuster, Backing Plate, Hold-down Springs — any four.)*

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** (i.e., "OR" between two full questions). Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Design and Analysis Focus

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Equivalent]`**

**A single leading shoe internal expanding drum brake has the following data:**
- Drum diameter $D = 320 \text{ mm}$, so radius $R = 0.160 \text{ m}$.
- Effective lining radius $r_{eff} = 0.135 \text{ m}$ (i.e., the friction force is assumed concentrated at $0.85 R$).
- Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.35$.
- Hydraulic pressure in the wheel cylinder $P = 6 \text{ MPa}$.
- Piston diameter in the wheel cylinder $d = 28 \text{ mm}$ (two pistons).
- Shoe half-contact angle $\phi = 30°$.

**Answer the following:**

#### (a) Calculate the actuation force generated by the wheel cylinder. [7 marks]
**Step 1 — Piston cross-sectional area:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.028)^2}{4}$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi \times 7.84 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 6.158 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Force on one piston:**

$$F_{piston} = P \times A_p = 6 \times 10^6 \times 6.158 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{piston} = 3694.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total actuation force from both pistons (opposed):**

$$F_{cyl,total} = 2 \times F_{piston} = 2 \times 3694.5$$

$$\boxed{F_{cyl,total} = 7389 \text{ N} \approx 7.39 \text{ kN}}$$

#### (b) Compute the braking torque developed, and discuss the effect of self-energization. [7 marks]
**Step 1 — Brake factor for the leading shoe:**

$$BF_{leading} = \frac{\mu}{\tan\phi - \mu} = \frac{0.35}{\tan 30° - 0.35} = \frac{0.35}{0.5774 - 0.35} = \frac{0.35}{0.2274}$$

$$BF_{leading} = 1.539$$

**Step 2 — Effective input torque on the shoes (taking moment arm $a \approx r_{eff}$):**

$$T_{input} = F_{cyl,total} \times r_{eff} = 7389 \times 0.135 = 997.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Braking torque developed:**

$$T_b = BF \times T_{input} = 1.539 \times 997.5$$

$$\boxed{T_b = 1535.3 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.535 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Discussion of self-energization (3 marks within 7):**
The brake factor of **1.539 > 1** indicates that the friction torque **exceeds** the input torque by 53.9 %. This amplification is the **self-energization** effect: the friction force itself creates a moment about the anchor that pushes the shoe harder into the drum, multiplying the normal force beyond the hydraulic value. This is why drum brakes feel "grabby" and why they need only a small hydraulic input to produce a large braking torque. However, if $\mu$ were to increase to **0.58** (e.g., a high-friction lining or wet conditions), the denominator would approach zero and the brake would self-lock, locking the wheel.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Conceptual and Comparative Focus

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Equivalent]`**

#### (a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **duo-servo drum brake**. Why is it the most common type used on the rear axle of passenger cars? [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A duo-servo drum brake consists of **two shoes** (primary and secondary) linked at the bottom by a **floating anchor / adjuster link** rather than a fixed anchor pin. The wheel cylinder sits at the top, between the upper ends of the two shoes. A single adjuster screw at the floating anchor compensates for lining wear.

**Working:** When the wheel cylinder is pressurized, both shoes expand outward. The **primary (forward) shoe** is a leading shoe, so it self-energizes. The friction force it develops is transmitted **through the floating link** as an **additional actuation force** on the **secondary (rearward) shoe**, which is itself also a leading shoe for the **opposite** direction of drum rotation. The two shoes therefore "servo" each other, producing a multiplicative brake factor (often 2.0–2.5).

**Why common on rear axles:**
1. **Self-energization in both directions** of wheel rotation (forward and reverse).
2. **High brake factor** gives strong braking from modest hydraulic input.
3. **Built-in parking brake** capability — the same shoes can be actuated mechanically by a cable, drum, and lever for the parking function, eliminating a separate parking-brake caliper.
4. **Low cost** compared to a disc brake of equivalent torque capacity.

#### (b) Compare drum brakes and disc brakes on at least six parameters. Why is the rear axle of modern passenger cars still fitted with a drum brake despite the disc brake's superior heat dissipation? [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks within 7):**

| Parameter | Drum Brake | Disc Brake |
|---|---|---|
| Heat dissipation | Poor (enclosed) | Excellent (open rotor) |
| Brake fade resistance | Low | High |
| Brake factor | High (self-energizing) | Moderate |
| Wet performance | Poor (water trapped) | Excellent (centrifugal clearing) |
| Parking brake | Easily integrated | Needs separate mechanism |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Self-adjustment | Manual/automatic adjuster required | Self-adjusting (piston movement) |
| Pad/shoe wear indicator | Less visible | Easier to inspect |
| Weight | Heavier for same torque | Lighter |

**Reason rear axle still uses drum (3 marks within 7):**
1. **Cost reduction** — drum brakes are ~30–40 % cheaper than equivalent disc systems.
2. **Integrated parking brake** — the drum's mechanical simplicity allows a simple cable-actuated parking brake without an extra drum-in-hat unit.
3. **Lower rear braking demand** — under braking, the vehicle's weight transfers forward, so the **rear brakes do only ~30–40 % of total work**. They rarely suffer from severe thermal fade in normal driving.
4. **Long lining life** — because rear brakes are lightly loaded, drum linings often outlast front disc pads.
5. **Regulatory approval** — existing type-approval data and tooling for drum rear brakes make them attractive for low-/mid-segment vehicles.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Drum Brake Questions:**
> 1. **Sign in BF denominator** — Confusing leading vs. trailing sign. Always write both, side by side. *(Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence.)*
> 2. **Units** — Using `kgf/cm²` without converting to Pa. KTU strictly follows SI units. Multiply kgf/cm² by $9.81 \times 10^4$ to get Pa. *(Loss: 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Forgetting the +1 in $T_{input} = F \cdot a$** — Many students compute BF and then forget to multiply by the actual input torque. Always write $T_b = BF \times T_{input}$, not $T_b = BF \times F$ alone.
> 4. **Drawing a disc brake in a drum-brake question** — Examiners deduct 2 marks if the diagram shows a caliper and rotor instead of a drum and shoes.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark sub-parts** — A neat labeled sketch carries 2 marks of the 7. Always include one.
> 6. **Confusing the parking-brake mechanism** — In a duo-servo brake, the parking brake uses the **same shoes** via a cable, lever, and small expander. It does **not** use a separate drum.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Carry):**

- Drum brakes come in two principal geometries: **Internal Expanding** (automotive) and **External Contracting** (industrial/band brake).
- An internal expanding drum brake has **two shoes** (primary/secondary), a **wheel cylinder** at the top, a **fixed anchor pin** at the bottom (or a **floating link** in duo-servo), and a **return spring**.
- **Leading shoe** = self-energizing; **Trailing shoe** = de-energizing. Leading shoes have a higher brake factor.
- **Brake factor (BF)** = friction torque / input torque. For leading: $BF = \mu / (\tan\phi - \mu)$. For trailing: $BF = \mu / (\tan\phi + \mu)$.
- The four practical types of internal drum brake are: **SLS, TLS (Two Leading), Duo-Servo, and STS (Single Trailing)**.
- **Duo-servo** is the most common rear-axle brake because of bidirectional self-energization, high BF, and easy parking-brake integration.
- **Drum brake disadvantages:** poor heat dissipation, brake fade at >300 °C, water-trapping (poor wet performance), heavier than equivalent disc.
- **Drum brake advantages:** low cost, self-energization, integrated parking brake, long lining life, failsafe spring-applied parking.
- **Pneumatic actuation** (heavy vehicles): 0.6–0.8 MPa air pressure → S-cam → shoes. Standard brake-valve pressure is **0.65 MPa**.
- **Thermal expansion of cast-iron drum**: $\alpha \approx 11 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$. A 350 °C rise in a 280 mm drum causes ~1 mm radial growth — more than the radial clearance, leading to **pedal-travel increase and thermal fade**.
- **Standard drum diameters:** 180–280 mm (passenger car), 320–420 mm (truck/bus).
- **Always state units** in SI (N, m, Pa, °C, kg). Always include a **neat labeled sketch** in 7-mark sub-parts.
- **KTU Board favourite topics from this module:** (i) Leading vs. trailing shoe derivation, (ii) BF calculation, (iii) Duo-servo working, (iv) Drum vs. disc comparison, (v) Pneumatic/S-cam actuation in commercial vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Constructional Details" in an Automotive Chassis?

**Constructional Details** refer to the **physical anatomy, internal architecture, material composition, and assembly arrangement** of the major mechanical sub-systems that constitute a vehicle's chassis. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic dissects *how* each component is actually built — going beyond functional theory into the engineering reality of bearings, bushings, joints, splines, lubrication paths, and load-bearing members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Constructional Details* encompass the design, geometry, and assembly of the **steering system, axles, wheels, and tyres** — the four cornerstones of vehicle dynamics, ride, and directional control.

The major sub-systems covered under this topic are:

| Sub-System | Primary Engineering Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Steering System** | Converts driver input (rotary motion of steering wheel) into angular displacement of front wheels |
| **Axles** | Transmits power from differential to wheels; supports vehicle weight |
| **Wheels** | Supports tyre, provides mounting to hub/axle, houses brake assembly |
| **Tyres** | Only contact patch with road; transfers traction, braking, and cornering forces |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Imagine the human body:
- The **spine** = Chassis Frame
- The **shoulder & hip joints** = Axles (allowing rotation)
- The **hands & feet** = Wheels & Tyres (gripping the ground)
- The **nervous system signals to the brain** = Steering system (directing where the body moves)

Just as a doctor must understand the *exact anatomy* of bones, joints, and tendons to treat a patient, an automotive engineer must understand the *constructional details* of these four sub-systems to design, diagnose, or improve a vehicle.

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU-Reported Engineering Metrics:**
> - **Steering Ratio:** Typically **15:1 to 20:1** for passenger cars
> - **Caster Angle:** **3° to 5°** (positive)
> - **King Pin Inclination (KPI):** **7° to 10°**
> - **Tyre Aspect Ratio:** Modern low-profile tyres use **40 to 65**
> - **Wheel Standard Sizes:** 13", 14", 15", 16", 17", 18"
> - **Tyre Standard:** P-metric (P195/65R15) or LT-metric for light trucks
> - **Axle Load Capacity:** Front axle — **800 to 1200 kg** (typical sedan)

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examiners

The KTU board examiners frequently frame questions on **constructional details** because:

1. It tests the student's ability to *visualize* 3D assemblies from 2D textbook diagrams.
2. It bridges the gap between **theory (forces, moments)** and **practical engineering**.
3. It exposes students to **material selection, manufacturing processes, and tolerances** — true hallmarks of a Mechanical/Automobile Engineer's competence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Steering System — Constructional Sub-Components

The steering system can be broken down into **three logical blocks**, each with its own constructional anatomy.

### 2.1.1 Block A: Steering Wheel & Column Assembly

**Steering Wheel:**
- **Construction:** A rigid circular rim (typically **350–400 mm diameter**) made of *polyurethane (PU)* or *leather-wrapped steel skeleton*.
- **Hub:** Houses the **horn contact**, **airbag assembly**, and **cruise control switches** in modern cars.
- **Spokes:** 3 or 4 spokes transferring torque to the hub.

**Steering Column:**
- **Upper shaft (Collapsible):** Designed to collapse telescopically during a frontal crash — energy-absorbing feature.
- **Intermediate shaft (Universal Joints):** Connects upper shaft to steering gear; allows angular misalignment.
- **Lower shaft:** Splined connection to the steering gear input shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why collapsible column?** In a frontal crash at 50 km/h, the steering column must absorb **~1500 N of axial force** to prevent the wheel from intruding into the driver's chest — this is mandated by **AIS-052 (Indian Automotive Industry Standard)** and **ECE R12**.

### 2.1.2 Block B: Steering Gear Box

The gear box is the **torque-amplifying heart** of the steering system. Two principal types dominate:

**Type 1: Recirculating Ball Steering Gear (RBS)**
- Used in older/heavier vehicles (trucks, SUVs).
- **Construction:** A **worm gear** meshes with a **sector gear** via a series of **steel balls** circulating through a closed loop.
- **Working fluid:** Grease-packed (sealed unit) or oil-bath.
- **Advantage:** Low friction, high durability, handles heavy loads.

**Type 2: Rack and Pinion Steering Gear**
- Used in modern passenger cars (Maruti, Hyundai, Honda).
- **Construction:** A **circular pinion gear** at the end of the steering shaft engages a **linear toothed rack**. Rotation of pinion → linear motion of rack.
- **Mounting:** Rack is supported by **bushing or hydraulic mount** to isolate NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
- **Variants:** Mechanical, Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS), Electric Power Steering (EPS).

### 2.1.3 Block C: Steering Linkages

These connect the steering gear to the wheels.

| Component | Constructional Detail | Material |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pitman Arm** | Lever arm attached to steering arm of relay/middle rod | Forged steel |
| **Drag Link / Middle Rod** | Long tubular linkage transferring motion to both sides | Seamless steel tube |
| **Tie Rod** | Connects steering knuckle to relay rod; has **ball joints** at both ends | EN8 / EN24 steel |
| **Steering Knuckle (Hub Carrier)** | Forged Y-shaped arm; carries wheel hub, brake caliper, ball joints | Forged alloy steel |
| **King Pin (in older designs)** | Vertical pin allowing knuckle to pivot | Hardened steel |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern **McPherson strut** front suspensions, the king pin is **eliminated** — its function is taken over by the **strut bearing and ball joints**. KPI is now built into the geometry of the strut mount.

## 2.2 Axles — Constructional Analysis

### 2.2.1 Live Axle (Solid Axle)
- **Construction:** A single rigid beam housing the differential, connecting both rear wheels.
- **Used in:** Trucks, SUVs (older), rear-wheel-drive cars.
- **Components:** Axle housing (cast steel), axle shafts (forged steel), **C-clip or flange** for wheel attachment, semi-floating / full-floating / three-quarter floating designs.

### 2.2.2 Independent Suspension Axle (Dead Axle)
- **Construction:** Each wheel is mounted via its own short stub axle, allowing independent vertical motion.
- **Used in:** All modern passenger cars (front), most modern SUVs (rear).
- **Components:** Stub axle, hub bearing (sealed unit), drive shaft (CV joint), brake disc/caliper.

### 2.2.3 Axle Bearing Types
- **Tapered Roller Bearing:** Used in older live axles.
- **Ball Bearing:** Used in light-duty applications.
- **Hub Unit Bearing (HUB):** Sealed, pre-greased, pre-adjusted — modern standard.

## 2.3 Wheels — Constructional Anatomy

A typical automotive wheel consists of:

1. **Rim** — Outer cylindrical part that holds the tyre bead.
2. **Disc / Spider** — Central portion connecting rim to hub.
3. **Hole / Spoke pattern** — Reduces weight, aids brake cooling.
4. **Mounting holes** — 4, 5, or 6 lug pattern (e.g., **4×100**, **5×114.3**).
5. **Centre bore** — Pilots the wheel onto the hub.
6. **Valve stem hole** — For tubeless tyre inflation.

**Types of Wheels:**
| Type | Construction | Weight | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Steel Wheel** | Rolled steel disc + rim, welded together | Heavy | Low |
| **Alloy Wheel** | Cast or forged aluminium/magnesium alloy | Light | High |
| **Forged Wheel** | Forged billet aluminium | Lightest | Highest |
| **Composite Wheel** | Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer | Ultra-light | Premium |

## 2.4 Tyres — Constructional Analysis

A pneumatic radial tyre (modern standard) is a marvel of layered engineering.

### 2.4.1 Tyre Construction — Layer by Layer (Inside Out)

| Layer | Material | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inner Liner** | Halobutyl rubber | Air retention (replaces old inner tube) |
| **Carcass (ply)** | Polyester / Nylon / Rayon cords | Structural backbone, resists pressure |
| **Belt Package** | Steel belts + nylon overlays | Stabilizes tread, reduces rolling resistance |
| **Tread** | Synthetic + natural rubber + silica + carbon black | Road contact, wear, grip |
| **Sidewall** | Rubber compound | Flexibility, protects carcass |
| **Bead** | Steel wire bundles (high tensile) | Anchors tyre to rim |
| **Bead Filler** | Rubber + steel | Stiffens lower sidewall |

### 2.4.2 Tyre Designations

**P-metric designation (e.g., 195/65 R15 91H):**
- **195** = Section width in **mm**
- **65** = Aspect ratio (sidewall height = 65% of width)
- **R** = Radial construction
- **15** = Rim diameter in **inches**
- **91** = Load index (max **615 kg** per tyre)
- **H** = Speed symbol (max **210 km/h**)

### 2.4.3 Tyre Types

- **Diagonal (Bias) Tyre:** Plies cross at 30–40°. Stiffer sidewall. Used in commercial vehicles/older cars.
- **Radial Tyre:** Plies run radially (90° to centreline). Flexible sidewall, rigid tread. Standard for modern cars.
- **Tubeless Tyre:** Has inner liner; uses rim seal. Standard today.
- **Run-Flat Tyre:** Reinforced sidewall supports vehicle even at 0 psi (e.g., Bridgestone RFT, Michelin PAX).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Steering Ratio (SR)** | $SR = \frac{\text{Steering wheel rotation}}{\text{Wheel rotation}}$ | ratio | Determines steering effort |
| **Steering Geometry — KPI Scrub Radius Effect** | $r_{scrub} = r \cdot \cos(KPI) - r_{kingpin\,offset}$ | mm | Steering feel, brake pull |
| **Ackermann Condition** | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{w}{l}$ | degrees | Perfect cornering geometry |
| **Tyre Outer Diameter** | $D = 2 \times \left(W \times \frac{AR}{100} + R_{rim}\right)$ | mm | Speedometer calibration |
| **Revs per km** | $N = \frac{1000}{D \times \pi \times 10^{-6}}$ | revs/km | Odometer accuracy |
| **Tyre Load Capacity (approx.)** | $Load = LI \times 0.4536 \times 9.81$ | N (from LI in kg) | Vehicle payload |
| **Wheel Revolutions for 1 km** | $Revs = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | revs | For ABS/ESC tuning |
| **Rolling Radius (effective)** | $R_e = 0.965 \times R_{static}$ | m | Used in vehicle dynamics |
| **Tyre Slip Ratio** | $s = \frac{\omega R_e - V}{\max(\omega R_e, V)}$ | dimensionless | Traction control |
| **Self-Aligning Torque (SAT)** | $T_{SAT} = F_y \cdot t_{pneumatic\,trail}$ | N·m | Steering feel, returnability |

> **Where:** $\delta_o$ = outer wheel steer angle, $\delta_i$ = inner wheel steer angle, $w$ = track width, $l$ = wheelbase, $W$ = section width, $AR$ = aspect ratio, $R_{rim}$ = rim radius, $LI$ = load index, $D$ = outer diameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all your answer sheets, **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. This is a frequent KTU board key mistake.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The constructional details discussed above are not just academic — they directly influence:

1. **Crash Safety:** Collapsible column, energy-absorbing steering gear housings.
2. **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Rack mounting bushes, wheel balancing weights.
3. **Fuel Efficiency:** Low rolling resistance tyres, lightweight forged wheels.
4. **Vehicle Dynamics:** Ackermann geometry, KPI, caster, camber — all built into the *construction* of the suspension/steering.
5. **Serviceability:** HUB bearings are sealed units — no greasing required. Tyres are direction-specific (asymmetric tread).
6. **Autonomous Vehicles:** EPS (Electric Power Steering) enables lane-keep assist, automatic parking.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ackermann Steering Geometry

The **Ackermann condition** ensures that during a turn, the inner and outer wheels rotate about a **common instantaneous centre**, eliminating tyre scrub.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let:
- $w$ = track width (distance between king pins)
- $l$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- $R$ = radius of turn
- $\delta_i$ = inner wheel steer angle
- $\delta_o$ = outer wheel steer angle

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup

For a vehicle turning about a point $O$ at radius $R$ from the rear axle centreline:

The **inner wheel** has a smaller turn radius, so $\delta_i > \delta_o$.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Draw the right triangle formed by the rear axle centre, the turn centre $O$, and the inner wheel's contact patch.

From the geometry:
$$\tan(\delta_i) = \frac{l}{R - \frac{w}{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Similarly, for the outer wheel:
$$\tan(\delta_o) = \frac{l}{R + \frac{w}{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Express $R$ from the first equation:
$$R - \frac{w}{2} = \frac{l}{\tan \delta_i}$$
$$R = \frac{l}{\tan \delta_i} + \frac{w}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the second equation:
$$\tan(\delta_o) = \frac{l}{\frac{l}{\tan \delta_i} + \frac{w}{2} + \frac{w}{2}} = \frac{l}{\frac{l}{\tan \delta_i} + w}$$

**Step 5:** Take the reciprocal and simplify:

$$\frac{1}{\tan \delta_o} = \frac{\frac{l}{\tan \delta_i} + w}{l} = \frac{1}{\tan \delta_i} + \frac{w}{l}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $\frac{1}{\tan} = \cot$:

$$\boxed{\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{w}{l}}$$

This is the **Ackermann Condition**. For 100% Ackermann, the steering linkage must be dimensioned to satisfy this exactly. In practice, vehicles use **60–80% Ackermann** to balance tight-corner agility with high-speed stability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tyre Outer Diameter Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

Given a tyre designation **P 195/65 R15**, calculate the outer diameter.

### 3.2.2 Given Data
- Section width $W$ = 195 mm
- Aspect ratio $AR$ = 65% = 0.65
- Rim diameter $R_{rim\_diam}$ = 15 inches = 15 × 25.4 = **381 mm**

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Convert rim diameter to mm.
$$R_{rim\_diam} = 15 \times 25.4 = 381 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate sidewall height $H$.
$$H = W \times \frac{AR}{100} = 195 \times \frac{65}{100} = 126.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate outer diameter.
$$D = 2 \times H + R_{rim\_diam}$$
$$D = 2 \times 126.75 + 381 = 253.5 + 381 = 634.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to inches for cross-verification.
$$D = \frac{634.5}{25.4} = 24.98 \text{ inches} \approx 25 \text{ inches}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the rolling radius (used in vehicle dynamics).
$$R_e = \frac{D}{2} \times 10^{-3} = \frac{634.5}{2} \times 10^{-3} = 0.31725 \text{ m}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steering Ratio & Wheel-to-Wheel Turning

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A passenger car has a steering wheel rotation of **540° lock-to-lock** and a wheel rotation of **30° lock-to-lock on each side**. Find the steering ratio and the inner wheel maximum steer angle.

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Step 1:** Total wheel rotation (one side) = 30°. The wheel can rotate 30° left + 30° right = **60° total**.

**Step 2:** Steering ratio:
$$SR = \frac{540°}{60°} = 9$$

**Step 3:** Ackermann-based check (if $w = 1.5$ m, $l = 2.5$ m, $\delta_o = 30°$):

Using $\cot \delta_o = \cot(30°) = 1.732$:

$$\cot \delta_i = \cot \delta_o - \frac{w}{l} = 1.732 - \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 1.732 - 0.6 = 1.132$$

$$\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(1.132) = 41.46°$$

**Step 4:** Practical consideration: Real linkages provide ~70% Ackermann, so:
$$\delta_i^{actual} = 0.7 \times (41.46 - 30) + 30 = 0.7 \times 11.46 + 30 = 8.02 + 30 = 38.02°$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Tyre Revs per km

**Given:** Tyre designation **205/55 R16**

**Step 1:** Rim diameter in mm: $16 \times 25.4 = 406.4$ mm.

**Step 2:** Sidewall height: $H = 205 \times 0.55 = 112.75$ mm.

**Step 3:** Outer diameter: $D = 2(112.75) + 406.4 = 225.5 + 406.4 = 631.9$ mm.

**Step 4:** Circumference: $C = \pi \times D = 3.1416 \times 631.9 = 1985.5$ mm = 1.9855 m.

**Step 5:** Revolutions per km:
$$N = \frac{1000}{C} = \frac{1000}{1.9855} = 503.6 \text{ revs/km}$$

**Step 6:** For a journey of 350 km: total revs = $350 \times 503.6 = 1,76,260$ revolutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about "speedometer error" or "rolling radius vs. static radius", remember that manufacturers use a **rolling radius correction factor of 0.965** to account for tyre deflection under load. The dynamic effective radius is **less** than the static geometric radius.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Answers

| Parameter | Recirculating Ball Steering | Rack and Pinion Steering |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Construction** | Worm + sector + ball bearings | Pinion + linear rack |
| **Friction** | Low (rolling contact) | Low (sliding contact) |
| **Steering Feel** | Slightly less direct | More direct, sharper |
| **Weight** | Heavier | Lighter |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | Lower (mass-produced) |
| **Application** | Trucks, heavy SUVs | Modern passenger cars |
| **Power Assist** | Hydraulic common | Hydraulic or Electric |
| **Serviceability** | Adjustable lash | Limited adjustment |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper 2]**
**Q:** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a *rack and pinion* steering gear.

**Model Answer:**

A rack and pinion steering gear consists of a **circular pinion gear** (a) mounted on the lower end of the steering column. The pinion meshes with a **linear toothed rack** (b) housed in a metal tube. The rack is supported at both ends by **bushing or hydraulic mount** to isolate vibration.

**Working Principle:**
When the driver rotates the steering wheel, the rotational motion is transferred via the steering column to the **pinion gear**. The pinion teeth engage the rack teeth, converting rotational motion into **linear (translational) motion** of the rack. The rack is connected to **tie rods** at both ends, which transmit this linear motion to the **steering knuckles**, turning the wheels about their king pins (or ball joint pivots).

**Key Constructional Features:**
- Pinion: Helical teeth for smooth, quiet operation
- Rack: Forged steel, induction-hardened teeth
- Mounting: Rubber bushings or hydraulic bushings for NVH isolation
- Sealing: Rubber boots to prevent dirt ingress
- Variants: Mechanical / Hydraulic (HPS) / Electric (EPS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it is preferred for modern cars:** Direct steering feel, compact size, light weight, easy integration with power steering systems, and low manufacturing cost due to high-volume production.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
**Q:** Explain the constructional features of a modern *tubeless radial tyre* with a neat sketch. List its advantages over a bias-ply tyre.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (from inside out):**

1. **Inner Liner:** Halobutyl rubber — provides air retention without an inner tube.
2. **Carcass (Ply):** Polyester or rayon cords running **radially (90° to centreline)** — provides primary strength and flexibility.
3. **Belt Package:** Two layers of **steel wire belts** at opposing angles, capped by a **nylon overlay** — stabilizes the tread and improves wear.
4. **Tread:** Wear-resistant rubber compound with silica and carbon black — provides grip and channels water.
5. **Sidewall:** Flexible rubber protecting the carcass — carries markings like size, brand, DOT code.
6. **Bead:** High-tensile steel wire bundles — mechanically anchors the tyre to the rim.
7. **Bead Filler:** Stiff rubber-steel compound — stabilizes the lower sidewall.

**Advantages over Bias-Ply Tyre:**

| Feature | Radial Tyre | Bias-Ply Tyre |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sidewall flexibility** | High — flexes independently | Low — moves as a unit |
| **Tread stability** | Excellent | Average |
| **Tyre life** | 50,000 – 80,000 km | 30,000 – 40,000 km |
| **Rolling resistance** | 20–30% lower | Higher → more fuel |
| **Heat dissipation** | Better (steel belts radiate) | Poorer |
| **Speed capability** | Higher | Limited |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |

**Diagrams/Sketch hint:** Draw the cross-section showing all 7 layers labeled (1 to 7) with the bead seated on the rim.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** out of a choice of two (internal choice within a module).

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester Examination]**

**Q:** (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional details of a recirculating ball steering gear**. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** over the rack and pinion type. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Explain the **Ackermann steering geometry** with a neat diagram. Derive the condition for perfect Ackermann steering. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Constructional Details of Recirculating Ball Steering Gear (RBSG):**

The RBSG consists of the following constructional elements:

1. **Input Shaft (a):** Carries a precisely machined **worm gear** at its lower end. Connected to the steering column via splines.

2. **Worm Wheel / Ball Nut (b):** A nut threaded internally to match the worm. The thread has a **groove for steel balls**.

3. **Steel Balls (c):** High-grade chrome steel balls (~5–7 mm diameter) that recirculate through a **closed circuit** between the worm and the nut. They roll instead of slide, drastically reducing friction.

4. **Return Guide / Return Tube (d):** Channels the steel balls back from one end of the ball nut to the other, completing the recirculation loop.

5. **Sector Gear (e):** Cut teeth on the outer periphery of the ball nut mesh with a **sector gear (or peg)** on the **pitman shaft**.

6. **Pitman Shaft / Sector Shaft (f):** Carries the sector gear and the **pitman arm** externally. Output shaft of the steering gear.

7. **Housing (g):** Cast iron housing holds all components; filled with **EP (Extreme Pressure) grease** for life.

8. **Adjusting Screws:** Two screws adjust the **mesh preload** of the sector gear and **worm bearing preload**.

**Working:**
When the steering wheel rotates, the worm rotates → balls roll → ball nut moves axially (slightly) → sector gear on nut rotates the pitman shaft → pitman arm swings through an arc → moves the drag link → turns the wheels.

**Advantages over Rack and Pinion:**
- Handles very high steering loads (used in trucks, heavy SUVs).
- Smoother operation due to rolling friction of balls.
- Long service life.
- Less affected by wheel kickback.

**Disadvantages:**
- Heavier and bulkier.
- More complex manufacturing.
- Slightly less direct steering feel.
- Higher cost.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Ackermann Steering Geometry — Explanation & Derivation:**

**Concept:** In a turn, the inner wheel must steer through a **larger angle** than the outer wheel, because the inner wheel travels on a **smaller radius** of turn. If both wheels steered through the same angle, the tyres would **scrub sideways** (drag), causing wear and instability.

**Diagram Hint:** Draw a top view showing:
- Rear axle at the bottom
- Two front wheels at top (left and right)
- Turn centre to the left of the vehicle
- Inner wheel (closer to turn centre) with angle $\delta_i$
- Outer wheel (farther from turn centre) with angle $\delta_o$
- Wheelbase $l$ and track width $w$ marked

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 above for the complete 6-step derivation)

Final result:
$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{w}{l}$$

**Practical Implementation:**
- 100% Ackermann is rarely used because it is best only at low speeds and large steer angles.
- Most road cars use **60–80% Ackermann** to balance:
  - Tight cornering (needs high Ackermann)
  - High-speed stability (needs low Ackermann)
- **Racing cars (e.g., F1)** use **< 50% Ackermann** for high-speed cornering precision.

**Steering geometry also includes:**
- **Caster angle** (3° to 5°) — for straight-line stability.
- **Camber angle** (–0.5° to 0°) — for even tyre wear.
- **Toe-in / Toe-out** — for compensating bump-steer.
- **King Pin Inclination (KPI)** (7° to 10°) — for self-centring torque.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q:** (a) With a neat sketch, explain the **constructional details of a steering column** used in a modern passenger car. Highlight the **safety features** built into it. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Explain the **constructional features of a tubeless radial tyre** with a labelled diagram. Discuss the **function of each layer** and the **meaning of the designation 205/55 R16 91V**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Steering Column Construction:**

The modern steering column is far more than just a shaft — it is a **safety-critical, multi-function assembly**.

**Components:**

1. **Steering Wheel Hub (1):** Mounted on the top of the column. Houses horn contact, airbag inflator, and control switches.

2. **Upper Shaft (2):** Splined at both ends; connected to the wheel hub and intermediate shaft.

3. **Collapsible Mechanism (3):** Two telescoping tubes that **collapse telescopically** during a frontal impact. Designed using **controlled bending elements** or **plastic inserts with shear pins** that deform at a precise load (~1500 N).

4. **Stalk Switches:** Mounted on either side of the upper column:
   - Left: **Turn indicator, headlight dip, high beam**
   - Right: **Wiper, washer, rear defogger**
   - Modern cars: **Cruise control buttons, audio controls**

5. **Bearing Support (4):** A **ball bearing or polymer bushing** supports the column at the firewall penetration point.

6. **Intermediate Shaft (5):** A short shaft with **two universal joints** (often a **Cardan joint** or **constant-velocity joint**). Allows angular and axial misalignment between upper and lower shafts.

7. **Lower Shaft (6):** Splined connection to the steering gear input shaft.

8. **Anti-THEFT Lock (7):** A **folding pawl** engages a notch in the column to lock the steering when the key is removed.

9. **Energy Absorber:** The lower portion of the column has a **controlled-yield section** that bends plastically on impact.

**Safety Features (Built-in):**

- **AIS-052 / ECE R12 Compliance:** Prevents rearward intrusion of column into the driver's chest.
- **Collapsible Design:** Multi-stage energy absorption.
- **Airbag Compatibility:** Hub is designed to house the airbag module; column transfers airbag reaction loads.
- **Lock Mechanism:** Anti-theft protection.
- **NVH Isolation:** Rubber couplings reduce vibration transfer.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Tubeless Radial Tyre — Construction:**

(Refer to Question 2 of Part A for detailed layer description.)

**Diagram Hint:** Cross-sectional view showing:
- Tread (outermost)
- Two steel belts
- Polyester carcass plies (radial)
- Inner liner
- Sidewall
- Bead area seated on rim
- Label each layer

**Function of Each Layer:** (Tabulated for clarity)

| Layer | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Tread | Road contact, grip, wear resistance |
| Steel Belts | Stabilize tread, reduce rolling resistance |
| Nylon Overlay | High-speed stability, holds belts in place |
| Carcass Ply | Carries inflation pressure load |
| Inner Liner | Air retention |
| Sidewall | Flexibility, protects carcass |
| Bead | Anchors tyre to rim |
| Bead Filler | Stiffens lower sidewall |

**Meaning of 205/55 R16 91V:**

- **205** = Section width = **205 mm**
- **55** = Aspect ratio = sidewall height is **55% of 205 mm = 112.75 mm**
- **R** = **Radial** construction
- **16** = Rim diameter = **16 inches = 406.4 mm**
- **91** = Load index = max load per tyre = **615 kg**
- **V** = Speed symbol = max safe speed = **240 km/h**

**Calculated Outer Diameter:**
$$D = 2 \times 112.75 + 406.4 = 631.9 \text{ mm} \approx 24.88 \text{ inches}$$

**Suitability for Indian Roads:** Speed rating V allows safe driving up to 240 km/h. Load index 91 is suitable for sedans and compact SUVs.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Sketching marks (3–4 marks per question):** Students often write paragraphs without neat labelled diagrams. KTU examiners specifically allocate marks for **labelled figures**. Always draw:
>    - The component cross-section
>    - Label all parts with **name + function** (e.g., "**Bead – anchors tyre to rim**").
>    - Use **pencil + ruler** for neatness.
>
> 2. **Confusing Rack & Pinion with Recirculating Ball:** Many students write a generic answer for both types. The examiner expects **specific constructional differences**. Always highlight: **balls in RBSG** vs **direct gear contact in R&P**.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Ackermann derivation steps:** Marks are split across 6 steps of derivation. Skipping any step loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Tyre designation wrong decoding:** Writing "R15" as "rim radius 15 cm" is a **fatal error**. It is rim **diameter** in **inches**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always specify **mm, kg, km/h, m, N·m, degrees**. Unit-less answers are penalized.
>
> 6. **Not using KTU terminology:** Use exact terms like *"pneumatic trail"*, *"king pin inclination"*, *"aspect ratio"*, *"Ackermann condition"*. Avoid colloquial terms like "front wheels of car" — use "front steerable wheels".
>
> 7. **No conclusions:** Always end with a short concluding sentence: *"Thus, the modern tubeless radial tyre provides superior ride, handling, and fuel efficiency over the older bias-ply tube-type tyre."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Constructional Details**

### A. Steering System
- [ ] Steering wheel diameter: **350–400 mm**, PU/leather over steel skeleton
- [ ] Steering column has **3 shafts**: upper (collapsible), intermediate (UJ), lower (splined)
- [ ] Collapsible column absorbs **~1500 N** on frontal impact (AIS-052)
- [ ] **Recirculating Ball:** uses steel balls in closed loop, worm + sector
- [ ] **Rack & Pinion:** pinion + linear rack, more direct feel, lighter, cheaper
- [ ] Pitman arm + drag link + tie rod + ball joints = steering linkage
- [ ] Modern McPherson strut eliminates king pin
- [ ] Ackermann condition: $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/l$
- [ ] Practical cars use **60–80% Ackermann**; F1 uses <50%
- [ ] KPI = 7° to 10°; Caster = 3° to 5°
- [ ] EPS = Electric Power Steering (modern); HPS = Hydraulic Power Steering (older)

### B. Axles
- [ ] **Live axle:** single rigid beam, used in trucks
- [ ] **Dead/Independent axle:** stub axles, used in modern cars
- [ ] **Semi-floating / Full-floating / 3/4-floating** axle designs
- [ ] **HUB bearings** are sealed, pre-greased, pre-adjusted
- [ ] **Stub axle** connects wheel hub to suspension upright

### C. Wheels
- [ ] Steel wheel: heavy, low cost (entry-level cars)
- [ ] Alloy wheel: cast/forged Al-Mg, lighter, better NVH
- [ ] Forged wheel: lightest, strongest, premium
- [ ] Mounting patterns: 4×100, 5×114.3, etc.
- [ ] Centre bore pilots wheel on hub

### D. Tyres
- [ ] **7 layers:** Tread → Nylon overlay → Steel belts → Carcass → Inner liner → Sidewall → Bead
- [ ] **Radial** plies run 90° to centreline (modern)
- [ ] **Bias plies** cross at 30–40° (older/commercial)
- [ ] **Tubeless** uses halobutyl inner liner (no inner tube)
- [ ] Aspect ratio = sidewall height / section width × 100
- [ ] Lower aspect ratio = lower-profile, sportier, harsher ride
- [ ] Load index 91 = 615 kg; 95 = 690 kg
- [ ] Speed symbol: S (180), T (190), H (210), V (240), W (270), Y (300)
- [ ] **Run-flat** tyres work even at 0 psi (reinforced sidewall)
- [ ] Effective rolling radius = 0.965 × static radius
- [ ] Odometer uses 1000 / circumference to compute revs/km

### E. Universal Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Ackermann: $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/l$
- [ ] Outer diameter: $D = 2W \cdot AR/100 + R_{rim}$
- [ ] Revs/km: $N = 1000 / (\pi D)$
- [ ] Tyre load: $Load = LI \times 0.4536 \times 9.81$ (in Newtons)
- [ ] Rolling radius: $R_e = 0.965 \times R_{static}$
- [ ] Slip ratio: $s = (\omega R - V) / \max(\omega R, V)$

### F. Frequently Asked Numerical Values
- [ ] Steering ratio: 15:1 to 20:1 (passenger cars)
- [ ] Lock-to-lock rotation: ~2.5 to 3 turns
- [ ] Wheel turn angle: ~30° each side
- [ ] KPI: 7° to 10°
- [ ] Caster: 3° to 5°
- [ ] Camber: –0.5° to 0°
- [ ] Toe-in: 1–3 mm (per wheel)
- [ ] Aspect ratio: 65 (comfort) to 40 (sport)
- [ ] Rim size: 13" to 18" (modern cars)

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In every KTU 2024 answer on **Constructional Details**, **always conclude** with a sentence on **why** that constructional feature exists (e.g., "The collapsible design enhances crash safety by controlled energy absorption"). This shows **engineering depth** and earns the **1–2 grace marks** that often decide the grade boundary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leading and Trailing Shoe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Leading and Trailing Shoe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In an **internal expanding drum brake**, two brake shoes press against the inner cylindrical surface of a rotating brake drum to generate the retarding torque. Depending on the **direction of drum rotation** and the **geometric orientation** of each shoe with respect to its anchor pin (pivot), each shoe is classified as either a **Leading Shoe** (also called *Primary*, *Self-energizing*, or *Self-energised shoe*) or a **Trailing Shoe** (also called *Secondary* or *Self-de-energising shoe*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:**
> A **Leading Shoe** is one in which the frictional force developed at the drum-shoe interface creates a moment (about the shoe's pivot) that **aids** the actuating (application) force — the shoe tends to "self-energise" and press harder against the drum as rotation increases.
>
> A **Trailing Shoe** is one in which the frictional force creates a moment that **opposes** the actuating force — the shoe is "self-de-energising" and becomes less effective as rotation increases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of opening a stuck window by pushing and sliding:**
> Imagine a window stuck in its frame. If you push the window outward while simultaneously **sliding your hand UP** along the edge, your sliding motion (friction force) HELPS the window pop outward — that is a **Leading Shoe** analogy: motion (friction) aids your push.
>
> Now imagine you push the window outward while sliding your hand **DOWN** the edge. The downward slide OPPOSES the outward push, making the window harder to open — that is the **Trailing Shoe** analogy: motion (friction) opposes your push.

## 1.3 Why the Classification Matters

- The same physical brake, with the drum rotating in the opposite direction, will **swap the classification of its shoes**.
- This is why vehicle drum brakes are **directional** (left-hand and right-hand brake assemblies are mirror images).
- The leading shoe typically generates **2 to 2.5 times** the friction torque of the trailing shoe for the **same actuator force**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU Problems:**
> - Coefficient of friction $\mu$ for brake lining: typically **0.3 to 0.45**
> - Self-energising factor (ratio of leading to trailing torque) typically **2 : 1**
> - Drum radius $r$, shoe width $w$, and shoe geometry: $a$, $b$, $c$ as defined below
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shoe Geometry and Free-Body Setup

Each shoe is modelled as a rigid lever pivoted at the **anchor pin (pivot O)**. Let us define the geometric distances from the pivot:

- $a$ = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the actuator force** $F$ (also called the **application arm**).
- $b$ = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the friction force** $\mu N$ (typically equal to the **drum radius** $r$).
- $c$ = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the normal force** $N$.

The actuator force $F$ is applied by a hydraulic wheel cylinder or a mechanical cam, pushing the shoe outward toward the drum surface. The drum pushes back on the shoe with a normal reaction $N$, while friction $\mu N$ acts tangentially.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Physics of Self-Energisation

**Step 1 — Moment balance about the anchor pin:**
The shoe is in rotational equilibrium about its pivot, so the sum of all moments must be zero.

**Step 2 — Identify direction of friction:**
The direction of friction force on the shoe is opposite to the relative motion of the drum surface against the shoe. This relative motion depends on drum rotation direction.

**Step 3 — Determine the shoe type:**
- If the friction force's moment about the pivot is in the **same sense** as the actuator force's moment → shoe is **leading** (self-energising).
- If opposite sense → shoe is **trailing** (self-de-energising).

**Step 4 — Solve for Normal Force $N$:**
The moment equation yields $N$ in terms of $F$, geometry, and $\mu$.

**Step 5 — Compute braking torque:**
The friction torque per shoe is $T = \mu N \cdot r$, where $r$ is the effective drum radius.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Leading Shoe Formula | Trailing Shoe Formula |
|---|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Normal reaction $N$ | $N = \dfrac{F \cdot a}{c - \mu b}$ | $N = \dfrac{F \cdot a}{c + \mu b}$ |
| 2 | Friction force $\mu N$ | $\mu N = \dfrac{\mu F a}{c - \mu b}$ | $\mu N = \dfrac{\mu F a}{c + \mu b}$ |
| 3 | Friction torque per shoe $T$ | $T_L = \dfrac{\mu F a r}{c - \mu b}$ | $T_T = \dfrac{\mu F a r}{c + \mu b}$ |
| 4 | Self-energising ratio $K$ | $K = \dfrac{T_L}{T_T} = \dfrac{c + \mu b}{c - \mu b}$ | — |
| 5 | Condition for stability | $\mu b < c$ (otherwise $N \to \infty$) | Always stable |
| 6 | Effective brake factor $C^*$ | $C^* = \dfrac{\mu a}{c - \mu b}$ | $C^* = \dfrac{\mu a}{c + \mu b}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorise the **sign of the $\mu b$ term** — minus for leading, plus for trailing. It is the single most common error in board answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Design Trade-off:** Automotive drum brakes typically use a **Leading–Trailing (LT)** configuration: one shoe is leading and the other trailing. This provides moderate, stable braking suitable for passenger cars.
- **Twin Leading Shoe (TLS) brakes** are used in heavy commercial vehicles where maximum braking torque is required and stability is controlled by **dual-direction actuation**.
- **Duo-Servo brakes** use an additional **adjuster link** between the two shoes so that the leading shoe's reaction partially actuates the trailing shoe — a very common design in older American passenger cars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Free-Body Diagram Conventions

Consider a single shoe pivoted at anchor pin O. The drum surface contacts the shoe at the friction lining. The shoe experiences three forces:

1. **Actuator (application) force $F$** at distance $a$ from pivot.
2. **Normal reaction $N$** from drum on shoe at distance $c$ from pivot (acts to oppose outward motion of shoe).
3. **Friction force $\mu N$** at distance $b$ from pivot (acts tangentially to drum surface).

We assume the shoe is in static (moment) equilibrium just before slipping occurs.

## 3.2 Derivation — Trailing Shoe (Self-De-Energising)

For a **trailing shoe**, the friction force acts in a direction such that its moment about the pivot **opposes** the actuator moment. Both $N$ and $\mu N$ resist the actuator's rotation about the pivot.

**Step 1:** Write the moment equilibrium about pivot O.

$$
\sum M_O = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \cdot a - N \cdot c - (\mu N) \cdot b = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Group the $N$ terms on the right-hand side.

$$
F \cdot a = N \cdot c + \mu N \cdot b
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $N$.

$$
F \cdot a = N (c + \mu b)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$.

$$
N = \frac{F \cdot a}{c + \mu b}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the friction torque. The friction force $\mu N$ acts at the drum surface, at radius $r$ from the drum centre.

$$
T_T = (\mu N) \cdot r = \frac{\mu \cdot F \cdot a \cdot r}{c + \mu b}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $\mu$ increases, $N$ decreases — the shoe becomes **less effective** with higher friction. This is **self-de-energisation**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Leading Shoe (Self-Energising)

For a **leading shoe**, the friction force acts in a direction such that its moment about the pivot **aids** the actuator moment. The friction force therefore *helps* press the shoe outward against the drum.

**Step 1:** Write moment equilibrium about O. Note the sign change for the $\mu N$ term.

$$
\sum M_O = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \cdot a - N \cdot c + (\mu N) \cdot b = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$
F \cdot a = N \cdot c - \mu N \cdot b
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $N$.

$$
F \cdot a = N (c - \mu b)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$.

$$
N = \frac{F \cdot a}{c - \mu b}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the friction torque.

$$
T_L = (\mu N) \cdot r = \frac{\mu \cdot F \cdot a \cdot r}{c - \mu b}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $\mu$ increases, the denominator $(c - \mu b)$ shrinks, making $N$ and $T_L$ grow rapidly. The shoe **self-energises** — friction creates more friction. The system becomes unstable when $\mu b \to c$, giving $N \to \infty$.

## 3.4 Self-Energising Factor (Analytical Matrix)

The ratio of leading-shoe torque to trailing-shoe torque for identical actuation is a key KTU question:

$$
K = \frac{T_L}{T_T} = \frac{\mu F a r \, / \, (c - \mu b)}{\mu F a r \, / \, (c + \mu b)}
$$

Cancel common terms:

$$
K = \frac{c + \mu b}{c - \mu b}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight — Effect of $\mu$ on the factor $K$:**
> - At $\mu = 0$: $K = 1$ (no self-energisation; both shoes identical).
> - At $\mu = 0.4$, $b = r = 100$ mm, $c = 150$ mm: $K = \dfrac{150 + 40}{150 - 40} = \dfrac{190}{110} \approx 1.73$.
> - At $\mu = 0.5$: $K = \dfrac{200}{100} = 2.0$.
> - At $\mu = 0.6$: $K = \dfrac{210}{90} \approx 2.33$.

## 3.5 Solved Analytical Example (Worked-Out Problem)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]:**
A single-leading-shoe drum brake has the following geometry: actuator arm $a = 80$ mm, distance to normal force $c = 120$ mm, distance to friction force $b = 100$ mm. The actuator force is $F = 1500$ N and $\mu = 0.35$. Drum radius $r = 150$ mm. Calculate (i) the normal force on the leading shoe, (ii) the friction torque generated.

**Solution:**

**(i) Normal force** — apply leading-shoe formula:

$$
N_L = \frac{F \cdot a}{c - \mu b}
$$

Compute the denominator numerically:

$$
c - \mu b = 120 - (0.35)(100) = 120 - 35 = 85 \text{ mm}
$$

Substitute all values:

$$
N_L = \frac{1500 \times 80}{85} = \frac{120{,}000}{85} \approx 1411.76 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Friction torque:**

$$
T_L = (\mu N_L) \cdot r = (0.35)(1411.76)(0.150) \approx 74.12 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare with a trailing shoe** having the same geometry:
> $N_T = \dfrac{120{,}000}{155} \approx 774.19$ N, giving $T_T \approx 40.65$ N·m.
> The leading shoe produces **~82 % more torque** for the same actuator force.

## 3.6 Equilibrium and Stability Conditions

**Stability condition** for a leading shoe: the denominator must remain positive.

$$
c - \mu b > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu < \frac{c}{b}
$$

If $\mu \geq c/b$, the system becomes unstable — small perturbations cause the shoe to "lock on" suddenly. Designers must keep operating $\mu$ well below this critical value. A safety factor of 2 or more is typical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical:** If $b = 100$ mm and $c = 150$ mm, the limiting $\mu$ is $1.5$ — unattainable in practice, so safe. But if $b = 120$ mm and $c = 150$ mm, limiting $\mu$ is $1.25$ — still safe. However if $c = 130$ mm and $b = 150$ mm, limiting $\mu$ is $0.87$ — close to real values, requiring careful material selection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Drum Brake Shoe Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysDrum[Drum Brake Assembly] --> dirCheck{Direction of Drum Rotation}
    dirCheck --> shoeLead[Leading Shoe Primary]
    dirCheck --> shoeTrail[Trailing Shoe Secondary]
    shoeLead --> charA1[Friction Moment Aids Actuator]
    shoeLead --> charA2[Self Energising Effect]
    shoeLead --> charA3[Normal Force Increased]
    shoeLead --> charA4[Higher Friction Torque]
    shoeTrail --> charB1[Friction Moment Opposes Actuator]
    shoeTrail --> charB2[Self De Energising Effect]
    shoeTrail --> charB3[Normal Force Reduced]
    shoeTrail --> charB4[Lower Friction Torque]
    charA4 --> brakeEff[Total Brake Torque]
    charB4 --> brakeEff
    brakeEff --> appln[Vehicle Application]
    appln --> use1[Passenger Cars LT Configuration]
    appln --> use2[Heavy Trucks TLS Configuration]
```

## 4.2 Force and Moment Analysis Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Drum Rotation Initiates] --> fric[Friction Force mu N Acts]
    fric --> moment{Moment About Pivot}
    moment --> same[Same Sense as Actuator]
    moment --> opp[Opposite Sense as Actuator]
    same --> leadRes[Leading Shoe Result]
    leadRes --> leadEq1[N equals Fa divided by c minus mu b]
    leadRes --> leadEq2[TL equals mu Fa r divided by c minus mu b]
    opp --> trailRes[Trailing Shoe Result]
    trailRes --> trailEq1[N equals Fa divided by c plus mu b]
    trailRes --> trailEq2[TT equals mu Fa r divided by c plus mu b]
    leadEq2 --> ratio[Self Energising Factor K]
    trailEq2 --> ratio
    ratio --> outEq[K equals c plus mu b divided by c minus mu b]
```

## 4.3 Brake Configuration Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cfgBase[Drum Brake Configurations] --> cfgLT[Leading Trailing LT]
    cfgBase --> cfgTLS[Twin Leading Shoe TLS]
    cfgBase --> cfgDS[Duo Servo DS]
    cfgLT --> ltProp1[One Leading One Trailing Shoe]
    cfgLT --> ltProp2[Moderate Stable Braking]
    cfgLT --> ltProp3[Used in Passenger Cars]
    cfgTLS --> tlsProp1[Both Shoes are Leading in their Active Direction]
    cfgTLS --> tlsProp2[Maximum Braking Torque]
    cfgTLS --> tlsProp3[Used in Heavy Commercial Vehicles]
    cfgDS --> dsProp1[Adjusting Link Transfers Force Between Shoes]
    cfgDS --> dsProp2[Servo Effect Amplifies Trailing Shoe]
    cfgDS --> dsProp3[Direction Sensitive]
```
