**4. Tire Bead Seat Interface:**
The tire bead seats against the rim with an airtight seal (in tubeless designs) via an elastomeric **hump** on the rim.

**Sketch (Textual Representation):**

```
              ┌────────────── Rim Flange ──────────────┐
              │   ╲                              ╱     │
              │    ╲────── Bead Seat (5°) ──────╱      │
              │                                        │
              │   ←─── Drop Center / Well ───→         │
              │                                        │
              │    ╱────── Bead Seat (5°) ──────╲      │
              │   ╱                              ╲     │
              └────────────── Rim Flange ──────────────┘
                          │          │
              ┌───────────┘          └───────────┐
              │   Disc / Web (solid/spoke)        │
              │   Ventilation holes (if any)      │
              └────────────┬──────────┬────────────┘
                           │   Hub    │
                           │ [PCD Holes]
                           │   [Pilot Bore]
                           │ [Bearing Seat]
                           └────────────
```

#### Part (b) — Wheel Materials and Forged vs Cast Aluminum (7 Marks)

**Common Wheel Materials:**
1. **Steel** — Stamped and welded; used in mass-market cars (Maruti, Tata).
2. **Cast Aluminum Alloy (A356)** — Low-pressure die-cast; used in mid-segment cars.
3. **Forged Aluminum (6061-T6)** — Hot-forged and CNC-machined; used in performance cars.
4. **Magnesium Alloy (AZ91)** — Lightest; used in motorsport.
5. **Carbon Fiber Composite** — Used in F1 and hypercars.

**Two Advantages of Forged Aluminum over Cast Aluminum:**
1. **Higher Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** Forging aligns the grain flow in the direction of load, providing ~30% higher tensile strength than cast aluminum for the same mass. This allows a thinner, lighter disc.
2. **Better Impact Resistance and Fatigue Life:** Forged wheels are ductile and absorb impact by plastic deformation, whereas cast wheels are brittle and can crack catastrophically upon impact with potholes or kerbs.
3. **Better Dimensional Accuracy:** CNC machining of forged blanks provides tighter tolerances for PCD and offset dimensions.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Explain the four primary functions of an automobile wheel with relevant equations. A passenger car of mass 1800 kg has a wheelbase of 2.6 m and a center of gravity height of 0.6 m. Compute the front and rear axle loads under a deceleration of 0.7g. Assume equal static load distribution."**

#### Part (a) — Four Functions of a Wheel (7 Marks)

A wheel in an automobile performs four critical functions:

**1. Vertical Load Support:** The wheel supports the vehicle's static weight and dynamic inertial loads. The static load per wheel on a level surface:

$$F_{z,\text{static}} = \frac{m \cdot g}{4}$$

**2. Torque Transmission (Driving and Braking):** The wheel receives engine torque (driving) and brake torque (decelerating) and converts them to longitudinal tractive force at the contact patch:

$$F_{x} = \frac{T_{\text{wheel}}}{r_{\text{dyn}}}, \quad F_{x,\text{max}} = \mu \cdot F_{z}$$

**3. Steering and Cornering Force Generation:** When steered, the wheel develops a slip angle $\alpha$ and generates a lateral force:

$$F_{y} = C_{\alpha} \cdot \alpha$$

**4. Suspension Compliance:** The tire's vertical spring rate (180–280 kN/m) acts in series with the suspension spring, providing secondary ride isolation.

#### Part (b) — Load Transfer Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 1800$ kg
- $L = 2.6$ m
- $h_{cg} = 0.6$ m
- $a_x = 0.7g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Static distribution: 50:50 front and rear

**Step 1: Static Axle Loads**

$$W_{\text{front,static}} = W_{\text{rear,static}} = \frac{m \cdot g}{2} = \frac{1800 \times 9.81}{2} = 8829 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal Load Transfer**

$$\Delta F_{z,\text{long}} = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h_{cg}}{L} = \frac{1800 \times 6.867 \times 0.6}{2.6}$$

$$\Delta F_{z,\text{long}} = \frac{7416.36}{2.6} = 2852.45 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Dynamic Axle Loads Under Braking**

$$F_{z,\text{front,braking}} = 8829 + 2852.45 = 11681.45 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{z,\text{rear,braking}} = 8829 - 2852.45 = 5976.55 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Per-Wheel Loads**

$$F_{z,\text{front,per wheel}} = \frac{11681.45}{2} = 5840.73 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{z,\text{rear,per wheel}} = \frac{5976.55}{2} = 2988.28 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation**
The front axle is now carrying **~32%** more load than its static share, while the rear axle is reduced by **~32%**. This shift in vertical load directly impacts brake sizing — front brakes must be larger to handle the increased energy and tractive demand.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve for per-wheel values:** Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if you only give the per-axle value when the question says "per wheel."
> 2. **Using 50:50 static assumption when the question specifies a different distribution.** Always re-read carefully.
> 3. **Mixing up $L$ (wheelbase) and $T$ (track width)** in the load transfer formula.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of $g = 9.81$ m/s²** explicitly. Examiners expect this in the "Given" section.
> 5. **Sign error in load transfer:** Forward deceleration transfers load *to* the front, not away from it. Direction of inertia force must be clear in free-body diagrams.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- ✅ A wheel has **two main parts**: the **rim** (outer metal ring) and the **tire** (elastomeric cover). Some classifications also separate the **hub** and **disc/web**.
- ✅ The **four functions** are: Load Support, Torque Transmission, Steering, Suspension Compliance.
- ✅ **Rim cross-sections** to know: DC, SDC, FH, EH — modern tubeless passenger cars use **FH or EH**.
- ✅ **Bead seat angle** is universally **5°** for passenger car rims.
- ✅ **Static load per wheel (4-wheeler):** $F_z = mg/4$. **Per axle:** $F_z = mg/2$.
- ✅ **Longitudinal load transfer formula:** $\Delta F_z = m a_x h_{cg} / L$
- ✅ **Lateral load transfer formula:** $\Delta F_z = m a_y h_{cg} / T$
- ✅ **Maximum tractive force:** $F_{x,\text{max}} = \mu F_z$ — this sets the limit for both acceleration and braking.
- ✅ **Cornering force in linear region:** $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ — valid for $\alpha \approx 4°$ to $6°$ only.
- ✅ **Standard PCD values:** 100, 101.6, 108, 112, 114.3, 120 mm.
- ✅ **Standard rim diameters:** 13″ through 20″ (increments of 1″).
- ✅ **Tire pressure (passenger):** 0.1 to 0.3 MPa.
- ✅ **Unsprung mass rule:** 1 kg of unsprung mass = 6–8 kg of sprung mass (ride/handling equivalent).
- ✅ **Forged aluminum** is preferred over **cast aluminum** for: higher strength, better ductility, lower weight.
- ✅ **Slip angle** = $\tan^{-1}(v_y / v_x) \approx v_y / v_x$ (small-angle approximation).
- ✅ **Wheel rotational inertia (solid disc):** $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$.
- ✅ **Wheel angular velocity:** $\omega = v / r_{\text{dyn}}$.
- ✅ **Drop center** in the rim exists to allow tire mounting/demounting.
- ✅ **Tubeless tires** rely on the airtight seal between the tire bead and the rim bead seat (often with an additional HUMP on the rim).
- ✅ **Wheel offset (ET)** is Positive, Zero, or Negative — affects steering king-pin geometry and bearing load.
- ✅ Under **braking**: load transfers **forward** (to front wheels). Under **acceleration**: load transfers **rearward** (to rear wheels).
- ✅ Under **left turn**: load transfers **rightward** (to right wheels).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Classification" in Automotive Chassis?

In the context of **PCAUT303 - Automotive Chassis (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Classification"** refers to the systematic grouping of various chassis sub-systems (steering, suspension, brakes, axles, wheels & tyres) based on their **constructional features, operating principles, energy source, geometric arrangement, and the type of motion they permit or restrict**.

Classification is not a mere listing — it is an **engineering taxonomy** that helps a designer, service engineer, or examiner understand the **functional hierarchy** and the **trade-offs** (cost, weight, complexity, NVH, safety) between different sub-systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Vocabulary)**
> *Classification is the orderly arrangement of automotive chassis components into distinct categories based on their functional role, energy conversion mechanism, structural layout, and the relative motion they permit between the vehicle body and the road surface.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body"

Think of an automobile chassis the way a doctor thinks about a human body:

| Human Body | Automobile Equivalent | Classification Done By |
|---|---|---|
| Skeleton | Frame / Chassis | Type of frame (ladder, monocoque, space) |
| Muscles | Suspension system | Independent / Rigid / Semi-rigid |
| Hands turning the wheel | Steering system | Manual / Power / Steer-by-wire |
| Foot on the brake pedal | Braking system | Mechanical / Hydraulic / Electric |
| Foot bones | Axles | Live / Dead / Stub |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember Module-4 sub-systems (used by KTU toppers):** **"S-S-B-A-W"** → **S**teering, **S**uspension, **B**rakes, **A**xles, **W**heels & Tyres. Every component in Module-4 falls under one of these five pillars, and the *classification* of each pillar forms the foundation of this module.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters (Engineering Perspective)

1. **Design Selection** – A SUV needs a *non-independent rigid axle* for off-road durability; a sports car needs an *independent double-wishbone* for precise handling. Classification guides this choice.
2. **Service & Diagnostics** – A workshop technician identifies the *type* of brake (disc vs. drum) before ordering spare parts.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** – AIS / ECE / FMVSS standards apply differently to *power-assisted* vs. *manual* steering.
4. **Academic Rigor** – KTU board questions on "Classify…" are worth **3 to 14 marks** and test whether the student understands the **principle of grouping**, not just rote listing.

---

## 1.4 Master Classification Tree (Module-4)

The overarching classification of the **automotive chassis sub-systems** covered in Module 4 is presented below in a single, exam-ready tree:

```text
AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS SUB-SYSTEMS (Module-4 PCAUT303)
│
├── 1. STEERING SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Energy Source → Manual | Power-Assisted (Hydraulic / Electric / Electro-Hydraulic)
│     ├── (b) By Geometry     → Ackermann | Davis | Mar-Ack (Anti-Ackermann)
│     ├── (c) By Construction → Rack & Pinion | Recirculating Ball | Worm & Sector | Cam & Lever
│     ├── (d) By Axle Position → Front | Rear | Four-Wheel
│     └── (e) By Drive Type   → Mechanical | Hydraulic | Electric | Steer-by-Wire
│
├── 2. SUSPENSION SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Structural Linkage → Independent | Non-Independent (Rigid) | Semi-Independent
│     ├── (b) By Spring Medium     → Coil | Leaf | Torsion Bar | Air | Rubber | Hydraulic
│     ├── (c) By Damping Element   → Hydraulic | Gas-Hydraulic | Magneto-Rheological | Friction
│     └── (d) By Active Control    → Passive | Semi-Active | Active | Adaptive
│
├── 3. BRAKING SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Energy Source   → Mechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric | Hybrid (Regen)
│     ├── (b) By Construction    → Drum (Internal Expanding Shoe) | Disc (Caliper-Actuated Pad)
│     ├── (c) By Application    → Service | Secondary | Parking | Emergency / Hill-Hold
│     └── (d) By Control Logic  → Single-Circuit | Dual-Circuit (X / H / LL / TT split)
│
├── 4. AXLES
│     ├── (a) By Function         → Front (Steering) | Rear (Drive) | Stub
│     ├── (b) By Load Transfer    → Live (Full-Floating) | Three-Quarter Floating | Semi-Floating
│     └── (c) By Drive           → Driven | Non-Driven (Dead)
│
└── 5. WHEELS & TYRES
      ├── (a) By Wheel Type      → Disc (Pressed Steel / Cast Alloy) | Wire-Spoke | Forged
      ├── (b) By Tyre Construction → Diagonal (Bias) | Radial | Belted-Bias | Tubeless | Tubed
      └── (c) By Tread Pattern   → Rib | Lug | Block | Rib-Lug | Directional | Asymmetric
```

This tree is the **single most important diagram** for Module-4 classification questions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question says *"Classify the steering system with neat sketches"*, examiners award **2 marks** for the correct **principle of classification** (e.g., "by construction") and **2 marks** each for explaining the **sub-types** with diagrams. A 14-mark answer must cover at least **3 principles** (energy, geometry, construction) with labelled sketches.

---

## 1.5 Symbol Conventions Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Sprung mass per wheel | kg |
| $k$ | Spring stiffness | N/m |
| $c$ | Damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | rad/s |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| $F_s$ | Steering effort | N |
| $r$ | Steering-arm effective radius | m |
| $P_b$ | Brake-line hydraulic pressure | Pa |
| $\mu$ | Tyre–road friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| $T_b$ | Brake torque | N·m |

These symbols will be referenced in the formula sheet of Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section deconstructs each classification branch, then provides the **cheat-sheet table** that KTU examiners expect students to memorise.

---

## 2.1 Classification of Steering System

### 2.1.1 By Construction (Mechanical Gear Type)

The mechanical gearbox multiplies and redirects the driver's torque from the steering column to the pitman arm.

| Sub-Type | Key Feature | Typical Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Rack & Pinion** | Linear toothed rack engaged by a circular pinion; direct conversion of rotary → linear motion. | Hatchbacks, sedans, most modern cars. |
| **Recirculating Ball** | Ball-bearing nut runs on a worm; converts rotary → rotary through a sector shaft; large gear reduction. | Heavy trucks, commercial vehicles, older SUVs. |
| **Worm & Sector** | Simple worm meshes with a toothed sector on the pitman shaft. | Light commercial vehicles, vintage cars. |
| **Cam & Lever (Ross)** | Cam-shaped worm with lever-type follower; compact. | Older passenger cars (now obsolete). |

### 2.1.2 By Energy Source

```text
Power-Assisted Steering
├── Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS) – engine-driven pump, rotary valve
├── Electro-Hydraulic (EHPS) – electric motor drives hydraulic pump (BMW, Citroën)
├── Electric Power Steering (EPS) – electric motor directly on column/rack
│     ├── Column-Assist EPS (C-EPS)
│     ├── Pinion-Assist EPS (P-EPS)
│     └── Rack-Assist EPS (R-EPS)
└── Steer-by-Wire (SBW) – no mechanical link, X-by-wire technology
```

### 2.1.3 By Steering Geometry (Ackermann vs. Davis vs. Mar-Ack)

The **Ackermann condition** ensures that during a turn, all wheels rotate about a **single instantaneous centre (IC)** to avoid tyre scrub.

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$$

where:
- $\delta_o$ = steering angle of the outer wheel (rad)
- $\delta_i$ = steering angle of the inner wheel (rad)
- $w$ = wheelbase — **wait, $w$ is track-width** (m)
- $L$ = wheelbase (m)

> **Note the corrected symbols:** $w$ (track-width) is used in the Ackermann equation, not the wheelbase. This is a classic KTU trap.

**Davis steering** uses a *trapezoidal* linkage giving slightly different angles, and **Mar-Ackermann** (Anti-Ackermann) is used in **racing** where the outer wheel needs *more* angle than Ackermann to maintain high lateral grip.

### 2.1.4 By Axle Position

| Position | Application |
|---|---|
| Front-axle steering | Universal — almost all road vehicles |
| Rear-axle steering | Active rear-steer (BMW, Porsche, long-wheelbase trucks) |
| Four-wheel steering (4WS) | Crane trucks, heavy off-roaders, luxury sedans |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Suspension System

### 2.2.1 By Structural Linkage

1. **Independent Suspension** – motion of one wheel does NOT affect the other on the same axle.
   - Double-Wishbone (Short-Long Arm, SLA)
   - MacPherson Strut
   - Multi-Link
   - Semi-Trailing Arm
   - Swing-Axle (rare)

2. **Non-Independent (Rigid / Live Axle)** – both wheels are connected by a single beam.
   - Hotchkiss Drive Axle
   - Torque-Tube Drive Axle

3. **Semi-Independent** – connected by a torsion beam.
   - Twist-Beam Rear Axle (VW, Skoda)

### 2.2.2 By Spring Medium

| Spring Type | Energy Storage Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Coil spring | Helical wound wire, $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | Front suspension (most cars) |
| Leaf spring | Cantilever beam in bending | Rear of commercial vehicles, SUVs |
| Torsion bar | Twist of a bar, $\theta = \dfrac{32TL}{\pi d^4 G}$ | Older cars, some off-roaders |
| Air spring | Compressed air in bellows, $pV = nRT$ | Heavy CVs, luxury cars (Airmatic) |
| Rubber spring | Compressive elasticity | Secondary springs, engine mounts |

### 2.2.3 By Active Control (Increasingly Important for KTU 2024)

| Class | Energy Input | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Passive | None | Conventional dampers |
| Semi-Active | Controlled (skyhook logic) | Continuous Damping Control (CDC), Bilstein |
| Active | Continuous external force | Active Body Control (ABC), Mercedes |
| Adaptive | Mode-switching (Comfort / Sport) | Audi Drive Select, MagneRide (MR) |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Braking System

### 2.3.1 By Construction

| Brake Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drum brake | Internal expanding shoes press against a cylindrical drum | Low cost, good parking brake integration | Heat fade, complex self-energising geometry |
| Disc brake | Caliper squeezes pads against a rotating disc | Excellent heat dissipation, self-cleaning, linear response | Higher cost, prone to squeal |

### 2.3.2 By Energy Source

- **Mechanical** (cable/rod, mostly for parking brakes)
- **Hydraulic** (master cylinder → wheel cylinder — universal for service brakes)
- **Pneumatic** (air brakes, heavy CVs, buses)
- **Electric** (regenerative EV braking)
- **Electromechanical** (brake-by-wire, e.g., Bosch iBooster)

### 2.3.3 By Circuit Configuration (Critical for Safety)

```text
Dual-Circuit Brake Configurations
├── H-Split  – front and rear circuits separated (most common passenger car)
├── X-Split  – diagonally opposite wheels (Audi, VW) – better balance under heavy braking
├── LL-Split – left front+rear vs. right front+rear (rare, asymmetric)
└── TT-Split – front pair vs. rear pair (some heavy CVs)
```

---

## 2.4 Classification of Axles

### 2.4.1 By Load-Carrying Capacity (Floating Methods)

| Type | Bearing Location | Max Load | Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Floating** | Hub bearings carry only weight; axle shaft transmits torque only | Very high | Trucks, buses |
| **Three-Quarter Floating** | Hub bearing + axle shaft partially share load | High | Medium trucks |
| **Semi-Floating** | Axle shaft carries both torque and the wheel load | Moderate | Passenger cars |
| **Stub axle** | Kingpin-carried, used in front steering | Low | Almost all light vehicles |

---

## 2.5 Classification of Wheels and Tyres

### 2.5.1 Tyre Construction

| Type | Ply Orientation | Heat Dissipation | Rolling Resistance | Today |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diagonal (Bias) | 30–40° alternating | Low | High | Obsolete (2/3-wheelers) |
| Radial | 90° to centreline | High | Low | Universal |
| Belted-bias | Bias + radial belts | Medium | Medium | Niche |
| Tubeless | Inner liner of halobutyl | Excellent | Low | Universal in cars |

### 2.5.2 Tread Patterns

- **Rib** – circumferential grooves, used on steering axle of trucks.
- **Lug** – large blocks, traction (drive axle).
- **Block** – symmetric blocks, off-road (M/T tyres).
- **Rib-Lug** – combination, all-position.
- **Directional** – V-pattern, high-speed wet grip.
- **Asymmetric** – different inboard/outboard tread, premium UHP tyres.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is exam gold.** Memorise the equations, the symbols, and the units. KTU questions on this topic frequently ask *"Derive the condition for Ackermann steering"* or *"Show that the natural frequency of a sprung mass is…".*

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$ | $\delta_o, \delta_i$ in rad; $w$ = track-width (m); $L$ = wheelbase (m) | Ackermann steering |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\delta_i}{\delta_o} = \dfrac{L + w \tan \delta_o}{L - w \tan \delta_o}$ | Same as above | Davis steering approximation |
| 3 | $T_s = F_s \cdot r = \dfrac{P_b \cdot A_b \cdot \eta_b \cdot r}{i_s}$ | $T_s$ steering torque (N·m); $F_s$ effort (N); $r$ arm (m); $P_b$ pressure (Pa); $A_b$ piston area (m²); $\eta_b$ efficiency; $i_s$ gear ratio | Power steering |
| 4 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\omega_n$ rad/s; $k$ N/m; $m$ kg | Suspension natural frequency |
| 5 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{km}}$ | $\zeta$ dimensionless; $c$ N·s/m | Damping ratio |
| 6 | $T_b = 2 \mu F_n r_{eff}$ | $T_b$ brake torque (N·m); $\mu$ friction coeff.; $F_n$ normal force (N); $r_{eff}$ effective radius (m) | Drum & disc brake |
| 7 | $F_b = P_b \cdot A_w$ | $F_b$ brake clamp force (N); $P_b$ line pressure (Pa); $A_w$ wheel-cylinder piston area (m²) | Hydraulic brake |
| 8 | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ | $a_{max}$ m/s²; $\mu$ friction coeff.; $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Maximum deceleration |
| 9 | $P_{air} V_{res} = \text{const}$ (isothermal) | $P_{air}$ Pa; $V_{res}$ m³ | Air-spring |
| 10 | $M_f = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot h_{cg}$ | $M_f$ friction-induced moment (N·m); $h_{cg}$ CoG height (m) | Brake bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Pitfall:** The Ackermann equation uses **track-width $w$**, not wheelbase $L$. Students frequently swap them. Always check the diagram: $w$ is the *horizontal distance between the two king-pins*; $L$ is the *longitudinal distance between front and rear axles*.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Classification

1. **Vehicle Homologation (AIS-008, AIS-009)** – Indian regulations group vehicles by *brake-circuit type* and *steering-energy source*. The classification tree decides which tests apply.
2. **Servo-Mechanism Design** – Power-steering control algorithms depend on whether the assist is *hydraulic* (rotary-valve model) or *electric* (motor-current model).
3. **Crash Safety (NCAP)** – Disc vs. drum classification drives fade-resistance and pedal-feel decisions.
4. **NVH Tuning** – Independent vs. non-independent suspension classification changes unsprung-mass and wheel-hop behaviour.
5. **EV Transition** – *Regenerative* (electric) braking classification is the new frontier; understanding it is critical for KTU 2024 syllabi.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **heart of the 14-mark KTU questions**. Three cornerstone derivations are worked out in full, with **every algebraic step shown**. No shortcuts, no skips.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Ackermann Steering Condition

**Goal:** To prove that for all four wheels to rotate about a single instantaneous centre without tyre scrub,

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$$

### 3.1.1 Setup (Refer to the diagram in Section 4)

Consider a vehicle turning to the left at low speed. Let:

- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- $w$ = track-width (distance between the two king-pins / steering pivots)
- $R$ = radius of the turn measured to the midpoint of the rear axle
- $R_o$ = radius for the outer (right) wheel
- $R_i$ = radius for the inner (left) wheel
- $\delta_o$ = steering angle of the outer wheel
- $\delta_i$ = steering angle of the inner wheel

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** From the geometry, the inner wheel is closer to the turn centre. So:

$$R_i = R - \dfrac{w}{2}, \qquad R_o = R + \dfrac{w}{2}$$

**Step 2:** For the vehicle to take a pure-rolling turn, each wheel's velocity vector must be perpendicular to the line joining that wheel to the turn centre. Hence:

$$\tan \delta_i = \dfrac{L}{R_i}, \qquad \tan \delta_o = \dfrac{L}{R_o}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the difference of the cotangents. Recall that $\cot x = \dfrac{1}{\tan x}$. So:

$$\cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_i}{L}, \qquad \cot \delta_o = \dfrac{R_o}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $R_o = R + \tfrac{w}{2}$ and $R_i = R - \tfrac{w}{2}$:

$$R_o - R_i = \left(R + \dfrac{w}{2}\right) - \left(R - \dfrac{w}{2}\right) = w$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the **Ackermann condition**.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Illustration

A car has wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and track-width $w = 1.5$ m. If the outer wheel is steered by $\delta_o = 20°$, find $\delta_i$.

$$\cot \delta_i = \cot 20° - \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 2.7475 - 0.6 = 2.1475$$

$$\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(2.1475) = 24.97° \approx 25°$$

**Interpretation:** The inner wheel must steer *more* than the outer wheel — counter-intuitive but geometrically necessary.

### 3.1.4 KTU Mark-Split for This Derivation

| Step | Marks Awarded |
|---|---|
| Free-body / geometry diagram | 2 |
| Step 1: defining $R_i, R_o$ | 1 |
| Step 2: tangents of steering angles | 1 |
| Step 3: cotangent conversion | 1 |
| Step 4: subtraction | 1 |
| Step 5: substitution | 1 |
| Step 6: final equation | 1 |
| Numerical example | 2 |
| **Total** | **10** (part of 14) |

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Natural Frequency of Sprung Mass on Suspension

**Goal:** Derive $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ and the damped response.

### 3.2.1 Mathematical Model

A single wheel with **sprung mass** $m$ is supported by a spring (stiffness $k$) and a damper (coefficient $c$). The road input is $x(t)$ and the sprung-mass displacement is $y(t)$. The relative displacement is $z = y - x$.

### 3.2.2 Equation of Motion

Newton's second law on the sprung mass:

$$m \ddot{y} + c(\dot{y} - \dot{x}) + k(y - x) = 0$$

Define $z = y - x$. Then $\ddot{y} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{x}$ and $\dot{y} = \dot{z} + \dot{x}$. Substituting:

$$m(\ddot{z} + \ddot{x}) + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{x}$$

### 3.2.3 Free Vibration Case

For free vibration, set $\ddot{x} = 0$:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

### 3.2.4 Standard-Form Conversion

Divide throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{z} + \dfrac{c}{m} \dot{z} + \dfrac{k}{m} z = 0$$

Compare with the standard second-order form:

$$\ddot{z} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = 0$$

Equating coefficients:

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \dfrac{c}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{km}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2.5 Three Damping Cases

| Case | Condition | Response |
|---|---|---|
| Under-damped | $\zeta < 1$ | Decaying sinusoid: $z(t) = A e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$, where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |
| Over-damped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return |

### 3.2.6 Numerical Illustration

For a passenger car: $m = 350$ kg per corner, $k = 25\,000$ N/m.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{25\,000}{350}} = \sqrt{71.43} = 8.45 \text{ rad/s} = \dfrac{8.45}{2\pi} \approx 1.345 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the **typical 1.0–1.5 Hz ride frequency** that luxury car manufacturers target. Above 1.5 Hz the human body perceives vibration; below 1.0 Hz causes motion sickness.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Braking Force and Maximum Deceleration

**Goal:** Show that the maximum theoretical deceleration is $a_{max} = \mu g$.

### 3.3.1 Free-Body Diagram

For a vehicle of total weight $W = mg$ on a flat road with friction coefficient $\mu$ between tyre and road, the maximum braking force is:

$$F_{b,max} = \mu W = \mu m g$$

### 3.3.2 Application of Newton's Second Law

$$F_{b,max} = m a_{max}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\mu m g = m a_{max}$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$a_{max} = \mu g \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Illustration

For dry asphalt, $\mu \approx 0.9$:

$$a_{max} = 0.9 \times 9.81 = 8.83 \text{ m/s}^2 = 0.9 g$$

The stopping distance from $v_0$ is:

$$s = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

For $v_0 = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s:

$$s = \dfrac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 0.9 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{771.6}{17.66} = 43.7 \text{ m}$$

This is the **standard ABS-equipped braking distance** quoted in owner manuals.

### 3.3.4 KTU Board Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Favourite Sub-Question:** *"A vehicle of mass 1500 kg is braked from 90 km/h to rest over 45 m. Calculate the average coefficient of friction."*
> Solution: $v_0^2 = 25^2 = 625$ m²/s², $2 a s = v_0^2 \Rightarrow a = 625 / (2 \times 45) = 6.94$ m/s², hence $\mu = a/g = 6.94 / 9.81 = 0.71$ — typical wet-road value.

---

## 3.4 Master Analytical Matrix (Quick-Reference)

| Sub-Topic | Governing Equation | Limiting Case | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ackermann steering | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ | At very small $\delta_o$, $\delta_i \approx \delta_o + w/L$ (linear) | 7 marks |
| Suspension natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Frequency of human body 4–8 Hz, so $k/m$ tuned to 1.3 Hz | 7 marks |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | $\zeta = 0.25$–$0.4$ typical for ride comfort | 3 marks |
| Brake torque | $T_b = 2 \mu F_n r_{eff}$ | Self-energising shoes: $\mu \to 2\mu$ | 5 marks |
| Max deceleration | $a_{max} = \mu g$ | ABS threshold just below $\mu$ peak | 3 marks |
| Spring energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | At $\zeta = 1$, $E_{dis}$ per cycle is maximum | 2 marks |

```mermaid
    shoeR[SECONDARY SHOE]

    shoeR --> drum
    shoeL -.-> pivot1
    shoeR -.-> pivot1
    act --> shoeL
    act --> shoeR
```

This block diagram represents the **duo-servo drum brake** where the secondary shoe *self-energises* because of friction-induced rotation of the drum, increasing its effective $\mu$ by a factor of $1 + f \tan\alpha$ where $f$ is the friction coefficient and $\alpha$ is the shoe-rotation half-angle.
