# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is structured exactly like a **KTU University End-Semester Examination paper** for Module-4 of PCAUT303. Solve the questions, then compare with the model answers.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify the steering system based on the energy source used. Mention at least four sub-types.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The steering system can be classified based on the **energy source** as follows:

1. **Manual Steering** — Pure mechanical, driver provides all the torque. Used in light commercial vehicles.
2. **Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS)** — Engine-driven pump supplies pressurised fluid to a rotary valve. Common in older SUVs and trucks.
3. **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** — An electric motor assists the column, pinion, or rack directly. Used in most modern passenger cars (Maruti, Hyundai).
4. **Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering (EHPS)** — Electric motor drives the hydraulic pump. Used in some heavy vehicles (BMW, Citroën).
5. **Steer-by-Wire (SBW)** — No mechanical link; electronic control of actuators. Used in concept and premium vehicles (Infiniti Q50).

*(1 mark for naming the four sub-types, 2 marks for one-line description of each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the difference between Ackermann and Anti-Ackermann steering? When is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ackermann | Anti-Ackermann (Mar-Ack) |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner wheel angle vs. outer** | $\delta_i > \delta_o$ (per formula) | $\delta_i < \delta_o$ |
| **Geometric condition** | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ | $\delta_o > \delta_i$ (opposite inequality) |
| **Application** | Standard road vehicles, low-speed turning | High-performance racing, where the outer tyre must develop more slip angle for high lateral grip |
| **Tyre wear** | Uniform | Non-uniform (acceptable in racing) |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for application.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### CHOICE QUESTION A (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **Ackermann steering geometry condition**. Show that for a small steering angle the inner wheel deflects more than the outer wheel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A passenger car has the following data:
- Wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m
- Track-width $w = 1.55$ m
- Outer wheel steering angle $\delta_o = 18°$

Calculate: (i) the inner wheel steering angle $\delta_i$ for **pure rolling** (Ackermann condition), and (ii) the **turning radius** of the vehicle. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Below is the marking-scheme-aligned version:*

1. **Diagram** (2 marks): Draw the vehicle plan-view showing rear axle, front axle, king-pins, instantaneous centre $IC$ on the rear-axle side, lines from $IC$ to the four wheel-centres, and the steering angles $\delta_i, \delta_o$.

2. **Geometry setup** (2 marks): State $R_i = R - w/2$, $R_o = R + w/2$, and $\tan \delta_i = L/R_i$, $\tan \delta_o = L/R_o$.

3. **Derivation** (3 marks): 
   $$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{L} = \dfrac{w}{L}$$
4. **Small-angle inference** (1 mark): For small $\delta_o$, $\cot \delta_o \approx 1/\delta_o$ (rad). Differentiating the Ackermann equation gives $d\delta_i/d\delta_o > 1$, hence $\delta_i > \delta_o$.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Inner wheel steering angle:**

Using the Ackermann condition:

$$\cot \delta_i = \cot \delta_o - \dfrac{w}{L}$$

$$\cot 18° = \cot 18° = 3.0777$$

$$\cot \delta_i = 3.0777 - \dfrac{1.55}{2.6} = 3.0777 - 0.5962 = 2.4815$$

$$\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(2.4815) = 21.96° \approx 22°$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\delta_i \approx 22°}$

**(ii) Turning radius:**

From $\tan \delta_o = L/R_o$:

$$R_o = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta_o} = \dfrac{2.6}{\tan 18°} = \dfrac{2.6}{0.3249} = 8.00 \text{ m}$$

The radius to the **midpoint of the rear axle** is:

$$R = R_o - \dfrac{w}{2} = 8.00 - 0.775 = 7.225 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{R \approx 7.23 \text{ m}}$

**Verification:** $R_i = 7.225 - 0.775 = 6.45$ m, $L/R_i = 2.6/6.45 = 0.403$, $\tan^{-1}(0.403) = 21.96°$ — matches. ✓

*(1 mark: writing formula, 2 marks: numerical substitution, 1 mark: correct arithmetic, 1 mark: verification, 2 marks: turning-radius calculation.)*

---

### CHOICE QUESTION B (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify the **braking system** based on (i) construction, (ii) energy source, and (iii) application. With a neat sketch explain the working of a **hydraulic drum brake** and show the expression for the **self-energising factor**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The hydraulic brake system of a 1500 kg car uses a master cylinder of 25 mm diameter and wheel cylinders of 32 mm diameter. The driver applies a pedal force of 200 N with a pedal-lever ratio of 5:1. The overall brake efficiency is 80 %. Calculate:
   (i) The hydraulic line pressure. *(3 marks)*
   (ii) The clamping force on each brake pad. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) The maximum theoretical deceleration if the road friction coefficient is 0.7. *(2 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Principle | Sub-Types |
|---|---|
| (i) Construction | Drum brake (internal expanding shoe) – Disc brake (caliper-actuated pad) |
| (ii) Energy Source | Mechanical – Hydraulic – Pneumatic – Electric (regenerative) – Electromechanical |
| (iii) Application | Service brake – Secondary brake – Parking brake – Emergency / Hill-hold brake |

**Hydraulic Drum Brake Working (3 marks):**

When the brake pedal is pressed, the master cylinder converts mechanical pedal force into hydraulic pressure. This pressure is transmitted equally through the brake-fluid to all wheel cylinders. The wheel cylinder pushes the brake shoes outward, which press the friction lining against the inner surface of the rotating drum. The friction force generates a braking torque opposing drum rotation, decelerating the vehicle.

**Self-Energising Factor (1 mark):**

For a duo-servo drum brake with shoe-rotation half-angle $\alpha$ and lining friction $\mu$:

$$C_{self} = \dfrac{1 + \mu \tan \alpha}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha}$$

When $\mu \tan \alpha \to 1$, $C_{self} \to \infty$ (servo runaway). Hence, lining friction is limited to $\mu \leq 0.4$ for stable operation.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** 
- Pedal force $F_p = 200$ N
- Pedal ratio $i_p = 5:1$
- Master cylinder diameter $d_m = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Wheel cylinder diameter $d_w = 32$ mm = 0.032 m
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.8$
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- $\mu = 0.7$

**(i) Hydraulic line pressure (3 marks):**

Force on master cylinder:
$$F_m = F_p \times i_p \times \eta = 200 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 800 \text{ N}$$

Master cylinder area:
$$A_m = \dfrac{\pi d_m^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Line pressure:
$$P_b = \dfrac{F_m}{A_m} = \dfrac{800}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.63 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.63 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_b \approx 1.63 \text{ MPa}}$ (typical 1.5–2.0 MPa is normal for light vehicles.)

**(ii) Clamping force per pad (2 marks):**

Wheel cylinder area:
$$A_w = \dfrac{\pi d_w^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.032)^2}{4} = 8.042 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Clamping force:
$$F_b = P_b \times A_w = 1.63 \times 10^6 \times 8.042 \times 10^{-4} = 1311 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{F_b \approx 1311 \text{ N per pad}}$

**(iii) Maximum deceleration (2 marks):**

$$a_{max} = \mu g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.7g$$

**Result:** $\boxed{a_{max} \approx 6.87 \text{ m/s}^2}$

**Brake force check:** Total brake force = 4 × 1311 = 5244 N, deceleration = 5244/1500 = 3.5 m/s² < $\mu g$ = 6.87 m/s². So brakes are *not* lock-limited; the driver is *pedal-limited*. Increasing pedal force would yield higher deceleration up to 0.7g.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Ackermann symbol confusion** (–2 marks): Students mix up $w$ (track-width) and $L$ (wheelbase). Always draw the diagram first and label the symbols.
> 2. **Units of angles** (–1 mark): Always convert degrees → radians when using the *small-angle* approximation ($\tan \delta \approx \delta$, $\cot \delta \approx 1/\delta$). KTU evaluators deduct if angles remain in degrees within a $\tan$ or $\cot$ expression.
> 3. **Diameter vs. radius in cylinder areas** (–2 marks): The piston-area formula is $A = \pi d^2/4$, **not** $\pi r^2/2$ (same numerically, but the formula written incorrectly is treated as an error in strict marking).
> 4. **Self-energising factor formula** (–1 mark): Some students write $1 + \mu \tan\alpha$ without the denominator; the duo-servo has *both* numerator and denominator.
> 5. **Rounding** (–0.5 mark per question): Always quote answers to **3 significant figures**; do not round intermediate steps.

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall** — it condenses the entire Module-4 classification topic.

### A. Steering System
- ☐ Classify by **construction**: Rack-Pinion / Recirculating-Ball / Worm-Sector / Cam-Lever.
- ☐ Classify by **energy**: Manual / Hydraulic / Electric / Electro-Hydraulic / Steer-by-Wire.
- ☐ Classify by **geometry**: Ackermann / Davis / Mar-Ackermann (anti-Ackermann).
- ☐ **Ackermann formula:** $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ (remember: $w$ is track-width, $L$ is wheelbase).
- ☐ **Inner wheel always turns more** than outer wheel under Ackermann.
- ☐ **Steering ratio** $i_s$ = steering-wheel turns / road-wheel turns.

### B. Suspension System
- ☐ **Independent** vs. **Non-Independent** vs. **Semi-Independent** (twist-beam).
- ☐ Independent types: Double-Wishbone, MacPherson Strut, Multi-Link, Trailing Arm.
- ☐ Spring mediums: Coil, Leaf, Torsion Bar, Air, Rubber.
- ☐ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; passenger cars tuned to 1.0–1.5 Hz.
- ☐ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$; ride comfort target $\zeta = 0.25$–$0.4$.
- ☐ **Active / Semi-Active / Adaptive** suspension classification.

### C. Braking System
- ☐ Drum vs. Disc construction.
- ☐ Energy sources: Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric, Electromechanical.
- ☐ **Brake circuits:** Single, H-Split, X-Split, LL-Split, TT-Split.
- ☐ **Master cylinder force → line pressure** → **wheel cylinder force → friction torque**.
- ☐ **Self-energising factor** for duo-servo drum: $C_{self} = (1 + \mu \tan\alpha)/(1 - \mu \tan\alpha)$.
- ☐ **Max deceleration:** $a_{max} = \mu g$.
- ☐ **Stopping distance:** $s = v_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$.

### D. Axles
- ☐ **Full-floating** for trucks, **semi-floating** for cars, **stub** for front-steering.
- ☐ Live axle (drive) vs. Dead axle (non-driven).

### E. Wheels & Tyres
- ☐ Disc (pressed-steel, cast-alloy, forged) vs. wire-spoke.
- ☐ **Bias (diagonal)** vs. **radial** tyre construction.
- ☐ **Tubeless** is universal in modern cars.
- ☐ Tread patterns: Rib, Lug, Block, Rib-Lug, Directional, Asymmetric.

### F. Universal Constants to Memorise
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ☐ Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa (for air-spring questions)
- ☐ Standard tyre pressure: 30–35 psi (≈ 200–240 kPa)

### G. Conversion Quick-Table (Save in Your Brain)
| Quantity | Conversion |
|---|---|
| km/h → m/s | Multiply by $5/18$ |
| psi → kPa | Multiply by 6.895 |
| mm → m | Divide by 1000 |
| ft-lb → N·m | Multiply by 1.356 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 14-Mark Strategy:**
> 1. Read the question twice.
> 2. Draw the diagram first (it carries 2–3 marks even before calculation).
> 3. State the governing equation *with all symbols defined*.
> 4. Substitute, simplify, verify units, then compute.
> 5. Provide a one-sentence engineering interpretation of the result.

Best of luck with your **PCAUT303 – Automotive Chassis** examination! 🚗💨
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wheel Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Wheel Dimensions** refer to the standardized set of geometric, structural, and load-carrying parameters that define an automotive wheel–tyre assembly. These parameters govern **ride comfort, vehicle dynamics, load capacity, speed capability, gearing calibration, and instrument-cluster accuracy (speedometer/odometer)**.

The automotive wheel is a compound assembly consisting of two principal sub-systems:
1. **Tyre (Pneumatic Rubber Component):** Provides traction, cushioning, and load support via compressed air.
2. **Wheel Rim (Metallic Component):** Provides the rigid mounting structure for the tyre and bolts to the wheel hub.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wheel dimensions are classified under **Module 4 – Wheels, Tyres, and Axles**. The dimensional nomenclature is standardized globally by **ETRTO (European Tyre and Rim Technical Organisation)** and **TRA (Tyre & Rim Association, USA)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Tyre is the Shoe, the Rim is the Sole"

Imagine you are buying a sports shoe. The label on the box reads something like **"UK 9, Width D, Leather Upper, Rubber Sole"**. In the exact same way, a tyre sidewall reads:

**"205/55 R16 91V"**

Breaking this analogy down:

| Shoe Label Parameter | Tyre Equivalent | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| UK 9 | 205 | Foot width / Section width in **mm** |
| Width D | 55 | Cushion thickness ratio / Aspect ratio in **%** |
| Rubber Sole | R16 | Construction type / Rim diameter in **inches** |
| (Hidden) | 91 | Load index |
| (Hidden) | V | Speed symbol |

So when a KTU examiner asks "Explain wheel dimensions", the student must immediately recognize that **205/55 R16** is *not* a random code — it is a **legally binding engineering specification** that dictates how the car will ride, brake, corner, and report speed.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are universally used in KTU board examinations:

- **1 inch = 25.4 mm** (exact SI conversion for rim diameters)
- **Standard inflation pressure for testing:** 220 kPa (passenger car reference)
- **Standard load (for load index derivation):** Varies with LI number (see Section 2)
- **Aspect ratio standard ranges:**
  - **Ultra-low profile:** ≤ 35
  - **Low profile:** 40 – 55
  - **Standard profile:** 60 – 70
  - **High profile / 70-series and above:** 70 – 85 (SUVs, light trucks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheel vs Tyre Definition Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Wheel** = Rim + Disc + Spokes (metallic structure)
> - **Tyre** = Tread + Sidewall + Bead + Ply + Inner liner (rubber + fabric)
> - **Wheel Assembly** = Wheel + Tyre + Air + Valve

---

## 1.4 Classification of Wheel Dimensional Parameters

Wheel dimensions are categorized into **four KTU-standard families**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Family 1 — Geometric Dimensions:** Section width, Section height, Overall diameter, Rim diameter, Bead seat diameter, Flange height, Rim width.
>
> **Family 2 — Structural / Construction Parameters:** Ply rating, Bias (Diagonal) vs Radial, Tubeless vs Tubed, Aspect ratio class.
>
> **Family 3 — Load & Speed Ratings:** Load Index (LI), Speed Symbol (SS), Ply Rating (PR), Load Range (LR).
>
> **Family 4 — Kinematic / Performance Parameters:** Revolutions per km (or per mile), Static loaded radius, Rolling radius, Revolutions per minute at rated speed.

This four-family classification is the **examiner's mental map**. Every Part-B question on this topic will fall into one of these families.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Tyre Size Designation — Decoded Step-by-Step

A modern European metric-tyre designation follows the format:

$$
\text{W} \;\; / \;\; \text{AR} \;\; \text{C} \;\; \text{RD}
$$

where each token carries a strict engineering meaning.

### Token 1: W — Section Width
- **Definition:** The linear distance (in **mm**) between the two outermost sidewalls of an inflated, unloaded tyre, measured at the widest point.
- **Range in production:** 125 mm (small cars) → 325 mm (heavy SUVs / light trucks).
- **Governs:** Contact patch width, lateral grip, steering response, aerodynamic drag.

### Token 2: AR — Aspect Ratio (Profile)
- **Definition:** The ratio of the **section height** to the **section width**, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

where **H = Section height** (distance from bead seat to outer tread) and **W = Section width**.

- **Physical meaning:** A lower AR means a **shorter sidewall** (stiffer, sportier, harsher ride). A higher AR means a **taller sidewall** (softer ride, more flex, more sidewall deformation under cornering).

### Token 3: C — Construction Code

| Code | Meaning | KTU Exam Point |
|---|---|---|
| **R** | Radial | Most common in modern vehicles |
| **B** or **D** | Bias (Diagonal) | Older vehicles, heavy commercial |
| **RF** | Reinforced | Light-truck / van variants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Radial vs Bias — Examiner's Favourite Comparison:**
> - **Radial:** Ply cords run perpendicular to the direction of travel. Provides larger contact patch, lower rolling resistance, better fuel economy, cooler running.
> - **Bias:** Ply cords run diagonally (30°–60°). Stronger sidewalls, better for off-road / heavy load, harsher ride.

### Token 4: RD — Rim Diameter
- Measured in **inches** (legacy industry standard retained for compatibility).
- Standard passenger-car rims: **13" to 22"**. Heavy commercial: **22.5", 24.5"**.

---

## 2.2 The Master Formula: Overall (Outside) Tyre Diameter

This is the **single most-tested formula** in this module.

$$
D_o = \text{RD} \times 25.4 + 2H
$$

Since $H = \dfrac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W$:

$$
\boxed{\; D_o = \text{RD} \times 25.4 + 2 \times \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W \;}
$$

All quantities must be in **millimetres**. $D_o$ is the **free (unloaded) overall diameter** used for speedometer calibration.

---

## 2.3 Revolutions Per Unit Distance (Speedometer Calibration)

The number of revolutions the wheel makes per kilometre (or mile) determines whether the speedometer reads correctly.

$$
\text{rev/km} = \frac{1000}{\pi \times D_o \,[\text{m}]} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\pi \times D_o \,[\text{mm}]}
$$

$$
\text{rev/mile} = \frac{1609.344 \times 1000}{\pi \times D_o \,[\text{mm}]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** When a customer up-sizes from 195/65 R15 to 205/55 R16, $D_o$ changes. If left uncorrected, the **speedometer will under-read** (typical mistake). The Engine ECU must be re-flashed or the speedometer driven gear swapped to compensate.

---

## 2.4 Static Loaded Radius ($R_s$)

The radius of the tyre under vehicle weight at recommended inflation pressure. Empirically:

$$
R_s \approx 0.96 \times \frac{D_o}{2} \quad \text{(passenger car approximation)}
$$

For commercial vehicles with bias-ply tyres, the multiplier drops to **0.93 – 0.95** due to greater sidewall deflection.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Section Height | $H = \dfrac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W$ | mm | AR is in % |
| 2 | Overall Diameter | $D_o = \text{RD} \times 25.4 + 2H$ | mm | **Most important** |
| 3 | Revolutions per km | $\text{Rev/km} = \dfrac{10^6}{\pi \, D_o}$ | rev/km | $D_o$ in mm |
| 4 | Revolutions per mile | $\text{Rev/mile} = \dfrac{1.609344 \times 10^6}{\pi \, D_o}$ | rev/mile | $D_o$ in mm |
| 5 | Static Loaded Radius | $R_s \approx 0.96 \times \dfrac{D_o}{2}$ | mm | Passenger car |
| 6 | Rolling Radius | $R_r \approx 0.97 \times \dfrac{D_o}{2}$ | mm | Under motion |
| 7 | Vehicle Speed | $V = \dfrac{2\pi N R_r}{60}$ | m/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 8 | Section Width Ratio | — | — | From size designation |
| 9 | Load Index Lookup | Empirical table | kg | See Section 2.6 |
| 10 | Speed Symbol Lookup | Empirical table | km/h | See Section 2.6 |

---

## 2.6 Load Index (LI) & Speed Symbol (SS)

These are **two-digit** and **one-letter** codes respectively, **appended after** the rim diameter.

### Load Index (Partial Table — Common Values)

| LI | 75 | 80 | 85 | 88 | 91 | 94 | 97 | 100 | 103 | 106 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| kg | 387 | 450 | 515 | 560 | 615 | 670 | 730 | 800 | 875 | 950 |

### Speed Symbol (Partial Table)

| SS | L | N | P | Q | R | S | T | U | H | V | W | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| km/h | 120 | 140 | 150 | 160 | 170 | 180 | 190 | 200 | 210 | 240 | 270 | 300 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **R** in "205/55 **R**16" (Radial construction) with **R** in the Speed Symbol table (170 km/h). They are **two completely different "R"s**! The construction code sits *between* aspect ratio and rim diameter; the speed symbol sits *after* the load index at the very end of the string.

---

## 2.7 Wheel Rim Dimensions (The Metal Side)

A rim is specified by:

1. **Rim diameter (D):** Bead seat diameter in inches.
2. **Rim width (J-code):** Measured in inches. E.g., **6J**, **7J**, **8J**.
3. **Flange height (H):** Drop-centre rim, typically **1.5 mm – 2.5 mm**.
4. **Bead seat angle:** Typically **5°** for passenger cars, **15°** for commercial (drop-centre vs wide-base).
5. **Offset (ET):** Distance in mm from the rim's mounting face to its centre plane. Positive, zero, or negative.

$$
\text{ET} = \frac{\text{Disc width}}{2} - \text{Inset}
$$

where **Inset** is the distance from the wheel centre plane to the inboard rim flange.

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility Summary

- **Speedometer calibration:** Relies on $D_o$ (Section 2.3).
- **Final drive ratio selection:** Rolling radius determines effective gear ratio.
- **ABS/TCS tuning:** Wheel-speed sensors measure $\omega$; ECU converts via $R_r$.
- **Suspension geometry:** Wheel dimensions define unsprung mass, scrub radius, and steering king-pin offset.
- **Load compliance:** Load Index must satisfy $\text{LI}_{\text{per tyre}} \times 4 \geq \text{GVW}$.
- **Clearance packaging:** Overall diameter must fit the wheel well with full suspension compression and steering lock.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Tyre Diameter from Size Designation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the closed-form expression for the overall (free) diameter of a tyre given its metric designation $W / \text{AR} \, \text{R} \, \text{RD}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the geometric primitives

A cross-section of the inflated tyre has three key vertical dimensions:
- Lower sidewall height = $H$ (from bead seat to widest point of tread shoulder).
- Upper sidewall height = $H$ (symmetric).
- Rim diameter = $\text{RD}$ in inches.

The overall diameter spans from the **bottom contact point** to the **top contact point** along the vertical axis of symmetry.

### Step 2 — Convert rim diameter to millimetres

$$
\text{RD}_{\text{mm}} = \text{RD} \times 25.4 \quad \text{(since 1 inch = 25.4 mm exactly)}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the sidewall contribution

By definition of aspect ratio (Section 2.1, Token 2):

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H = \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the three components

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= \text{RD}_{\text{mm}} + H + H \\
&= \text{RD} \times 25.4 + 2H \\
&= \text{RD} \times 25.4 + 2 \times \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Master Equation

$$
\boxed{\; D_o \,[\text{mm}] = 25.4 \cdot \text{RD} + 0.02 \cdot \text{AR} \cdot W \;}
$$

> **Conversion logic recap:** $2 \times \dfrac{\text{AR}}{100} = \dfrac{\text{AR}}{50} = 0.02 \cdot \text{AR}$. This numerical shortcut saves 30 seconds per calculation in the exam hall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Revolutions per Kilometre

**Problem Statement:** Derive the number of complete wheel revolutions per kilometre of vehicle travel.

### Step 1 — Circumference of the rolling wheel

For an undeformed wheel (theoretical assumption used for calibration):

$$
C = \pi \times D_o
$$

where $D_o$ is in the same linear unit as the desired travel distance.

### Step 2 — Normalize to kilometres

If $D_o$ is in **mm**, convert to **km**:

$$
D_o \,[\text{km}] = \frac{D_o \,[\text{mm}]}{10^6}
$$

### Step 3 — Revolutions per km

$$
\text{Rev/km} = \frac{1 \text{ km}}{C \,[\text{km}]} = \frac{1}{\pi \cdot D_o \,[\text{km}]} = \frac{10^6}{\pi \cdot D_o \,[\text{mm}]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Rev/km} = \frac{10^6}{\pi \cdot D_o} \; \Big[ \frac{\text{rev}}{\text{km}} \Big] \;}
$$

> **Conversion logic recap:** $\dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot D_o \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^6}{\pi \cdot D_o}$. Common student error: forgetting the $10^6$ factor, which yields a number 1,000,000 times too large.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vehicle Speed from Wheel RPM

**Problem Statement:** Express vehicle speed in m/s and km/h as a function of wheel angular speed $N$ (rpm) and rolling radius $R_r$.

### Step 1 — Linear distance per revolution

$$
s_{\text{rev}} = 2\pi R_r \quad [\text{m/rev}]
$$

### Step 2 — Distance per minute

$$
s_{\min} = 2\pi R_r \cdot N \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to seconds

$$
V = \frac{2\pi R_r N}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to km/h

$$
\boxed{\; V = \frac{2\pi R_r N \times 60}{1000} = \frac{0.377 \cdot R_r \,[\text{m}] \cdot N}{1} \;\; [\text{km/h}] \;}
$$

> **Conversion logic recap:** Multiply m/s by 3.6 to get km/h: $\dfrac{2\pi}{60} \times 3.6 = 0.37699 \approx 0.377$. The **magic number 0.377** is a frequent exam short-cut.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** A car is fitted with **205/55 R16** tyres.
- Section width: $W = 205$ mm
- Aspect ratio: $\text{AR} = 55$
- Rim diameter: $\text{RD} = 16$ inches

**Find:** (i) Section height, (ii) Overall diameter, (iii) Revolutions per km, (iv) Speed at 1000 rpm wheel speed.

### Solution

**(i) Section Height**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W \\
  &= \frac{55}{100} \times 205 \\
  &= 0.55 \times 205 \\
  &= 112.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Overall Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 25.4 \times 16 + 2 \times 112.75 \\
    &= 406.4 + 225.5 \\
    &= 631.9 \text{ mm} \\
    &= 0.6319 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Revolutions per km**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rev/km} &= \frac{10^6}{\pi \times 631.9} \\
              &= \frac{10^6}{1985.0} \\
              &= 503.78 \text{ rev/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Vehicle speed at $N = 1000$ rpm**

Take $R_r \approx 0.97 \times \dfrac{D_o}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= 0.97 \times \frac{0.6319}{2} = 0.97 \times 0.31595 = 0.30647 \text{ m} \\
V &= \frac{2\pi \times 0.30647 \times 1000}{60} \\
  &= \frac{1925.5}{60} \\
  &= 32.09 \text{ m/s} \\
  &= 32.09 \times 3.6 \\
  &= 115.5 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $H = 112.75$ mm
- $D_o = 631.9$ mm
- Rev/km $= 503.78$
- $V = 115.5$ km/h

---

## 3.5 Comparative Dimension Matrix — Real-World Tyre Sizes

| Vehicle Segment | Designation | $W$ [mm] | $H$ [mm] | $D_o$ [mm] | Rev/km |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compact Hatch | 175/70 R14 | 175 | 122.5 | 599.9 | 530.8 |
| Mid-size Sedan | 205/55 R16 | 205 | 112.75 | 631.9 | 503.8 |
| Compact SUV | 215/60 R17 | 215 | 129.0 | 689.8 | 461.6 |
| Luxury Saloon | 245/45 R18 | 245 | 110.25 | 677.7 | 469.8 |
| Sports Coupe | 255/35 R19 | 255 | 89.25 | 671.7 | 473.9 |
| Light Truck | 215/75 R16 | 215 | 161.25 | 748.9 | 425.1 |

> **Observation:** Although section widths increase from 175 → 255, the overall diameter does **not** scale linearly because aspect ratio decreases. This is exactly why up-sizing wheels while keeping the same $D_o$ is the safe upgrade path (preserves speedometer accuracy).

---

## 3.6 Sensitivity Analysis: What happens if AR drops by 10?

If a customer changes from **205/55 R16** to **225/45 R17**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o^{\text{old}} &= 25.4 \times 16 + 0.02 \times 55 \times 205 = 406.4 + 225.5 = 631.9 \text{ mm} \\
D_o^{\text{new}} &= 25.4 \times 17 + 0.02 \times 45 \times 225 = 431.8 + 202.5 = 634.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\Delta D_o = +2.4$ mm (≈ 0.4% increase) → Speedometer error is **negligible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Viva:** Always cross-check up-sizing by ensuring $\vert \Delta D_o \vert \leq 3\%$ of the stock value. Otherwise, the speedometer will be legally non-compliant and the ABS tuning will be off.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Tyre Dimensional Parameters

The following Mermaid diagram captures the engineering relationships between every wheel-dimension parameter without attempting a literal cross-sectional drawing (which would be inaccurate in ASCII). This is the **block-level functional map** the examiner expects students to internalize.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Size Designation String: W/AR R RD] --> nodeB[Section Width W]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Aspect Ratio AR percent]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Construction Code R or B]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Rim Diameter RD inch]

    nodeC --> nodeF[Section Height H]
    nodeB --> nodeF

    nodeF --> nodeG[Overall Diameter Do]
    nodeE --> nodeG

    nodeG --> nodeH[Revolutions per km]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Speedometer Calibration]

    nodeG --> nodeJ[Static Loaded Radius Rs]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Rolling Radius Rr]

    nodeK --> nodeL[Vehicle Speed V]
    nodeM[Wheel RPM N] --> nodeL

    nodeN[Load Index LI] --> nodeO[Maximum Load per Tyre]
    nodeP[Speed Symbol SS] --> nodeL

    nodeQ[Ply Rating PR] --> nodeO
    nodeR[Rim Width J code] --> nodeO
    nodeR --> nodeS[Wheel Offset ET]

    nodeD --> nodeT[Sidewall Stiffness]
    nodeT --> nodeK
    nodeT --> nodeU[Ride Comfort Index]

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeO fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow: From Size String to Vehicle Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Input: 205/55 R16] --> step2[Parse W=205, AR=55, RD=16]
    step2 --> step3[Compute H = 0.55 x 205 = 112.75 mm]
    step3 --> step4[Compute Do = 25.4 x 16 + 2 x 112.75 = 631.9 mm]
    step4 --> step5[Compute Rev per km = 503.78]
    step4 --> step6[Compute Rr = 0.97 x Do/2]
    step6 --> step7[Compute V at given N]
    step5 --> step8[Calibrate Speedometer]
    step7 --> step9[Validate ABS Wheel Speed Sensor]
    step8 --> step10[Compliance Check Done]
    step9 --> step10

    style step1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style step4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style step10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Section Parameter Map (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Tyre_Assembly
        nodeA1[Tread - Outer surface] --> nodeA2[Section Width W]
        nodeA3[Sidewall] --> nodeA4[Section Height H]
        nodeA5[Bead] --> nodeA6[Rim Seat Diameter]
        nodeA7[Rim] --> nodeA8[Rim Diameter RD]
        nodeA9[Inner Liner] --> nodeA10[Tubeless Tube Type]
    end

    nodeA2 --> nodeB1[Do calculation]
    nodeA4 --> nodeB1
    nodeA8 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1[Overall Diameter Do] --> nodeC1[Performance Output]

    style nodeB1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeC1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
> 1. The **gold-coloured central node** ($D_o$) is the master output — every other performance metric depends on it.
> 2. The **blue arrows** in the architecture diagram show the logical data flow from raw designation to final vehicle-level outcome.
> 3. The block-level map makes clear that **construction code (R vs B)** does not enter the $D_o$ equation but heavily influences the **rolling radius** (via sidewall stiffness).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the metric tyre size designation. Use the example 205/55 R16 91V to illustrate each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words expected):**

The designation **205/55 R16 91V** is decoded as follows:
- **205 mm** → Section width (widest cross-section of the inflated tyre).
- **55** → Aspect ratio (section height = 55% of section width = 112.75 mm).
- **R** → Radial construction (ply cords perpendicular to direction of travel).
- **16** → Rim (bead-seat) diameter in inches = 16 × 25.4 = 406.4 mm.
- **91** → Load index = 615 kg per tyre.
- **V** → Speed symbol = 240 km/h maximum rated speed.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for overall format, 1 mark for first three tokens, 1 mark for LI + SS interpretation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define "Aspect Ratio" of a tyre. Why is a low aspect ratio preferred for high-performance sports cars?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words expected):**

**Aspect Ratio** is the ratio of a tyre's section height to its section width, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100
$$

A **low aspect ratio** (e.g., 35 or 40) gives a **shorter sidewall**, which:
1. Reduces sidewall flex during hard cornering → **sharper steering response**.
2. Provides a larger, more stable **contact patch** under load.
3. Allows fitment of **larger diameter rims** for bigger brake discs.

Hence sports cars use 35–45 series tyres for precision handling. (1 mark definition + 2 marks justification.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** A passenger car is fitted with **215/60 R17** tyres. Calculate:
   (i) Section height
   (ii) Overall diameter in mm
   (iii) Number of complete revolutions per kilometre

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the difference between **radial** and **bias (diagonal)** tyre construction. Discuss how this choice affects rolling resistance, heat dissipation, and tyre life.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $W = 215$ mm, $\text{AR} = 60$, $\text{RD} = 17$ in.

**(i) Section Height**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W \\
  &= \frac{60}{100} \times 215 \\
  &= 0.60 \times 215 \\
  &= 129 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Overall Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 25.4 \times \text{RD} + 2H \\
    &= 25.4 \times 17 + 2 \times 129 \\
    &= 431.8 + 258 \\
    &= 689.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Revolutions per km**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rev/km} &= \frac{10^6}{\pi \times D_o} \\
              &= \frac{10^6}{\pi \times 689.8} \\
              &= \frac{10^6}{2167.12} \\
              &= 461.44 \text{ rev/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers (Part a):** $H = 129$ mm, $D_o = 689.8$ mm, Rev/km $= 461.44$.

---

#### Part (b) — Radial vs Bias Construction

| Parameter | Radial Tyre | Bias (Diagonal) Tyre |
|---|---|---|
| **Ply Orientation** | 90° to direction of travel | 30°–60° to direction of travel |
| **Contact Patch** | Larger, flatter, more uniform | Smaller, rounded |
| **Rolling Resistance** | Lower (15–20% less) | Higher |
| **Fuel Economy** | Better | Worse |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Superior (plies flex independently) | Inferior (inter-ply friction) |
| **Sidewall Flexibility** | High (soft sidewall, rigid tread) | Uniform flex |
| **Tyre Life** | Longer (less heat build-up) | Shorter |
| **Steering Precision** | High at low AR | Better at high AR / heavy load |
| **Typical Application** | Passenger cars, modern LCVs | Vintage cars, certain off-road and heavy commercial applications |

**Engineering Reasoning (Part b):**
- In a **radial** tyre, only the **tread band** deforms at the contact patch; sidewalls flex independently. This decoupling → less hysteresis loss → cooler running.
- In a **bias** tyre, all plies are interlaced; every part of the carcass flexes simultaneously, generating internal friction and heat. Hence lower fuel economy and shorter life.
- Modern passenger vehicles universally adopt **radial** construction because of its superior balance of comfort, life, and economy.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the following terms with suitable diagrams/sketches:
   (i) Section width
   (ii) Section height
   (iii) Overall diameter
   (iv) Static loaded radius
   (v) Rolling radius

**(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle travels at **90 km/h** with **215/65 R15** tyres. Calculate:
   (i) Wheel RPM
   (ii) Confirm whether the same wheel can be safely upgraded to **225/60 R15** without violating the $\pm 3\%$ overall-diameter rule.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Definitions (1.4 marks each)

- **(i) Section Width:** The linear distance between the outermost points of the inflated, unloaded tyre's sidewalls, measured at the widest cross-section. **Unit: mm.**
- **(ii) Section Height:** The distance from the bead seat to the outermost point of the tread, measured radially on the sidewall. Equals $(AR/100) \times W$.
- **(iii) Overall Diameter:** The maximum outside diameter of the inflated, unloaded tyre — equals rim diameter (in mm) plus twice the section height.
- **(iv) Static Loaded Radius:** The radius of the tyre when supporting the vehicle's weight at recommended cold inflation pressure, with the vehicle stationary. Approximately $0.96 \times D_o/2$.
- **(v) Rolling Radius:** The effective radius under motion. It is slightly larger than the static loaded radius because of centrifugal and gyroscopic effects. Approximately $0.97 \times D_o/2$.

---

#### Part (b) — Speed & Compatibility Calculation

**Given:** $V = 90$ km/h, Stock tyre = 215/65 R15.

**Step 1 — Compute Stock Overall Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{stock}} &= \frac{65}{100} \times 215 = 139.75 \text{ mm} \\
D_{o,\text{stock}} &= 25.4 \times 15 + 2 \times 139.75 = 381 + 279.5 = 660.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Radius**

$$
R_r = 0.97 \times \frac{660.5}{2} = 0.97 \times 330.25 = 320.34 \text{ mm} = 0.32034 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Wheel RPM**

$$
V = \frac{2\pi R_r N}{60} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = \frac{60 V}{2\pi R_r} = \frac{60 \times 90 \times 1000 / 3600}{2\pi \times 0.32034}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1500}{2.0127} = 745.3 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute New Tyre Overall Diameter (225/60 R15)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{new}} &= \frac{60}{100} \times 225 = 135 \text{ mm} \\
D_{o,\text{new}} &= 25.4 \times 15 + 2 \times 135 = 381 + 270 = 651 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the $\pm 3\%$ Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Permissible range} &= D_{o,\text{stock}} \pm 3\% \\
&= 660.5 \pm 19.815 \\
&= [640.685, \; 680.315] \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $D_{o,\text{new}} = 651$ mm lies within $[640.7, 680.3]$, the upgrade is **safe and compliant**.

**Final Answers (Part b):**
- $N = 745.3$ rpm
- New $D_o = 651$ mm → **Upgrade is permissible** (deviation $= -1.44\%$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where students typically lose marks on Wheel Dimensions questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in rim diameter:** Students forget to convert inches to mm (multiply by 25.4). Common error: writing $D_o = 16 + 2 \times 112.75 = 240.75$ (numerically absurd). **Penalty: full marks lost for the diameter sub-question.**
>
> 2. **Confusing construction "R" with speed symbol "R":** The "R" in 205/**R**16 means *Radial*, while the "R" in load/speed tables means 170 km/h. They are **independent**. Students who write "R = 170 km/h" inside the size designation lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Aspect ratio interpretation:** Students write "55 = 55 mm height" instead of "55% of section width". This costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Rev/km unit mismatch:** Forgetting the $10^6$ factor. Always show the unit explicitly: rev/km.
>
> 5. **Skipping the conversion logic:** Examiners award a partial mark for the *formula setup* even if arithmetic is wrong. Always write the formula first, then substitute.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the $\pm 3\%$ rule:** When answering upgrade questions, students compute $D_o$ correctly but never state whether the new value lies in the legal band. This costs the "compliance" marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Wheel Dimensions**

- [ ] **Size string format:** $W$ / AR Construction $RD$ LI SS — know all six tokens.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $D_o = 25.4 \times RD + 2 \times (AR/100) \times W$. Always in **mm**.
- [ ] **Section height** $H = 0.01 \times AR \times W$. Lower AR → stiffer sidewall, sportier handling, harsher ride.
- [ ] **Rev/km** $= 10^6 / (\pi \times D_o)$. Used for speedometer and odometer calibration.
- [ ] **Rolling radius** $\approx 0.97 \times D_o / 2$. Use $R_r$ (not $D_o/2$) for vehicle speed equations.
- [ ] **Speed equation shortcut:** $V \text{ [km/h]} \approx 0.377 \times R_r \text{ [m]} \times N \text{ [rpm]}$.
- [ ] **Upgrade rule of thumb:** $\vert \Delta D_o \vert \leq 3\%$ of stock. Outside this → speedometer legally non-compliant.
- [ ] **Load Index (LI):** 2- or 3-digit number; lookup table gives max load in kg per tyre. Total permissible load = LI × 4 must be ≥ GVW.
- [ ] **Speed Symbol (SS):** Single letter; ranges L (120 km/h) to Y (300 km/h). Vehicle's $V_{\max}$ must be ≤ SS.
- [ ] **Construction codes:** **R** = Radial (modern standard); **B/D** = Bias (older / heavy duty).
- [ ] **Rim designation:** Diameter (inches) × Width (J-code in inches). Example: **16 × 7J**.
- [ ] **Wheel Offset (ET):** Positive ET → wheel sits inboard; negative ET → wheel pokes outward. Critical for suspension clearance.
- [ ] **Static vs Rolling radius:** Static ≈ $0.96 \times D_o/2$; Rolling ≈ $0.97 \times D_o/2$.
- [ ] **Tubeless vs Tubed:** Modern tubeless tyres run cooler and deflate slowly; obsolete tubes retain pressure better if punctured but run hotter.
- [ ] **Ply Rating (PR):** Strength indicator; 6 PR = standard passenger; 10 PR = light truck.
- [ ] **Sidewall marking:** Date code (DOT XXXX YY MM), manufacturer, size, max load, max pressure — all in one string.
- [ ] **Real-world impact:** Wheel dimensions affect speedometer accuracy, ABS tuning, ride comfort, cornering grip, fuel economy, and legal compliance.

---

*End of KTU Premium Notes — PCAUT303 Module 4: Wheel Dimensions*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Function of Tyres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure and Function of Tyres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **automotive tyre (pneumatic tyre)** is a toroidal, air-pressurized, flexible composite structure mounted on the wheel rim of a vehicle. It serves as the **sole contact patch** between the vehicle and the road surface, and is the only component of the chassis system that transmits all forces (vertical, longitudinal, and lateral) generated during vehicle operation. Modern tyres are engineered rubber-fabric composites built from vulcanized natural/synthetic rubber reinforced with textile cords (rayon, nylon, polyester, or steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAUT303 - Module 4):**
> Tyres are classified under the "Wheels and Tyres" unit. Examiners frequently test the *relationship between tyre geometry, load index, speed symbol, and dynamic performance*. Memorize the designation format (e.g., **P215/65R15 95H**) as it appears in nearly every Board paper.

## 1.2 Functions of a Tyre

A pneumatic tyre must simultaneously perform **six critical functions** while supporting the entire vehicle mass:

1. **Load Support** – Carry the vertical weight of the vehicle (static + dynamic loads including bump impulses).
2. **Traction & Driving Force Transmission** – Convert engine torque into forward/rearward motion through friction at the contact patch.
3. **Braking Force Transmission** – Transmit braking torque to the road surface via frictional grip.
4. **Steering & Cornering** – Provide lateral (cornering) stiffness to allow directional changes.
5. **Shock Absorption** – Cushion the chassis from road irregularities through viscoelastic deformation of the sidewall and air spring.
6. **Roll/Speed Control** – Maintain dimensional stability at high speeds and under thermal loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:**
> Standard inflation pressure for passenger car tyres = **30–35 psi (2.1–2.4 bar ≈ 210–240 kPa)**, while commercial vehicle tyres run at **80–110 psi (5.5–7.5 bar)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shoe of the Vehicle"

Imagine a marathon runner wearing a pair of high-performance running shoes. The shoe is the **only interface** between the runner's body and the hard, uneven road. Now scale this analogy to a 1.5-tonne car:

| Body Part (Human) | Vehicle Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Shoe sole tread | Tyre tread | Grip on the surface (traction) |
| Shoe foam midsole | Air-filled cavity + sidewall | Shock absorption |
| Shoe upper | Sidewall + ply cords | Maintains shape under load |
| Shoe lace (binding) | Bead bundle | Locks shoe to foot (= wheel rim) |
| Heel cup | Bead apex / filler | Anchors structure to base |

Just as a marathon runner cannot win a race in flimsy slippers, an SUV cannot corner safely on bald tyres. The tyre is, in essence, the **"athletic footwear"** of the automobile — engineered for grip, comfort, and durability under extreme cyclic loading.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** If you remember only one thing — *the tyre is the ONLY part of your car that touches the road*. Every Newton of force your engine produces, every Newton of braking force, and every lateral g-force during cornering must pass through this roughly **手掌-sized (hand-sized) contact patch* of about 150–250 cm².
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Tyre — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

A modern radial tyre is constructed from **eight primary components** layered from the outside in:

### (a) Tread
- The outermost rubber layer that contacts the road.
- Patterned with **grooves, sipes, and lugs** to channel water (hydroplaning resistance) and provide mechanical grip.
- Made from a high-traction, wear-resistant rubber compound (often a blend of SBR, butadiene rubber, and silica).
- Typical depth: **8 mm** (new), legally worn out at **1.6 mm**.

### (b) Cap Ply (Jacket)
- A thin layer of nylon or polyester fabric wrapped over the steel belts.
- **Purpose:** Maintains dimensional stability at high speeds (≥ 200 km/h). It counteracts the centrifugal growth of the tread — known as *untripping* resistance.

### (c) Steel Belts (Belt Plies)
- Two (or sometimes more) layers of steel cords embedded in rubber.
- Located directly beneath the tread.
- Provide **tread rigidity, puncture resistance, and high-speed stability**.
- Oriented at opposing angles (typically +18° and −18°) to the centerline.

### (d) Body Ply (Carcass)
- The main load-bearing structure made of polyester, rayon, or nylon cords.
- In **radial tyres**, the cords run *radially* (90° to the centerline) — from bead to bead.
- In **bias-ply tyres**, the cords run diagonally at 30–40° to the centerline, alternating in each ply.

### (e) Sidewall
- The flexible rubber region between the tread shoulder and the bead.
- Contains the **moulded-in designation markings** (size, type, manufacturer, DOT code).
- Provides the bulk of the **vertical compliance** (cushioning action).

### (f) Bead Bundle
- A ring of high-tensile steel wires coated in rubber.
- Mechanically and frictionally locks the tyre to the wheel rim.
- Must withstand the hoop stress generated by inflation pressure.

### (g) Bead Filler (Apex)
- A triangular rubber wedge above the bead bundle.
- Stiffens the lower sidewall and provides a smooth transition of forces from bead to sidewall.

### (h) Inner Liner
- An airtight rubber layer (typically halobutyl compound) bonded to the inside of the carcass.
- Replaces the inner tube in **tubeless tyres**.

## 2.2 Classification of Tyres

| Classification Basis | Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Carcass Construction** | Bias-ply (Diagonal) | Nylon/polyester cords at 30–40°; stiffer sidewall |
| | Radial | Cords at 90°; longer life, better fuel economy, cooler running |
| **Air Retention** | Tube-type | Uses separate inner tube |
| | Tubeless | Airtight inner liner + ribbed bead seal |
| **Application** | Passenger Car (PCR) | Low profile, comfort-oriented |
| | Light Truck (LT) | Higher load capacity, stiffer sidewall |
| | Heavy Commercial (TBR) | Deep tread, designed for highway/load cycles |
| **Special Type** | Run-flat | Reinforced sidewall allows 80 km at 80 km/h after puncture |
| | Self-sealing | Inner sealant layer plugs small punctures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Radial Dominates Today:**
> Radial tyres produce **30% less rolling resistance**, run **20°C cooler**, and offer **50% longer tread life** than bias-ply. Over 98% of all new passenger cars worldwide use radial construction. This is a common 3-mark Board question.

## 2.3 Tyre Designation System — The "Tyre Code"

A typical marking is read as: **P 215/65 R 15 95 H**

| Position | Code | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **P** | Tyre type (P = Passenger, LT = Light Truck, T = Temporary) |
| 2 | **215** | Section Width (W) in **mm** |
| 3 | **65** | Aspect Ratio (H/W × 100) in **%** |
| 4 | **R** | Construction (R = Radial, B = Bias-ply, D = Diagonal) |
| 5 | **15** | Rim Diameter (D_r) in **inches** |
| 6 | **95** | Load Index (LI) — 95 = **690 kg** per tyre |
| 7 | **H** | Speed Symbol — H = **210 km/h** maximum |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any numerical on this topic in KTU examinations. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Section Height (H) | $H = \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W$ | mm | AR = Aspect Ratio (number) |
| 2 | Overall Diameter (D_o) | $D_o = 2H + D_r$ | mm | D_r is in **inches**; convert to mm (×25.4) if required |
| 3 | Overall Diameter (in inches) | $D_o = 2H + D_r$ | inches | When H is in inches: $H_{in} = \frac{W_{mm} \times \text{AR}}{25.4 \times 100}$ |
| 4 | Rolling Radius (R_r) | $R_r \approx 0.965 \times \frac{D_o}{2}$ | mm | Loaded condition (tread deflects ~3.5%) |
| 5 | Rolling Circumference (C_r) | $C_r = 2 \pi R_r$ | mm | Used for odometer calibration |
| 6 | Revolutions per km (N) | $N = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{C_r}$ | rev/km | Inverse of rolling circumference |
| 7 | Vehicle Speed (V) | $V = \frac{\pi \times D_o \times N_{rpm} \times 60}{10^6}$ | km/h | $N_{rpm}$ = wheel rpm |
| 8 | Vehicle Speed (alt) | $V = \frac{2 \pi R_r \times N_{rpm} \times 60}{1000}$ | km/h | Cleaner form |
| 9 | Static Load per Tyre | $F_{static} = \frac{m \times g}{4}$ | N | For 4-tyre vehicle, equal distribution |
| 10 | Load Index to Mass | Refer to LI table | kg | 95 → 690 kg; 100 → 800 kg; 110 → 1060 kg |
| 11 | Ply Rating (PR) → Load | Empirical table | kg | Higher PR → higher load capacity |
| 12 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100$ | % | Direct percentage |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Pipe-Symbol Alert in Markdown Tables**
> In KTU answer sheets and the markdown table above, the absolute value or "such that" symbol uses `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to avoid breaking table syntax. In LaTeX, write `$\mid$` instead of `$\vert x \vert$` inside any row.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulae are not academic — they directly drive:

1. **Speedometer Calibration** – ECU uses tyre rev/km to compute vehicle speed. Changing to a larger tyre without recalibrating will cause speedometer under-reading.
2. **Odometer Accuracy** – The trip meter counts tyre revolutions × C_r. Mismatched tyres on an axle cause ABS sensor faults.
3. **Gear Ratio Design** – Powertrain engineers use rolling radius to compute tractive effort: $F_t = \frac{T \times i_g \times i_0 \times \eta}{R_r}$.
4. **Fuel Economy** – Lower rolling resistance (from radial design + silica compounds) saves ~5–8% fuel.
5. **EV Range** – Critical for electric vehicles; every 100 N of extra rolling resistance reduces range by 1–2%.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Diameter from Tyre Designation

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car tyre is marked **205/55 R 16**. Compute its overall diameter in mm and in inches.

**Given Data:**
- Section width, $W = 205$ mm
- Aspect ratio, $AR = 55$
- Construction: Radial (R)
- Rim diameter, $D_r = 16$ inches

**Step 1 — Compute Section Height (H):**
By definition, the aspect ratio is the ratio of section height to section width, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100$$

Rearranging for H:

$$H = \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{55}{100} \times 205 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.55 \times 205 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
  &= 112.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Rim Diameter to mm:**
The rim diameter is given in inches. We use the conversion factor: 1 inch = **25.4 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r (\text{in mm}) &= 16 \times 25.4 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
                   &= 406.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Overall Diameter (D_o):**
The overall diameter is the sum of twice the section height plus the rim diameter (since the height appears on top and bottom of the rim):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 2H + D_r \\[4pt]
    &= 2(112.75) + 406.4 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 225.5 + 406.4 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 631.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Inches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o (\text{inch}) &= \frac{631.9}{25.4} \text{ inches} \\[4pt]
                  &= 24.878 \text{ inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This computed value (~24.88 inches) is critical when designing the wheel well arch. A 5% increase in section height would push the diameter to ~660 mm, which may foul the wheel arch liner during full suspension compression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rolling Circumference and Revolutions per km

**Problem Statement:** Using the tyre from Derivation 1 (D_o = 631.9 mm), determine:
(a) The rolling radius under load.
(b) The rolling circumference.
(c) The number of revolutions per km of vehicle travel.

**Step 1 — Rolling Radius (R_r):**
Under load, the tyre tread deflects by approximately 3.5% of the section height. The effective (rolling) radius is empirically:

$$R_r \approx 0.965 \times \frac{D_o}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= 0.965 \times \frac{631.9}{2} \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.965 \times 315.95 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 304.89 \text{ mm} \approx 0.3049 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Circumference (C_r):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_r &= 2 \pi R_r \\[4pt]
    &= 2 \times \pi \times 304.89 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 1915.6 \text{ mm} = 1.9156 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Revolutions per km (N):**
For the vehicle to travel 1 km = 1000 m, the tyre must rotate N times such that $N \times C_r = 1000$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{C_r} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{1000}{1.9156} \text{ rev/km} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 522.05 \text{ rev/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check:** A typical 16-inch passenger tyre rolls about **500–530 rev/km**. Our value of 522 lies in this expected range, confirming the result is dimensionally and physically consistent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vehicle Speed from Wheel RPM

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle fitted with **205/55 R 16** tyres has its wheel rotating at **N_w = 850 rpm** (engine at cruise). Calculate the vehicle speed in km/h.

**Step 1 — Use the relationship between speed, wheel circumference, and rotational speed:**

$$V = C_r \times N_w \times \frac{60}{1000}$$

where V is in km/h, C_r in metres, and N_w in rpm.

**Step 2 — Substitute (using C_r = 1.9156 m from Derivation 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 1.9156 \text{ m} \times 850 \text{ rpm} \times \frac{60}{1000} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.9156 \times 850 \times 0.06 \text{ km/h} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.9156 \times 51 \text{ km/h} \\[4pt]
  &= 97.69 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using the diameter form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{\pi \times D_o \times N_w \times 60}{10^6} \quad \text{(with } D_o \text{ in mm)} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi \times 631.9 \times 850 \times 60}{10^6} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{3.1416 \times 631.9 \times 51000}{10^6} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{1.0121 \times 10^8}{10^6} \\[4pt]
  &= 101.2 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Small discrepancy alert:** The two methods differ by ~3.5%. This is because the first uses the *rolling* radius (which accounts for tread deflection) while the second uses the *unloaded* overall diameter. In practice, **always use the rolling radius for speed calculations** — this is how ECUs and ABS systems compute vehicle speed.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Load Index to Mass Conversion (Inverse Method)

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car has a kerb mass of 1500 kg. The manufacturer specifies **215/60 R 16 95H** tyres. Determine the minimum number of tyres required to safely carry the vehicle, and verify whether the load index is adequate.

**Step 1 — Standard Load Index Table (selected values):**

| Load Index | Mass per Tyre (kg) |
|---|---|
| 91 | 615 |
| 92 | 630 |
| 93 | 650 |
| 94 | 670 |
| **95** | **690** |
| 96 | 710 |
| 100 | 800 |
| 110 | 1060 |

**Step 2 — Compute the static load per tyre (4-tyre vehicle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{per tyre}} &= \frac{m \times g}{4} \\[4pt]
                    &= \frac{1500 \times 9.81}{4} \text{ N} \\[4pt]
                    &= 3678.75 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mass equivalent (divide by g):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{per tyre}} &= \frac{3678.75}{9.81} \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
                    &= 375 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Load Index 95:**

Load Index 95 = **690 kg/tyre**. Since 375 kg ≪ 690 kg, the tyres are **safely rated** with a safety margin of (690 − 375)/690 = **45.7%**.

**Step 4 — Account for Dynamic Load Transfer:**
Under hard braking, ~70% of the load transfers to the front axle. The dynamic front load per tyre becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{front, dynamic}} &= 0.70 \times \frac{1500 \times 9.81}{2} \\[4pt]
                          &= 5149 \text{ N} = 525 \text{ kg per front tyre}
\end{aligned}
$$

Even under this worst case, 525 kg < 690 kg — **the design is safe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the safety margin matters:** KTU examiners often ask "Is the tyre adequately rated?" Always include a *dynamic load transfer* analysis in your answer. Students who ignore this lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Radial vs Bias-Ply

| Performance Parameter | Bias-Ply | Radial | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tread Life | 25,000 km | 60,000 km | Radial |
| Rolling Resistance | High | Low (~30% less) | Radial |
| Heat Build-up | High | Low (runs 20°C cooler) | Radial |
| Sidewall Flex | Stiff | Flexible | Radial (better comfort) |
| Puncture Resistance | Higher (thicker) | Lower (thinner) | Bias |
| Cornering Stiffness | Lower | Higher | Radial |
| Steering Response | Slower | Sharper | Radial |
| Load Capacity at Same Inflation | Higher | Lower (compensated by LI) | Bias |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher | Bias |
| Modern Use | Truck/bus, vintage cars | Passenger cars (98% market) | Radial |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Tyre Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TREAD - Outer rubber layer, road contact, 8mm deep] --> B[CAP PLY - Nylon overlay, high-speed stability]
    B --> C[STEEL BELTS - Two layers, opposing angles, puncture resistance]
    C --> D[BODY PLY CARCASS - Polyester cords radial 90 deg, main load bearer]
    D --> E[SIDEWALL - Flexible rubber, designation markings, cushioning]
    D --> F[INNER LINER - Halobutyl airtight layer, replaces tube]
    D --> G[BEAD APEX - Triangular rubber wedge, stiffens lower sidewall]
    G --> H[BEAD BUNDLE - High tensile steel wire ring, locks to rim]
    H --> I[BEAD SEAT - Ribbed contact zone, airtight seal on rim]
    I --> J[WHEEL RIM - 16 inch alloy or steel, supports bead]

    style B fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#3182ce,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#4299e1,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#63b3ed,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#4299e1,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow of a Tyre

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENG[ENGINE TORQUE] --> TX[TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
    TX --> DIFF[DIFFERENTIAL]
    DIFF --> HALF[HALF SHAFT]
    HALF --> WHL[WHEEL HUB]
    WHL --> BEAD[BEAD INTERFACE]
    BEAD --> CARCASS[CARCASS PLIES]
    CARCASS --> BELT[STEEL BELTS]
    BELT --> TREAD[TREAD BLOCK]
    TREAD --> RD[ROAD SURFACE]
    RD --> F1[VERTICAL LOAD]
    RD --> F2[LONGITUDINAL TRACTION]
    RD --> F3[LATERAL CORNERING]
    F1 --> SPR[SUSPENSION SYSTEM]
    F2 --> POW[POWERTRAIN RESPONSE]
    F3 --> ST[STEERING FEEDBACK]
    SPR --> CHS[CHASSIS DYNAMICS]
    POW --> CHS
    ST --> CHS

    style ENG fill:#2d3748,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style RD fill:#744210,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style CHS fill:#22543d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style TREAD fill:#1a365d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Tyre Construction Layers (Simplified)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OUTER_REGION
        L1[TREAD RUBBER COMPOUND]
        L2[CAP PLY - NYLON FABRIC]
    end
    subgraph MID_REGION
        L3[STEEL BELT 1 - PLUS 18 DEG]
        L4[STEEL BELT 2 - MINUS 18 DEG]
        L5[POLYESTER CARCASS PLIES RADIAL]
    end
    subgraph INNER_REGION
        L6[INNER LINER HALOBUTYL]
        L7[SIDEWALL RUBBER]
        L8[BEAD FILLER APEX]
        L9[STEEL BEAD BUNDLE]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
    L5 --> L7
    L7 --> L8 --> L9

    style L1 fill:#742a2a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L5 fill:#1a365d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L9 fill:#22543d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Force Transmission Through a Tyre

```mermaid
graph TD
    VEH[VEHICLE MASS W] --> FZ[VERTICAL FORCE Fz]
    ENG2[ENGINE TORQUE M] --> FX[LONGITUDINAL FORCE Fx]
    STEER[STEERING ANGLE DELTA] --> FY[LATERAL FORCE Fy]
    FZ --> CP[CONTACT PATCH 200 sq cm]
    FX --> CP
    FY --> CP
    CP --> RD2[ROAD NORMAL AND TANGENTIAL REACTIONS]
    RD2 --> RESP[DYNAMIC VEHICLE RESPONSE ACCEL BRAKE TURN]

    style VEH fill:#2d3748,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style CP fill:#c05621,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style RD2 fill:#744210,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style RESP fill:#22543d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice how all three force vectors (F_x, F_y, F_z) converge at the **contact patch**. The tyre is the only component that converts the vehicle's mechanical energy (engine torque) and the driver's inputs (brake, steering) into road reactions through this single small zone. This is why **contact patch mechanics** is a KTU favourite.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any SIX primary functions of a pneumatic tyre in an automobile.**

**Model Answer:**

A pneumatic tyre performs the following functions:

1. **Load Support:** Carries the vertical static and dynamic weight of the vehicle through inflation pressure.
2. **Traction Transmission:** Transfers engine driving torque to the road via frictional grip at the contact patch.
3. **Braking Force Transmission:** Conveys hydraulic/regenerative braking torque to the road surface.
4. **Cornering/Lateral Force Transmission:** Provides lateral stiffness for directional control during turns.
5. **Shock Absorption:** Cushions the chassis from road irregularities through sidewall flex and air spring action.
6. **Speed/Dimensional Stability:** Maintains shape at high speeds through cap ply and steel belt reinforcement.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark answer requires **at least 6 points**, each in one line. Examiners give **0.5 mark per correct function** — partial marking is generous if you list 8+.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Decode the tyre marking "P 215/65 R 15 95 H" and state the meaning of each component.**

**Model Answer:**

The marking decodes as follows:

| Code | Meaning | Value |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Vehicle type | Passenger car |
| **215** | Section width | 215 mm |
| **65** | Aspect ratio (H/W) | 65% |
| **R** | Construction | Radial |
| **15** | Rim diameter | 15 inches |
| **95** | Load index | 690 kg/tyre |
| **H** | Speed symbol | 210 km/h max |

Therefore, the tyre has a section height of (65/100) × 215 = **139.75 mm** and a maximum safe speed of **210 km/h** with a load capacity of **690 kg** per tyre.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constructional details of a **tubeless radial-ply pneumatic tyre**, naming all major components and stating the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car is fitted with **tyres of size 185/70 R 14**. Compute:
  (i) Section height of the tyre.
  (ii) Overall diameter in mm and in inches.
  (iii) Rolling circumference under loaded condition.
  (iv) Number of revolutions per km of vehicle travel.
  (v) Vehicle speed when the wheel rotates at 900 rpm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A tubeless radial-ply tyre consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Tread** — The patterned outer rubber that contacts the road, providing grip and water evacuation.
2. **Cap Ply** — A nylon fabric overlay that prevents centrifugal growth at high speed.
3. **Steel Belts** — Two layers of steel cord at opposing angles (±18°), giving tread rigidity and puncture resistance.
4. **Body Ply (Carcass)** — Polyester cords running radially (90° to the centreline) from bead to bead, carrying the inflation pressure load.
5. **Sidewall** — Flexible rubber that flexes under load and carries the designation markings.
6. **Bead Bundle** — High-tensile steel wire ring that locks the tyre onto the rim.
7. **Bead Apex/Filler** — A rubber wedge that stiffens the lower sidewall for smooth force transition.
8. **Inner Liner** — A halobutyl rubber coating on the inside that provides airtightness, eliminating the need for a separate inner tube.
9. **Rim Flange Contact** — Bead seat ribs that ensure an airtight seal against the wheel rim.

**In tubeless construction**, the inner liner + bead-rib interface replaces the inner tube. The advantage is slower pressure loss in case of puncture, lower weight, and better heat dissipation.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Section width W = 185 mm; Aspect ratio AR = 70; Rim diameter D_r = 14 inches.

**(i) Section Height (H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\text{AR}}{100} \times W \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{70}{100} \times 185 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.70 \times 185 = 129.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Overall Diameter (D_o):**

Convert rim diameter to mm: $D_r = 14 \times 25.4 = 355.6$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 2H + D_r \\[4pt]
    &= 2(129.5) + 355.6 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 259.0 + 355.6 = 614.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

In inches: $D_o = 614.6 / 25.4 = 24.20$ inches

**(iii) Rolling Circumference (C_r):**

Rolling radius: $R_r = 0.965 \times \frac{614.6}{2} = 296.55$ mm = 0.2966 m

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_r &= 2 \pi R_r = 2 \times \pi \times 296.55 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
    &= 1863.1 \text{ mm} = 1.8631 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Revolutions per km:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000}{1.8631} = 536.7 \text{ rev/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Vehicle Speed at N_w = 900 rpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= C_r \times N_w \times \frac{60}{1000} \\[4pt]
  &= 1.8631 \times 900 \times 0.06 \\[4pt]
  &= 100.61 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) H = 129.5 mm; (ii) D_o = 614.6 mm = 24.20 inches; (iii) C_r = 1.863 m; (iv) N = 536.7 rev/km; (v) V = 100.6 km/h.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **radial-ply and bias-ply** tyre constructions with respect to at least eight performance parameters. State why radial tyres have become the universal choice for passenger cars. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A commercial truck uses **tyres of size 295/80 R 22.5 with load index 152/148 L**. The truck's gross vehicle weight is 18,000 kg distributed equally on two axles. Determine:
  (i) Section height of the tyre.
  (ii) Overall diameter in mm.
  (iii) Whether the load index is adequate for the truck, considering a 30% overload margin.
  (iv) The maximum permissible speed from the speed symbol L.
  **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A comparison is presented in **Section 3.5 (Analytical Comparison Matrix)** above. Key points:

- **Carcass Cord Angle:** Radial = 90° to centreline; Bias = 30–40° alternating.
- **Tread Life:** Radial ≫ Bias (60,000 km vs 25,000 km).
- **Rolling Resistance:** Radial is 30% lower.
- **Heat Dissipation:** Radial runs ~20°C cooler.
- **Sidewall Flex:** Radial sidewalls flex independently of the tread (due to rigid belts), giving better comfort and traction.
- **Cornering Stiffness:** Radial is higher → sharper steering response.
- **Puncture Resistance:** Bias is better (thicker sidewalls).
- **Cost:** Bias is cheaper to manufacture.

**Why Radial Dominates:** Lower rolling resistance → 5–8% better fuel economy; cooler running → longer life; better traction and steering → safer handling. The minor disadvantages (higher cost, slightly lower puncture resistance) are outweighed by these benefits, leading to 98% market share in passenger cars.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** W = 295 mm; AR = 80; D_r = 22.5 inches; LI = 152/148 (dual); Speed symbol = L.

**(i) Section Height (H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{80}{100} \times 295 = 236.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Overall Diameter:**

$D_r = 22.5 \times 25.4 = 571.5$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 2(236.0) + 571.5 = 472.0 + 571.5 = 1043.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Load Adequacy Check:**

- LI 152 (single) = **3550 kg/tyre**
- LI 148 (dual) = **3150 kg/tyre** (per tyre in dual configuration)

Static load per tyre (6-tyre configuration, 3 axles — assume equal distribution):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{per tyre}} &= \frac{18{,}000}{6} = 3000 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

With 30% overload margin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{required}} &= 3000 \times 1.30 = 3900 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** 3900 kg > 3550 kg (single LI). Therefore, the load index is **NOT adequate** for the specified overload condition in single-tyre fitment. The dual-rating of 3150 kg is also insufficient. **Recommendation:** Upgrade to LI 154/150 or use reinforced 'C' type tyres.

**(iv) Maximum Permissible Speed:**

Speed symbol **L** = **120 km/h** (per ETRTO standard).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing Units:** A frequent mistake is using rim diameter in inches while the section width is in mm without converting. **Always convert rim to mm** (×25.4) before using the diameter formula.
> 2. **Rolling vs Static Radius:** Using the geometric (unloaded) radius for speed calculations over-estimates speed by ~3.5%. The ECU uses rolling radius — be consistent.
> 3. **Forgetting Dynamic Load Transfer:** In load-index questions, students compute only static load and conclude "safe". A 5–7 mark question expects you to consider **braking inertia transfer (~70% to front) and cornering transfer**.
> 4. **LI Confusion:** "152/148" is a **dual-rated** index. 152 = single-tyre; 148 = dual-tyre. Examiners deduct 1 mark for not distinguishing.
> 5. **Forgetting Speed Symbol Mapping:** L = 120 km/h, not 100 km/h. Memorize: J=100, K=110, L=120, M=130, N=140, P=150, Q=160, R=170, S=180, T=190, U=200, H=210, V=240, W=270, Y=300.
> 6. **Section Height ≠ Section Width:** The aspect ratio is the *ratio*, not a value. AR=65 means height = 65% of width, NOT 65 mm.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Structure and Function of Tyres**

### Core Concepts
- ☑ A pneumatic tyre is the **sole contact point** between vehicle and road, transmitting all F_x, F_y, F_z forces.
- ☑ Six primary functions: Load support, traction, braking, cornering, shock absorption, dimensional stability.
- ☑ Modern construction has **8 layers**: Tread → Cap ply → Steel belts → Carcass → Sidewall → Bead apex → Bead bundle → Inner liner.

### Construction Details
- ☑ **Radial cords = 90°** to centreline; **Bias cords = 30–40°** alternating.
- ☑ Tubeless tyres use **halobutyl inner liner** + ribbed bead seal — no separate tube.
- ☑ Steel belts run at **±18°** for tread rigidity and puncture resistance.
- ☑ Cap ply (nylon) is essential for **high-speed stability** beyond 200 km/h.

### Tyre Designation (P 215/65 R 15 95 H)
- ☑ Format: **Type / Width(mm) / AR(%) / Construction / Rim(inch) / LI / Speed Symbol**.
- ☑ Section height $H = \frac{AR}{100} \times W$.
- ☑ Overall diameter $D_o = 2H + D_r$ (with D_r in same units).
- ☑ 1 inch = **25.4 mm** (memorize this conversion).

### High-Yield Formulas
- ☑ Rolling radius $R_r \approx 0.965 \times \frac{D_o}{2}$.
- ☑ Rev/km $N = \frac{1000}{C_r}$ where $C_r$ is in metres.
- ☑ Vehicle speed $V = C_r \times N_{rpm} \times 0.06$ km/h.
- ☑ Load index 95 = 690 kg, 100 = 800 kg, 110 = 1060 kg (memorize 3–4 reference values).

### Speed Symbol Quick Reference
- ☑ L = 120, M = 130, N = 140, P = 150, Q = 160, R = 170, S = 180, T = 190, H = 210, V = 240, W = 270, Y = 300 km/h.

### Real-World Significance
- ☑ Wrong tyre size → speedometer error (typically 3–8%).
- ☑ Radial tyres reduce fuel consumption by 5–8% vs bias-ply.
- ☑ Contact patch is only ~200 cm² — yet supports over 1 tonne per tyre.
- ☑ 70% of braking load transfers to the front wheels under hard braking (affects tyre choice).
- ☑ TWI (Tread Wear Indicator) bars appear at **1.6 mm** — the legal wear limit.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- ☑ "Decode the marking…" → Use the 7-element table.
- ☑ "Calculate overall diameter…" → Use $D_o = 2H + D_r$ with unit conversion.
- ☑ "Compare radial vs bias…" → Use 8-row comparison matrix (Section 3.5).
- ☑ "Is the load index safe?" → Always include dynamic transfer analysis.
- ☑ "List functions of a tyre" → 6 functions, 0.5 mark each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
