topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Classification" in Automotive Chassis?

In the context of **PCAUT303 - Automotive Chassis (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Classification"** refers to the systematic grouping of various chassis sub-systems (steering, suspension, brakes, axles, wheels & tyres) based on their **constructional features, operating principles, energy source, geometric arrangement, and the type of motion they permit or restrict**.

Classification is not a mere listing — it is an **engineering taxonomy** that helps a designer, service engineer, or examiner understand the **functional hierarchy** and the **trade-offs** (cost, weight, complexity, NVH, safety) between different sub-systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Vocabulary)**
> *Classification is the orderly arrangement of automotive chassis components into distinct categories based on their functional role, energy conversion mechanism, structural layout, and the relative motion they permit between the vehicle body and the road surface.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body"

Think of an automobile chassis the way a doctor thinks about a human body:

| Human Body | Automobile Equivalent | Classification Done By |
|---|---|---|
| Skeleton | Frame / Chassis | Type of frame (ladder, monocoque, space) |
| Muscles | Suspension system | Independent / Rigid / Semi-rigid |
| Hands turning the wheel | Steering system | Manual / Power / Steer-by-wire |
| Foot on the brake pedal | Braking system | Mechanical / Hydraulic / Electric |
| Foot bones | Axles | Live / Dead / Stub |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember Module-4 sub-systems (used by KTU toppers):** **"S-S-B-A-W"** → **S**teering, **S**uspension, **B**rakes, **A**xles, **W**heels & Tyres. Every component in Module-4 falls under one of these five pillars, and the *classification* of each pillar forms the foundation of this module.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters (Engineering Perspective)

1. **Design Selection** – A SUV needs a *non-independent rigid axle* for off-road durability; a sports car needs an *independent double-wishbone* for precise handling. Classification guides this choice.
2. **Service & Diagnostics** – A workshop technician identifies the *type* of brake (disc vs. drum) before ordering spare parts.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** – AIS / ECE / FMVSS standards apply differently to *power-assisted* vs. *manual* steering.
4. **Academic Rigor** – KTU board questions on "Classify…" are worth **3 to 14 marks** and test whether the student understands the **principle of grouping**, not just rote listing.

---

## 1.4 Master Classification Tree (Module-4)

The overarching classification of the **automotive chassis sub-systems** covered in Module 4 is presented below in a single, exam-ready tree:

```text
AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS SUB-SYSTEMS (Module-4 PCAUT303)
│
├── 1. STEERING SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Energy Source → Manual | Power-Assisted (Hydraulic / Electric / Electro-Hydraulic)
│     ├── (b) By Geometry     → Ackermann | Davis | Mar-Ack (Anti-Ackermann)
│     ├── (c) By Construction → Rack & Pinion | Recirculating Ball | Worm & Sector | Cam & Lever
│     ├── (d) By Axle Position → Front | Rear | Four-Wheel
│     └── (e) By Drive Type   → Mechanical | Hydraulic | Electric | Steer-by-Wire
│
├── 2. SUSPENSION SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Structural Linkage → Independent | Non-Independent (Rigid) | Semi-Independent
│     ├── (b) By Spring Medium     → Coil | Leaf | Torsion Bar | Air | Rubber | Hydraulic
│     ├── (c) By Damping Element   → Hydraulic | Gas-Hydraulic | Magneto-Rheological | Friction
│     └── (d) By Active Control    → Passive | Semi-Active | Active | Adaptive
│
├── 3. BRAKING SYSTEM
│     ├── (a) By Energy Source   → Mechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric | Hybrid (Regen)
│     ├── (b) By Construction    → Drum (Internal Expanding Shoe) | Disc (Caliper-Actuated Pad)
│     ├── (c) By Application    → Service | Secondary | Parking | Emergency / Hill-Hold
│     └── (d) By Control Logic  → Single-Circuit | Dual-Circuit (X / H / LL / TT split)
│
├── 4. AXLES
│     ├── (a) By Function         → Front (Steering) | Rear (Drive) | Stub
│     ├── (b) By Load Transfer    → Live (Full-Floating) | Three-Quarter Floating | Semi-Floating
│     └── (c) By Drive           → Driven | Non-Driven (Dead)
│
└── 5. WHEELS & TYRES
      ├── (a) By Wheel Type      → Disc (Pressed Steel / Cast Alloy) | Wire-Spoke | Forged
      ├── (b) By Tyre Construction → Diagonal (Bias) | Radial | Belted-Bias | Tubeless | Tubed
      └── (c) By Tread Pattern   → Rib | Lug | Block | Rib-Lug | Directional | Asymmetric
```

This tree is the **single most important diagram** for Module-4 classification questions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question says *"Classify the steering system with neat sketches"*, examiners award **2 marks** for the correct **principle of classification** (e.g., "by construction") and **2 marks** each for explaining the **sub-types** with diagrams. A 14-mark answer must cover at least **3 principles** (energy, geometry, construction) with labelled sketches.

---

## 1.5 Symbol Conventions Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Sprung mass per wheel | kg |
| $k$ | Spring stiffness | N/m |
| $c$ | Damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | rad/s |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| $F_s$ | Steering effort | N |
| $r$ | Steering-arm effective radius | m |
| $P_b$ | Brake-line hydraulic pressure | Pa |
| $\mu$ | Tyre–road friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| $T_b$ | Brake torque | N·m |

These symbols will be referenced in the formula sheet of Section 2.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section deconstructs each classification branch, then provides the **cheat-sheet table** that KTU examiners expect students to memorise.

---

## 2.1 Classification of Steering System

### 2.1.1 By Construction (Mechanical Gear Type)

The mechanical gearbox multiplies and redirects the driver's torque from the steering column to the pitman arm.

| Sub-Type | Key Feature | Typical Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Rack & Pinion** | Linear toothed rack engaged by a circular pinion; direct conversion of rotary → linear motion. | Hatchbacks, sedans, most modern cars. |
| **Recirculating Ball** | Ball-bearing nut runs on a worm; converts rotary → rotary through a sector shaft; large gear reduction. | Heavy trucks, commercial vehicles, older SUVs. |
| **Worm & Sector** | Simple worm meshes with a toothed sector on the pitman shaft. | Light commercial vehicles, vintage cars. |
| **Cam & Lever (Ross)** | Cam-shaped worm with lever-type follower; compact. | Older passenger cars (now obsolete). |

### 2.1.2 By Energy Source

```text
Power-Assisted Steering
├── Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS) – engine-driven pump, rotary valve
├── Electro-Hydraulic (EHPS) – electric motor drives hydraulic pump (BMW, Citroën)
├── Electric Power Steering (EPS) – electric motor directly on column/rack
│     ├── Column-Assist EPS (C-EPS)
│     ├── Pinion-Assist EPS (P-EPS)
│     └── Rack-Assist EPS (R-EPS)
└── Steer-by-Wire (SBW) – no mechanical link, X-by-wire technology
```

### 2.1.3 By Steering Geometry (Ackermann vs. Davis vs. Mar-Ack)

The **Ackermann condition** ensures that during a turn, all wheels rotate about a **single instantaneous centre (IC)** to avoid tyre scrub.

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$$

where:
- $\delta_o$ = steering angle of the outer wheel (rad)
- $\delta_i$ = steering angle of the inner wheel (rad)
- $w$ = wheelbase — **wait, $w$ is track-width** (m)
- $L$ = wheelbase (m)

> **Note the corrected symbols:** $w$ (track-width) is used in the Ackermann equation, not the wheelbase. This is a classic KTU trap.

**Davis steering** uses a *trapezoidal* linkage giving slightly different angles, and **Mar-Ackermann** (Anti-Ackermann) is used in **racing** where the outer wheel needs *more* angle than Ackermann to maintain high lateral grip.

### 2.1.4 By Axle Position

| Position | Application |
|---|---|
| Front-axle steering | Universal — almost all road vehicles |
| Rear-axle steering | Active rear-steer (BMW, Porsche, long-wheelbase trucks) |
| Four-wheel steering (4WS) | Crane trucks, heavy off-roaders, luxury sedans |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Suspension System

### 2.2.1 By Structural Linkage

1. **Independent Suspension** – motion of one wheel does NOT affect the other on the same axle.
   - Double-Wishbone (Short-Long Arm, SLA)
   - MacPherson Strut
   - Multi-Link
   - Semi-Trailing Arm
   - Swing-Axle (rare)

2. **Non-Independent (Rigid / Live Axle)** – both wheels are connected by a single beam.
   - Hotchkiss Drive Axle
   - Torque-Tube Drive Axle

3. **Semi-Independent** – connected by a torsion beam.
   - Twist-Beam Rear Axle (VW, Skoda)

### 2.2.2 By Spring Medium

| Spring Type | Energy Storage Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Coil spring | Helical wound wire, $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | Front suspension (most cars) |
| Leaf spring | Cantilever beam in bending | Rear of commercial vehicles, SUVs |
| Torsion bar | Twist of a bar, $\theta = \dfrac{32TL}{\pi d^4 G}$ | Older cars, some off-roaders |
| Air spring | Compressed air in bellows, $pV = nRT$ | Heavy CVs, luxury cars (Airmatic) |
| Rubber spring | Compressive elasticity | Secondary springs, engine mounts |

### 2.2.3 By Active Control (Increasingly Important for KTU 2024)

| Class | Energy Input | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Passive | None | Conventional dampers |
| Semi-Active | Controlled (skyhook logic) | Continuous Damping Control (CDC), Bilstein |
| Active | Continuous external force | Active Body Control (ABC), Mercedes |
| Adaptive | Mode-switching (Comfort / Sport) | Audi Drive Select, MagneRide (MR) |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Braking System

### 2.3.1 By Construction

| Brake Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drum brake | Internal expanding shoes press against a cylindrical drum | Low cost, good parking brake integration | Heat fade, complex self-energising geometry |
| Disc brake | Caliper squeezes pads against a rotating disc | Excellent heat dissipation, self-cleaning, linear response | Higher cost, prone to squeal |

### 2.3.2 By Energy Source

- **Mechanical** (cable/rod, mostly for parking brakes)
- **Hydraulic** (master cylinder → wheel cylinder — universal for service brakes)
- **Pneumatic** (air brakes, heavy CVs, buses)
- **Electric** (regenerative EV braking)
- **Electromechanical** (brake-by-wire, e.g., Bosch iBooster)

### 2.3.3 By Circuit Configuration (Critical for Safety)

```text
Dual-Circuit Brake Configurations
├── H-Split  – front and rear circuits separated (most common passenger car)
├── X-Split  – diagonally opposite wheels (Audi, VW) – better balance under heavy braking
├── LL-Split – left front+rear vs. right front+rear (rare, asymmetric)
└── TT-Split – front pair vs. rear pair (some heavy CVs)
```

---

## 2.4 Classification of Axles

### 2.4.1 By Load-Carrying Capacity (Floating Methods)

| Type | Bearing Location | Max Load | Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Floating** | Hub bearings carry only weight; axle shaft transmits torque only | Very high | Trucks, buses |
| **Three-Quarter Floating** | Hub bearing + axle shaft partially share load | High | Medium trucks |
| **Semi-Floating** | Axle shaft carries both torque and the wheel load | Moderate | Passenger cars |
| **Stub axle** | Kingpin-carried, used in front steering | Low | Almost all light vehicles |

---

## 2.5 Classification of Wheels and Tyres

### 2.5.1 Tyre Construction

| Type | Ply Orientation | Heat Dissipation | Rolling Resistance | Today |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diagonal (Bias) | 30–40° alternating | Low | High | Obsolete (2/3-wheelers) |
| Radial | 90° to centreline | High | Low | Universal |
| Belted-bias | Bias + radial belts | Medium | Medium | Niche |
| Tubeless | Inner liner of halobutyl | Excellent | Low | Universal in cars |

### 2.5.2 Tread Patterns

- **Rib** – circumferential grooves, used on steering axle of trucks.
- **Lug** – large blocks, traction (drive axle).
- **Block** – symmetric blocks, off-road (M/T tyres).
- **Rib-Lug** – combination, all-position.
- **Directional** – V-pattern, high-speed wet grip.
- **Asymmetric** – different inboard/outboard tread, premium UHP tyres.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is exam gold.** Memorise the equations, the symbols, and the units. KTU questions on this topic frequently ask *"Derive the condition for Ackermann steering"* or *"Show that the natural frequency of a sprung mass is…".*

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$ | $\delta_o, \delta_i$ in rad; $w$ = track-width (m); $L$ = wheelbase (m) | Ackermann steering |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\delta_i}{\delta_o} = \dfrac{L + w \tan \delta_o}{L - w \tan \delta_o}$ | Same as above | Davis steering approximation |
| 3 | $T_s = F_s \cdot r = \dfrac{P_b \cdot A_b \cdot \eta_b \cdot r}{i_s}$ | $T_s$ steering torque (N·m); $F_s$ effort (N); $r$ arm (m); $P_b$ pressure (Pa); $A_b$ piston area (m²); $\eta_b$ efficiency; $i_s$ gear ratio | Power steering |
| 4 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\omega_n$ rad/s; $k$ N/m; $m$ kg | Suspension natural frequency |
| 5 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{km}}$ | $\zeta$ dimensionless; $c$ N·s/m | Damping ratio |
| 6 | $T_b = 2 \mu F_n r_{eff}$ | $T_b$ brake torque (N·m); $\mu$ friction coeff.; $F_n$ normal force (N); $r_{eff}$ effective radius (m) | Drum & disc brake |
| 7 | $F_b = P_b \cdot A_w$ | $F_b$ brake clamp force (N); $P_b$ line pressure (Pa); $A_w$ wheel-cylinder piston area (m²) | Hydraulic brake |
| 8 | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ | $a_{max}$ m/s²; $\mu$ friction coeff.; $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Maximum deceleration |
| 9 | $P_{air} V_{res} = \text{const}$ (isothermal) | $P_{air}$ Pa; $V_{res}$ m³ | Air-spring |
| 10 | $M_f = \dfrac{w \cdot L}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot h_{cg}$ | $M_f$ friction-induced moment (N·m); $h_{cg}$ CoG height (m) | Brake bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Pitfall:** The Ackermann equation uses **track-width $w$**, not wheelbase $L$. Students frequently swap them. Always check the diagram: $w$ is the *horizontal distance between the two king-pins*; $L$ is the *longitudinal distance between front and rear axles*.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Classification

1. **Vehicle Homologation (AIS-008, AIS-009)** – Indian regulations group vehicles by *brake-circuit type* and *steering-energy source*. The classification tree decides which tests apply.
2. **Servo-Mechanism Design** – Power-steering control algorithms depend on whether the assist is *hydraulic* (rotary-valve model) or *electric* (motor-current model).
3. **Crash Safety (NCAP)** – Disc vs. drum classification drives fade-resistance and pedal-feel decisions.
4. **NVH Tuning** – Independent vs. non-independent suspension classification changes unsprung-mass and wheel-hop behaviour.
5. **EV Transition** – *Regenerative* (electric) braking classification is the new frontier; understanding it is critical for KTU 2024 syllabi.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **heart of the 14-mark KTU questions**. Three cornerstone derivations are worked out in full, with **every algebraic step shown**. No shortcuts, no skips.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Ackermann Steering Condition

**Goal:** To prove that for all four wheels to rotate about a single instantaneous centre without tyre scrub,

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$$

### 3.1.1 Setup (Refer to the diagram in Section 4)

Consider a vehicle turning to the left at low speed. Let:

- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- $w$ = track-width (distance between the two king-pins / steering pivots)
- $R$ = radius of the turn measured to the midpoint of the rear axle
- $R_o$ = radius for the outer (right) wheel
- $R_i$ = radius for the inner (left) wheel
- $\delta_o$ = steering angle of the outer wheel
- $\delta_i$ = steering angle of the inner wheel

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** From the geometry, the inner wheel is closer to the turn centre. So:

$$R_i = R - \dfrac{w}{2}, \qquad R_o = R + \dfrac{w}{2}$$

**Step 2:** For the vehicle to take a pure-rolling turn, each wheel's velocity vector must be perpendicular to the line joining that wheel to the turn centre. Hence:

$$\tan \delta_i = \dfrac{L}{R_i}, \qquad \tan \delta_o = \dfrac{L}{R_o}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the difference of the cotangents. Recall that $\cot x = \dfrac{1}{\tan x}$. So:

$$\cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_i}{L}, \qquad \cot \delta_o = \dfrac{R_o}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $R_o = R + \tfrac{w}{2}$ and $R_i = R - \tfrac{w}{2}$:

$$R_o - R_i = \left(R + \dfrac{w}{2}\right) - \left(R - \dfrac{w}{2}\right) = w$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the **Ackermann condition**.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Illustration

A car has wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and track-width $w = 1.5$ m. If the outer wheel is steered by $\delta_o = 20°$, find $\delta_i$.

$$\cot \delta_i = \cot 20° - \dfrac{1.5}{2.5} = 2.7475 - 0.6 = 2.1475$$

$$\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(2.1475) = 24.97° \approx 25°$$

**Interpretation:** The inner wheel must steer *more* than the outer wheel — counter-intuitive but geometrically necessary.

### 3.1.4 KTU Mark-Split for This Derivation

| Step | Marks Awarded |
|---|---|
| Free-body / geometry diagram | 2 |
| Step 1: defining $R_i, R_o$ | 1 |
| Step 2: tangents of steering angles | 1 |
| Step 3: cotangent conversion | 1 |
| Step 4: subtraction | 1 |
| Step 5: substitution | 1 |
| Step 6: final equation | 1 |
| Numerical example | 2 |
| **Total** | **10** (part of 14) |

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Natural Frequency of Sprung Mass on Suspension

**Goal:** Derive $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ and the damped response.

### 3.2.1 Mathematical Model

A single wheel with **sprung mass** $m$ is supported by a spring (stiffness $k$) and a damper (coefficient $c$). The road input is $x(t)$ and the sprung-mass displacement is $y(t)$. The relative displacement is $z = y - x$.

### 3.2.2 Equation of Motion

Newton's second law on the sprung mass:

$$m \ddot{y} + c(\dot{y} - \dot{x}) + k(y - x) = 0$$

Define $z = y - x$. Then $\ddot{y} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{x}$ and $\dot{y} = \dot{z} + \dot{x}$. Substituting:

$$m(\ddot{z} + \ddot{x}) + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{x}$$

### 3.2.3 Free Vibration Case

For free vibration, set $\ddot{x} = 0$:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = 0$$

### 3.2.4 Standard-Form Conversion

Divide throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{z} + \dfrac{c}{m} \dot{z} + \dfrac{k}{m} z = 0$$

Compare with the standard second-order form:

$$\ddot{z} + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = 0$$

Equating coefficients:

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \dfrac{c}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{km}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2.5 Three Damping Cases

| Case | Condition | Response |
|---|---|---|
| Under-damped | $\zeta < 1$ | Decaying sinusoid: $z(t) = A e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$, where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |
| Over-damped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return |

### 3.2.6 Numerical Illustration

For a passenger car: $m = 350$ kg per corner, $k = 25\,000$ N/m.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{25\,000}{350}} = \sqrt{71.43} = 8.45 \text{ rad/s} = \dfrac{8.45}{2\pi} \approx 1.345 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the **typical 1.0–1.5 Hz ride frequency** that luxury car manufacturers target. Above 1.5 Hz the human body perceives vibration; below 1.0 Hz causes motion sickness.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Braking Force and Maximum Deceleration

**Goal:** Show that the maximum theoretical deceleration is $a_{max} = \mu g$.

### 3.3.1 Free-Body Diagram

For a vehicle of total weight $W = mg$ on a flat road with friction coefficient $\mu$ between tyre and road, the maximum braking force is:

$$F_{b,max} = \mu W = \mu m g$$

### 3.3.2 Application of Newton's Second Law

$$F_{b,max} = m a_{max}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\mu m g = m a_{max}$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$a_{max} = \mu g \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Illustration

For dry asphalt, $\mu \approx 0.9$:

$$a_{max} = 0.9 \times 9.81 = 8.83 \text{ m/s}^2 = 0.9 g$$

The stopping distance from $v_0$ is:

$$s = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

For $v_0 = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s:

$$s = \dfrac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 0.9 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{771.6}{17.66} = 43.7 \text{ m}$$

This is the **standard ABS-equipped braking distance** quoted in owner manuals.

### 3.3.4 KTU Board Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Favourite Sub-Question:** *"A vehicle of mass 1500 kg is braked from 90 km/h to rest over 45 m. Calculate the average coefficient of friction."*
> Solution: $v_0^2 = 25^2 = 625$ m²/s², $2 a s = v_0^2 \Rightarrow a = 625 / (2 \times 45) = 6.94$ m/s², hence $\mu = a/g = 6.94 / 9.81 = 0.71$ — typical wet-road value.

---

## 3.4 Master Analytical Matrix (Quick-Reference)

| Sub-Topic | Governing Equation | Limiting Case | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ackermann steering | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ | At very small $\delta_o$, $\delta_i \approx \delta_o + w/L$ (linear) | 7 marks |
| Suspension natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Frequency of human body 4–8 Hz, so $k/m$ tuned to 1.3 Hz | 7 marks |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | $\zeta = 0.25$–$0.4$ typical for ride comfort | 3 marks |
| Brake torque | $T_b = 2 \mu F_n r_{eff}$ | Self-energising shoes: $\mu \to 2\mu$ | 5 marks |
| Max deceleration | $a_{max} = \mu g$ | ABS threshold just below $\mu$ peak | 3 marks |
| Spring energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | At $\zeta = 1$, $E_{dis}$ per cycle is maximum | 2 marks |

## 4.5 Brake Self-Energising Geometry (Drum Brake)

```mermaid
graph LR
    shoeL[PRIMARY SHOE]
    shoeR[SECONDARY SHOE]
    drum[BRAKE DRUM]
    pivot1[ANCHOR PIN]
    act[HYDRAULIC WHEEL CYLINDER FORCE F]

    shoeL --> drum
    shoeR --> drum
    shoeL -.-> pivot1
    shoeR -.-> pivot1
    act --> shoeL
    act --> shoeR
```

This block diagram represents the **duo-servo drum brake** where the secondary shoe *self-energises* because of friction-induced rotation of the drum, increasing its effective $\mu$ by a factor of $1 + f \tan\alpha$ where $f$ is the friction coefficient and $\alpha$ is the shoe-rotation half-angle.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is structured exactly like a **KTU University End-Semester Examination paper** for Module-4 of PCAUT303. Solve the questions, then compare with the model answers.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify the steering system based on the energy source used. Mention at least four sub-types.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The steering system can be classified based on the **energy source** as follows:

1. **Manual Steering** — Pure mechanical, driver provides all the torque. Used in light commercial vehicles.
2. **Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS)** — Engine-driven pump supplies pressurised fluid to a rotary valve. Common in older SUVs and trucks.
3. **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** — An electric motor assists the column, pinion, or rack directly. Used in most modern passenger cars (Maruti, Hyundai).
4. **Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering (EHPS)** — Electric motor drives the hydraulic pump. Used in some heavy vehicles (BMW, Citroën).
5. **Steer-by-Wire (SBW)** — No mechanical link; electronic control of actuators. Used in concept and premium vehicles (Infiniti Q50).

*(1 mark for naming the four sub-types, 2 marks for one-line description of each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the difference between Ackermann and Anti-Ackermann steering? When is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ackermann | Anti-Ackermann (Mar-Ack) |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner wheel angle vs. outer** | $\delta_i > \delta_o$ (per formula) | $\delta_i < \delta_o$ |
| **Geometric condition** | $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ | $\delta_o > \delta_i$ (opposite inequality) |
| **Application** | Standard road vehicles, low-speed turning | High-performance racing, where the outer tyre must develop more slip angle for high lateral grip |
| **Tyre wear** | Uniform | Non-uniform (acceptable in racing) |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for application.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### CHOICE QUESTION A (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **Ackermann steering geometry condition**. Show that for a small steering angle the inner wheel deflects more than the outer wheel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A passenger car has the following data:
- Wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m
- Track-width $w = 1.55$ m
- Outer wheel steering angle $\delta_o = 18°$

Calculate: (i) the inner wheel steering angle $\delta_i$ for **pure rolling** (Ackermann condition), and (ii) the **turning radius** of the vehicle. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Below is the marking-scheme-aligned version:*

1. **Diagram** (2 marks): Draw the vehicle plan-view showing rear axle, front axle, king-pins, instantaneous centre $IC$ on the rear-axle side, lines from $IC$ to the four wheel-centres, and the steering angles $\delta_i, \delta_o$.

2. **Geometry setup** (2 marks): State $R_i = R - w/2$, $R_o = R + w/2$, and $\tan \delta_i = L/R_i$, $\tan \delta_o = L/R_o$.

3. **Derivation** (3 marks): 
   $$\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{L} = \dfrac{w}{L}$$
4. **Small-angle inference** (1 mark): For small $\delta_o$, $\cot \delta_o \approx 1/\delta_o$ (rad). Differentiating the Ackermann equation gives $d\delta_i/d\delta_o > 1$, hence $\delta_i > \delta_o$.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Inner wheel steering angle:**

Using the Ackermann condition:

$$\cot \delta_i = \cot \delta_o - \dfrac{w}{L}$$

$$\cot 18° = \cot 18° = 3.0777$$

$$\cot \delta_i = 3.0777 - \dfrac{1.55}{2.6} = 3.0777 - 0.5962 = 2.4815$$

$$\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(2.4815) = 21.96° \approx 22°$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\delta_i \approx 22°}$

**(ii) Turning radius:**

From $\tan \delta_o = L/R_o$:

$$R_o = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta_o} = \dfrac{2.6}{\tan 18°} = \dfrac{2.6}{0.3249} = 8.00 \text{ m}$$

The radius to the **midpoint of the rear axle** is:

$$R = R_o - \dfrac{w}{2} = 8.00 - 0.775 = 7.225 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{R \approx 7.23 \text{ m}}$

**Verification:** $R_i = 7.225 - 0.775 = 6.45$ m, $L/R_i = 2.6/6.45 = 0.403$, $\tan^{-1}(0.403) = 21.96°$ — matches. ✓

*(1 mark: writing formula, 2 marks: numerical substitution, 1 mark: correct arithmetic, 1 mark: verification, 2 marks: turning-radius calculation.)*

---

### CHOICE QUESTION B (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify the **braking system** based on (i) construction, (ii) energy source, and (iii) application. With a neat sketch explain the working of a **hydraulic drum brake** and show the expression for the **self-energising factor**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The hydraulic brake system of a 1500 kg car uses a master cylinder of 25 mm diameter and wheel cylinders of 32 mm diameter. The driver applies a pedal force of 200 N with a pedal-lever ratio of 5:1. The overall brake efficiency is 80 %. Calculate:
   (i) The hydraulic line pressure. *(3 marks)*
   (ii) The clamping force on each brake pad. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) The maximum theoretical deceleration if the road friction coefficient is 0.7. *(2 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Principle | Sub-Types |
|---|---|
| (i) Construction | Drum brake (internal expanding shoe) – Disc brake (caliper-actuated pad) |
| (ii) Energy Source | Mechanical – Hydraulic – Pneumatic – Electric (regenerative) – Electromechanical |
| (iii) Application | Service brake – Secondary brake – Parking brake – Emergency / Hill-hold brake |

**Hydraulic Drum Brake Working (3 marks):**

When the brake pedal is pressed, the master cylinder converts mechanical pedal force into hydraulic pressure. This pressure is transmitted equally through the brake-fluid to all wheel cylinders. The wheel cylinder pushes the brake shoes outward, which press the friction lining against the inner surface of the rotating drum. The friction force generates a braking torque opposing drum rotation, decelerating the vehicle.

**Self-Energising Factor (1 mark):**

For a duo-servo drum brake with shoe-rotation half-angle $\alpha$ and lining friction $\mu$:

$$C_{self} = \dfrac{1 + \mu \tan \alpha}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha}$$

When $\mu \tan \alpha \to 1$, $C_{self} \to \infty$ (servo runaway). Hence, lining friction is limited to $\mu \leq 0.4$ for stable operation.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** 
- Pedal force $F_p = 200$ N
- Pedal ratio $i_p = 5:1$
- Master cylinder diameter $d_m = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Wheel cylinder diameter $d_w = 32$ mm = 0.032 m
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.8$
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- $\mu = 0.7$

**(i) Hydraulic line pressure (3 marks):**

Force on master cylinder:
$$F_m = F_p \times i_p \times \eta = 200 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 800 \text{ N}$$

Master cylinder area:
$$A_m = \dfrac{\pi d_m^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Line pressure:
$$P_b = \dfrac{F_m}{A_m} = \dfrac{800}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.63 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.63 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_b \approx 1.63 \text{ MPa}}$ (typical 1.5–2.0 MPa is normal for light vehicles.)

**(ii) Clamping force per pad (2 marks):**

Wheel cylinder area:
$$A_w = \dfrac{\pi d_w^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.032)^2}{4} = 8.042 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Clamping force:
$$F_b = P_b \times A_w = 1.63 \times 10^6 \times 8.042 \times 10^{-4} = 1311 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{F_b \approx 1311 \text{ N per pad}}$

**(iii) Maximum deceleration (2 marks):**

$$a_{max} = \mu g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.7g$$

**Result:** $\boxed{a_{max} \approx 6.87 \text{ m/s}^2}$

**Brake force check:** Total brake force = 4 × 1311 = 5244 N, deceleration = 5244/1500 = 3.5 m/s² < $\mu g$ = 6.87 m/s². So brakes are *not* lock-limited; the driver is *pedal-limited*. Increasing pedal force would yield higher deceleration up to 0.7g.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Ackermann symbol confusion** (–2 marks): Students mix up $w$ (track-width) and $L$ (wheelbase). Always draw the diagram first and label the symbols.
> 2. **Units of angles** (–1 mark): Always convert degrees → radians when using the *small-angle* approximation ($\tan \delta \approx \delta$, $\cot \delta \approx 1/\delta$). KTU evaluators deduct if angles remain in degrees within a $\tan$ or $\cot$ expression.
> 3. **Diameter vs. radius in cylinder areas** (–2 marks): The piston-area formula is $A = \pi d^2/4$, **not** $\pi r^2/2$ (same numerically, but the formula written incorrectly is treated as an error in strict marking).
> 4. **Self-energising factor formula** (–1 mark): Some students write $1 + \mu \tan\alpha$ without the denominator; the duo-servo has *both* numerator and denominator.
> 5. **Rounding** (–0.5 mark per question): Always quote answers to **3 significant figures**; do not round intermediate steps.

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall** — it condenses the entire Module-4 classification topic.

### A. Steering System
- ☐ Classify by **construction**: Rack-Pinion / Recirculating-Ball / Worm-Sector / Cam-Lever.
- ☐ Classify by **energy**: Manual / Hydraulic / Electric / Electro-Hydraulic / Steer-by-Wire.
- ☐ Classify by **geometry**: Ackermann / Davis / Mar-Ackermann (anti-Ackermann).
- ☐ **Ackermann formula:** $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = w/L$ (remember: $w$ is track-width, $L$ is wheelbase).
- ☐ **Inner wheel always turns more** than outer wheel under Ackermann.
- ☐ **Steering ratio** $i_s$ = steering-wheel turns / road-wheel turns.

### B. Suspension System
- ☐ **Independent** vs. **Non-Independent** vs. **Semi-Independent** (twist-beam).
- ☐ Independent types: Double-Wishbone, MacPherson Strut, Multi-Link, Trailing Arm.
- ☐ Spring mediums: Coil, Leaf, Torsion Bar, Air, Rubber.
- ☐ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; passenger cars tuned to 1.0–1.5 Hz.
- ☐ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$; ride comfort target $\zeta = 0.25$–$0.4$.
- ☐ **Active / Semi-Active / Adaptive** suspension classification.

### C. Braking System
- ☐ Drum vs. Disc construction.
- ☐ Energy sources: Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric, Electromechanical.
- ☐ **Brake circuits:** Single, H-Split, X-Split, LL-Split, TT-Split.
- ☐ **Master cylinder force → line pressure** → **wheel cylinder force → friction torque**.
- ☐ **Self-energising factor** for duo-servo drum: $C_{self} = (1 + \mu \tan\alpha)/(1 - \mu \tan\alpha)$.
- ☐ **Max deceleration:** $a_{max} = \mu g$.
- ☐ **Stopping distance:** $s = v_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$.

### D. Axles
- ☐ **Full-floating** for trucks, **semi-floating** for cars, **stub** for front-steering.
- ☐ Live axle (drive) vs. Dead axle (non-driven).

### E. Wheels & Tyres
- ☐ Disc (pressed-steel, cast-alloy, forged) vs. wire-spoke.
- ☐ **Bias (diagonal)** vs. **radial** tyre construction.
- ☐ **Tubeless** is universal in modern cars.
- ☐ Tread patterns: Rib, Lug, Block, Rib-Lug, Directional, Asymmetric.

### F. Universal Constants to Memorise
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ☐ Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa (for air-spring questions)
- ☐ Standard tyre pressure: 30–35 psi (≈ 200–240 kPa)

### G. Conversion Quick-Table (Save in Your Brain)
| Quantity | Conversion |
|---|---|
| km/h → m/s | Multiply by $5/18$ |
| psi → kPa | Multiply by 6.895 |
| mm → m | Divide by 1000 |
| ft-lb → N·m | Multiply by 1.356 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 14-Mark Strategy:**
> 1. Read the question twice.
> 2. Draw the diagram first (it carries 2–3 marks even before calculation).
> 3. State the governing equation *with all symbols defined*.
> 4. Substitute, simplify, verify units, then compute.
> 5. Provide a one-sentence engineering interpretation of the result.

Best of luck with your **PCAUT303 – Automotive Chassis** examination! 🚗💨
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Properties of Pneumatic Tyres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Properties of Pneumatic Tyres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pneumatic Tyre?

A **pneumatic tyre** is a toroidal, flexible, air-filled structural component made of rubber compounds, fabric plies, steel belts, and bead wires that transfers the entire vehicle load to the road surface while providing traction, cushioning, steering control, and damping of road-induced vibrations. The term *pneumatic* (from Greek *pneuma* = air/wind) specifically refers to the pressurized air inside the carcass that bears the load.

In the context of **KTU Module 4**, we study the tyre from two perspectives:

- **Static Properties** — Behaviour when the vehicle is stationary or moving at constant velocity (load-deflection, contact patch geometry, dimensional standards).
- **Dynamic Properties** — Behaviour when forces, slips, and accelerations are actively varying (slip angle, slip ratio, cornering force, aligning torque, relaxation length).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to memorize verbatim:**
> A pneumatic tyre is the only component of an automobile that is in contact with the road; therefore, its properties directly govern vehicle handling, ride comfort, braking performance, and directional stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Tyre as a Springy Foot

Imagine you are walking on a slippery floor wearing soft rubber shoes filled with a thin air cushion. The shoe:

1. **Squashes and spreads** when you step down (this is the *static load-deflection*).
2. **Bends sideways** when you walk at an angle, generating a side force to push you forward (this is *cornering force due to slip angle*).
3. **Takes time to react** when you suddenly change direction — it has to "catch up" by twisting through its length (this is *relaxation length*).
4. **Pushes back straight** when released, like a self-righting spring (this is *aligning torque*).

That rubber shoe is essentially what a pneumatic tyre does, only with much higher stiffness, precision, and a complex multi-layered construction. The pressurised air acts like a **silent, omnipresent spring** that stores and returns energy in all three directions: vertical (load), longitudinal (drive/brake), and lateral (cornering).

## 1.3 Basic Tyre Construction (Recap)

A modern radial-ply pneumatic tyre consists of:

- **Bead** — Steel wire bundle that anchors the tyre to the rim.
- **Sidewall** — Flexible rubber that flexes during cornering and bump.
- **Carcass (ply)** — Polyester/rayon/nylon fabric layers (radial in modern tyres) that bear the inflation pressure.
- **Belt** — Steel belts under the tread for stiffness and tread stability.
- **Tread** — Outer rubber with grooves for grip, water evacuation, and wear resistance.
- **Inner Liner** — Halogenated butyl rubber that retains air.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard dimensional notation: A tyre marked **205/55 R16** means:
> - **205** mm tread width
> - **55%** aspect ratio (sidewall height = 55% of width)
> - **R** = Radial construction
> - **16** inches rim diameter

## 1.4 Why Study Static AND Dynamic Properties?

| Property Type | When Studied | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Vehicle parked, slow manoeuvres, design stage | Determines load capacity, ride height, steering geometry, scrub radius |
| **Dynamic** | Driving, cornering, braking | Determines vehicle dynamics response, ABS tuning, ESP intervention, driver feel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Favourite:** Almost every vehicle dynamics question in the ESE will start with a slip-angle vs cornering-force graph, or a slip-ratio vs longitudinal-force graph. Master these two relationships first.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Properties of Pneumatic Tyres

### 2.1.1 Tyre Size & Dimensional Standards
The standard international sizing uses the **ISO metric** or **P-metric** system. For a tyre **B/AR R D**:

$$
B = \text{Tyre width (mm)}, \quad AR = \text{Aspect ratio} = \frac{H}{B} \times 100
$$

$$
H = \text{Sidewall height} = B \times \frac{AR}{100}
$$

$$
D_{od} = D + 2H = D + 2B \cdot \frac{AR}{100}
$$

Where $D$ is the rim diameter (inches × 25.4 to get mm), and $D_{od}$ is the overall diameter.

### 2.1.2 Load Carrying Capacity (Static)

The vertical load $F_z$ that a tyre can support is related to inflation pressure $P_i$ and contact patch area $A_c$ by the simple force balance:

$$
F_z = P_i \cdot A_c
$$

For a perfectly flexible, frictionless tyre (purely theoretical), the contact patch is a **perfect ellipse** with semi-axes $a$ (longitudinal) and $b$ (lateral):

$$
A_c = \pi \cdot a \cdot b
$$

In practice, real tyres exhibit a **flat-bottomed** (truncated) contact patch, and load is shared between inflation pressure and carcass stiffness.

### 2.1.3 Vertical Deflection (Static Spring Rate)

The static vertical spring rate $K_{tz}$ relates vertical load to vertical deflection $\delta_z$:

$$
F_z = K_{tz} \cdot \delta_z
$$

Typical passenger car values: **200–300 N/mm** per tyre.

### 2.1.4 Effective Rolling Radius ($R_e$)

The effective rolling radius is the ratio of linear velocity $V_x$ to angular velocity $\omega$ in pure rolling:

$$
R_e = \frac{V_x}{\omega}
$$

It is **always less than** the loaded radius $R_L$, because the tyre is compressed and the wheel rotates about an instantaneous centre slightly above the road.

### 2.1.5 Static Stiffness — Vertical, Lateral, Longitudinal

| Stiffness | Symbol | Typical Range (Passenger Car) | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vertical | $K_{tz}$ | 200–300 N/mm | Up-down |
| Lateral | $K_{ty}$ | 150–250 N/mm | Sideways |
| Longitudinal | $K_{tx}$ | 200–400 N/mm | Fore-aft |

## 2.2 Dynamic Properties of Pneumatic Tyres

### 2.2.1 Slip Angle ($\alpha$)

When a tyre is rolling and a sideways (lateral) force is applied, the wheel's heading deviates from its actual direction of travel by an angle $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \delta - \theta_v
$$

Where $\delta$ is the steering angle and $\theta_v$ is the vehicle heading angle relative to the wheel's plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A tyre cannot instantaneously generate a lateral force at zero slip angle.** Even at $\alpha = 0$, in pure rolling, lateral force is zero. The slip angle is the *fundamental cause* of cornering force.

### 2.2.2 Slip Ratio ($\kappa$)

When a tractive or braking torque is applied, the wheel's circumferential speed differs from the road speed. The slip ratio is defined as:

$$
\kappa = \frac{R_e \cdot \omega - V_x}{\max(R_e \cdot \omega,\, V_x)} \times 100\%
$$

- **Positive $\kappa$** → Traction (driving) — wheel spinning faster than road.
- **Negative $\kappa$** → Braking — wheel rotating slower than road.

### 2.2.3 Cornering Force ($F_y$)

The lateral force generated by a slip angle. The simplest linear model:

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha
$$

Where $C_\alpha$ is the **cornering stiffness** (N/rad). For a real, non-linear tyre, the empirical **Pacejka Magic Formula** describes the full S-curve:

$$
F_y = D \cdot \sin\!\left(C \cdot \arctan\!\left(B\,\alpha - E\,(B\,\alpha - \arctan(B\,\alpha))\right)\right)
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Peak cornering force (≈ $\mu \cdot F_z$)
- $C$ = Shape factor (controls the curvature at origin)
- $B$ = Stiffness factor (controls the initial slope $B \cdot C \cdot D$)
- $E$ = Curvature factor (controls the peak position)

### 2.2.4 Aligning Torque ($M_z$)

When a slip angle exists, the contact patch pressure distribution is asymmetric, producing a self-aligning moment about the steering axis:

$$
M_z = -C_{mk} \cdot \alpha
$$

$C_{mk}$ is the **aligning stiffness** (N·m/rad). The negative sign indicates the torque opposes the slip direction.

### 2.2.5 Pneumatic Trail ($t_p$)

The distance from the centre of the contact patch to the point where the resultant lateral force acts. Related to aligning torque by:

$$
t_p = -\frac{M_z}{F_y} = \frac{C_{mk}}{C_\alpha}
$$

Typical value: **30–60 mm** for a passenger car tyre.

### 2.2.6 Camber Thrust ($F_{yc}$)

A camber angle $\gamma$ (tilt of the wheel from vertical) generates a lateral force even at zero slip angle:

$$
F_{yc} = C_\gamma \cdot \gamma
$$

Where $C_\gamma$ is the **camber stiffness** (N/rad).

### 2.2.7 Relaxation Length ($\sigma_\alpha$, $\sigma_\kappa$)

When slip angle is suddenly applied, the cornering force does **not** appear instantly — it builds up exponentially along the contact length $L_p$:

$$
\frac{dF_y}{dx} = -\frac{F_y - F_{y,\text{ss}}}{\sigma_\alpha}
$$

Where $\sigma_\alpha$ is the **lateral relaxation length** (typical: 50–150 mm). In time domain, at constant slip angle, the force builds as:

$$
F_y(t) = F_{y,\text{ss}} \left(1 - e^{-V_x \cdot t / \sigma_\alpha}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering implication:** At very high speeds (above ~120 km/h), the relaxation lag means the driver must "anticipate" steering inputs; this is why modern EPS systems include active damping and preview control.

### 2.2.8 Friction Circle / Friction Ellipse

The maximum combined longitudinal and lateral force a tyre can transmit is limited by road friction:

$$
\left(\frac{F_x}{\mu F_z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{F_y}{\mu F_z}\right)^2 \le 1
$$

This is the **friction circle** (a true circle if $\mu_x = \mu_y$, an ellipse otherwise).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = (H/B) \times 100$ | % | $H$ = sidewall height, $B$ = width |
| 2 | Overall Diameter | $D_{od} = D + 2B(AR/100)$ | mm | $D$ in mm |
| 3 | Static Load | $F_z = P_i \cdot A_c$ | N | $P_i$ = inflation pressure, $A_c$ = contact area |
| 4 | Effective Rolling Radius | $R_e = V_x / \omega$ | m | Always $< R_L$ |
| 5 | Slip Ratio | $\kappa = (R_e \omega - V_x)/\max(R_e\omega, V_x)$ | – | $+ve$ traction, $-ve$ braking |
| 6 | Slip Angle | $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$ | rad | Steering minus heading |
| 7 | Cornering Force (linear) | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | N | $C_\alpha$ in N/rad |
| 8 | Cornering Force (Pacejka) | $F_y = D \sin(C \arctan(B\alpha - E(B\alpha - \arctan B\alpha)))$ | N | Full non-linear S-curve |
| 9 | Aligning Torque | $M_z = -C_{mk} \cdot \alpha$ | N·m | Opposes slip |
| 10 | Pneumatic Trail | $t_p = C_{mk} / C_\alpha$ | m | ~30–60 mm |
| 11 | Camber Thrust | $F_{yc} = C_\gamma \cdot \gamma$ | N | Force at zero slip |
| 12 | Relaxation (spatial) | $dF_y/dx = -(F_y - F_{y,ss})/\sigma_\alpha$ | N/m | $\sigma_\alpha$ ~ 50–150 mm |
| 13 | Friction Circle | $(F_x/\mu F_z)^2 + (F_y/\mu F_z)^2 \le 1$ | – | Combined grip limit |
| 14 | Vertical Spring Rate | $K_{tz} = F_z / \delta_z$ | N/mm | Static deflection slope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise equations 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 13** — these six formulas cover ~80% of KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Dynamics Simulation (Carsim, ADAMS, MATLAB/Simulink):** Every modern simulation uses the Pacejka Magic Formula to model tyre behaviour.
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS):** Uses the longitudinal-force vs slip-ratio curve to find the optimal slip for maximum braking.
- **Electronic Stability Program (ESP):** Compares driver steering input with measured yaw rate and applies differential braking based on cornering force and aligning torque.
- **Tyre Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS):** Use static load-deflection and inflation-pressure relationships to detect pressure loss.
- **Race Car Setup:** Engineers tune camber, pressure, and slip angle to maximise the friction ellipse utilisation in cornering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Rolling Radius from First Principles

Consider a free-rolling wheel with angular velocity $\omega$ and vehicle speed $V_x$. The instantaneous centre of rotation is at a height $h$ above the road, where the rubber enters and leaves the contact patch.

**Step 1:** The kinematic constraint for pure rolling (no slip) demands that the velocity of the wheel centre equals the forward vehicle velocity.

$$
V_c = V_x
$$

**Step 2:** The wheel centre is at a height equal to the loaded radius $R_L$ above the ground.

$$
h_{\text{centre}} = R_L
$$

**Step 3:** The tangential velocity of the wheel at the contact patch (perceived as the rotation of the wheel about its axle) must match the road speed in the rolling direction. In pure rolling:

$$
V_x = R_e \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 4:** Because the tyre carcass deflects under load, the effective radius is less than the unloaded (free) radius. The relationship is:

$$
R_e = R_L - \delta_{ze}
$$

Where $\delta_{ze}$ is the **effective vertical deflection**, typically 1–2% of the free radius for a properly inflated passenger tyre.

**Step 5:** Solving for $R_e$ explicitly:

$$
R_e = \frac{V_x}{\omega}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The instantaneous centre of rotation migrates from the geometric centre of the wheel towards the road as load increases. This is why the loaded radius is not the same as the geometric radius — the rubber compresses, and the wheel "sits down" by the amount $\delta_{ze}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip Ratio from Kinematic Constraint

**Step 1:** Start with a wheel rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ on a vehicle moving at longitudinal velocity $V_x$.

**Step 2:** The longitudinal velocity of the wheel *circumferentially* (as if the tyre were rigid) is:

$$
V_{cx} = R_e \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 3:** Define the slip velocity at the contact patch:

$$
V_{s} = R_e \cdot \omega - V_x
$$

**Step 4:** For the **driving (tractive) case**, the wheel spins faster than the road ($R_e \omega > V_x$). For the **braking case**, the wheel spins slower ($R_e \omega < V_x$). To unify, we normalise by the larger of the two:

$$
\kappa = \frac{R_e \cdot \omega - V_x}{\max(R_e \cdot \omega,\; V_x)} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5:** For the typical small-slip regime where $V_x > R_e \omega$ (braking):

$$
\kappa = \frac{V_x - R_e \cdot \omega}{V_x} = 1 - \frac{R_e \cdot \omega}{V_x}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $V_x = R_e \omega_0$, where $\omega_0$ is the free-rolling angular velocity:

$$
\kappa = 1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_0}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** At $\kappa = 0$, the wheel is free-rolling with no slip. At $\kappa = 1$ (i.e., 100%), the wheel is locked ($\omega = 0$, pure sliding). At $\kappa = -1$, the wheel is spinning with no forward motion (burnout).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cornering Force and Slip Angle

Consider a small element of rubber in the contact patch of length $ds$, subjected to a lateral force $dF_y$ per unit length $q(s)$.

**Step 1:** The lateral deflection of a rubber element at position $s$ along the contact length $L_p$ is related to slip angle and lateral compliance:

$$
y(s) = \alpha \cdot s - y_{f}(s)
$$

Where $y_f(s)$ is the lateral displacement already "used up" by deformation.

**Step 2:** The lateral force per unit length is proportional to the local lateral deflection through the lateral stiffness per unit length $k_y$:

$$
q(s) = k_y \cdot y(s) = k_y (\alpha s - y_f)
$$

**Step 3:** The total lateral force is the integral over the contact length:

$$
F_y = \int_0^{L_p} q(s) \, ds = k_y \int_0^{L_p} (\alpha s - y_f) \, ds
$$

**Step 4:** If we assume the rubber "remembers" its rest position and slips back with a relaxation mechanism, the steady-state balance gives:

$$
F_y = k_y \cdot L_p \cdot \alpha - F_{y,\text{drag}}
$$

**Step 5:** In the linear, small-slip regime, the drag term is negligible, and the cornering stiffness is:

$$
C_\alpha = k_y \cdot L_p
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha
$$

> **Conversion logic:** As $\alpha$ increases, the cornering force rises linearly up to about 4–6° (for a passenger car), then saturates and finally falls as the tyre slides laterally. The slope of the initial line is the cornering stiffness — a key metric for steering feel and handling response.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pneumatic Trail and Aligning Torque

**Step 1:** In the contact patch, the lateral force $F_y$ is not applied at the centre of the patch — it is applied at a point shifted **rearward** by the pneumatic trail $t_p$.

**Step 2:** This shift creates a moment about the wheel centre:

$$
M_z = F_y \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 3:** Sign convention: $M_z$ is taken as positive when it tends to **reduce** the slip angle (self-aligning). The lateral force pushes the front of the wheel outward, creating a torque that rotates the wheel back toward the straight-ahead position.

**Step 4:** The sign of the aligning torque in KTU convention is therefore:

$$
M_z = -F_y \cdot t_p = -C_\alpha \cdot \alpha \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 5:** Define aligning stiffness:

$$
C_{mk} = C_\alpha \cdot t_p
$$

So:

$$
M_z = -C_{mk} \cdot \alpha
$$

**Step 6:** Solving for pneumatic trail:

$$
t_p = \frac{C_{mk}}{C_\alpha}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Higher $t_p$ gives stronger self-centring force on the steering wheel (more "road feel"), but it also makes the steering heavier at low speeds. This is why power steering is mandatory in modern cars — it compensates for the $M_z$ at parking speeds without sacrificing the high-speed feedback.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Relaxation Length (Dynamic Response)

**Step 1:** Consider a tyre entering the contact patch at the leading edge ($s = 0$) and leaving at $s = L_p$.

**Step 2:** The lateral deflection $y$ satisfies a first-order lag differential equation:

$$
\sigma_\alpha \cdot \frac{dy}{ds} + y = \alpha \cdot s
$$

Where $\sigma_\alpha$ is the relaxation length.

**Step 3:** At steady state, $dy/ds = 0$, so $y = \alpha s$, and the cornering force per unit length is $q = k_y \alpha s$. Total force:

$$
F_{y,\text{ss}} = \frac{1}{2} k_y L_p^2 \alpha
$$

**Step 4:** The actual time-domain response to a step change in slip angle $\alpha_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$
F_y(t) = F_{y,\text{ss}} \left(1 - e^{-V_x t / \sigma_\alpha}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** At time $t = \sigma_\alpha / V_x$, the force reaches $1 - e^{-1} \approx 63.2\%$ of its steady-state value.

**Step 6:** The corresponding time constant is:

$$
\tau_\alpha = \frac{\sigma_\alpha}{V_x}
$$

For $V_x = 30$ m/s (~108 km/h) and $\sigma_\alpha = 0.1$ m:

$$
\tau_\alpha = \frac{0.1}{30} \approx 3.3 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Even this small lag is responsible for high-speed directional stability issues. At 60 m/s (~216 km/h), $\tau_\alpha$ halves to 1.67 ms, but the driver reaction time (~150 ms) is two orders of magnitude larger — so the lag matters mostly in autonomous and stability-control systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Static vs Dynamic

| Aspect | Static Property | Dynamic Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Time scale** | Quasi-static, seconds | Milliseconds |
| **Key input** | Vertical load $F_z$, inflation pressure $P_i$ | Slip angle $\alpha$, slip ratio $\kappa$ |
| **Key output** | Deflection $\delta_z$, contact area $A_c$ | Cornering force $F_y$, longitudinal force $F_x$, aligning torque $M_z$ |
| **Governing physics** | Air spring + carcass flexure | Viscoelastic rubber friction + relaxation |
| **Measuring tool** | Load-deflection rig, plunger test | Flat-track tyre tester, machine-vision slip rig |
| **Design metric** | Load index, ply rating, section width | Cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$, magic-formula coefficients |
| **KTU question type** | Numerical dimensioning | Graph interpretation, magic formula, ESP logic |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define slip angle and cornering stiffness of a pneumatic tyre. Explain why a tyre cannot develop cornering force at zero slip angle.**

**Model Answer:**

**Slip Angle ($\alpha$):** It is the angle between the direction in which the wheel is pointing (its plane of rotation) and the actual direction in which the tyre is moving. Mathematically:

$$
\alpha = \delta - \theta_v
$$

**Cornering Stiffness ($C_\alpha$):** It is the rate of change of lateral (cornering) force with respect to slip angle at zero slip. In the linear regime:

$$
C_\alpha = \left. \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial \alpha} \right|_{\alpha = 0}
$$

Units: N/rad.

**Why no force at zero slip angle:** Cornering force is generated by the *lateral deformation* of the tyre's contact patch. At exactly $\alpha = 0$, the contact patch is symmetric, with the leading and trailing halves deforming equally in opposite directions, producing zero net lateral shear. Only when the wheel is forced to deviate from its travel direction does asymmetric deformation build up, generating a net $F_y$. This is an outcome of the **Pacejka Magic Formula** and the basic elasticity of the rubber.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme (3 marks):** Definition of slip angle (1) + Definition of cornering stiffness (1) + Reason for zero force at zero slip (1).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is pneumatic trail? How is it related to aligning torque and cornering force?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pneumatic trail ($t_p$):** It is the distance, measured along the contact patch, from the geometric centre of the contact patch to the point of application of the resultant lateral force. It arises because the lateral pressure distribution in the contact patch is asymmetric (weighted toward the rear of the patch when a slip angle is present).

**Relation to aligning torque:**

$$
M_z = -F_y \cdot t_p
$$

**Relation to cornering force and stiffness:**

$$
t_p = \frac{C_{mk}}{C_\alpha} = -\frac{M_z}{F_y}
$$

Typical value: **30–60 mm** for passenger car tyres. A larger $t_p$ gives stronger self-centring torque at the steering wheel but increases steering effort at low speeds, which is why power steering is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme (3 marks):** Definition of pneumatic trail (1) + Equation for $M_z$ (1) + Relation $t_p = C_{mk}/C_\alpha$ (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer **one** of the two alternatives.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the static and dynamic properties of pneumatic tyres. Discuss how cornering stiffness, slip angle, and aligning torque influence vehicle handling. (7 marks)

**(b)** A passenger car travelling at $V_x = 25$ m/s has front and rear cornering stiffnesses $C_{\alpha f} = 40{,}000$ N/rad and $C_{\alpha r} = 35{,}000$ N/rad respectively. The vehicle mass is 1500 kg with wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m, and the front axle is at distance $a = 1.2$ m from CG. Calculate the steady-state yaw rate gain (understeer gradient) and determine whether the vehicle is understeer, neutral steer, or oversteer. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Static Properties** (3.5 marks):

1. **Dimensional standards** — Tyre is identified by section width, aspect ratio, construction type (R for radial), and rim diameter. Example: 205/55 R16.

2. **Load carrying capacity** — Vertical load is supported by inflation pressure acting on the contact patch area. $F_z = P_i \cdot A_c$.

3. **Static spring rate $K_{tz}$** — Vertical deflection per unit load. $K_{tz} = 200$–$300$ N/mm for passenger cars.

4. **Effective rolling radius $R_e = V_x/\omega$** — Always less than the loaded radius due to tyre compression.

5. **Contact patch geometry** — Elliptical in theory (semi-axes $a$, $b$ with $A_c = \pi a b$), flat-bottomed in practice.

**Dynamic Properties** (2 marks):

1. **Slip angle $\alpha$** and **slip ratio $\kappa$** — fundamental inputs.
2. **Cornering force $F_y$** — generated by slip angle; linear $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ or full Pacejka S-curve.
3. **Aligning torque $M_z$** — self-centring moment due to pneumatic trail.
4. **Camber thrust $F_{yc} = C_\gamma \gamma$**.
5. **Relaxation length $\sigma_\alpha$** — dynamic lag.
6. **Friction circle** — combined $F_x$ and $F_y$ limit.

**Influence on Handling** (1.5 marks):

- Higher $C_\alpha$ → sharper turn-in response and better lane-change quickness.
- Larger aligning torque → better straight-line stability and "road feel".
- Excessive cornering stiffness at the rear → oversteer and risk of spin.
- Excessive at the front → understeer (push) and slow response.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V_x = 25$ m/s
- $C_{\alpha f} = 40{,}000$ N/rad
- $C_{\alpha r} = 35{,}000$ N/rad
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $L = 2.6$ m
- $a = 1.2$ m, hence $b = L - a = 1.4$ m

**Step 1: Understeer gradient formula**

The understeer gradient $K_{us}$ is given by:

$$
K_{us} = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}
$$

Where $W_f$ and $W_r$ are the axle weights. Assuming equal weight distribution by CG location is **incorrect**; we use the load transfer approach. For steady-state analysis at low lateral acceleration, the axle weights are simply the static loads (treating the CG as a fixed reference at low $a_y$):

$$
W_f = m g \cdot \frac{b}{L} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \frac{1.4}{2.6} \approx 7925 \text{ N}
$$

$$
W_r = m g \cdot \frac{a}{L} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \frac{1.2}{2.6} \approx 6792 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Compute understeer gradient**

$$
K_{us} = \frac{7925}{40000} - \frac{6792}{35000}
$$

$$
K_{us} = 0.1981 - 0.1941 = 0.0041 \text{ rad/(m/s}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to deg/(km/h²)** for KTU standard:

$$
K_{us} \text{ [deg/(km/h)}^2] = K_{us} \text{ [rad/(m/s}^2\text{)]} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times \frac{1000^2}{3600^2}
$$

$$
K_{us} = 0.0041 \times 57.296 \times 0.07716 \approx 0.0181 \text{ deg/(km/h)}^2
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

- $K_{us} > 0$ → **Understeer** vehicle (safest, most consumer cars).
- $K_{us} = 0$ → Neutral steer.
- $K_{us} < 0$ → Oversteer (race cars, unstable for novices).

> **Answer:** $K_{us} \approx 0.018$ deg/(km/h)², which is **positive** → the vehicle is **understeer**.

**Step 5: Yaw rate gain**

The yaw rate gain (steady-state yaw rate per unit steering angle) is:

$$
\frac{\psi}{\delta} = \frac{V_x/L}{1 + K_{us} \cdot V_x^2}
$$

Substituting $V_x = 25$ m/s, $L = 2.6$ m, $K_{us} = 0.0041$:

$$
K_{us} \cdot V_x^2 = 0.0041 \times 625 = 2.5625
$$

$$
\frac{\psi}{\delta} = \frac{25/2.6}{1 + 2.5625} = \frac{9.615}{3.5625} \approx 2.70 \text{ rad/s per rad}
$$

Or in familiar units:

$$
\frac{\psi}{\delta} = 2.70 \text{ s}^{-1} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 154.7 \text{ deg/s per deg}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** A higher understeer gradient reduces yaw rate gain — the car turns less per degree of steering wheel input. The denominator $1 + K_{us} V_x^2$ is the understeer factor, which grows with the square of speed.

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between cornering force and slip angle for a pneumatic tyre, including the non-linear Pacejka Magic Formula. Sketch the typical $F_y$ vs $\alpha$ curve and label the peak, saturation, and sliding regions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A radial-ply passenger car tyre is marked **195/65 R15**. The wheel is 15 inches in diameter. The vehicle carries 450 kg on this wheel at an inflation pressure of 220 kPa. Compute: (i) the overall diameter in mm, (ii) the sidewall height in mm, (iii) the theoretical contact patch area in cm² assuming an elliptical patch with $b = 0.85 a$, and (iv) the average contact pressure. Comment on whether the contact pressure equals the inflation pressure. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Derivation of $F_y$–$\alpha$ Linear Relation (3 marks):**

For a small element of rubber in the contact patch of length $ds$ and lateral stiffness per unit length $k_y$:

$$
dF_y = k_y \cdot y(s)\, ds
$$

With $y(s) = \alpha s$ in the linear regime and integrating from $s = 0$ to $s = L_p$:

$$
F_y = k_y \alpha \int_0^{L_p} s\, ds = \frac{1}{2} k_y L_p^2 \alpha
$$

Defining cornering stiffness $C_\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2} k_y L_p^2$:

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \alpha
$$

**Pacejka Magic Formula (3 marks):**

The empirical full-range model:

$$
F_y = D \sin\!\left(C \arctan\!\left(B \alpha - E\left(B \alpha - \arctan(B \alpha)\right)\right)\right)
$$

Parameters:
- $D = \mu F_z$ — peak value
- $B \cdot C \cdot D$ — initial slope (= $C_\alpha$)
- $C$ — shape factor
- $E$ — curvature factor (controls peak location)

**Sketch and Regions (1 mark):**

```
Fy
 |         ___________
 |        /           \
 |       /  Peak       \
 |      /              \
 |     /                \
 |    / Linear region    \  Saturation
 |   /                    \___________________
 |  /                                       \____
 | /                                                \____ Sliding
 |/_________________________________________________________ alpha
 0    4-6 deg            10-12 deg
```

- **Linear region:** $0 \le \alpha \le 4°–6°$ — Slope $= C_\alpha$.
- **Peak region:** $\alpha \approx 10°$ — Maximum $F_y \approx \mu F_z$.
- **Saturation/Fall:** $\alpha > 12°$ — $F_y$ decreases as rubber slides.
- **Sliding limit:** $\alpha \to 90°$ — $F_y = \mu_s F_z$ (kinetic friction only).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:** Tyre 195/65 R15, $F_z = 450 \times 9.81 = 4414.5$ N, $P_i = 220$ kPa.

**(i) Overall diameter $D_{od}$:**

Rim diameter in mm:

$$
D = 15 \times 25.4 = 381 \text{ mm}
$$

Sidewall height:

$$
H = 195 \times \frac{65}{100} = 126.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Overall diameter:

$$
D_{od} = D + 2H = 381 + 2(126.75) = 381 + 253.5 = 634.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Sidewall height:**

$$
H = 126.75 \text{ mm} \approx 127 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical contact patch area:**

For an ellipse with $b = 0.85 a$:

$$
A_c = \pi a b = \pi a (0.85 a) = 0.85 \pi a^2
$$

From $F_z = P_i A_c$:

$$
A_c = \frac{F_z}{P_i} = \frac{4414.5}{220 \times 10^3} = 0.02007 \text{ m}^2 = 200.7 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
a^2 = \frac{A_c}{0.85 \pi} = \frac{0.02007}{2.6704} = 7.516 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
a = 0.0867 \text{ m} = 8.67 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
b = 0.85 \times 8.67 = 7.37 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
A_c = \pi \times 8.67 \times 7.37 \approx 200.7 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**(iv) Average contact pressure:**

$$
P_{\text{contact}} = \frac{F_z}{A_c} = \frac{4414.5}{0.02007} \approx 220 \text{ kPa} = P_i
$$

**Comment:**

The contact pressure equals the inflation pressure by construction (force balance). In reality, however:

- The contact patch is **not uniform** in pressure — it has a peak near the leading edge and a trough at the trailing edge due to carcass bending.
- The **crown** of the tread (centre ribs) carries slightly more load at the centre of the patch than at the edges.
- The carcass plies themselves carry **some of the load** through their bending stiffness; hence the contact pressure is **not exactly** equal to the gauge pressure, but the difference is small (5–10%) for a properly inflated radial tyre.
- Over-inflation makes the patch smaller and pressure spikes sharper; under-inflation enlarges the patch but introduces higher carcass flexure and heat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — Students often forget the negative sign in $M_z = -C_{mk} \alpha$ and lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Units confusion** — Cornering stiffness must be in **N/rad**, not N/deg. Converting degrees to radians is mandatory.
> 3. **Mixing up slip ratio definitions** — Two definitions exist (one with $V_x$ in denominator, one with $R_e \omega$); using both inconsistently in one answer loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Neglecting the effect of $V_x$ on relaxation length** — Always quote relaxation length in **metres** (spatial), not in seconds. To convert to time constant: $\tau = \sigma / V_x$.
> 5. **Friction circle vs friction ellipse** — These are different; an ellipse assumes $\mu_x \ne \mu_y$ (e.g., on wet roads, lateral grip is more degraded).
> 6. **Failing to label diagrams** — In 7-mark questions, an unlabelled sketch of the $F_y$ vs $\alpha$ curve loses 2 marks. Always mark: origin, linear region, peak, saturation, and slide region.
> 7. **Incorrect aspect-ratio formula** — Some students write $H = B \times AR$ instead of $H = B \times (AR/100)$; this single error cascades through the entire solution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - [ ] **Pneumatic tyre** = air-filled, flexible, multi-component (bead, sidewall, carcass, belt, tread, inner liner).
> - [ ] **Static load balance:** $F_z = P_i \cdot A_c$ — fundamental.
> - [ ] **Slip ratio** $\kappa = (R_e \omega - V_x)/\max(R_e\omega, V_x)$ — drives $F_x$.
> - [ ] **Slip angle** $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$ — drives $F_y$.
> - [ ] **Cornering force (linear):** $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$. **(Non-linear):** Pacejka Magic Formula.
> - [ ] **Aligning torque:** $M_z = -C_{mk} \alpha = -F_y \cdot t_p$.
> - [ ] **Pneumatic trail:** $t_p = C_{mk} / C_\alpha$ ~ 30–60 mm.
> - [ ] **Camber thrust:** $F_{yc} = C_\gamma \gamma$.
> - [ ] **Relaxation length:** $\sigma_\alpha$ ~ 50–150 mm. Time constant $\tau = \sigma_\alpha / V_x$.
> - [ ] **Friction circle:** $(F_x/\mu F_z)^2 + (F_y/\mu F_z)^2 \le 1$.
> - [ ] **Tyre size:** 195/65 R15 → 195 mm wide, 65% aspect ratio, radial, 15" rim.
> - [ ] **Understeer gradient:** $K_{us} = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$ — positive means understeer.
> - [ ] **Yaw rate gain:** $\dot{\psi}/\delta = (V_x/L)/(1 + K_{us} V_x^2)$ — denominator is the understeer factor.
> - [ ] **Effective rolling radius** $R_e < R_L < R_0$ (free radius).
> - [ ] **Magic Formula parameters:** $D = \mu F_z$, slope at origin $= B C D = C_\alpha$, $E$ shifts the peak.
> - [ ] **KTU emphasis:** Graphs (linear region, peak, saturation, slide), formula derivations, and 1–2 numerical problems per question paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tyre Materials # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Tyre Materials** refer to the carefully engineered combination of elastomers (rubber compounds), reinforcement fabrics/wires, fillers, chemical additives, and metallic components that together form a modern pneumatic tyre. Each material is selected based on specific mechanical, thermal, and durability requirements dictated by the tyre's functional zone (tread, sidewall, bead, carcass, belt, inner liner). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAUT303 – Module 4):** Students must understand the role of *Natural Rubber (NR)*, *Synthetic Rubbers (SBR, BR, IIR, EPDM)*, *Reinforcement materials (Steel, Nylon, Polyester, Rayon, Aramid)*, *Fillers (Carbon Black, Silica)*, and *Curing additives (Sulphur, Zinc Oxide, Accelerators, Antioxidants)*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tyre as a Layered Sandwich" Imagine a tyre as a **multi-layered architectural sandwich** designed for hostile service: - The **Tread** is the *shoe sole* that grips the road. - The **Belt and Ply** act like *steel rebar in reinforced concrete*, providing structural skeleton. - The **Sidewall** is the *flexible skin* that absorbs shocks and displays information. - The **Inner Liner** is the *airtight balloon* preventing pressure loss. - The **Bead** is the *steel clasp* that locks the tyre to the rim, like a belt buckle on a trouser. Each layer is made of *different material combinations* precisely formulated to handle *specific mechanical loads* — vertical load, cornering force, braking torque, and impact absorption. ## 1.3 Key Material Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > A tyre engineer evaluates materials on the following **standard metrics** (in **bold**): > - **Hardness (Shore A):** typically **55 – 75** for passenger treads. > - **Tensile Strength:** **15 – 25 MPa** for typical tread compounds. > - **Elongation at Break:** **300% – 600%** for natural rubber compounds. > - **Heat Build-up (ΔT):** should remain **< 30 °C** under dynamic loading. > - **Rebound Resilience:** **30% – 70%** depending on application. > - **Specific Gravity:** **1.10 – 1.20** for most rubber vulcanizates. > - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** typically **−60 °C to −40 °C** for NR. ## 1.4 Primary Material Categories in a Tyre A modern pneumatic tyre consists of **four fundamental material groups**: 1. **Elastomers (Rubber Polymers):** Provide flexibility, elasticity, and hysteresis. 2. **Reinforcement Materials:** Provide dimensional stability and load-bearing capacity. 3. **Fillers:** Enhance mechanical strength, wear resistance, and processability. 4. **Chemical Additives (Compounding Agents):** Enable vulcanization, ageing resistance, and bonding. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Elastomers (Rubber Polymers) Elastomers form **40% – 60%** of a tyre by weight. They are long-chain polymers with weak intermolecular forces that allow large reversible deformations. ### 2.1.1 Natural Rubber (NR) - Source: Latex from *Hevea brasiliensis* tree. - Structure: *cis-1,4-polyisoprene*. - Key Properties: - High tensile strength (**≥ 20 MPa**). - Excellent resilience (**≥ 70%** rebound). - Low heat build-up — ideal for **heavy-duty truck tyres**. - Limitation: Poor oil/ozone resistance. ### 2.1.2 Synthetic Rubbers | Elastomer | Full Form | Key Property | Typical Tyre Use | |---|---|---|---| | **SBR** | Styrene-Butadiene Rubber | Good wear resistance, wet grip | Tread (passenger car) | | **BR** | Polybutadiene Rubber | High resilience, low Tg | Sidewall, tread blend | | **IIR** | Butyl Rubber | Low air permeability | Inner liner | | **EPDM** | Ethylene-Propylene-Diene Monomer | Ozone/weather resistance | Sidewall, white sidewall | | **IIR-Halogenated** | Halo-butyl | Bondable, low permeability | Inner liner (modern) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** *Why is butyl rubber (IIR) used for inner liners?* > Because its **air permeability coefficient is ~10× lower** than natural rubber, making it the gold standard for **air retention** in tubeless tyres. ## 2.2 Reinforcement Materials Reinforcement provides the **structural backbone** and resists inflation pressure, load, and impact. ### 2.2.1 Textile Reinforcements | Material | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Modulus | Key Use | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Rayon** | 400 – 600 | High | Carcass ply (premium) | Excellent dimensional stability | | **Nylon** | 600 – 900 | Very High | Carcass (truck/bus) | Fatigue resistant | | **Polyester** | 700 – 1000 | High | Carcass (passenger) | Low creep, widely used | | **Aramid (Kevlar)** | 2000 – 3000 | Ultra-High | Cap ply, run-flat | Lightweight, expensive | ### 2.2.2 Metallic Reinforcements - **Steel Wire (Bead & Belt):** - Composition: High-carbon steel wire (0.7% – 1.0% C), brass-coated for **rubber–steel adhesion**. - Tensile strength: **2000 – 2800 MPa**. - Used in **bead cores (wrapped, single, multi-wire)** and **steel belts (1+2, 2+2 layer)**. > [!NOTE] > **Brass plating** on steel wire (typically **60% Cu / 40% Zn, 0.2 – 0.5 µm thick**) is *mandatory* to form a **Cu–Sulphur bond** with the rubber during vulcanization, preventing separation of the rubber–steel interface. ## 2.3 Fillers Fillers are **non-reactive additives** that improve mechanical and processing properties. ### 2.3.1 Carbon Black (CB) - Most widely used filler — **25% – 35%** by weight of rubber compound. - Grades: **N330 (HAF – High Abrasion Furnace)** for treads. - Functions: Reinforcement, UV protection, conductivity, anti-wear. - Particle size: **20 – 60 nm**; structure (DBP absorption): **80 – 130 cm³/100g**. ### 2.3.2 Silica (SiO₂) - Increasingly used in **“绿色轮胎” (Green Tyres)** for **low rolling resistance**. - Requires **silane coupling agents (e.g., Si-69 / TESPT)** for rubber bonding. - Reduces rolling resistance by **20% – 30%** vs. carbon black only. ## 2.4 Chemical Additives (Compounding Agents) | Additive | Function | Typical Loading (phr) | |---|---|---| | **Sulphur** | Vulcanizing (cross-linking) agent | 1.5 – 3.0 | | **ZnO (Zinc Oxide)** | Activator | 3 – 5 | | **Stearic Acid** | Activator/dispersant | 1 – 2 | | **Accelerators (CBS, TBBS, MBT)** | Speed up vulcanization | 0.5 – 1.5 | | **Antioxidants (6PPD, TMQ)** | Prevent ozone/oxidative ageing | 1 – 3 | | **Antiozonants (Waxes)** | Migrate to surface, form protective film | 1 – 3 | | **Plasticizers (Aromatic Oils)** | Improve processability | 5 – 20 | | **Peptizers** | Reduce viscosity during mixing | 0.1 – 0.5 | > [!NOTE] > **phr = parts per hundred rubber** — the universal unit in rubber compounding. ## 2.5 Tyre Component-to-Material Mapping (Reference Table) | Tyre Component | Primary Materials | Engineering Function | |---|---|---| | **Tread Cap** | NR + SBR + BR + CB + Silica | Wear, grip, rolling resistance | | **Tread Base (Under-tread)** | NR + CB (high loading) | Hysteresis, heat dissipation | | **Sidewall** | NR + BR + EPDM (optional) | Flex, ozone resistance | | **Carcass (Ply)** | Polyester / Nylon / Rayon + NR skim | Load-bearing, pressure retention | | **Belt (Steel)** | Steel cord + NR compound | Stability, puncture resistance | | **Cap Ply (Nylon Overlay)** | Nylon 6,6 | High-speed stability | | **Bead** | Steel wire + rubber apex | Rim fit, anchoring | | **Inner Liner** | Halogenated IIR (CIIR/BIIR) | Air retention | | **Chafer / Apex** | Stiff NR compound | Bead protection, sidewall stiffness | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Material selection directly determines the 3 magical triangle trade-offs:** *Rolling Resistance, Wear Life, Wet Grip*. - The choice of **silica vs. carbon black** is the single biggest lever for fuel-efficient ("Green") tyres. - The **bead apex compound** stiffness controls steering response and ride harshness. - The **inner liner** must remain flexible down to **−40 °C** for arctic operation. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Numerical Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Standard Range | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shore A Hardness (typical tread) | $H_A$ | 55 – 75 | Shore A | | 2 | Tensile Strength (TS) | $\sigma_{TS} = F_{max}/A_0$ | 15 – 25 | MPa | | 3 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b = (L_b - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | 300 – 600 | % | | 4 | Modulus at 300% elongation | $M_{300} = F_{300\%}/A_0$ | 8 – 15 | MPa | | 5 | Air Permeability (NR) | $P_{NR}$ | $\sim 0.7$ | $\times 10^{-10}\,\text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm/cm}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{cmHg}$ | | 6 | Air Permeability (IIR) | $P_{IIR}$ | $\sim 0.07$ | Same as above | | 7 | Permeability Ratio | $R_P = P_{NR}/P_{IIR} \approx 10$ | — | Dimensionless | | 8 | Rubber Volume Fraction | $\phi_r = V_r/(V_r + V_f)$ | 0.55 – 0.75 | — | | 9 | Filler Loading (phr) | $\text{phr} = (m_{additive}/m_{rubber}) \times 100$ | CB: 30 – 80 | phr | | 10 | Brass Coating Cu:Zn Ratio | Standard $60:40$ | ± 5% | % by mass | | 11 | Vulcanization Temperature | $T_v$ | 140 – 170 | °C | | 12 | Ply Load Index (PLY RATING) | PR | 4 – 22 (LT) | — | | 13 | Cured Tyre Weight (passenger) | $W$ | 7 – 12 | kg | | 14 | Cured Tyre Weight (truck) | $W$ | 50 – 75 | kg | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume Fraction of Rubber in a Tread Compound **Given:** A tyre tread compound is formulated with the following recipe: - Natural Rubber (NR): 50 phr (specific gravity = 0.92) - SBR: 30 phr (specific gravity = 0.94) - BR: 20 phr (specific gravity = 0.91) - Carbon Black (N330): 60 phr (specific gravity = 1.80) - Oil (Plasticizer): 15 phr (specific gravity = 0.95) -
