| Wear Pattern | Visual Signature | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|-------------|------------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Shoulder Wear** (both edges worn, centre intact) | "Ribbed bone" profile | Under-inflation (excessive contact patch deflection) | Re-inflate to CIP, then rotate |
| **Centre Wear** (centre smooth, edges intact) | "Smiley face" profile | Over-inflation (contact patch bulges in the middle) | Deflate to CIP, then rotate |
| **One-sided Shoulder Wear** (one edge only) | "Knife-edge" profile | Excessive camber (or bent stub axle) | Wheel-alignment → rotate |
| **Cupping / Scalloping** (alternating high-low spots) | Washboard pattern | Worn shock absorbers / wheel imbalance | Replace dampers / rebalance → rotate |
| **Feathering** (edge sharp one way, smooth the other) | Saw-tooth feel | Incorrect toe setting | Toe alignment → rotate |

## 2.3 The Five Standard Rotation Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Selection Matrix** (high-yield for KTU 14-mark derivations)
>
> | Vehicle / Tyre Configuration | Recommended Pattern | Spare Tyre Usage |
> |-----------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
> | **Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD)** with non-directional, same-size tyres | **Forward Cross (X-Pattern)** | Optional |
> | **Front-Wheel Drive (FWD)** with non-directional, same-size tyres | **Rearward Cross (Rearward-X)** | Optional |
> | **All-Wheel Drive (AWD / 4WD)** | **X-Pattern or Five-Tyre Rotation** | Highly recommended |
> | **Directional Tyres (uni-rotational)** | **Front-to-Rear only** (no left-right swap) | Same — front-to-rear |
> | **Staggered Fitment** (rear wider/larger than front) | **Side-to-Side (rear) + Front-to-Rear (front)** | Same axle only |
> | **Full-Size Spare (matching)** | **Five-Tyre Rotation** | Included in cycle |

### 2.3.1 The Forward Cross (X-Pattern) — RWD Cars

```
   ┌──────┐     ┌──────┐
   │  FL  │     │  FR  │      (FL = Front-Left,  FR = Front-Right)
   └──┬───┘     └──┬───┘
      │  ╲       ╱  │
      │   ╲     ╱   │
      │    ╲   ╱    │
      │     ╲ ╱     │
   ┌──┴───┐     ┌──┴───┐
   │  RL  │     │  RR  │     (RL = Rear-Left,  RR = Rear-Right)
   └──────┘     └──────┘

   FL ──→ RR
   FR ──→ RL
   RL ──→ FR
   RR ──→ FL
```

### 2.3.2 The Rearward Cross — FWD Cars

```
   FL ──→ RL
   FR ──→ RR
   RL ──→ FR
   RR ──→ FL
```

### 2.3.3 Five-Tyre Rotation (with matching spare)

```
   FL ──→ RR
   FR ──→ RL
   RL ──→ SPARE (now at front)
   RR ──→ FL
   SPARE ──→ FR
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations required for KTU 2024 numericals on this topic**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Average Tread Wear Rate** | $\displaystyle W \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{new}} \;-\; T_{\text{current}}}{D}$ | $\text{mm/km}$ | $T_{\text{new}} \approx 8\text{ mm}$ |
| 2 | **Remaining Tread Life** | $\displaystyle D_{\text{rem}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{current}} \;-\; T_{\min}}{W}$ | $\text{km}$ | $T_{\min}=1.6\text{ mm}$ (passenger) |
| 3 | **Tyre Rolling Radius** | $\displaystyle r_{r} \;=\; \frac{r_{\text{rim}} \;+\; H}{2}$ | $\text{m}$ | $H$ = Section height |
| 4 | **Front / Rear Wear Ratio (FWD)** | $\displaystyle k \;=\; \frac{W_{f}}{W_{r}} \;\approx\; 1.6 \text{ – } 2.2$ | — | Empirical |
| 5 | **Rotation Interval (km)** | $\displaystyle N_{r} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{new}} \;-\; T_{\min}}{W_{\text{avg}} \;\times\; k_{\text{asym}}}$ | $\text{km}$ | $k_{\text{asym}} \approx 1.5$ |
| 6 | **Loaded Tyre Deflection (static)** | $\displaystyle \delta \;\approx\; 0.10 \text{ – } 0.12 \;\times\; H$ | $\text{m}$ | For CIP check |
| 7 | **Speedometer Error from Radius Mismatch** | $\displaystyle \varepsilon_{s} \;=\; \frac{\Delta r_{r}}{r_{r,\text{ref}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Limit: ±5% (AIS-044) |

> **NOTATION GLOSSARY**
> - $T_{\text{new}}$ — new-tyre tread depth, **mm**
> - $T_{\text{current}}$ — measured tread depth, **mm**
> - $T_{\min}$ — legally allowed minimum, **mm** (passenger car = **1.6**)
> - $D$ — distance travelled since new, **km**
> - $W$ — wear rate, **mm / km**
> - $H$ — tyre section height, **m**
> - $r_{\text{rim}}$ — rim radius, **m**
> - $k$ — empirical front/rear wear ratio

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Concept

- **OEM Maintenance Schedules** — every major manufacturer (Maruti, Hyundai, Tata, Mahindra) prescribes rotation at the **5,000 km / 6-month** service interval in the owner's manual.
- **Commercial Fleet Operators** (KSRTC, Ola, Uber) — rotation intervals shortened to **15,000 – 25,000 km** due to retread economics.
- **Motorsport (Rally / F4)** — corner-specific rotation after every stint to balance grip during endurance races.
- **EV Applications** — instantaneous torque from electric motors accelerates rear-tyre wear; manufacturers now recommend **staggered patterns** for dual-motor EVs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical, and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Rotation Pattern

> **Decision Algorithm for KTU Practical / Viva**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify drivetrain} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{FWD, RWD, AWD} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Inspect sidewall for rotation arrow} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Directional or not} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Measure front vs rear section width} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Staggered or square} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Check spare-tyre equivalence} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Five-tyre or four-tyre pattern} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Match the matrix entry from §2.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical 1 — Average Wear Rate and Remaining Life

> **Problem [KTU Model Question]:** A new passenger car radial tyre has a tread depth of **8.0 mm**. After **30,000 km**, the centre tread depth is measured as **3.2 mm**. Determine (a) the average wear rate, and (b) the remaining serviceable life in km before reaching the legal minimum of **1.6 mm**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute average wear rate $W$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \;&=\; \frac{T_{\text{new}} \;-\; T_{\text{current}}}{D} \\[4pt]
   \;&=\; \frac{8.0 \;\text{mm} \;-\; 3.2 \;\text{mm}}{30{,}000 \;\text{km}} \\[4pt]
   \;&=\; \frac{4.8 \;\text{mm}}{30{,}000 \;\text{km}} \\[4pt]
   \;&=\; 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \;\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute remaining life $D_{\text{rem}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{rem}} \;&=\; \frac{T_{\text{current}} \;-\; T_{\min}}{W} \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; \frac{3.2 \;\text{mm} \;-\; 1.6 \;\text{mm}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm/km}} \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; \frac{1.6 \;\text{mm}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm/km}} \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; 10{,}000 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At the current wear rate, the tyre will reach the legal scrap threshold after **another 10,000 km**. The owner should plan for replacement before this distance is covered.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — FWD Front/Rear Wear Ratio

> **Problem:** A FWD car uses the **Rearward-Cross** pattern. After one rotation cycle (15,000 km), the front tyres are worn by **2.4 mm** and the rear tyres by **1.3 mm**. Find the front-to-rear wear ratio $k$ and recommend whether the existing 15,000-km interval should be **shortened or extended**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $k$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \;&=\; \frac{W_f}{W_r} \;=\; \frac{2.4 \;\text{mm / cycle}}{1.3 \;\text{mm / cycle}} \\[4pt]
  \;&\approx\; 1.846
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with empirical range:**

$$
1.6 \;\le\; k \;\le\; 2.2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = 1.846 \;\in\; \text{range (acceptable FWD behaviour)}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since $k$ is **above the median** of the empirical range (1.9), the front tyres are wearing disproportionately fast. The owner should **shorten the rotation interval by ~10–15%**, i.e., adopt a new interval of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{new}} \;&=\; N_{\text{old}} \times \left(1 \;-\; \frac{k - 1.6}{2.2 - 1.6}\right) \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; 15{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.246}{0.6}\right) \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; 15{,}000 \times (1 - 0.41) \\[4pt]
                \;&\approx\; 8{,}850 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A new interval of **~8,000 – 9,000 km** is recommended.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical 3 — Speedometer Error from Mismatched Rolling Radius

> **Problem:** After rotation, a rear tyre is mounted on the front. The original front rolling radius was **0.315 m**; the new front radius is **0.320 m** because the rear section is taller. If the speedometer is calibrated for **0.315 m**, what is the displayed speed when the true speed is **80 km/h**? (Compliance check against AIS-044 ±5% limit.)

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the speed-radius relation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedometer reading} \;&=\; v_{\text{true}} \times \frac{r_{\text{ref}}}{r_{\text{actual}}} \\[4pt]
                              \;&=\; 80 \times \frac{0.315}{0.320} \\[4pt]
                              \;&\approx\; 78.75 \;\text{km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the percentage error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{s} \;&=\; \frac{78.75 \;-\; 80}{80} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; -1.5625\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The error magnitude is **1.56%**, which is **within the ±5%** AIS-044 limit — the configuration is legally compliant. The negative sign indicates the speedometer **under-reads** (a safer bias for the driver).

---

## 3.5 Procedural Checklist for Performing a Rotation (Workshop Steps)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1.} \quad & \text{Park on level ground, chock wheels, engage handbrake.} \\
\text{2.} \quad & \text{Loosen lug nuts (don't remove) on all four wheels.} \\
\text{3.} \quad & \text{Jack up the vehicle at manufacturer-approved lift points.} \\
\text{4.} \quad & \text{Remove wheels and inspect each tyre for damage / TWI.} \\
\text{5.} \quad & \text{Measure tread depth with a calibrated depth gauge.} \\
\text{6.} \quad & \text{Check cold inflation pressure; record on the job card.} \\
\text{7.} \quad & \text{Re-mount wheels per the chosen pattern (§2.3).} \\
\text{8.} \quad & \text{Tighten lugs in a star sequence to manufacturer torque (e.g., 110 N·m).} \\
\text{9.} \quad & \text{Lower the vehicle; final torque-check after 50 km.} \\
\text{10.} \quad & \text{Update the service logbook with the date, odometer, and pattern used.}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Tyre-Rotation Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Arrives for Service] --> B{Drivetrain Type?}
    B -- FWD --> C{Same-size Tyres Front and Rear?}
    B -- RWD --> D{Same-size Tyres Front and Rear?}
    B -- AWD --> E{Available Matching Spare?}
    C -- Yes --> F{Directional Tyre?}
    C -- No --> G[Staggered Fitment Detected]
    D -- Yes --> F
    D -- No --> G
    F -- Yes --> H[Use Front-to-Rear Pattern]
    F -- No --> I[Use Rearward Cross FWD]
    E -- Yes --> J[Use Five-Tyre Rotation]
    E -- No --> K[Use X-Pattern AWD Four-Tyre]
    G --> L[Side-to-Side Rear plus Front-to-Rear Front]
    H --> M[Measure Tread Depth and CIP]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{TWI Reached on Any Tyre?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Flag Tyre for Replacement]
    N -- No --> P[Mark Rotation Logbook]
    P --> Q[Release Vehicle]
    O --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Tyre-Rotation Service Bay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTAKE[Intake Bay]
        A1[Vehicle Reception] --> A2[Odometer Record]
        A2 --> A3[Customer Complaint Log]
    end
    subgraph INSPECT[Inspection Station]
        B1[Tread Depth Gauge] --> B2[Visual Wear Pattern Analysis]
        B2 --> B3[Cold Inflation Pressure Check]
        B3 --> B4[Lug Nut Torque Verification]
    end
    subgraph DECIDE[Decision Module]
        C1[Pattern Selection Matrix] --> C2[Rotation Pattern Lock]
        C2 --> C3[Service Card Update]
    end
    subgraph EXEC[Execution Bay]
        D1[Wheel Removal] --> D2[Pattern-Specific Re-mount]
        D2 --> D3[Star Torque Sequence]
        D3 --> D4[Lower and Re-check]
    end
    subgraph QC[Quality Control]
        E1[Re-torque at 50 km] --> E2[Speedometer Error Check]
        E2 --> E3[Customer Sign-off]
    end
    INTAKE --> INSPECT --> DECIDE --> EXEC --> QC
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Wear-Pattern Recognition Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Inspect Tyre Tread] --> W2{Where is Rubber Lost?}
    W2 -- Both Shoulders --> P1[Under-inflation Wear]
    W2 -- Centre Only --> P2[Over-inflation Wear]
    W2 -- One Edge Only --> P3[Excess Camber Wear]
    W2 -- Alternating Pits --> P4[Cupping from Worn Dampers]
    W2 -- Saw-tooth Edge --> P5[Toe Misalignment Feathering]
    P1 --> R1[Re-inflate to CIP then Rotate]
    P2 --> R2[Deflate to CIP then Rotate]
    P3 --> R3[Wheel Align then Rotate]
    P4 --> R4[Replace Dampers then Rotate]
    P5 --> R5[Toe Align then Rotate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each wear pattern triggers a *corrective action* **before** the rotation is performed. Skipping the corrective step and rotating alone is a common workshop mistake that simply re-distributes the underlying defect.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define the term "tyre rotation" and state **any four** benefits of performing periodic tyre rotation on a passenger car.*
>
> **Model Answer (target length: ~80 words):**
>
> **Tyre rotation** is the systematic interchanging of tyre positions on a vehicle at prescribed intervals to equalise tread wear.
>
> **Benefits:**
> 1. Promotes **uniform tread wear** across all four tyres, extending casing life.
> 2. Maintains **balanced handling and braking** by equalising grip levels.
> 3. Protects **driveline components** (differentials, CV joints) from cyclic loading due to rolling-radius mismatch.
> 4. Ensures **OEM warranty compliance** and improves overall safety.
> 5. Reduces **total cost of ownership** by deferring replacement.
>
> *(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List the **different standard rotation patterns** used in automotive practice. State **one specific application** of each.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | # | Pattern | Specific Application |
> |---|---------|----------------------|
> | 1 | **Forward Cross (X-Pattern)** | Rear-wheel-drive cars with non-directional, same-size tyres |
> | 2 | **Rearward Cross** | Front-wheel-drive cars with non-directional, same-size tyres |
> | 3 | **Front-to-Rear** | Directional tyres (uni-rotational) |
> | 4 | **Side-to-Side** | Staggered fitment vehicles — rear axle only |
> | 5 | **Five-Tyre Rotation** | AWD vehicles with full-size matching spare |
>
> *(Correctly tabulated list with one matching application per pattern → 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for confusing *Forward Cross* with *Rearward Cross* — remember: **FWD → Rearward Cross** (rearward because the rearward-moving front tyres go to the rear).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for failing to mention the *inspection step* before rotation (tread depth, CIP, wear pattern).
> - Students **lose 2 marks** in numericals for **forgetting units** (mm/km, km, %).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not distinguishing **directional** vs **non-directional** tyres.
> - For **5-mark sub-parts**, students must include a **labelled sketch**; otherwise lose 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** *Explain in detail the **procedure of tyre rotation** for a **front-wheel-drive car** with non-directional tyres. Include a clearly labelled **sketch** of the pattern and state the **inspection steps** to be carried out before rotation. **(7 marks)***
>
> **(b)** *A passenger radial tyre has a new tread depth of **8.5 mm**. After **25,000 km** the centre tread depth is **3.4 mm**. Determine: (i) the average wear rate in **mm/km**; (ii) the **remaining serviceable life** in km before the legal minimum of **1.6 mm**; (iii) the **recommended rotation interval** in km for a FWD car with $k_{\text{asym}} = 1.5$. **(7 marks)***

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**1. Pre-Rotation Inspection (2 marks):**
- Park the vehicle on level ground, chock the rear wheels, and engage the parking brake.
- Loosen (do not remove) all lug nuts; jack up the car at the manufacturer-approved points.
- Visually inspect each tyre for cuts, bulges, embedded objects, and **Tyre Wear Indicator (TWI)** contact.
- Measure the **tread depth** at the centre, inner shoulder, and outer shoulder of each tyre using a depth gauge.
- Record the **cold inflation pressure (CIP)** for each position; re-inflate to OEM specification.
- Note the wear pattern (shoulder / centre / one-sided / cupping) — flag any abnormality for wheel-alignment check.

**2. Pattern Selection (1 mark):** For a FWD car with non-directional, same-size tyres, the **Rearward Cross** pattern is recommended.

**3. Pattern — Rearward Cross (2 marks):**

```
   FL ──→ RL
   FR ──→ RR
   RL ──→ FR
   RR ──→ FL
```

   *(The diagonal/cross movement applies only between opposite corners; the front axle moves straight to the rear axle on the same side.)*

**4. Execution (1 mark):**
- Remove the wheels, swap them per the diagram, and re-mount.
- Tighten lug nuts in a **star (criss-cross) sequence** to manufacturer torque (e.g., 110 N·m for typical sedans).
- Lower the vehicle; perform a **re-torque check** after 50 km.
- Update the service logbook with date, odometer reading, and pattern used.

**5. Post-Rotation Test (1 mark):** A short low-speed road test to confirm no imbalance, vibration, or pull.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**(i) Average wear rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \;&=\; \frac{8.5 \;-\; 3.4}{25{,}000} \;\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{km}} \\[4pt]
  \;&=\; \frac{5.1 \;\text{mm}}{25{,}000 \;\text{km}} \\[4pt]
  \;&=\; 2.04 \times 10^{-4} \;\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Remaining life:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{rem}} \;&=\; \frac{3.4 \;-\; 1.6}{2.04 \times 10^{-4}} \;\text{km} \\[4pt]
                \;&=\; \frac{1.8 \;\text{mm}}{2.04 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm/km}} \\[4pt]
                \;&\approx\; 8{,}824 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Recommended rotation interval:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{r} \;&=\; \frac{T_{\text{new}} \;-\; T_{\min}}{W_{\text{avg}} \times k_{\text{asym}}} \\[4pt]
      \;&=\; \frac{8.5 \;-\; 1.6}{(2.04 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.5} \;\text{km} \\[4pt]
      \;&=\; \frac{6.9 \;\text{mm}}{3.06 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm/km}} \\[4pt]
      \;&\approx\; 22{,}549 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $N_r \approx 22{,}549$ km is well above the empirical 5,000–10,000 km guideline, suggesting either very mild usage or under-estimation of asymmetric wear. A practical interval of **10,000 km** is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** *Compare the **Forward Cross** and **Rearward Cross** rotation patterns. For which drivetrain is each preferred, and **why**? Include **sketches**. **(7 marks)***
>
> **(b)** *A FWD car is fitted with staggered tyres (front: 205/55 R16, rear: 225/45 R17). The owner complains of **premature rear-tyre wear** and asks for a rotation schedule. Recommend: (i) the appropriate **rotation pattern**; (ii) the **rotation interval** in km, given a measured wear rate of $1.8 \times 10^{-4}$ mm/km on the front and $2.1 \times 10^{-4}$ mm/km on the rear; (iii) any **TWO additional maintenance steps** that should accompany the rotation. **(7 marks)***

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Forward Cross (X) | Rearward Cross |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Drivetrain** | RWD | FWD |
| **Front-to-Rear Movement** | Diagonal (front-left → rear-right) | Straight across (front-left → rear-left) |
| **Rear-to-Front Movement** | Diagonal (rear-left → front-right) | Diagonal (rear-left → front-right) |
| **Front Tyre Destination** | Opposite-side rear | Same-side rear |
| **Reason for the Pattern** | In RWD, rear tyres get *drive* wear but less *steering* wear — swapping diagonally distributes both | In FWD, front tyres do *all three* jobs (steer, brake, drive) — moving them straight to the rear lets them "rest" on drive and steering while recovering shoulder wear |

**Sketches (2 marks):**

**Forward Cross (RWD):**
```
   FL ──→ RR
   FR ──→ RL
   RL ──→ FR
   RR ──→ FL
```

**Rearward Cross (FWD):**
```
   FL ──→ RL
   FR ──→ RR
   RL ──→ FR
   RR ──→ FL
```

**Key Reason (2 marks):** The choice is dictated by *which axle does the most work*. RWD sends drive torque rearward, so rear tyres benefit from being promoted to the front; FWD does the opposite. The "cross" component in both patterns equalises the *lateral* (left ↔ right) asymmetry introduced by road camber and cornering habits.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**(i) Rotation Pattern (2 marks):** Because the rear tyres are **wider and of larger diameter (225/45 R17)** than the front (205/55 R16), this is a **staggered fitment**. The correct rotation is:

- **Rear axle:** Side-to-side swap only (RR ↔ RL)
- **Front axle:** Front-to-rear only (FL → RL, FR → RR) — provided the rolling radii remain within the AIS-044 ±5% speedometer tolerance.

**Combined pattern (2 marks):**
```
   FL ──→ RL        RL ──→ FL
   FR ──→ RR        RR ──→ FR
   (No cross-swaps across the diagonal — staggered fitment prohibits this)
```

**(ii) Rotation Interval (2 marks):** Use the **higher** of the two wear rates to set the conservative interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{r} \;&=\; \frac{T_{\text{new}} \;-\; T_{\min}}{W_{\max} \times k_{\text{asym}}} \\[4pt]
      \;&=\; \frac{8.0 \;\text{mm} - 1.6 \;\text{mm}}{(2.1 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.5} \;\text{km} \\[4pt]
      \;&=\; \frac{6.4 \;\text{mm}}{3.15 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm/km}} \\[4pt]
      \;&\approx\; 20{,}317 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Practical recommendation:** **~10,000 km** (a safety factor of ~2× to account for dynamic loading peaks).

**(iii) Two additional maintenance steps (1 mark):**
1. **Verify the cold inflation pressure** on all four positions — staggered tyres often have different recommended pressures (e.g., 32 psi front / 35 psi rear).
2. **Perform a wheel alignment check** — premature rear-tyre wear in a staggered setup is frequently caused by **rear toe misalignment** or worn rear trailing-arm bushes.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam**

- **Definition:** Tyre rotation = periodic interchanging of tyre positions to equalise wear.
- **Standard interval:** **5,000 – 10,000 km** (or 6 months).
- **Legal minimum tread depth:** **1.6 mm** (passenger), **5 mm** (commercial heavy vehicles in India).
- **Five canonical patterns:**
  - Forward Cross → **RWD**
  - Rearward Cross → **FWD**
  - Front-to-Rear → **Directional** tyres
  - Side-to-Side → **Staggered** fitment (rear)
  - Five-Tyre → **AWD + spare**
- **Wear-pattern diagnostic table:**

| Pattern | Cause | Action |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Both shoulders | Under-inflation | Re-inflate → rotate |
| Centre only | Over-inflation | Deflate → rotate |
| One side only | Excess camber | Align → rotate |
| Cupping | Worn dampers | Replace → rotate |
| Feathering | Toe error | Toe-align → rotate |

- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  - $W = (T_{\text{new}} - T_{\text{current}}) / D$ — wear rate
  - $D_{\text{rem}} = (T_{\text{current}} - T_{\min}) / W$ — remaining life
  - $k = W_f / W_r$ — FWD wear ratio (typical 1.6 – 2.2)
- **Workshop procedure:** Inspect → Measure → Re-inflate → Re-mount (star torque) → Re-torque at 50 km.
- **Critical pitfall:** *Always* correct the underlying wear cause **before** rotation — rotation alone is a **redistribution**, not a **cure**.
- **Staggered fitment rule:** Never cross-rotate between front and rear if the rolling radii differ by more than **±5%**.
- **Directional-tyre rule:** Never swap left ↔ right — only front ↔ rear.
- **Examiner's tip:** In 14-mark answers, **always include a labelled sketch** of the chosen pattern (worth 1–2 marks) and **state the inspection step** before rotation (worth 1 mark).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wheel Balancing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheel Balancing** is the process of compensating for the unequal distribution of mass in a wheel-tire assembly so that it rotates about its true axis of symmetry without generating centrifugal forces or couples. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based education, it is defined as:

> The equalization of mass distribution around the axis of rotation of a wheel-tire assembly such that the principal axis of inertia coincides with the geometric axis of rotation, thereby eliminating undesirable vibration, uneven tire wear, and steering instability.

The wheel-tire assembly is considered **balanced** when the principal inertia axis coincides with the rotational axis. Any deviation creates a mass eccentricity, which when rotated produces a centrifugal force vector rotating with the wheel, transmitting vibration to the suspension and steering system.

### Types of Imbalance

| Imbalance Type | Definition | Visual Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Imbalance** | Single heavy spot; center of mass offset from rotation axis in a single plane | Up-down bounce (hop) at one position per revolution |
| **Dynamic Imbalance** | Two equal heavy spots 180° apart in different axial planes | Side-to-side wobble / shimmy, twice per revolution |
| **Couple Imbalance** | Two equal masses on opposite sides, creating a pure couple with no net force | Wobble without vertical bounce |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 boards frequently test the **difference between static and dynamic imbalance**. Static is corrected in a single plane; dynamic requires correction in two planes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are spinning a **bicycle wheel** with a small piece of chewing gum stuck on one side of the rim. The wheel will not rotate smoothly — you will feel a strong **wobble** and a periodic "thud" in your hands once every revolution. That thud is the **centrifugal force** generated by the gum (the heavy spot) trying to fly outward as the wheel spins.

Now imagine gluing **two identical pieces of gum on opposite sides of the wheel**, but at different distances from the center. The wheel will still wobble, but now you'll feel a **side-to-side shaking** (like a washing machine with an unbalanced load). This is **dynamic imbalance** — two equal forces, but separated axially, create a rotating **couple**.

**Wheel balancing is simply finding where to glue tiny counter-weights (or remove material) to cancel out these unwanted forces and couples.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Wheel Balancing Matters (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - Prevents premature **tire wear** (scalloping, cupping)
> - Eliminates **steering wheel vibration** above 50 km/h
> - Reduces load on **wheel bearings, suspension bushings, and shock absorbers**
> - Improves **ride comfort** and **vehicle safety**
> - Extends tire life by up to **60%** (industry benchmark)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard Tolerances:**
  - Passenger car wheels: **≤ 5 grams** static residual unbalance
  - Motorcycle wheels: **≤ 2 grams**
  - Commercial truck wheels: **≤ 50 grams**
- **Units of Unbalance:** **gram-millimeter (g·mm)** or **ounce-inch (oz·in)**
  - 1 oz·in = 28.35 g·mm (approximately)
- **Service Speed for Balancing:** Typically performed at speeds equivalent to **80–120 km/h** simulation
- **Mounting Cone Tolerance:** Hub bore concentricity must be within **0.05 mm**

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wheel Unbalance

When a wheel of mass $M$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) with a mass eccentricity $e$ (distance between geometric axis and principal inertia axis), an unbalanced centrifugal force is generated:

$$F = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$$

This force is **not static** — it rotates with the wheel at the same angular velocity. It can be resolved into:
- **Vertical component** → causes hop (vertical bounce)
- **Horizontal component** → causes wobble (lateral shake)

### 2.1.1 Why Unbalance Grows with Speed

Because the centrifugal force is proportional to $\omega^2$, doubling the rotational speed **quadruples** the unbalanced force. This is why vibration typically becomes noticeable only above **50–60 km/h** and becomes severe at highway speeds.

### 2.1.2 Resolution of Forces in a Single Plane

For a single heavy spot at radius $r$ from the center, with mass $m$ (the unbalanced mass):
$$F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula**. The product $m \cdot r$ (mass × radius) is called the **unbalance** and is measured in **g·mm**.

## 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Balancing — The Distinction

### Static Balancing
A wheel is **statically balanced** if it can rest in any angular position without rotating due to gravity — i.e., the center of gravity lies on the rotational axis. This only addresses a **single-plane** heavy spot and is sufficient for **narrow tires** (motorcycles, vintage cars).

### Dynamic Balancing
A wheel is **dynamically balanced** when it rotates without any couple, i.e., the principal axis of inertia coincides with the rotation axis in **two correction planes** (typically the inner and outer rim flanges). This is mandatory for **modern wide, low-profile tires** where the mass distribution along the axle is critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A wheel can be **statically balanced but dynamically unbalanced**. This is the most common KTU conceptual trap.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force from unbalance | Newtons (N) | Force generated by heavy spot |
| 2 | $U = m \cdot r$ | Unbalance magnitude | gram-mm (g·mm) | Direct reading from balancer |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s | Convert rpm to rad/s |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Peripheral (tire) speed | m/s | Speed check |
| 5 | $F_c = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | Total centrifugal force on wheel | N | Full wheel analysis |
| 6 | $C = m \cdot r \cdot d$ | Couple unbalance (dynamic) | g·mm² | Two-plane imbalance |
| 7 | $\omega^2 = \left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$ | Square of angular velocity | (rad/s)² | Force scaling |
| 8 | $f_{vib} = \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Vibration frequency | Hz | Steering column resonance |
| 9 | $a = e \cdot \omega^2$ | Centripetal acceleration | m/s² | Hub acceleration |
| 10 | $\Delta U_{1,2} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1+d_2} \cdot U_{total}$ | Plane split ratio | g·mm | Static-to-dynamic conversion |

Where:
- $m$ = unbalanced mass (g)
- $r$ = radius of unbalance location (mm)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $D$ = tire diameter (m)
- $e$ = mass eccentricity (m)
- $d$ = axial distance between correction planes (mm)
- $U$ = unbalance (g·mm)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Production:** Every new car wheel-tire assembly is dynamically balanced at the factory using automated balancers with laser measurement.
- **Service Centers:** Balancing is part of routine tire rotation every **10,000 km** or at every tire change.
- **Performance Vehicles:** Formula 1 and high-performance cars use **tire uniformity grading** and match-mounting, where the tire is rotated on the rim to minimize the combined unbalance of the assembly.
- **Trucking Industry:** Large truck wheels are balanced to within **50 g** with the loaded vehicle mass in mind.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force from a Single Heavy Spot

**Given:**
- A wheel of mass $M$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$
- A small unbalanced mass $m$ located at radius $r$ from the rotation axis
- The geometric axis and the inertia axis are separated by eccentricity $e$

**Step 1:** The unbalanced mass $m$ undergoes circular motion of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$. The centripetal acceleration is:
$$a_c = r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 2:** By Newton's second law, the centripetal force required to keep mass $m$ in circular motion is:
$$F_c = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 3:** Since the wheel is rigid, this force is transmitted to the axle through the spokes/rim. The reactive force at the bearing is equal and opposite:
$$F_{bearing} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 4:** Expressing angular velocity in terms of rpm $N$:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$F = m \cdot r \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying the constant:
$$\left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{3600} = \frac{\pi^2}{900} \approx 0.01097$$

So:
$$F \approx 0.01097 \cdot m \cdot r \cdot N^2 \quad \text{(with } m \text{ in kg, } r \text{ in m, } F \text{ in N)}$$

> This derivation is **central to KTU numerical problems** — students are typically asked to compute unbalance force at a given speed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Static-to-Dynamic Unbalance Split (Two-Plane Method)

**Given:**
- A wheel-tire assembly with total unbalance $U_{total}$
- Correction planes $P_1$ (outer rim flange) and $P_2$ (inner rim flange)
- Axial distance between planes = $L$
- Unbalance located at axial position $x$ from $P_1$

**Step 1:** By the principle of moments, the unbalance at plane $P_1$ is:
$$U_1 = U_{total} \cdot \frac{L - x}{L}$$

**Step 2:** The unbalance at plane $P_2$ is:
$$U_2 = U_{total} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 3:** The angle between the two unbalances (when $x \neq L/2$) defines the **dynamic couple**:
$$C = U_1 \cdot U_2 \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4:** For a wheel balancer reading, the machine measures $U_1$ and $U_2$ directly and indicates the angular position for placement of correction weights.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A passenger car wheel of total mass 35 kg has a static unbalance equivalent to 30 g at a radius of 250 mm. Calculate the centrifugal force at:
1. 60 km/h (city driving)
2. 120 km/h (highway driving)

**Given:**
- $m = 30 \text{ g} = 0.030 \text{ kg}$
- $r = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$
- Tire diameter $D \approx 0.6 \text{ m}$, so radius $R \approx 0.30 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Find rotational speed at 60 km/h**

Linear speed $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$

Rotational speed:
$$N = \frac{v}{2\pi R} = \frac{16.67}{2\pi \times 0.30} = \frac{16.67}{1.885} \approx 8.84 \text{ rev/s}$$

In rpm:
$$N = 8.84 \times 60 \approx 530 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Compute angular velocity**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 530}{60} \approx 55.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute centrifugal force at 60 km/h**
$$F_1 = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 0.030 \times 0.250 \times (55.5)^2$$
$$F_1 = 0.0075 \times 3080 \approx 23.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for 120 km/h (double the linear speed → double rpm → quadruple force)**

$N_{120} = 1060 \text{ rpm}$, $\omega_{120} = 111 \text{ rad/s}$

$$F_2 = 0.030 \times 0.250 \times (111)^2 = 0.0075 \times 12321 \approx 92.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Compare and conclude**
$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = 4 \quad \text{(confirms the } \omega^2 \text{ relationship)}$$

> **Conclusion:** A mere 30 g of unbalance generates **23 N at city speed** and **92 N at highway speed** — equivalent to roughly **9 kg of constant vertical force** on the suspension at 120 km/h. This is why drivers perceive vibration only above certain speeds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Balancing Methods Comparison

| Method | Plane(s) | Application | Equipment Cost | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bubble Balancer (Static)** | Single (ground) | Motorcycle, narrow tires, off-road | Very Low | ±5 g |
| **Spin Balancer (Off-Car, Dynamic)** | Two (rim flanges) | All modern passenger car wheels | Medium | ±1 g |
| **On-Car Road Force Balancer** | Two (tire contact patch) | High-end service, runout correction | High | ±1 g |
| **Match Mounting** | N/A — tire rotation on rim | OEM and premium aftermarket | Specialized | Variable |
| **Self-Balancing Tires (Centramatic)** | Internal | Heavy trucks, RVs | Specialized | ±10 g |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Static Imbalance — Heavy Spot Force Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel-Tire Assembly] --> B[Rotate at Angular Velocity omega]
    B --> C{Is there mass eccentricity e?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Heavy Spot at radius r generates Centrifugal Force F = m*r*omega squared]
    D --> E[Force vector rotates with wheel]
    E --> F[Vertical component causes HOP bounce once per revolution]
    C -- No --> G[Statically Balanced - no vertical vibration]
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Imbalance — Two-Plane Couple Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heavy Spot in Plane P1 Left] --> C[Force F1 = m1*r1*omega squared]
    B[Heavy Spot in Plane P2 Right] --> D[Force F2 = m2*r2*omega squared]
    C --> E{F1 and F2 are equal but axially separated?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Pure Couple C = F * d generated]
    F --> G[Couple rotates with wheel]
    G --> H[Causes WOBBLE twice per revolution]
    E -- No --> I[Net force + couple = combined static and dynamic imbalance]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Wheel Balancer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputSection [Input Stage]
        A1[Wheel Mounted on Cone Adapter] --> A2[Hub Bore Aligned within 0.05 mm]
        A2 --> A3[Wheel Guard Closed for Safety]
    end

    subgraph SensingStage [Sensing Stage]
        B1[Drive Motor Spins Wheel to 80 to 120 rpm] --> B2[Piezoelectric or Strain Gauge Sensors on Spindle]
        B2 --> B3[Sensors Detect Vibration Amplitude and Phase]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingStage [Computation Stage]
        C1[Microprocessor Converts Signal] --> C2[Calculates Unbalance U1 and U2 in g mm]
        C2 --> C3[Determines Angular Position for Correction Weights]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [Output Stage]
        D1[Digital Display Shows Inner Plane Reading] --> D2[Digital Display Shows Outer Plane Reading]
        D2 --> D3[LED or Laser Indicator Marks Weight Position]
    end

    InputSection --> SensingStage
    SensingStage --> ProcessingStage
    ProcessingStage --> OutputStage
```

## 4.4 Wheel Balancer Correction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Wheel Mounted]) --> Clean[Clean Rim Flanges Remove Old Weights]
    Clean --> Enter[Enter Wheel Dimensions Diameter and Width]
    Enter --> Spin[Initiate Automatic Spin Cycle]
    Spin --> Read[Read Unbalance Values U1 and U2 from Display]
    Read --> Decision{U1 and U2 within tolerance 5g?}
    Decision -- No --> Place[Place Corrective Weight at Indicated Angle]
    Place --> ReSpin[Re-spin to Verify]
    ReSpin --> Read
    Decision -- Yes --> Done([Balancing Complete])
```

## 4.5 Effect of Unbalance vs Vehicle Speed (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mass Eccentricity e in mm] --> B[Centrifugal Force F proportional to omega squared]
    B --> C{Vehicle Speed}
    C -- Below 40 km/h --> D[Vibration imperceptible to driver]
    C -- 50 to 80 km/h --> E[Steering wheel vibration begins, tire wear starts]
    C -- Above 100 km/h --> F[Severe vibration, loss of steering control risk, suspension damage]
    E --> G[Symptom: Scalloped tire wear pattern]
    F --> H[Symptom: Driver fatigue, brake judder, bearing failure]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define static and dynamic wheel imbalance. State the practical significance of each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static Imbalance (1.5 marks):** Static imbalance is a condition in a wheel-tire assembly where the center of gravity does not lie on the axis of rotation. It results from a single heavy spot causing a mass eccentricity in one plane. Practically, it produces a **vertical hop or bounce** once per revolution and is corrected by placing a single counterweight opposite the heavy spot.

**Dynamic Imbalance (1.5 marks):** Dynamic imbalance is a condition where two equal heavy masses are located 180° apart in different axial planes, producing no net force but a rotating **couple**. Practically, it causes a **side-to-side wobble or shimmy** twice per revolution and is critical in modern wide tires. It is corrected using **two correction planes** (inner and outer rim flanges) with weights placed at different angles.

---

### Question 2: Why is dynamic balancing necessary even after a wheel has been statically balanced? Give a numerical justification.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A wheel that is statically balanced has its **center of gravity on the rotation axis**, eliminating the vertical hop. However, the **principal axis of inertia** may still be inclined to the rotation axis due to mass distribution along the wheel's width (2 marks).

This results in a **rotating couple** (dynamic imbalance) causing side-to-side wobble. **Numerical Justification (1 mark):** Consider two masses of 25 g each placed 200 mm apart axially. Even though the system is statically balanced (zero net force), the couple generated at 1000 rpm is:

$$C = m \cdot r \cdot d \cdot \omega^2 = 0.025 \times 0.200 \times 0.200 \times (104.7)^2 \approx 10.96 \text{ N·m}$$

This couple is sufficient to cause measurable steering wheel vibration. Hence, **dynamic balancing is mandatory** for all modern automotive wheels.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between static and dynamic wheel imbalance. Describe how each is detected and corrected on a modern computerised wheel balancer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car wheel of 0.6 m diameter has a static unbalance of 40 g at a radius of 300 mm. Calculate:
   (i) The centrifugal force generated at 80 km/h
   (ii) The corresponding force at 160 km/h
   (iii) Comment on the trend and its engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define Static Imbalance (1 mark)**

Static imbalance exists when the wheel's center of gravity does not lie on the axis of rotation due to a single heavy spot in one plane. The wheel will not rest in any arbitrary position.

**Step 2: Sketch and Effect (1.5 marks)**

```
        Heavy Spot (m) at radius r
              ●
             /|
            / |
           /  | r
          /   |
   ______/____|________ Axis of rotation
         O
```

The heavy spot creates a centrifugal force $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ that rotates with the wheel. The vertical component causes a once-per-revolution hop. Detection is done by placing the wheel on a bubble balancer or by using the static mode of a spin balancer. Correction is by a single weight placed **180° opposite** the heavy spot in the same plane.

**Step 3: Define Dynamic Imbalance (1.5 marks)**

Dynamic imbalance occurs when two equal heavy masses are located on opposite sides of the wheel at **different axial positions** (different distances from the wheel center along its width). Although the net force is zero (the masses are equal and opposite in the radial direction), the axial separation creates a rotating **couple**.

**Step 4: Detection on Computerised Balancer (2 marks)**

A modern spin balancer:
1. Spins the wheel to a calibrated rpm (typically 80–120 rpm simulation equivalent)
2. Piezoelectric sensors on the spindle measure vibration amplitude and phase
3. The processor separates the signal into two planes (inner and outer)
4. The display indicates the magnitude (in grams) and angular position of correction weights for **both** planes
5. The technician glues clip-on or stick-on weights and re-spins for verification

**Step 5: Correction (1 mark)**

- Static only: One weight in single plane
- Dynamic: Two weights, in two planes, at different angles as indicated by the machine. Tolerance ≤ 5 g per plane for passenger cars.

---

### Solution A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Tire diameter $D = 0.6$ m → Tire radius $R = 0.30$ m
- Unbalance mass $m = 40$ g $= 0.040$ kg
- Unbalance radius $r = 300$ mm $= 0.300$ m
- Speeds: 80 km/h and 160 km/h

**Step 1: Find rotational speed at 80 km/h (1 mark)**

$$v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

$$N_1 = \frac{v_1}{2\pi R} = \frac{22.22}{2\pi \times 0.30} = \frac{22.22}{1.885} \approx 11.79 \text{ rev/s} = 707.4 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Find angular velocity (1 mark)**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 707.4}{60} \approx 74.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute force at 80 km/h (1.5 marks)**

$$F_1 = m \cdot r \cdot \omega_1^2 = 0.040 \times 0.300 \times (74.07)^2$$
$$F_1 = 0.012 \times 5486.4 \approx 65.84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: For 160 km/h — double the speed means double rpm (1 mark)**

$$N_2 = 1414.8 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_2 = 148.14 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute force at 160 km/h (1.5 marks)**

$$F_2 = 0.040 \times 0.300 \times (148.14)^2 = 0.012 \times 21945.5 \approx 263.35 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Comment (1 mark)**

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{263.35}{65.84} = 4.00 \quad \text{(exact } \omega^2 \text{ scaling)}$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- Doubling the vehicle speed **quadruples** the unbalance force
- A 40 g imbalance that produces ~66 N at 80 km/h generates ~263 N at 160 km/h (equivalent to hanging a 27 kg mass on the wheel)
- This explains why vibration complaints emerge **only at highway speeds**
- Routine wheel balancing is essential to prevent tire wear, suspension damage, and driver fatigue

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a computerised dynamic wheel balancer. List the key components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various sources of wheel unbalance in an automobile. Explain the technique of **match mounting** and its role in reducing imbalance. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution B (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for the complete block diagram. Key stages are:

1. **Wheel Mounting Stage** — Cone adapter, hub-centric clamping
2. **Drive & Spin Stage** — Belt-driven or direct-drive motor spins the wheel
3. **Sensing Stage** — Piezoelectric crystals or strain gauges on the spindle measure force
4. **Signal Processing Stage** — Microprocessor with FFT and phase-detection algorithms
5. **Display Stage** — LCD/LED panel shows $U_1$ (inner) and $U_2$ (outer) values in grams, plus angular position

**Key Components and Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cone Adapters** | Self-centering the wheel on the balancer spindle |
| **Drive Motor** | Spins wheel at constant calibrated rpm |
| **Piezoelectric Sensors** | Convert mechanical vibration to electrical signal |
| **Strain Gauges** | Measure spindle deflection due to unbalance force |
| **Microprocessor** | Performs FFT, separates static and dynamic components |
| **Display Unit** | Shows magnitude (g) and angle (°) for both planes |
| **Weight Tray & Caliper** | Pre-measured weights and placement tool |
| **Wheel Guard** | Safety interlock |
| **Calibration Weight** | Reference for self-test (typically 50 g or 100 g) |

---

### Solution B (b) — 7 Marks

**Sources of Wheel Unbalance (3 marks):**

1. **Tire manufacturing defects** — non-uniformity in ply, sidewall thickness, or rubber density
2. **Rim manufacturing defects** — eccentric bolt holes, off-center hub bore, runout
3. **Valve stem weight** — although small (~10 g), it acts as a fixed heavy spot
4. **Tire wear pattern** — uneven wear from prior imbalance creates new imbalance
5. **Wheel damage** — dents, bent flanges from potholes
6. **Mounting errors** — dirt on rim, improper centering on hub
7. **Material loss** — chunks missing from tire (e.g., from curb impacts)

**Match Mounting Technique (4 marks):**

Match mounting is the practice of **rotating the tire on the rim** to align the tire's "high point" (heaviest spot of the tire uniformity) with the rim's "low point" (lightest spot of the rim). 

**Process:**
1. Mount tire on rim and inflate
2. Measure the tire's high point using a runout gauge
3. Mark both the tire and rim at their respective high/low points
4. Inflate and rotate tire on rim until the high points are diametrically opposite
5. Re-inflate to operating pressure
6. Mount on the vehicle and re-measure unbalance — typically reduced by **50–80%** before adding any weights

**Role in Reducing Imbalance:**
- It compensates for manufacturing variations in both tire and rim
- It minimizes the **amount of correction weight** needed
- Improves ride comfort, especially for low-profile high-performance tires
- Mandatory at the OEM level for premium vehicles

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s when computing rotational speed, or g to kg when computing force. Always show the conversion step.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Static and Dynamic:** A static-only balance cannot correct dynamic imbalance. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 
> 3. **Missing the $\omega^2$ Relationship:** When speed doubles, force **quadruples** — not doubles. This is a high-weightage test point.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch:** In 7-mark questions, a labeled diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Effect on Suspension:** Don't just compute force — comment on its real-world effect (tire wear, bearing damage, driver fatigue).
> 
> 6. **Wrong Plane for Correction Weight:** Students often place weights on the tire sidewall. Correction weights go on the **rim flange**, not the tire.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Wheel Balancing** = equalizing mass distribution so that the inertia axis coincides with the rotation axis
- **Static Imbalance** → single heavy spot → vertical **hop** (1× per revolution) → **one-plane correction**
- **Dynamic Imbalance** → two heavy spots 180° apart axially → side wobble (2× per revolution) → **two-plane correction**
- **Core Formula:** $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ — force grows with the **square** of speed
- **Unbalance Unit:** $U = m \cdot r$ in **gram-millimeter (g·mm)**
- **Speed-RPM Conversion:** $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{2\pi R}$, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- **Tolerance:** ≤ **5 g** for passenger cars, ≤ **2 g** for motorcycles, ≤ **50 g** for trucks
- **Balancing Methods:** Bubble (static) → Spin balancer (dynamic) → On-car road force balancer (premium) → Match mounting (OEM)
- **Key Components of Computerised Balancer:** Drive motor, piezoelectric sensors, microprocessor, display unit, cone adapter, calibration weight
- **Match Mounting:** Align tire's high point with rim's low point → reduces correction weight needed
- **Vibration Threshold:** Driver perceives imbalance typically above **50–60 km/h**
- **Service Interval:** Every **10,000 km** or at every tire change
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** $\omega^2$ scaling, static vs dynamic distinction, balancer block diagram, match mounting procedure
- **SI Unit Reminder:** Always use **kg, m, rad/s, N** in formulas; convert at the start

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Boards:** Numerical problems on wheel balancing almost always involve the $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ formula. Memorize the unit conversions and the $\omega^2$ relationship — these are worth **3–4 marks** by themselves.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wheel Unbalance

When a wheel of mass $M$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) with a mass eccentricity $e$ (distance between geometric axis and principal inertia axis), an unbalanced centrifugal force is generated:

$$F = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$$

This force is **not static** — it rotates with the wheel at the same angular velocity. It can be resolved into:
- **Vertical component** → causes hop (vertical bounce)
- **Horizontal component** → causes wobble (lateral shake)

### 2.1.1 Why Unbalance Grows with Speed

Because the centrifugal force is proportional to $\omega^2$, doubling the rotational speed **quadruples** the unbalanced force. This is why vibration typically becomes noticeable only above **50–60 km/h** and becomes severe at highway speeds.

### 2.1.2 Resolution of Forces in a Single Plane

For a single heavy spot at radius $r$ from the center, with mass $m$ (the unbalanced mass):
$$F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula**. The product $m \cdot r$ (mass × radius) is called the **unbalance** and is measured in **g·mm**.

## 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Balancing — The Distinction

### Static Balancing
A wheel is **statically balanced** if it can rest in any angular position without rotating due to gravity — i.e., the center of gravity lies on the rotational axis. This only addresses a **single-plane** heavy spot and is sufficient for **narrow tires** (motorcycles, vintage cars).

### Dynamic Balancing
A wheel is **dynamically balanced** when it rotates without any couple, i.e., the principal axis of inertia coincides with the rotation axis in **two correction planes** (typically the inner and outer rim flanges). This is mandatory for **modern wide, low-profile tires** where the mass distribution along the axle is critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A wheel can be **statically balanced but dynamically unbalanced**. This is the most common KTU conceptual trap.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force from unbalance | Newtons (N) | Force generated by heavy spot |
| 2 | $U = m \cdot r$ | Unbalance magnitude | gram-mm (g·mm) | Direct reading from balancer |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s | Convert rpm to rad/s |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Peripheral (tire) speed | m/s | Speed check |
| 5 | $F_c = M \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | Total centrifugal force on wheel | N | Full wheel analysis |
| 6 | $C = m \cdot r \cdot d$ | Couple unbalance (dynamic) | g·mm² | Two-plane imbalance |
| 7 | $\omega^2 = \left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$ | Square of angular velocity | (rad/s)² | Force scaling |
| 8 | $f_{vib} = \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Vibration frequency | Hz | Steering column resonance |
| 9 | $a = e \cdot \omega^2$ | Centripetal acceleration | m/s² | Hub acceleration |
| 10 | $\Delta U_{1,2} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1+d_2} \cdot U_{total}$ | Plane split ratio | g·mm | Static-to-dynamic conversion |

Where:
- $m$ = unbalanced mass (g)
- $r$ = radius of unbalance location (mm)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $D$ = tire diameter (m)
- $e$ = mass eccentricity (m)
- $d$ = axial distance between correction planes (mm)
- $U$ = unbalance (g·mm)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Production:** Every new car wheel-tire assembly is dynamically balanced at the factory using automated balancers with laser measurement.
- **Service Centers:** Balancing is part of routine tire rotation every **10,000 km** or at every tire change.
- **Performance Vehicles:** Formula 1 and high-performance cars use **tire uniformity grading** and match-mounting, where the tire is rotated on the rim to minimize the combined unbalance of the assembly.
- **Trucking Industry:** Large truck wheels are balanced to within **50 g** with the loaded vehicle mass in mind.
