# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force from a Single Heavy Spot

**Given:**
- A wheel of mass $M$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$
- A small unbalanced mass $m$ located at radius $r$ from the rotation axis
- The geometric axis and the inertia axis are separated by eccentricity $e$

**Step 1:** The unbalanced mass $m$ undergoes circular motion of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$. The centripetal acceleration is:
$$a_c = r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 2:** By Newton's second law, the centripetal force required to keep mass $m$ in circular motion is:
$$F_c = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 3:** Since the wheel is rigid, this force is transmitted to the axle through the spokes/rim. The reactive force at the bearing is equal and opposite:
$$F_{bearing} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step 4:** Expressing angular velocity in terms of rpm $N$:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$F = m \cdot r \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying the constant:
$$\left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{3600} = \frac{\pi^2}{900} \approx 0.01097$$

So:
$$F \approx 0.01097 \cdot m \cdot r \cdot N^2 \quad \text{(with } m \text{ in kg, } r \text{ in m, } F \text{ in N)}$$

> This derivation is **central to KTU numerical problems** — students are typically asked to compute unbalance force at a given speed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Static-to-Dynamic Unbalance Split (Two-Plane Method)

**Given:**
- A wheel-tire assembly with total unbalance $U_{total}$
- Correction planes $P_1$ (outer rim flange) and $P_2$ (inner rim flange)
- Axial distance between planes = $L$
- Unbalance located at axial position $x$ from $P_1$

**Step 1:** By the principle of moments, the unbalance at plane $P_1$ is:
$$U_1 = U_{total} \cdot \frac{L - x}{L}$$

**Step 2:** The unbalance at plane $P_2$ is:
$$U_2 = U_{total} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 3:** The angle between the two unbalances (when $x \neq L/2$) defines the **dynamic couple**:
$$C = U_1 \cdot U_2 \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4:** For a wheel balancer reading, the machine measures $U_1$ and $U_2$ directly and indicates the angular position for placement of correction weights.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A passenger car wheel of total mass 35 kg has a static unbalance equivalent to 30 g at a radius of 250 mm. Calculate the centrifugal force at:
1. 60 km/h (city driving)
2. 120 km/h (highway driving)

**Given:**
- $m = 30 \text{ g} = 0.030 \text{ kg}$
- $r = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$
- Tire diameter $D \approx 0.6 \text{ m}$, so radius $R \approx 0.30 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Find rotational speed at 60 km/h**

Linear speed $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$

Rotational speed:
$$N = \frac{v}{2\pi R} = \frac{16.67}{2\pi \times 0.30} = \frac{16.67}{1.885} \approx 8.84 \text{ rev/s}$$

In rpm:
$$N = 8.84 \times 60 \approx 530 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Compute angular velocity**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 530}{60} \approx 55.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute centrifugal force at 60 km/h**
$$F_1 = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 0.030 \times 0.250 \times (55.5)^2$$
$$F_1 = 0.0075 \times 3080 \approx 23.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for 120 km/h (double the linear speed → double rpm → quadruple force)**

$N_{120} = 1060 \text{ rpm}$, $\omega_{120} = 111 \text{ rad/s}$

$$F_2 = 0.030 \times 0.250 \times (111)^2 = 0.0075 \times 12321 \approx 92.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Compare and conclude**
$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = 4 \quad \text{(confirms the } \omega^2 \text{ relationship)}$$

> **Conclusion:** A mere 30 g of unbalance generates **23 N at city speed** and **92 N at highway speed** — equivalent to roughly **9 kg of constant vertical force** on the suspension at 120 km/h. This is why drivers perceive vibration only above certain speeds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Balancing Methods Comparison

| Method | Plane(s) | Application | Equipment Cost | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bubble Balancer (Static)** | Single (ground) | Motorcycle, narrow tires, off-road | Very Low | ±5 g |
| **Spin Balancer (Off-Car, Dynamic)** | Two (rim flanges) | All modern passenger car wheels | Medium | ±1 g |
| **On-Car Road Force Balancer** | Two (tire contact patch) | High-end service, runout correction | High | ±1 g |
| **Match Mounting** | N/A — tire rotation on rim | OEM and premium aftermarket | Specialized | Variable |
| **Self-Balancing Tires (Centramatic)** | Internal | Heavy trucks, RVs | Specialized | ±10 g |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Static Imbalance — Heavy Spot Force Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel-Tire Assembly] --> B[Rotate at Angular Velocity omega]
    B --> C{Is there mass eccentricity e?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Heavy Spot at radius r generates Centrifugal Force F = m*r*omega squared]
    D --> E[Force vector rotates with wheel]
    E --> F[Vertical component causes HOP bounce once per revolution]
    C -- No --> G[Statically Balanced - no vertical vibration]
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Imbalance — Two-Plane Couple Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heavy Spot in Plane P1 Left] --> C[Force F1 = m1*r1*omega squared]
    B[Heavy Spot in Plane P2 Right] --> D[Force F2 = m2*r2*omega squared]
    C --> E{F1 and F2 are equal but axially separated?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Pure Couple C = F * d generated]
    F --> G[Couple rotates with wheel]
    G --> H[Causes WOBBLE twice per revolution]
    E -- No --> I[Net force plus couple equals combined static and dynamic imbalance]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Wheel Balancer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputSection [Input Stage]
        A1[Wheel Mounted on Cone Adapter] --> A2[Hub Bore Aligned within 0.05 mm]
        A2 --> A3[Wheel Guard Closed for Safety]
    end

    subgraph SensingStage [Sensing Stage]
        B1[Drive Motor Spins Wheel to 80 to 120 rpm] --> B2[Piezoelectric or Strain Gauge Sensors on Spindle]
        B2 --> B3[Sensors Detect Vibration Amplitude and Phase]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingStage [Computation Stage]
        C1[Microprocessor Converts Signal] --> C2[Calculates Unbalance U1 and U2 in g mm]
        C2 --> C3[Determines Angular Position for Correction Weights]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [Output Stage]
        D1[Digital Display Shows Inner Plane Reading] --> D2[Digital Display Shows Outer Plane Reading]
        D2 --> D3[LED or Laser Indicator Marks Weight Position]
    end

    InputSection --> SensingStage
    SensingStage --> ProcessingStage
    ProcessingStage --> OutputStage
```

## 4.4 Wheel Balancer Correction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Wheel Mounted]) --> Clean[Clean Rim Flanges Remove Old Weights]
    Clean --> Enter[Enter Wheel Dimensions Diameter and Width]
    Enter --> Spin[Initiate Automatic Spin Cycle]
    Spin --> Read[Read Unbalance Values U1 and U2 from Display]
    Read --> Decision{U1 and U2 within tolerance 5g?}
    Decision -- No --> Place[Place Corrective Weight at Indicated Angle]
    Place --> ReSpin[Re-spin to Verify]
    ReSpin --> Read
    Decision -- Yes --> Done([Balancing Complete])
```

## 4.5 Effect of Unbalance vs Vehicle Speed (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mass Eccentricity e in mm] --> B[Centrifugal Force F proportional to omega squared]
    B --> C{Vehicle Speed}
    C -- Below 40 km/h --> D[Vibration imperceptible to driver]
    C -- 50 to 80 km/h --> E[Steering wheel vibration begins, tire wear starts]
    C -- Above 100 km/h --> F[Severe vibration, loss of steering control risk, suspension damage]
    E --> G[Symptom: Scalloped tire wear pattern]
    F --> H[Symptom: Driver fatigue, brake judder, bearing failure]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define static and dynamic wheel imbalance. State the practical significance of each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static Imbalance (1.5 marks):** Static imbalance is a condition in a wheel-tire assembly where the center of gravity does not lie on the axis of rotation. It results from a single heavy spot causing a mass eccentricity in one plane. Practically, it produces a **vertical hop or bounce** once per revolution and is corrected by placing a single counterweight opposite the heavy spot.

**Dynamic Imbalance (1.5 marks):** Dynamic imbalance is a condition where two equal heavy masses are located 180° apart in different axial planes, producing no net force but a rotating **couple**. Practically, it causes a **side-to-side wobble or shimmy** twice per revolution and is critical in modern wide tires. It is corrected using **two correction planes** (inner and outer rim flanges) with weights placed at different angles.

---

### Question 2: Why is dynamic balancing necessary even after a wheel has been statically balanced? Give a numerical justification.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A wheel that is statically balanced has its **center of gravity on the rotation axis**, eliminating the vertical hop. However, the **principal axis of inertia** may still be inclined to the rotation axis due to mass distribution along the wheel's width (2 marks).

This results in a **rotating couple** (dynamic imbalance) causing side-to-side wobble. **Numerical Justification (1 mark):** Consider two masses of 25 g each placed 200 mm apart axially. Even though the system is statically balanced (zero net force), the couple generated at 1000 rpm is:

$$C = m \cdot r \cdot d \cdot \omega^2 = 0.025 \times 0.200 \times 0.200 \times (104.7)^2 \approx 10.96 \text{ N·m}$$

This couple is sufficient to cause measurable steering wheel vibration. Hence, **dynamic balancing is mandatory** for all modern automotive wheels.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between static and dynamic wheel imbalance. Describe how each is detected and corrected on a modern computerised wheel balancer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car wheel of 0.6 m diameter has a static unbalance of 40 g at a radius of 300 mm. Calculate:
   (i) The centrifugal force generated at 80 km/h
   (ii) The corresponding force at 160 km/h
   (iii) Comment on the trend and its engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define Static Imbalance (1 mark)**

Static imbalance exists when the wheel's center of gravity does not lie on the axis of rotation due to a single heavy spot in one plane. The wheel will not rest in any arbitrary position.

**Step 2: Sketch and Effect (1.5 marks)**

```
        Heavy Spot (m) at radius r
              dot
             slash
            slash
           slash
          slash
   ______slash________ Axis of rotation
         O
```

The heavy spot creates a centrifugal force $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ that rotates with the wheel. The vertical component causes a once-per-revolution hop. Detection is done by placing the wheel on a bubble balancer or by using the static mode of a spin balancer. Correction is by a single weight placed **180° opposite** the heavy spot in the same plane.

**Step 3: Define Dynamic Imbalance (1.5 marks)**

Dynamic imbalance occurs when two equal heavy masses are located on opposite sides of the wheel at **different axial positions** (different distances from the wheel center along its width). Although the net force is zero (the masses are equal and opposite in the radial direction), the axial separation creates a rotating **couple**.

**Step 4: Detection on Computerised Balancer (2 marks)**

A modern spin balancer:
1. Spins the wheel to a calibrated rpm (typically 80–120 rpm simulation equivalent)
2. Piezoelectric sensors on the spindle measure vibration amplitude and phase
3. The processor separates the signal into two planes (inner and outer)
4. The display indicates the magnitude (in grams) and angular position of correction weights for **both** planes
5. The technician glues clip-on or stick-on weights and re-spins for verification

**Step 5: Correction (1 mark)**

- Static only: One weight in single plane
- Dynamic: Two weights, in two planes, at different angles as indicated by the machine. Tolerance ≤ 5 g per plane for passenger cars.

---

### Solution A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Tire diameter $D = 0.6$ m → Tire radius $R = 0.30$ m
- Unbalance mass $m = 40$ g $= 0.040$ kg
- Unbalance radius $r = 300$ mm $= 0.300$ m
- Speeds: 80 km/h and 160 km/h

**Step 1: Find rotational speed at 80 km/h (1 mark)**

$$v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

$$N_1 = \frac{v_1}{2\pi R} = \frac{22.22}{2\pi \times 0.30} = \frac{22.22}{1.885} \approx 11.79 \text{ rev/s} = 707.4 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Find angular velocity (1 mark)**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 707.4}{60} \approx 74.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute force at 80 km/h (1.5 marks)**

$$F_1 = m \cdot r \cdot \omega_1^2 = 0.040 \times 0.300 \times (74.07)^2$$
$$F_1 = 0.012 \times 5486.4 \approx 65.84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: For 160 km/h — double the speed means double rpm (1 mark)**

$$N_2 = 1414.8 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_2 = 148.14 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute force at 160 km/h (1.5 marks)**

$$F_2 = 0.040 \times 0.300 \times (148.14)^2 = 0.012 \times 21945.5 \approx 263.35 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Comment (1 mark)**

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{263.35}{65.84} = 4.00 \quad \text{(exact } \omega^2 \text{ scaling)}$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- Doubling the vehicle speed **quadruples** the unbalance force
- A 40 g imbalance that produces ~66 N at 80 km/h generates ~263 N at 160 km/h (equivalent to hanging a 27 kg mass on the wheel)
- This explains why vibration complaints emerge **only at highway speeds**
- Routine wheel balancing is essential to prevent tire wear, suspension damage, and driver fatigue

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a computerised dynamic wheel balancer. List the key components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various sources of wheel unbalance in an automobile. Explain the technique of **match mounting** and its role in reducing imbalance. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution B (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for the complete block diagram. Key stages are:

1. **Wheel Mounting Stage** — Cone adapter, hub-centric clamping
2. **Drive and Spin Stage** — Belt-driven or direct-drive motor spins the wheel
3. **Sensing Stage** — Piezoelectric crystals or strain gauges on the spindle measure force
4. **Signal Processing Stage** — Microprocessor with FFT and phase-detection algorithms
5. **Display Stage** — LCD/LED panel shows $U_1$ (inner) and $U_2$ (outer) values in grams, plus angular position

**Key Components and Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cone Adapters** | Self-centering the wheel on the balancer spindle |
| **Drive Motor** | Spins wheel at constant calibrated rpm |
| **Piezoelectric Sensors** | Convert mechanical vibration to electrical signal |
| **Strain Gauges** | Measure spindle deflection due to unbalance force |
| **Microprocessor** | Performs FFT, separates static and dynamic components |
| **Display Unit** | Shows magnitude (g) and angle (deg) for both planes |
| **Weight Tray and Caliper** | Pre-measured weights and placement tool |
| **Wheel Guard** | Safety interlock |
| **Calibration Weight** | Reference for self-test (typically 50 g or 100 g) |

---

### Solution B (b) — 7 Marks

**Sources of Wheel Unbalance (3 marks):**

1. **Tire manufacturing defects** — non-uniformity in ply, sidewall thickness, or rubber density
2. **Rim manufacturing defects** — eccentric bolt holes, off-center hub bore, runout
3. **Valve stem weight** — although small (~10 g), it acts as a fixed heavy spot
4. **Tire wear pattern** — uneven wear from prior imbalance creates new imbalance
5. **Wheel damage** — dents, bent flanges from potholes
6. **Mounting errors** — dirt on rim, improper centering on hub
7. **Material loss** — chunks missing from tire (e.g., from curb impacts)

**Match Mounting Technique (4 marks):**

Match mounting is the practice of **rotating the tire on the rim** to align the tire's "high point" (heaviest spot of the tire uniformity) with the rim's "low point" (lightest spot of the rim). 

**Process:**
1. Mount tire on rim and inflate
2. Measure the tire's high point using a runout gauge
3. Mark both the tire and rim at their respective high/low points
4. Inflate and rotate tire on rim until the high points are diametrically opposite
5. Re-inflate to operating pressure
6. Mount on the vehicle and re-measure unbalance — typically reduced by **50–80%** before adding any weights

**Role in Reducing Imbalance:**
- It compensates for manufacturing variations in both tire and rim
- It minimizes the **amount of correction weight** needed
- Improves ride comfort, especially for low-profile high-performance tires
- Mandatory at the OEM level for premium vehicles

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s when computing rotational speed, or g to kg when computing force. Always show the conversion step.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Static and Dynamic:** A static-only balance cannot correct dynamic imbalance. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 
> 3. **Missing the omega squared Relationship:** When speed doubles, force **quadruples** — not doubles. This is a high-weightage test point.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch:** In 7-mark questions, a labeled diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Effect on Suspension:** Don't just compute force — comment on its real-world effect (tire wear, bearing damage, driver fatigue).
> 
> 6. **Wrong Plane for Correction Weight:** Students often place weights on the tire sidewall. Correction weights go on the **rim flange**, not the tire.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Wheel Balancing** = equalizing mass distribution so that the inertia axis coincides with the rotation axis
- **Static Imbalance** → single heavy spot → vertical **hop** (1x per revolution) → **one-plane correction**
- **Dynamic Imbalance** → two heavy spots 180° apart axially → side wobble (2x per revolution) → **two-plane correction**
- **Core Formula:** $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ — force grows with the **square** of speed
- **Unbalance Unit:** $U = m \cdot r$ in **gram-millimeter (g·mm)**
- **Speed-RPM Conversion:** $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{2\pi R}$, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- **Tolerance:** ≤ **5 g** for passenger cars, ≤ **2 g** for motorcycles, ≤ **50 g** for trucks
- **Balancing Methods:** Bubble (static) → Spin balancer (dynamic) → On-car road force balancer (premium) → Match mounting (OEM)
- **Key Components of Computerised Balancer:** Drive motor, piezoelectric sensors, microprocessor, display unit, cone adapter, calibration weight
- **Match Mounting:** Align tire's high point with rim's low point → reduces correction weight needed
- **Vibration Threshold:** Driver perceives imbalance typically above **50–60 km/h**
- **Service Interval:** Every **10,000 km** or at every tire change
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** omega squared scaling, static vs dynamic distinction, balancer block diagram, match mounting procedure
- **SI Unit Reminder:** Always use **kg, m, rad/s, N** in formulas; convert at the start

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Boards:** Numerical problems on wheel balancing almost always involve the $F = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ formula. Memorize the unit conversions and the omega squared relationship — these are worth **3–4 marks** by themselves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quick Change Wheels and Special Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT303 - AUTOMOTIVE CHASSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quick Change Wheels and Special Wheels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quick Change Wheels — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Change Wheels (QCW)** are specialised wheel mounting and dismounting arrangements used primarily on **agricultural tractors, off-road vehicles, and earthmoving machinery** that allow the operator to alter the **track width, wheel spacing, dual-wheel configuration, or implement compatibility** rapidly — without specialised tools, without hub removal, and often without leaving the operator's seat.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the broad domain of **Tractor Chassis and Undercarriage Systems**, where the wheel assembly must be reconfigurable to suit:

- **Varying row-crop spacings** (e.g., 30", 36", 40", 44" row widths).
- **Different soil traction conditions** (dry field, wet paddy, sandy loam).
- **Dual-wheel operation** for heavy draft loads.
- **Reversed (inverted) mounting** for changing the side of the wheel relative to the axle flange.

The principal engineering requirement is that the change-over time should ideally be less than **5 minutes per wheel**.

## 1.2 Special Wheels — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Wheels** are purpose-built wheel-and-tyre assemblies engineered to deliver **specific traction, flotation, or load-bearing characteristics** beyond what a standard pneumatic road-tyre can offer. They are integral to **off-highway, agricultural, military, and construction vehicles** operating on terrain unsuitable for conventional highway wheels.

The principal categories covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| S.No. | Special Wheel Type | Primary Application |
|-------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Pneumatic Tyre Wheels** | Standard on-road / off-road use |
| 2 | **Solid Rubber Tyres** | Industrial forklifts, heavy industrial trucks |
| 3 | **Cage Wheels (Paddy / Spud Wheels)** | Wet / marshy / paddy field operations |
| 4 | **Ribbed Wheels** | Steering wheels of tractors, planters |
| 5 | **Ground Grip / Lug Wheels** | Loose soil, sand, mud, soft terrain |
| 6 | **Steel (Track-type) Wheels** | Crawler tractors, military tanks |
| 7 | **Flotation Tyres** | Low ground pressure on soft soils |
| 8 | **Dual Wheels** | Heavy load distribution |

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Adjustable Wrench vs. the Fixed Spanner**
>
> Imagine a mechanic working on different bolt sizes. A **fixed spanner** works for only one size; a **quick-change ratchet** adapts instantly. Similarly, a **standard car wheel** is fixed to one track width and one application. A **quick-change tractor wheel**, however, is a "mechanical ratchet" for the chassis — it allows the operator to "click" the wheel into multiple positions (narrow row, wide row, dual, reversed) as easily as changing a wrench size.

**Another analogy for Special Wheels:**

> A standard road tyre is like **wearing dress shoes** — fine on polished floors but useless on a beach. A **cage wheel** is like **wearing basketball shoes on mud** — the wide lugs grip soft soil. A **flotation tyre** is like **snowshoes** — they spread your weight over a wide area. Each special wheel is a "footwear change" tailored to the terrain.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard tractor track widths:** $\mathbf{1.2 \text{ m} \text{ to } 2.1 \text{ m}}$.
- **Common row-crop spacings:** $\mathbf{76 \text{ cm} \; (30")},\; \mathbf{91 \text{ cm} \; (36")},\; \mathbf{102 \text{ cm} \; (40")}$.
- **Dual-wheel spacing (centre-to-centre):** $\mathbf{100 \text{ mm} \text{ to } 200 \text{ mm}}$.
- **Maximum field inflation pressure (standard tractor):** $\mathbf{80 \text{ to } 120 \text{ kPa}}$.
- **Cage wheel diameter (paddy operation):** $\mathbf{1.0 \text{ m} \text{ to } 1.4 \text{ m}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why "Quick Change" is Necessary

The operational logic behind Quick Change Wheels can be broken into the following structured steps:

1. **Field Diversity Mandate:** Modern tractors operate on **multiple crops** in a single year (e.g., wheat, cotton, sugarcane, paddy). Each crop demands a different **row spacing** and hence a different **track width**.
2. **Traction Variability:** A tractor working in **dry upland soil** needs high lug engagement; in **wet paddy fields**, it needs cage wheels for mud ejection.
3. **Draft Load Variability:** Heavy ploughing or disc harrowing demands **dual wheels** to reduce soil compaction and increase load-bearing capacity.
4. **Time Economics:** In commercial agriculture, downtime is costly. A "quick change" system reduces the wheel-replacement time from **~45 minutes (conventional)** to **~3–5 minutes (quick change)**.
5. **Tool Economy:** A quick change system uses **standard spanners** rather than specialised hub pullers or hydraulic presses.

## 2.2 Engineering Classification of Quick Change Mechanisms

The following classification is mandatory for KTU 2024 module-level questions:

| Mechanism | Engineering Principle | Typical Use |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Reversible Disc Wheel** | Wheel disc can be mounted with either dish facing inward or outward | Track width adjustment |
| **Sliding Hub / Adjustable Rim** | Hub slides on splined axle shaft | Varying track width in **6" increments** |
| **Dual Wheel Spacer** | Auxiliary wheel mounted via spacer/adapter plate | Heavy draft loads |
| **Side-Mounted Reversible Wheel** | Bolted to a flanged hub that can be unbolted and reversed | Inverting wheel side |
| **Telescopic Axle + Bolted Rim** | Axle shaft has multiple holes; rim position is bolted | Row-crop spacing |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula for Module 4 — Quick Change Wheels and Special Wheels. Use $\vert$ only inside the math mode, never inside the markdown table.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------|------------|-------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Effective Rolling Radius (approx.)** | $r_e = 0.95 \, r_t$ | m | For pneumatic tyres; $r_t$ = unloaded tyre radius |
| 2 | **Static Loaded Radius** | $r_s = r_e - \dfrac{h_c}{2}$ | m | $h_c$ = tyre section height (cross-section) |
| 3 | **Overall Diameter (with cage wheel)** | $D_{\text{overall}} = D_{\text{tyre}} + 2 \cdot L_c$ | m | $L_c$ = cage lug length, typically $0.15$–$0.30$ m |
| 4 | **Ground Pressure under Tyre** | $p_g = \dfrac{W_w}{A_c}$ | kPa | $W_w$ = load on wheel, $A_c$ = contact patch area |
| 5 | **Contact Patch Area (approx.)** | $A_c \approx 0.85 \cdot b \cdot L_c$ | m² | $b$ = tyre section width, $L_c$ = contact length |
| 6 | **Effective Track Width (Dual Wheel)** | $B_{\text{eff}} = B + 2 \cdot d_{\text{offset}}$ | m | $d_{\text{offset}}$ = dual-wheel spacer depth |
| 7 | **Wheel Reduction Ratio (Final Drive)** | $i_w = \dfrac{N_r}{N_p}$ | — | $N_r$ = ring gear teeth, $N_p$ = pinion teeth |
| 8 | **Wheel Speed (km/h)** | $v = 0.377 \cdot \dfrac{r_e \cdot N_e}{i_w \cdot i_g}$ | km/h | $N_e$ = engine rpm, $i_g$ = gear ratio |
| 9 | **Cage Wheel Lug Count** | $n_L \ge 12$ (typical $14$–$18$) | — | For self-cleaning in paddy fields |
| 10 | **Tyre Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{h_c}{b}$ | — | $AR = 0.85$–$1.0$ for tractor rear tyres |
| 11 | **Dual Wheel Load Sharing** | $W_{i} = \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{n_w}$ | N | Ideal case; $n_w$ = number of wheels per side |
| 12 | **Wheel Slip (traction)** | $s = 1 - \dfrac{v_{\text{actual}}}{v_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | — | Optimal for tractors: $0.10 \text{ to } 0.20$ |
| 13 | **Cage Wheel Circumferential Pitch** | $p_c = \dfrac{\pi D_c}{n_L}$ | m | $D_c$ = cage wheel pitch diameter |
| 14 | **Inversion Offset** | $\Delta x = t_d$ | m | $t_d$ = disc thickness (gives wheel side shift on reversal) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility of Quick Change & Special Wheels:**
>
> 1. **Agricultural Productivity:** A single tractor with QCW can service 4–5 different crops, increasing utilisation from ~600 hrs/yr to ~1500 hrs/yr.
> 2. **Soil Conservation:** Flotation tyres and dual wheels reduce soil compaction by **30–40%** versus single wheels.
> 3. **Military Mobility:** Special steel-lugged or composite track shoes enable tanks and APCs to traverse mud, snow, and sand.
> 4. **Construction Equipment:** Earthmovers use ribbed and cage wheels to operate in quarries, mines, and wetlands.
> 5. **Forensic Engineering:** Wheel track-width mismatches in tractors cause **row-crop damage** and **operator fatigue** — QCW mitigates this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Rolling Radius from Unloaded Tyre Radius

The relationship between the unloaded tyre radius $r_t$ and the effective rolling radius $r_e$ is empirical, derived from a **first-order energy balance** between stored elastic strain energy and the work done in moving the contact patch forward.

**Starting principle:** A pneumatic tyre flattens at the contact patch. The centre of the wheel moves forward by an effective radius less than the geometric radius. The empirical correction factor is $\mathbf{0.95}$ for typical agricultural pneumatic tyres (source: ASAE Standard S296.5, conceptually).

Let:
- $r_t$ = unloaded (free) tyre radius, m
- $r_e$ = effective rolling radius, m
- $k_e$ = empirical correction factor, dimensionless, $k_e = 0.95$

**Step 1: Define the empirical relation.**

$$
r_e = k_e \cdot r_t
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the standard value for $k_e$.**

$$
r_e = 0.95 \cdot r_t
$$

**Step 3: State the boundary condition.**

$$
0.90 \le k_e \le 0.97 \quad \text{(range covers bias-ply to radial tractor tyres)}
$$

**Step 4: Express the static loaded radius in terms of the effective rolling radius and the tyre section height $h_c$.**

The static loaded radius accounts for vertical compression under load:

$$
r_s = r_e - \frac{h_c}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the typical value $h_c = 0.30 \text{ m}$ (for a 16.9-28 tractor rear tyre).**

$$
r_s = 0.95 \cdot r_t - \frac{0.30}{2}
$$

$$
r_s = 0.95 \cdot r_t - 0.15
$$

**Step 6: Compute numerical example.** For $r_t = 0.70 \text{ m}$:

$$
r_e = 0.95 \times 0.70 = 0.665 \text{ m}
$$

$$
r_s = 0.665 - 0.15 = 0.515 \text{ m}
$$

**Real-world interpretation:** The vehicle's true ground speed is calculated using $r_e$, not $r_t$, otherwise the **speedometer will over-read by ~5%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Track-Width Adjustment via Reversible Disc Wheel

A reversible disc wheel provides two track widths by simply unbolting the wheel from the hub, turning it around (dish facing the opposite direction), and remounting.

**Geometric model:** When the disc is mounted with the dish facing **outward** (standard position), the wheel's inner rim face is closer to the tractor centreline. When reversed (dish facing **inward**), the inner rim face shifts outward by twice the disc thickness $t_d$.

**Step 1: Identify the variables.**

- $B_1$ = original track width (dish out), m
- $B_2$ = new track width (dish in), m
- $t_d$ = disc thickness, m
- $W_{\text{rim}}$ = rim width, m

**Step 2: Geometric relation when dish faces out.**

$$
B_1 = 2 \cdot \left( \frac{t_d}{2} + \frac{W_{\text{rim}}}{2} \right) + C_{\text{axle}}
$$

where $C_{\text{axle}}$ is the centre-to-centre axle stub length. For a single-wheel side:

$$
\frac{B_1}{2} = \frac{t_d}{2} + \frac{W_{\text{rim}}}{2}
$$

**Step 3: When dish is reversed (inward), the disc contribution flips side.**

$$
\frac{B_2}{2} = -\frac{t_d}{2} + \frac{W_{\text{rim}}}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the difference $\Delta B$ between the two configurations.**

$$
\Delta B = B_2 - B_1 = 2 \cdot \left( \frac{W_{\text{rim}}}{2} - \frac{t_d}{2} \right) - 2 \cdot \left( \frac{W_{\text{rim}}}{2} + \frac{t_d}{2} \right)
$$

$$
\Delta B = (W_{\text{rim}} - t_d) - (W_{\text{rim}} + t_d) = -2 t_d
$$

The magnitude of the adjustment is therefore:

$$
\vert \Delta B \vert = 2 t_d
$$

**Step 5: Insert a numerical value.** For $t_d = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$:

$$
\vert \Delta B \vert = 2 \times 0.006 = 0.012 \text{ m} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Critical observation.** A single reversible disc gives only $\mathbf{2 t_d}$ shift, typically $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 16 \text{ mm}}$. To get the full $\mathbf{76 \text{ to } 152 \text{ mm}}$ range needed for row crops, the system must combine the reversal with **sliding hub** positions or **offset rims**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dual-Wheel Load Distribution and Ground Pressure Reduction

The dual-wheel concept reduces ground pressure by spreading the wheel load over a larger contact area. Let us derive the ground pressure.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

- $W_{\text{total}}$ = total weight carried by one side of the rear axle, N
- $n_w$ = number of wheels per side
- $A_{c,s}$ = contact area of a single wheel, m²
- $A_{c,\text{total}}$ = total contact area for $n_w$ wheels, m²

**Step 2: Ideal load sharing (assume equal inflation and equal ground).**

$$
W_i = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{n_w}
$$

**Step 3: Total contact patch area.**

$$
A_{c,\text{total}} = n_w \cdot A_{c,s}
$$

**Step 4: Ground pressure under single wheel.**

$$
p_{g,s} = \frac{W_i}{A_{c,s}} = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{n_w \cdot A_{c,s}}
$$

**Step 5: Pressure ratio between single and dual configuration.**

$$
\frac{p_{g,\text{dual}}}{p_{g,\text{single}}} = \frac{1}{n_w}
$$

For $n_w = 2$ (dual wheel):

$$
p_{g,\text{dual}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot p_{g,\text{single}}
$$

**Step 6: Insert a numerical example.** Let $p_{g,\text{single}} = 120 \text{ kPa}$:

$$
p_{g,\text{dual}} = 0.5 \times 120 = 60 \text{ kPa}
$$

This is the fundamental reason dual wheels are used in **soft, wet, or sandy soil** — the **ground pressure is halved**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat (Examiners love this!):** The above formula assumes perfect load sharing. In practice, the **inner wheel of a dual pair carries ~5–10% more load** than the outer wheel due to camber and structural stiffness. Therefore the **realistic pressure reduction is ~40–45%**, not exactly 50%.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cage Wheel Lug Pitch and Self-Cleaning Criterion

A cage wheel must **self-clean** — every lug must release the mud it picks up before the next lug contacts the ground. Otherwise, the wheel "wheels up" with mud and loses traction.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

- $D_c$ = pitch diameter of cage wheel, m
- $n_L$ = number of lugs on the cage
- $p_c$ = circumferential pitch between lugs, m
- $s_L$ = spacing of tyre lugs (paddy tyres), m

**Step 2: Circumferential pitch equation.**

$$
p_c = \frac{\pi D_c}{n_L}
$$

**Step 3: Self-cleaning criterion.** The cage lug pitch must be **greater than** the tyre lug spacing so that mud is dropped in the gap between tyre lugs:

$$
p_c \ge s_L
$$

$$
\frac{\pi D_c}{n_L} \ge s_L
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the minimum number of lugs required.**

$$
n_L \le \frac{\pi D_c}{s_L}
$$

Wait — the inequality is the *other* way for max lugs. Let me correct: For guaranteed self-cleaning with **finite** lugs, the lug count must be **less than or equal to** the value that makes $p_c \ge s_L$ — but in practice, designers choose $n_L$ in the range $12 \text{ to } 18$.

**Step 5: Numerical example.** For $D_c = 1.2 \text{ m}$ and $s_L = 0.20 \text{ m}$:

$$
n_L = \frac{\pi \times 1.2}{0.20} = \frac{3.7699}{0.20} = 18.85
$$

So, choose $n_L = 18$ lugs (round down to ensure $p_c > s_L$).

**Step 6: Compute the resulting pitch.**

$$
p_c = \frac{\pi \times 1.2}{18} = 0.2094 \text{ m} = 209 \text{ mm} > s_L = 200 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Special Wheel Types

| Wheel Type | Soil Suitability | Traction (μ) | Ground Pressure (kPa) | Typical Application |
|------------|------------------|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Pneumatic (Standard)** | Dry / firm field | 0.4 – 0.5 | 80 – 120 | General tillage |
| **Pneumatic (Flotation)** | Soft / wet field | 0.35 – 0.45 | 40 – 60 | Spraying, harvesting |
| **Cage Wheel** | Paddy / marshy | 0.5 – 0.7 (with cleats) | 25 – 45 | Wet-land paddy |
| **Ground Grip / Lug** | Loose / sandy | 0.55 – 0.75 | 60 – 100 | Soft upland |
| **Ribbed** | Firm, smooth | 0.3 – 0.4 (steering only) | 90 – 130 | Front steering wheels |
| **Solid Rubber** | Industrial, paved | 0.6 – 0.8 (high) | 200 – 400 | Forklifts |
| **Steel (Crawler)** | Rocky, debris | 0.7 – 0.9 | 30 – 60 | Tanks, crawlers |

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Quick Change Reversible Disc Wheel

```
+---------------------------+
|   WHEEL DISC (DISH)       |
|                           |
|   dish OUT  <-----> dish IN    (reversible)
|                           |
|   |  t_d  |               |     t_d = disc thickness
+---|--------|---------------+
    |        |
    |        +--- HUB FLANGE (bolted)
    |
    +----------- TYRE & TUBE ASSEMBLY
```

**Annotations:**

- When the disc is mounted with **dish out** (standard), the wheel's centre plane is shifted **inward** by $t_d/2$.
- When the disc is mounted with **dish in** (reversed), the wheel's centre plane is shifted **outward** by $t_d/2$.
- Net track-width change: $\Delta B = 2 t_d \approx \mathbf{12 \text{ mm}}$ per reversal.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set 1]*

**Q: Define Quick Change Wheels. List any four field conditions where they are preferred over conventional wheel arrangements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Quick Change Wheels are wheel arrangements that allow rapid change of track width, dual-wheel configuration, or side mounting without specialised tools, typically in under 5 minutes per wheel.

> **Four field conditions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Varying row-crop spacings** — e.g., 30", 36", 40" rows.
> 2. **Wet/paddy field operations** — where cage wheels must be added.
> 3. **Heavy draft loads** — where dual wheels are required.
> 4. **Mixed upland + soft-soil farming** — track width and wheel type must be reconfigured.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**Q: Differentiate between a Cage Wheel and a Ribbed Wheel. State the typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cage Wheel (1.5 marks):**
> - Constructed as an open cylindrical cage with 12–18 angled lugs welded to two side rings.
> - Diameter typically 1.0–1.4 m.
> - Application: **Wet paddy and marshy field** operations; it allows mud to pass through and self-cleans.

> **Ribbed Wheel (1.5 marks):**
> - Smooth ribbed tread, no aggressive lugs.
> - Application: **Front steering wheels of tractors** for row-crop and inter-row cultivation; minimises crop damage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, June 2024]*

**Q: With the help of neat diagrams, explain the following Quick Change Wheel arrangements used in agricultural tractors:**

**(a) Reversible disc wheel for track-width adjustment (7 marks)**
**(b) Dual wheel arrangement for heavy draft loads (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Reversible Disc Wheel (7 marks)

**Step 1: Define (1 mark).**
A reversible disc wheel is a wheel whose disc is **symmetrically dished** and can be unbolted from the hub and remounted in the **opposite direction** to change the track width.

**Step 2: Working principle (2 marks).**
- When the dish faces **outward** (standard), the wheel's mounting face is closer to the tractor centreline → **narrow track**.
- When the dish faces **inward** (reversed), the wheel's mounting face is farther from the centreline → **wide track**.
- The shift per reversal is $2 t_d$, where $t_d$ is the disc thickness.

**Step 3: Sketch the two configurations (2 marks).**
A simple ASCII diagram was shown in Section 4.4 above. In the answer script, students should draw:
- Wheel cross-section with dish-out.
- Wheel cross-section with dish-in.
- Annotate the $2 t_d$ shift.

**Step 4: Calculate typical shift (1 mark).**
For $t_d = 6$ mm:

$$
\vert \Delta B \vert = 2 t_d = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: State advantage + limitation (1 mark).**
- *Advantage:* No new components needed; uses the same rim and tyre.
- *Limitation:* Provides only ~12 mm adjustment; multiple reversals or sliding hubs are needed for full row-crop range.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Dual Wheel Arrangement (7 marks)

**Step 1: Define (1 mark).**
A dual-wheel arrangement mounts **two wheels side-by-side** on the same axle stub, separated by a **spacer ring or adapter plate**, to double the contact area and halve the ground pressure.

**Step 2: Components (1 mark).**
- Inner wheel (carries the standard lugged tyre).
- Outer wheel (matched tyre, may be a plain ribbed or lugged type).
- Spacer ring (100–200 mm).
- Extended lug bolts.

**Step 3: Working principle + formula (2 marks).**
$$
p_{g,\text{dual}} = \frac{p_{g,\text{single}}}{n_w} = \frac{p_{g,\text{single}}}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example (1 mark).**
For $p_{g,\text{single}} = 120$ kPa:

$$
p_{g,\text{dual}} = 60 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Applications + advantages (2 marks).**
- Heavy ploughing in soft soil.
- Reduces soil compaction by ~40–50%.
- Increases tractive effort by 15–25%.
- *Disadvantage:* Increased unsprung mass, higher fuel consumption, and potential for uneven wear.

---

### Question B1B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Equivalent Module 4]*

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working of a Cage Wheel used in paddy field tractors. Why is it preferred over standard pneumatic tyres in wet conditions? (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the relationship between single-wheel ground pressure and dual-wheel ground pressure, and explain its significance in soil compaction. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Cage Wheel (7 marks)

**Step 1: Construction (2 marks).**
- Two side rings (steel) bolted to the wheel hub.
- 12–18 angled steel lugs welded between the rings, forming a cylindrical "cage".
- Pitch diameter: 1.0–1.4 m.
- Lug length: 150–300 mm.
- Mounted coaxially **outside** the pneumatic drive wheel.

**Step 2: Working in paddy (2 marks).**
- The cage lugs **engage the soft mud** and provide traction that the pneumatic tyre alone cannot.
- The cage lugs **self-clean** — mud is dropped in the gaps between the lugs as the wheel rotates.
- The cage floats the tractor on the wet soil, distributing weight over a large area.

**Step 3: Why preferred over pneumatic in wet conditions (3 marks).**
1. **Pneumatic tyres clog** with wet mud and become slippery.
2. Cage wheels have **open centres** that expel mud by centrifugal action.
3. Cage wheels have **lower ground pressure** (~25–45 kPa) versus pneumatic (~80–120 kPa).
4. Cage wheels provide **higher tractive coefficient** (μ ≈ 0.5–0.7) in mud.
5. Cage wheels prevent the tractor from **"wheel-deep sinkage"** in paddy.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Ground Pressure Derivation (7 marks)

The detailed derivation is in **Section 3.3** above. The answer must reproduce:

**Step 1: State the variables (1 mark).**
- $W_{\text{total}}$ = axle load, $n_w$ = wheels per side, $A_c$ = contact area.

**Step 2: Single-wheel ground pressure (1.5 marks).**
$$
p_{g,s} = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{A_{c,s}}
$$

**Step 3: Dual-wheel load sharing (1.5 marks).**
$$
W_i = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{n_w}, \quad n_w = 2
$$

**Step 4: Dual-wheel ground pressure (1.5 marks).**
$$
p_{g,d} = \frac{W_{\text{total}} / n_w}{A_{c,s}} = \frac{p_{g,s}}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Significance for soil compaction (1.5 marks).**
- Lower $p_g$ → less soil compaction → better root growth → higher yield.
- Typical reduction: ~40–50% in pressure.
- Critical in **paddy, sugarcane, and potato cultivation** where compaction destroys soil structure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Quick Change & Special Wheels Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in track-width derivation.** The shift is $2 t_d$, not $t_d$. Many students write $\Delta B = t_d$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing "effective rolling radius" with "static loaded radius".** $r_e$ is used for vehicle speed, $r_s$ is used for ground clearance. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Idealising dual-wheel load sharing.** Real-world load sharing is **not perfectly even** — the inner wheel carries ~5–10% more load. Writing $W_i = W_{\text{total}} / 2$ without this caveat loses a mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the self-cleaning criterion for cage wheels.** A cage wheel with $n_L = 6$ will not self-clean; the examiner expects $n_L \ge 12$ as a stated rule.
> 5. **Mixing up ribbed and lugged wheels.** Ribbed wheels are **steering wheels (front)**; lugged/ground-grip wheels are **drive wheels (rear)**. Confusing these loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the boundary condition in formulas.** Always state the range (e.g., $0.90 \le k_e \le 0.97$) — KTU 2024 scheme rewards "range" statements in 3-mark questions.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in long-answer questions.** A 14-mark question without a labelled diagram forfeits at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Quick Change Wheels & Special Wheels):**

- **Quick Change Wheel definition:** Wheel arrangement allowing rapid reconfiguration of track width, side, or dual setup without specialised tools.
- **Reversible disc wheel shift:** $\Delta B = 2 t_d$, typically $10$–$16$ mm per reversal.
- **Sliding hub system:** Adjusts track width in discrete increments (typically $4$" or $6$" — $100$–$150$ mm).
- **Dual wheel spacer thickness:** $100$–$200$ mm.
- **Dual-wheel ground pressure:** $p_{g,d} = p_{g,s} / 2$ (ideal), but ~40–50% reduction in practice.
- **Effective rolling radius:** $r_e = 0.95 \cdot r_t$ (empirical, ASAE).
- **Static loaded radius:** $r_s = r_e - h_c / 2$.
- **Cage wheel pitch diameter:** $1.0$–$1.4$ m; lug count $12$–$18$; lug length $150$–$300$ mm.
- **Cage wheel self-cleaning criterion:** $p_c = \pi D_c / n_L \ge s_L$ (tyre lug spacing).
- **Wheel slip target (tractor):** $s = 0.10$–$0.20$.
- **Aspect ratio of tractor rear tyres:** $0.85$–$1.0$.
- **Pneumatic vs Cage wheel traction coefficient:** Pneumatic $\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.5$, Cage $\mu \approx 0.5$–$0.7$.
- **Flotation tyre purpose:** Reduce $p_g$ to $40$–$60$ kPa on soft soil.
- **Ribbed wheel application:** Front steering; minimises crop damage during inter-row work.
- **Solid rubber tyre application:** Industrial forklifts; $p_g = 200$–$400$ kPa.
- **Steel crawler wheel application:** Military tanks; $\mu = 0.7$–$0.9$.
- **Track-width standards:** $1.2$–$2.1$ m; row-crop spacings $76, 91, 102$ cm.
- **Inflation pressure range (tractor field):** $80$–$120$ kPa.
- **Ground pressure hierarchy (lowest to highest):** Cage < Flotation < Pneumatic < Solid Rubber.
- **Examiner focus topics (high weightage):** Track-width derivation, dual-wheel ground pressure, cage wheel self-cleaning.
